# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the five major components of a robotic system.**

**Model Answer:**

A robotic system is composed of five integrated sub-systems:

1. **Manipulator (Mechanical Structure):** A series of rigid links connected by joints (revolute or prismatic) that form a kinematic chain. It provides the physical reach and structure. Example: 6-DOF articulated arm.

2. **End-Effector:** The tool or gripper attached to the robot's wrist flange that interacts directly with the workpiece. Examples include mechanical grippers, vacuum suction cups, and welding torches.

3. **Actuators and Drive System:** Energy conversion devices (servo motors, pneumatic cylinders, hydraulic pistons) that produce the mechanical motion at each joint. Transmission elements like harmonic drives and ball screws connect the actuator to the joint.

4. **Sensors:** Devices that gather internal state (encoders, resolvers) and external environment data (vision, force, proximity, LiDAR). They form the perception layer of the robot.

5. **Controller and Power Supply:** The "brain" (CPU + memory + I/O) executes the control algorithm and supervises motion. The power supply provides and conditions the required energy (AC mains, DC bus, compressed air, hydraulic fluid).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series Exam 1]`
**Differentiate between proprioceptive and exteroceptive sensors with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Proprioceptive Sensors | Exteroceptive Sensors |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Sense the **internal state** of the robot | Sense the **external environment** |
| **Quantities measured** | Joint angles, velocities, currents, accelerations | Object position, shape, force, distance |
| **Examples** | (i) Optical encoder; (ii) Resolver; (iii) Tachometer; (iv) IMU | (i) Vision camera (2D/3D); (ii) Force-torque sensor; (iii) LiDAR; (iv) Tactile array |
| **Analogy** | Human's proprioception (sense of own limb position) | Human's vision and touch |
| **Feedback role** | Essential for servo control loop | Essential for task-level adaptation |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different types of robot configurations based on joint arrangement. Draw a neat sketch of any two configurations and state two industrial applications of each.**

**Model Solution:**

The five standard robot configurations based on joint arrangement are:

**1. Cartesian / Gantry (PPP):** Three prismatic joints along mutually perpendicular axes (X, Y, Z). 
- *Sketch:* A rectangular gantry frame with a horizontal beam and a vertically sliding carriage carrying the tool.
- *Applications:* (i) CNC pick-and-place machines; (ii) 3D printers; (iii) Palletizing in warehouses.
- *Property:* Large rectangular workspace, highest positional accuracy.

**2. Cylindrical (RPP):** One revolute joint at the base and two prismatic joints (vertical and horizontal radial).
- *Sketch:* A vertical column that rotates about its axis, with a horizontal arm that extends radially and a vertical slider for Z-motion.
- *Applications:* (i) Spot welding in foundries; (ii) Machine tending on lathes.
- *Property:* Workspace is a cylindrical volume.

**3. Spherical / Polar (RRP):** Two revolute joints and one prismatic joint. The reach is along a radial line.
- *Sketch:* Like a human shoulder + elbow + telescopic forearm.
- *Applications:* (i) Die casting; (ii) Heavy-payload forging; (iii) Paint spraying in automotive.
- *Property:* Workspace is a spherical sector.

**4. SCARA (RRP):** Two parallel revolute joints in the horizontal plane and a vertical prismatic joint.
- *Sketch:* A horizontal arm with two R joints, and a vertical slider for Z.
- *Applications:* (i) High-speed PCB assembly; (ii) Pick-and-place of small components.
- *Property:* Compliant in XY, rigid in Z — perfect for vertical insertion.

**5. Articulated / Anthropomorphic (RRR):** Three revolute joints (waist, shoulder, elbow) plus a wrist.
- *Sketch:* Human arm with 3 R joints at the base + 3 R joints at the wrist.
- *Applications:* (i) Arc welding in automotive; (ii) Painting; (iii) Sealing.
- *Property:* Maximum dexterity, largest workspace per unit floor area.

> **Tip for diagrams:** Use neat block diagrams with the kinematic chain labeled (PPP, RPP, etc.) and an arrow showing the motion direction of each joint.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**With a neat block diagram, explain the various components of an industrial robot system and their functions.**

**Model Solution:**

A complete industrial robot system consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator):** Comprises the links, joints, bearings, and mechanical housings. Its function is to physically position and orient the end-effector. Joints can be revolute (rotation) or prismatic (linear). Total DOF = number of independently actuated joints.

2. **Drive System (Actuators + Transmission):** Provides the energy conversion and torque/speed conversion. Three main types:
   - **Electric:** BLDC/AC servo motors — most common; combined with harmonic or cycloidal gearboxes.
   - **Pneumatic:** Air cylinders and rotary actuators — used for simple, low-cost pick-place.
   - **Hydraulic:** Oil pistons and motors — for very high payloads (>500 kg).
   - **Transmission:** Gearboxes, belts, lead/ball screws, cables.

3. **Sensor System:** 
   - *Internal:* Encoders, resolvers, current sensors — close the joint servo loop.
   - *External:* Vision, force/torque, proximity, tactile — enable adaptive behavior.

4. **Controller:** The central processing unit. It (i) interprets the program, (ii) generates trajectory setpoints, (iii) executes the closed-loop servo algorithm (PID) at **1–8 kHz**, and (iv) handles I/O, communication (EtherCAT, PROFINET), and safety (E-stops, safety zones).

5. **Power Supply:** Provides energy to actuators and electronics. For electric robots: 3-phase 400 V AC → DC bus 48/72 V. For pneumatic: compressor + FRL. For hydraulic: pump + reservoir.

6. **End-Effector / Tooling:** The gripper, vacuum cup, welding torch, or custom tool that performs the actual task.

> **Flow of information:**
> Program → Controller → Servo Drives → Actuators → Joint Motion → End-Effector
> Encoder feedback → Controller (closed loop) — completes the control cycle.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the working principle of a robotic gripper. List and briefly describe the different types of grippers used in industrial robots.**

**Model Solution:**

A **robotic gripper** is the end-effector designed to grasp, hold, and release objects. Its working principle involves:
1. Actuation (electric, pneumatic, or hydraulic) to close/open fingers.
2. Contact force application on the object's surface.
3. Friction or form closure to maintain hold during manipulation.
4. Sensing (force, tactile) for adaptive grasp.

**Types of Grippers:**

1. **Mechanical / Finger Grippers:** 
   - Use two or more fingers (jaws) that close on the object.
   - Actuation: pneumatic cylinder, electric servo, or cam-driven.
   - Subtypes: *parallel*, *angular*, *three-finger (centric)*.
   - *Use:* General pick-and-place of rigid parts.

2. **Vacuum Grippers:**
   - Use suction cups connected to a vacuum source (Venturi ejector or vacuum pump).
   - Best for smooth, flat, non-porous surfaces.
   - *Use:* Sheet metal handling, glass panels, cardboard boxes, food packaging.

3. **Magnetic Grippers:**
   - Use permanent magnets (PM) or electromagnets (EM) to attract ferrous workpieces.
   - EM can be turned on/off; PM is energy-free but needs a stripper.
   - *Use:* Steel sheet handling in press shops.

4. **Adhesive Grippers:**
   - Use a tacky polymer surface that adheres to the object on contact.
   - Inspired by gecko feet.
   - *Use:* Delicate items, fabrics, glass, electronics.

5. **Servo-Electric Adaptive Grippers:**
   - Use BLDC motors and position/force control.
   - Can be programmed for variable stroke and force.
   - *Use:* Collaborative robots (cobots) with soft-touch requirements.

6. **Specialty / Multifinger Grippers (Anthropomorphic Hands):**
   - Multiple articulated fingers (e.g., Shadow Hand, Robotiq 3-Finger).
   - Used in research and advanced manipulation tasks.

**Selection parameters:** Object weight, surface texture, required precision, cycle time, and environment (clean, oily, hot).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Define the term 'Degree of Freedom' (DOF) of a robot. Calculate the DOF of a 4-DOF SCARA robot using Kutzbach–Grübler equation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is the number of independent parameters (translations and rotations) that uniquely specify the configuration (pose) of the robot's end-effector in 3D space. For a manipulator with $n$ joints, each contributing one DOF, the robot's DOF is typically $n$ (assuming no closed loops or passive joints).

**Generalized Kutzbach–Grübler Formula for Spatial Mechanism:**

$$M = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j} (6 - f_i)$$

where:
- $M$ = mobility / DOF
- $n$ = total number of links (including the fixed base)
- $j$ = number of joints
- $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$

**Step-by-Step Solution for SCARA Robot (R-R-P-R):**

**Step 1:** Identify the links and joints.
- SCARA has 5 links (1 fixed base + 4 moving links: link 1, link 2, vertical slider, wrist).
- Hence, $n = 5$, so $n - 1 = 4$ moving links.
- 4 joints: 3 revolute (R₁, R₂, R₄) and 1 prismatic (P₃).
- $n_R = 3$, $n_P = 1$, $n_{SP} = 0$, $n_C = 0$, $n_E = 0$.

**Step 2:** Apply the simplified equation:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5 \cdot n_R - 4 \cdot n_P$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(4) - 5(3) - 4(1) \\
  &= 24 - 15 - 4 \\
  &= 5 \text{ DOF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation.

The mathematical calculation yields **5 DOF**. However, one DOF (the passive compliance in the horizontal XY plane) is **not independently actuated**. Industrial SCARAs are therefore marketed as **4-axis robots** with 4 controlled motors.

> **Conclusion:** The SCARA robot possesses 5 mathematical DOF (4 controlled axes + 1 passive compliance), which makes it ideal for vertical assembly tasks like PCB component insertion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DOF with the number of joints** — DOF = number of *independent* parameters. A 6-joint arm has 6 DOF only if all joints are independently actuated. (Penalty: −1 mark per occurrence.)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to count the base link** in Kutzbach–Grübler formula. Always $n$ = moving links + 1 (for the fixed ground). Forgetting this gives a wrong answer by +1 DOF. (Penalty: −2 marks in 7-mark sub-parts.)
> 
> 3. **Listing only 4 components** when asked to list the 5 sub-systems. The five are: Manipulator, End-effector, Actuator, Sensor, Controller + Power Supply. (Penalty: −2 marks.)
> 
> 4. **Mixing "axes" and "DOF"** in non-articulated robots. In a Cartesian (PPP) robot, axes = DOF = 3. In a SCARA, mathematical DOF = 5, controlled axes = 4. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the closed-loop feedback** in diagrams. Every block diagram of a robotic system **must show** the encoder feedback arrow returning to the controller. Missing it = −1 mark.
> 
> 6. **Writing "motor" instead of "actuator"** in higher-mark questions. "Actuator" is the generic term; "motor" is a specific type. Use the precise term.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Robotic system** = integrated mechatronic assembly of 5 sub-systems: Manipulator, End-Effector, Actuators + Drives, Sensors, Controller + Power.
- **Two joint types:** Revolute (R, rotation) and Prismatic (P, linear). Compound = Spherical (3R), Cylindrical (2R+1P), Planar (3 DOF in plane).
- **5 standard configurations:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), SCARA (RRP), Articulated (RRR+RRR wrist).
- **DOF formula (Kutzbach–Grübler):** $M = 6(n-1) - 5n_R - 4n_P - 3n_C - 2n_E - 1 \cdot n_{SP}$.
- **Standard 6-DOF industrial robot = R-R-R-R-R-R** = 3 position DOF + 3 orientation DOF.
- **Sensors split:** Proprioceptive (encoders, resolvers, IMU) + Exteroceptive (vision, force, LiDAR, tactile).
- **Actuators:** Electric (most common), Pneumatic (fast, simple), Hydraulic (high force).
- **End-effectors:** Mechanical grippers, vacuum cups, magnetic grippers, adhesive, servo-electric, multifinger hands.
- **Controller** runs servo loop at **1–8 kHz**, uses PID or model-based control.
- **TCP velocity relation:** $\dot{x} = J(q) \dot{q}$ — Jacobian links joint rates to Cartesian rates.
- **Singularity:** $\det(J) = 0$ — robot loses DOF in Cartesian space; joint rates blow up.
- **Workspace of spherical arm with equal links:** $V = \frac{16\pi r^3}{3}$ (twice the volume of a solid sphere).
- **Power at joint:** $P_{electrical} = \frac{\tau \omega}{\eta_{gear} \cdot \eta_{motor}}$.
- **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy:** Repeatability is the ability to return to a taught point; accuracy is the ability to reach an absolute commanded point.
- **SCARA = Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm:** Compliant in XY, rigid in Z.
- **Harmonic drives:** High-ratio, zero-backlash, compact — used in robot wrists.
- **ISO 9409-1** is the standard mechanical flange interface for end-effectors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Measures in Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Safety Measures in Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Safety in Robotics

**Safety in Robotics** refers to the integrated set of engineering principles, hardware safeguards, software protocols, regulatory standards, and operational procedures designed to eliminate or adequately mitigate physical, electrical, and informational hazards arising from the deployment and operation of robotic systems within human-shared and industrial environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to **ISO 10218-1:2011** and **ISO 10218-2:2011** (the foundational international standards for industrial robot safety), safety is not a single component but a **layered, defense-in-depth architecture** that must be designed into the robot cell from the conceptual stage itself — not retrofitted after deployment.

The discipline of robotic safety addresses four fundamental pillars:

| Pillar | Focus |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical Safety** | Physical barriers, interlocks, sharp edges, crushing zones |
| **Electrical Safety** | Lockout/Tagout (LOTO), earthing, insulation, emergency power cutoff |
| **Control-System Safety** | Safety PLCs, redundant circuits, fault monitoring |
| **Operational Safety** | Risk assessment, training, SOPs, PPE |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Robotic Cell as a Modern Automobile:**

Think of an industrial robot cell exactly like a **modern car**:
- The **seatbelt** = Mechanical safety barrier (physical guard)
- The **airbag** = Emergency stop (E-Stop) circuit — activated *only* in crisis
- The **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** = Safety-rated monitored stop — prevents catastrophic motion
- The **adaptive cruise control** = Speed & Separation Monitoring (SSM) — dynamically maintains safe distance from a human
- The **crash test rating (NCAP)** = ISO 10218 certification — pre-validated safety performance
- The **driver's license** = Operator training and certification

Just as a car uses **redundant, overlapping safety systems** (belt + airbag + ABS + crumple zones) so that no single failure causes death, a robotic cell uses **redundant safety layers** (guard + interlock + E-Stop + light curtain + software limits) so that no single fault allows human injury.

## 1.3 Why Safety is Non-Negotiable in Robotics

Robotic systems possess unique danger profiles that distinguish them from conventional machinery:

- **High kinetic energy:** Industrial robots can operate at speeds exceeding **3 m/s** with payloads up to **1000 kg**, storing massive momentum.
- **Unpredictable motion patterns:** A 6-axis robot's end-effector can sweep through complex 3D envelopes, often outside the operator's intuitive line of sight.
- **High force capability:** Hydraulic and servo-driven actuators can exert forces capable of crushing human bone (failure load of the human femur ≈ **4000 N**).
- **Stored energy:** Pneumatic accumulators, hydraulic reservoirs, and capacitor banks retain lethal energy even after power removal.
- **Shared workspaces:** The rise of **Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC)** means humans and robots now occupy the same volume, dramatically increasing exposure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **International Federation of Robotics (IFR)** reported that in industrial robot accidents, approximately **77% of injuries occur during maintenance, programming, or setup tasks** — not during normal operation. This statistic is the central reason why safety measures such as Lockout/Tagout and reduced-speed maintenance modes are heavily emphasized in KTU Module 1.

## 1.4 Standardization Bodies and Key Standards

| Standard | Scope |
|---|---|
| **ISO 10218-1** | Safety requirements for the robot itself (manufacturer side) |
| **ISO 10218-2** | Safety requirements for the robot system / cell integration |
| **ISO/TS 15066** | Collaborative robot-specific guidelines (biomechanical limits) |
| **ISO 13849-1** | Safety-related parts of control systems (Performance Levels a–e) |
| **ISO 13855** | Positioning of safeguards w.r.t. approach speeds |
| **ANSI/RIA R15.06** | North American adoption of ISO 10218 |
| **IEC 61508** | Functional safety of electrical/electronic systems (SIL ratings) |
| **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.147** | Lockout/Tagout regulation (USA) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hazard Classification in Robotics

A **hazard** is any source of potential harm. In robotics, hazards are systematically classified as follows:

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Hazards
- **Crushing** — between robot and fixed structure
- **Shearing** — between two moving parts moving toward each other
- **Cutting / Severing** — sharp end-effectors, rotating tools
- **Entanglement** — rotating spindles, cabling
- **Stabbing / Puncture** — pointed grippers
- **Friction / Abrasion** — sliding contacts
- **High-pressure fluid injection** — hydraulic/pneumatic leaks

### 2.1.2 Electrical Hazards
- Direct contact with live conductors
- Arc flash from capacitor banks in servo drives
- Static discharge near flammable atmospheres

### 2.1.3 Thermal Hazards
- Hot motor housings, welding sparks, laser end-effectors

### 2.1.4 Ergonomic / Psychosocial Hazards
- Repetitive stress in HRC tasks
- Cognitive overload during human supervision

### 2.1.5 Environmental Hazards
- Noise from pneumatic exhausts
- Toxic fumes from welding/painting robots

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The "**Trinity of Damage Severity**" — Severity (S), Probability of Occurrence (P), and Frequency of Exposure (F) — together determine the **Risk Score (R)**, which drives the choice of mitigation strategy.

## 2.2 Risk Assessment Methodology

The standard risk assessment pipeline (per **ISO 12100:2010**) executed for every robotic cell:

1. **Identify** the machine limits and use scenarios
2. **Identify** all reasonably foreseeable hazards
3. **Estimate** the risk for each hazard (Severity × Probability)
4. **Evaluate** whether risk is tolerable
5. **Reduce** unacceptable risk through inherent safe design, safeguarding, and information for use

### 2.2.1 Risk Score Equation

The foundational risk equation used across KTU assessment questions is:

$$R = S \times P \times F$$

Where:
- $S$ = **Severity of potential harm** (1 = negligible, 2 = serious injury, 3 = death/loss of limb)
- $P$ = **Probability of occurrence** (1 = very low, 2 = possible, 3 = likely)
- $F$ = **Frequency / duration of exposure** (1 = rarely, 2 = frequent, 3 = continuous)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Risk Reduction Hierarchy (the "Three-Step Method")** — applied in strict order:
> 1. **Inherent Safe Design** (eliminate the hazard at source — e.g., reduce robot reach, lower payload)
> 2. **Safeguarding** (add guards, interlocks, light curtains, E-Stops)
> 3. **Information for Use** (warnings, training, PPE, signage)

This sequence is **mandatory** — you cannot skip to PPE if a guard would suffice. KTU examiners routinely test this ordering.

## 2.3 Safety Distance Calculation (ISO 13855)

The **minimum safety distance** from a safeguard (light curtain, mat, scanner) to the hazard point is calculated as:

$$S = K \times T + C$$

Where:
- $S$ = minimum safety distance, in **mm**
- $K$ = approach speed parameter, in **mm/s**
  - $K = 1600$ mm/s for walking approach (standard human walking speed)
  - $K = 2000$ mm/s for reaching over/through a safeguard
- $T$ = total stopping time of the **complete system**, in **seconds**
  - $T = t_i + t_r + t_s$
  - $t_i$ = reaction time of the safeguard (sensor latency)
  - $t_r$ = reaction time of the safety relay / PLC
  - $t_s$ = mechanical stopping time of the robot
- $C$ = intrusion distance beyond the safeguard into the hazard zone, in **mm**
  - $C = 850$ mm (reach-through) for vertical light curtains
  - $C = 0$ for floor-level mats

For **reach-through** safeguards (operator can reach *over* the safeguard), the corrected equation is:

$$S = K \times T + C - d$$

Where $d$ is the horizontal distance from the safeguard to the hazard point (must satisfy $d \geq 850$ mm or use reach-over formula).

## 2.4 Functional Safety — Performance Level (PL) and Safety Integrity Level (SIL)

### 2.4.1 Performance Level (PL) per ISO 13849-1

Five discrete levels (a → e) representing the reliability of safety control subsystems:

| PL | Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour (PFHd) | Equivalent Capability |
|---|---|---|
| **PL a** | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ | Basic |
| **PL b** | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ | — |
| **PL c** | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ | — |
| **PL d** | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ (slightly better) | **Standard for robot E-Stops** |
| **PL e** | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ | **Highest, used for collaborative operations** |

### 2.4.2 SIL per IEC 61508

| SIL | PFHd | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) |
|---|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 10–100 |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ | 100–1000 |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-7}$ | 1000–10000 |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-9}$ to $10^{-8}$ | 10000–100000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** PL **d** and SIL **2** are the most commonly required ratings for industrial robot E-Stop circuits. Collaborative robots typically require PL **d** or **e** on every safety function.

## 2.5 Collaborative Robot Safety (ISO/TS 15066)

The four permissible collaborative operation modes (often called "**The Four P's of Collaboration**"):

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — robot halts when human enters workspace; resumes only after human exits
2. **Hand-Guiding (HG)** — operator physically moves the robot via a validated handle; robot motion only as commanded
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — robot dynamically adjusts speed based on real-time measured human distance
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — robot's mechanical and control systems are designed so contact forces and pressures cannot exceed biomechanical thresholds

### 2.5.1 Biomechanical Limits (ISO/TS 15066)

| Body Region | Quasi-Static Contact Force (N) | Transient Contact Force (N) | Max Pressure (N/cm²) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Skull / Forehead | 130 | 175 | — |
| Face | 65 | 90 | — |
| Neck | 140 | 190 | — |
| Back | 210 | 250 | — |
| Chest / Abdomen | 140 | 320 | **140** |
| Pelvis | 210 | 320 | — |
| Upper Arms / Legs | 150 | 220 | — |
| Hands / Wrists | 140 | 200 | **160** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **PFL collaborative robots**, the maximum permissible transient contact force on the chest/abdomen is **320 N**, with a maximum sustained pressure of **140 N/cm²**. These are hard limits, not guidelines.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Score** | $R = S \times P \times F$ | dimensionless | $S,P,F \in \{1,2,3\}$ |
| **Safety Distance** | $S = K \times T + C$ | mm | $K = 1600$ mm/s (walking) |
| **Reach-Through** | $S = K \times T + C - d$ | mm | $C = 850$ mm |
| **Total Stop Time** | $T = t_i + t_r + t_s$ | s | sum of three delays |
| **PFHd (PL d)** | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-6}$ | /h | required for E-Stop |
| **Max Transient Force (PFL)** | $F_{max} = 320$ N (chest) | N | ISO/TS 15066 |
| **Max Pressure (PFL)** | $P_{max} = 140$ N/cm² (chest) | N/cm² | ISO/TS 15066 |
| **Approach Speed (reach)** | $K = 2000$ | mm/s | reaching motion |
| **Stopping Distance Kinematic** | $d_s = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2} a t^2$ | m | $a$ = deceleration |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Welding Cells:** Use **PL d** light curtains at calculated $S$ values; miscalculation by even 50 mm can be fatal.
- **Cobot Assembly:** Universal Robots, FANUC CRX, and ABB GoFa™ use **PFL** with skin-safe contact monitoring.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Use redundant **SIL 3** electronics to prevent any single-point failure during patient contact.
- **Warehouse AMRs:** Use **LiDAR safety scanners** with **SSM** to dynamically slow or stop when humans cross paths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Safety Distance from First Principles

We will derive the safety distance equation from a **kinematic model** of a human approaching a hazard.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider a human operator walking toward a robot hazard point. The safeguard (e.g., light curtain) is positioned at distance $S$ from the hazard. Upon breach of the safeguard, the system must:
1. Detect the breach (time $t_i$)
2. Process the signal in the safety relay (time $t_r$)
3. Physically decelerate and stop the robot (time $t_s$)

The total system stopping time is therefore:

$$T = t_i + t_r + t_s$$

### 3.1.2 Distance Travelled by Human During T

Assuming constant approach speed $K$ (mm/s), the human moves a distance:

$$d_{human} = K \times T$$

### 3.1.3 Intrusion Distance

Even after the safeguard triggers, the operator's hand or body can physically intrude into the hazard zone by an additional distance $C$ (the distance from the safeguard plane to the hazard point along the operator's reach vector).

### 3.1.4 Combining for Safety

The **minimum safety distance** must satisfy:

$$S \geq d_{human} + C$$

Substituting the expression for $d_{human}$:

$$S \geq K \times T + C$$

Which is precisely the ISO 13855 standard equation:

$$\boxed{S = K \times T + C}$$

This derivation reveals a key insight: $S$ is a **lower bound** — any installation with $S$ smaller than this value can be reached by a human before the system can stop, violating the safety function.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Light Curtain Safety Distance

**Problem:** A robotic palletizer operates in a cell protected by a vertical light curtain. The approach is by walking. The sensor has a reaction time $t_i = 30$ ms, the safety relay $t_r = 20$ ms, and the robot's mechanical stopping time $t_s = 250$ ms. Compute the minimum safety distance if the intrusion distance $C = 850$ mm.

### Step 1 — Total Stopping Time

$$T = t_i + t_r + t_s = 30 + 20 + 250 = 300 \text{ ms}$$

Convert to seconds for use with $K$ in mm/s:

$$T = 0.300 \text{ s}$$

### Step 2 — Select K

For walking approach, the ISO 13855 standard value is:

$$K = 1600 \text{ mm/s}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Safety Distance Equation

$$S = K \times T + C$$

$$S = 1600 \times 0.300 + 850$$

$$S = 480 + 850$$

$$\boxed{S = 1330 \text{ mm} = 1.33 \text{ m}}$$

### Step 4 — Sanity Check

- Operator moving at 1.6 m/s for 0.3 s covers 0.48 m
- Adding reach-through intrusion of 0.85 m
- Total: 1.33 m — physically reasonable for an industrial cell

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Reach-Through Safeguard

**Problem:** An operator reaches **over** a horizontal light curtain located at height 1.0 m. The hazard (a moving conveyor) is $d = 500$ mm beyond the curtain horizontally. Sensor delay $t_i = 15$ ms, relay $t_r = 10$ ms, robot $t_s = 180$ ms. Walking approach, $C = 850$ mm. Find $S$.

### Step 1 — Total Stopping Time

$$T = 15 + 10 + 180 = 205 \text{ ms} = 0.205 \text{ s}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Reach-Through Formula

$$S = K \times T + C - d$$

$$S = 1600 \times 0.205 + 850 - 500$$

$$S = 328 + 850 - 500$$

$$\boxed{S = 678 \text{ mm}}$$

### Step 3 — Validation Against Reach-Through Constraint

For reach-through, the standard mandates $d \geq 850$ mm. Here $d = 500$ mm < 850 mm, so the reach-over formula must be used, or the installation is non-compliant.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Risk Score Calculation

**Problem:** A 6-axis robot in a welding cell has:
- Severity: Could amputate a limb → $S = 3$
- Probability: Possible, has occurred in similar cells → $P = 2$
- Frequency: Operator inside the cell 4 hours/day → $F = 2$

Compute the risk score and recommend mitigation.

### Step 1 — Compute Risk Score

$$R = S \times P \times F = 3 \times 2 \times 2 = 12$$

### Step 2 — Interpret on the Risk Matrix

| R Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| 1–3 | Acceptable |
| 4–8 | Tolerable with controls |
| 9–27 | **Unacceptable — must reduce** |

$R = 12$ → **Unacceptable risk**. Immediate mitigation required.

### Step 3 — Apply the Risk Reduction Hierarchy

1. **Inherent:** Reduce robot reach by repositioning, reduce speed
2. **Safeguarding:** Install light curtain ($S \geq 1.5$ m), hard guarding on non-load sides, dual-channel E-Stop at PL d
3. **Information:** Operator training, lockout/tagout, arc-flash PPE, signage

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — PFL Force and Pressure Verification

**Problem:** A cobot gripper can exert peak transient force of 280 N on an operator's hand during a fault. The contact area of the gripper pad is 4 cm × 2 cm = 8 cm². Determine if this complies with ISO/TS 15066.

### Step 1 — Compare Force to Limit

Hand/wrist transient limit: $F_{limit} = 200$ N

$$F_{actual} = 280 \text{ N} > F_{limit} = 200 \text{ N}$$

> **Force criterion VIOLATED** — even before checking pressure.

### Step 2 — Compute Pressure for Completeness

$$P_{actual} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{280}{8} = 35 \text{ N/cm}^2$$

Pressure limit for hand: $P_{limit} = 160$ N/cm² → Pressure criterion is satisfied, but force dominates.

### Step 3 — Required Action

Either reduce gripper fault current limit, add compliance padding (increases $A$ and reduces $P$), or add a soft cover. Re-validate after redesign.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Mitigation vs. Hazard Type

| Hazard | Primary Mitigation | Secondary Mitigation | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Crushing (transient) | Light curtain | Safety mat | ISO 13855 |
| Crushing (collaborative) | PFL (force limit) | Soft skin padding | ISO/TS 15066 |
| Sharp end-effector | Mechanical guard | Tool-change interlock | ISO 10218-2 |
| Stored energy (pneumatic) | LOTO + bleed valves | Dump valves | OSHA 1910.147 |
| Unexpected startup | Two-hand control | Restart interlock | ISO 13851 |
| Over-speed (servo) | STO (Safe Torque Off) | SLS (Safe Limited Speed) | IEC 61800-5-2 |
| Welding arc exposure | Arc-flash PPE | Auto-dimming curtain | ANSI Z87.1 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Layered Safety Architecture of a Robotic Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Layer 0: Inherent Safe Design - Low payload, rounded edges, limited reach] --> L1
    L1[Layer 1: Physical Safeguards - Hard guards, fences, light curtains, safety mats] --> L2
    L2[Layer 2: Interlocks and Locks - Door interlocks, trapped-key systems, LOTO] --> L3
    L3[Layer 3: Control-System Safety - Safety PLC, dual-channel E-Stop, STO, SLS] --> L4
    L4[Layer 4: Operational Procedures - SOPs, training, signage, PPE] --> L5
    L5[Layer 5: Emergency Response - First aid, evacuation, incident reporting]
    
    L0 -.- INDEP[Independent Audit per ISO 12100]
    L1 -.- INDEP
    L2 -.- INDEP
    L3 -.- INDEP
    L4 -.- INDEP
    L5 -.- INDEP
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Flow of a Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant SEN as Safety Sensor (Light Curtain / Scanner)
    participant PLC as Safety PLC
    participant DRV as Robot Drive
    participant ROB as Robot Manipulator
    
    Note over OP,ROB: Normal Operation
    OP->>SEN: Approaches safeguarded zone
    SEN->>PLC: Signal OK (HIGH)
    PLC->>DRV: Enable (HIGH)
    DRV->>ROB: Motion allowed
    ROB-->>OP: Operating
    
    Note over OP,ROB: Breach Detected
    OP->>SEN: Enters safeguarded zone
    SEN->>PLC: Trip signal (LOW)
    PLC->>DRV: STO command (Safe Torque Off)
    DRV->>ROB: Decelerate and stop
    ROB-->>OP: Safe Stop Achieved
    
    Note over OP,ROB: Reset and Resume
    OP->>SEN: Exits zone
    SEN->>PLC: Reset (manual ack)
    PLC->>DRV: Re-enable
    DRV->>ROB: Resume motion
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Four Collaborative Robot Modes (ISO/TS 15066)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Collaborative Robot Operation] --> B[SRMS - Safety Rated Monitored Stop]
    A --> C[HG - Hand Guiding]
    A --> D[SSM - Speed and Separation Monitoring]
    A --> E[PFL - Power and Force Limiting]
    
    B --> B1[Human in zone: robot stops. Resumes only on exit]
    C --> C1[Operator physically drives robot via validated handle]
    D --> D1[LiDAR measures human distance. Robot speed scales inversely]
    E --> E1[Contact force and pressure capped below biomechanical limits]
    
    style A fill:#fff3b0
    style B fill:#a8dadc
    style C fill:#a8e6cf
    style D fill:#ffd3b6
    style E fill:#d4a5ff
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Risk Assessment Workflow (ISO 12100)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Machine Limits and Use Scenarios] --> B[Identify Hazards - Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal, Ergonomic]
    B --> C[Estimate Risk - R = S x P x F]
    C --> D{R Tolerable?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Document and Operate]
    D -->|No| F[Inherent Safe Design - reduce reach, speed, energy]
    F --> G[Safeguarding - guards, interlocks, sensors]
    G --> H[Information - training, PPE, signage]
    H --> C
    E --> I[Periodic Review]
    I --> A
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Dual-Channel Emergency Stop Circuit (PL d Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[24 V DC Safety Power Supply] --> K1[Safety Relay Channel 1 - NC Contact]
    P --> K2[Safety Relay Channel 2 - NC Contact]
    E1[E-Stop Pushbutton 1 - NC] --> K1
    E2[E-Stop Pushbutton 2 - NC] --> K2
    K1 --> AND[Dual-Channel AND Logic]
    K2 --> AND
    AND --> OUT[Safe Output to Robot STO Input]
    OUT --> ROB[Robot Drive - Torque Disabled]
    
    style P fill:#ffe082
    style K1 fill:#ef9a9a
    style K2 fill:#ef9a9a
    style AND fill:#a5d6a7
    style ROB fill:#90caf9
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This is a **Category 3 / PL d** architecture. Both channels must trip independently; a single channel failure is detected on the next cycle (cross-fault monitoring), preventing a single fault from disabling safety.

## 4.6 Block Diagram — Safe Stopping Distance vs. Approach Speed Graph (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given: Human Approach Speed K, Total Stop Time T, Intrusion C] --> B[Compute S = K x T + C]
    B --> C{Compare to Available Floor Space}
    C -->|S fits| D[Install safeguard at distance S]
    C -->|S too large| E[Reduce T: faster sensor, faster relay, faster brake]
    E --> B
    C -->|Cannot reduce T further| F[Use horizontal curtain above floor with reach-over analysis]
    F --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**List and briefly explain the four permissible modes of collaborative robot operation as defined in ISO/TS 15066.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four collaborative operation modes are:

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** When a human enters the collaborative workspace, the robot performs a safety-rated monitored stop. Motion is halted and only resumes after the human has exited and a manual reset is performed. (1 mark)

2. **Hand-Guiding (HG):** The operator physically grasps a dedicated, validated handle and manually moves the robot end-effector. The robot's servo system follows the operator's input with a force-sensing handle. (1 mark)

3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** A safety-rated sensor (typically LiDAR or vision) measures the real-time separation between operator and robot. Robot speed is dynamically reduced as separation decreases; the robot stops before contact if the minimum protective distance is breached. (0.5 mark)

4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Through inherent mechanical design (compliant joints, elastic actuators) and software limits, the robot's contact forces and pressures during incidental contact are kept below the biomechanical thresholds specified in ISO/TS 15066. (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Define the term "Performance Level (PL)" as per ISO 13849-1. State the PL required for an industrial robot emergency-stop circuit.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Performance Level (PL):** PL is a discrete level (a, b, c, d, or e) used to specify the ability of safety-related parts of a control system to perform a safety function under foreseeable conditions. It is quantified as the **Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour (PFHd)**. (2 marks)

| PL | PFHd (per hour) |
|---|---|
| a | $10^{-5}$ to $<10^{-4}$ |
| b | $10^{-6}$ to $<10^{-5}$ |
| c | $10^{-7}$ to $<10^{-6}$ |
| **d** | $10^{-7}$ to $<10^{-6}$ (slightly better) |
| e | $10^{-8}$ to $<10^{-7}$ |

**Required PL for industrial robot E-Stop: PL d** (or equivalently SIL 2 per IEC 61508). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) Derive the minimum safety distance formula $S = K \times T + C$ as specified in ISO 13855, clearly defining each term. Compute the minimum safety distance for a light curtain protecting a robotic welding cell, given:** (7 marks)

- Human approach: walking → $K = 1600$ mm/s
- Sensor reaction time: $t_i = 25$ ms
- Safety relay reaction time: $t_r = 15$ ms
- Robot mechanical stopping time: $t_s = 220$ ms
- Intrusion distance: $C = 850$ mm

**(b) With a suitable block diagram, explain the architecture of a dual-channel Category 3 / PL d emergency-stop circuit. Why is single-channel wiring inadequate? List three commonly observed safety violations in academic robotics labs.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Conceptual Derivation (3 marks)**

The safety distance must ensure that after a safeguard is breached, the human cannot reach the hazard point before the system has come to a complete stop. Let:

- $K$ = approach speed of the human body or body part (mm/s)
- $T = t_i + t_r + t_s$ = total stopping time of the system (s)
- $C$ = intrusion distance, the distance a body part can travel into the hazard zone beyond the safeguard (mm)

Distance covered by the human in time $T$:

$$d_{human} = K \times T$$

For safety, the safeguard must be placed at least $C$ mm further from the hazard than $d_{human}$:

$$S \geq K \times T + C$$

Thus the **minimum** safety distance is:

$$\boxed{S = K \times T + C}$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Computation (3 marks)**

Total stopping time:

$$T = t_i + t_r + t_s = 25 + 15 + 220 = 260 \text{ ms} = 0.260 \text{ s}$$

Substituting:

$$S = 1600 \times 0.260 + 850 = 416 + 850 = \boxed{1266 \text{ mm} \approx 1.27 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation (1 mark)**

The light curtain must be mounted at least 1.27 m from the nearest hazard point. Any installation closer violates ISO 13855 and is non-compliant.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Dual-Channel E-Stop Architecture (3 marks)**

A Category 3 / PL d E-Stop circuit uses **two independent channels** in parallel, each with its own normally-closed contact, its own wiring path, and its own monitoring input. Both channels must trip for the safety output to de-energize. A cross-fault monitoring circuit detects any single-channel failure (e.g., one contact welded shut) and latches the system into a safe state.

```
Channel 1: 24 V → E-Stop 1 (NC) → Safety Relay Input 1
Channel 2: 24 V → E-Stop 2 (NC) → Safety Relay Input 2
Both outputs → AND logic → Safe Output → Robot STO
```

**Step 2 — Why Single-Channel is Inadequate (2 marks)**

If a single contact welds shut (a documented failure mode in mechanical pushbuttons due to contact welding from arcing), a single-channel circuit would fail to trip on E-Stop press, and the dangerous failure would remain **undetected**. The robot would continue motion despite an E-Stop press, leading to serious injury. Dual-channel architecture with cross-fault monitoring detects this within one operating cycle, achieving PL d.

**Step 3 — Three Common Lab Safety Violations (2 marks)**

1. **Bypassed interlocks** — students jam the cell door interlock to keep the door open during debugging
2. **No E-Stop at operator station** — using only the controller's software stop, not a hardwired PL d E-Stop button
3. **No lockout/tagout during maintenance** — students swap end-effectors with the robot energized
4. **Light curtain mounted too close** to the hazard (violating ISO 13855 distance)
5. **Single emergency stop device shared across multiple robots** without proper risk assessment

*(Any three of the above earn full 2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]

**(a) Explain the concept of Risk Score in robotic safety. A robotic arm in an assembly cell has Severity = 2, Probability = 3, and Frequency = 3. Compute the risk score, classify it, and propose a mitigation strategy following the risk reduction hierarchy.** (7 marks)

**(b) With reference to ISO/TS 15066, define Power and Force Limiting (PFL). A cobot gripper can exert a peak transient force of 250 N over a contact patch of 6 cm² on an operator's hand. Using the appropriate biomechanical limits, determine whether this operation is compliant.** (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Step 1 — Risk Score Concept (2 marks)**

The risk score is a quantitative measure of the safety risk associated with a hazard in a robotic cell. It is computed as:

$$R = S \times P \times F$$

- $S$ = Severity of potential harm (1–3)
- $P$ = Probability of occurrence (1–3)
- $F$ = Frequency of exposure (1–3)

**Step 2 — Computation (1 mark)**

$$R = 2 \times 3 \times 3 = 18$$

**Step 3 — Classification (2 marks)**

| R Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| 1–3 | Acceptable |
| 4–8 | Tolerable with controls |
| 9–27 | **Unacceptable — must reduce** |

$R = 18$ → **Unacceptable risk**, mandatory reduction required before commissioning.

**Step 4 — Mitigation Strategy (2 marks) — Risk Reduction Hierarchy**

1. **Inherent Safe Design:** Reduce robot speed below 250 mm/s; replace sharp gripper with rounded compliant gripper; install soft padding on adjacent fixtures
2. **Safeguarding:** Install area scanner with SSM (Speed and Separation Monitoring), PL d E-Stop at every operator station
3. **Information for Use:** Operator training, lockout/tagout procedure, clear floor markings, PPE (safety glasses, steel-toe boots)

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — PFL Definition (3 marks)**

**Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** is a collaborative robot operation mode in which the robot's mechanical structure (e.g., compliant joints, elastic actuators) and its control system are designed to ensure that any incidental contact between the robot and a human cannot produce forces or pressures exceeding the **biomechanical thresholds** defined in **ISO/TS 15066:2016**. These thresholds are derived from biomedical studies on pain onset and minor injury thresholds. PFL is the most permissive collaborative mode, as it permits direct human-robot contact in operation.

**Step 2 — Identify Applicable Limits (2 marks)**

For the **hand / wrist** region:
- Maximum transient contact force: $F_{max} = 200$ N
- Maximum contact pressure: $P_{max} = 160$ N/cm²

**Step 3 — Compare Force (1 mark)**

$$F_{actual} = 250 \text{ N} > F_{max} = 200 \text{ N}$$

> **Force criterion VIOLATED** by 50 N (25% over limit)

**Step 4 — Compare Pressure (1 mark)**

$$P_{actual} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{250}{6} = 41.67 \text{ N/cm}^2 < P_{max} = 160 \text{ N/cm}^2$$

> **Pressure criterion SATISFIED**

**Step 5 — Overall Compliance and Required Action (1 mark)**

Because the **force** limit is exceeded, the cobot operation is **non-compliant** with ISO/TS 15066. Required corrective actions:
- Lower the gripper's servo current limit to cap transient force below 200 N
- Add a soft compliant cover on the gripper (reduces effective stiffness and spreads contact)
- Re-validate with a force gauge after redesign
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $K$ values:** $K = 1600$ mm/s is for *walking*, $K = 2000$ mm/s is for *reaching*. Examiners award **1 mark** for choosing the correct $K$.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Failing to convert ms → s before substituting into $S = K \times T + C$ loses **1–2 marks** even if the formula is otherwise correct.
> 3. **Forgetting the hierarchy:** Listing PPE before safeguarding in mitigation answers loses **1 mark**. Always follow: Inherent → Safeguarding → Information.
> 4. **Mixing PL and SIL:** PL (ISO 13849) and SIL (IEC 61508) are related but **not equivalent**. PL d ≈ SIL 2, but they use different computation methods.
> 5. **Skipping the reach-through check:** If $d < 850$ mm in reach-through problems, the standard reach-through formula is invalid — use the reach-over formula instead.
> 6. **Force vs. Pressure confusion:** In PFL problems, **both** force AND pressure must be checked independently; satisfying one does not imply satisfying the other.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Safety Measures in Robotics:**

- ✅ Safety in robotics is a **defense-in-depth, layered architecture** — never a single component
- ✅ **ISO 10218-1/2** governs industrial robots; **ISO/TS 15066** governs collaborative robots
- ✅ The **risk equation** is $R = S \times P \times F$; classify $R$ using the 1–3 / 4–8 / 9–27 tiers
- ✅ **Risk Reduction Hierarchy** (mandatory order): Inherent Safe Design → Safeguarding → Information for Use
- ✅ **Safety Distance** (ISO 13855): $S = K \times T + C$, with $K = 1600$ mm/s (walk) or $2000$ mm/s (reach)
- ✅ **Reach-through** adds a $-d$ correction: $S = K \times T + C - d$ (only valid if $d \geq 850$ mm)
- ✅ **Total stopping time** $T = t_i + t_r + t_s$ — always sum all three delays
- ✅ **Performance Level d (PL d)** is the standard for industrial E-Stops; PFHd between $10^{-7}$ and $10^{-6}$/h
- ✅ **SIL 2** (IEC 61508) is approximately equivalent to PL d
- ✅ **Four collaborative modes:** SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL
- ✅ **PFL biomechanical limits** — transient force on hand = 200 N; chest/abdomen = 320 N
- ✅ **Pressure limit** for hand = 160 N/cm²; for chest = 140 N/cm²
- ✅ **77% of robot injuries** occur during maintenance, programming, or setup → LOTO is critical
- ✅ **Lockout/Tagout (LOTO)** is mandatory before any maintenance task
- ✅ **Dual-channel + cross-fault monitoring** is the minimum for PL d
- ✅ **Three common hazard classes:** Mechanical, Electrical, Thermal (plus Ergonomic, Environmental)
- ✅ **Light curtains** are positioned by calculation, not by visual estimation

> **End of Notes — Safety Measures in Robotics (Module 1, PCRAT205)**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots: Manipulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Robotic Manipulator** is a programmable, multi-degree-of-freedom (multi-DOF) mechanical system comprising a series of rigid bodies (links) interconnected by joints, designed to perform complex tasks by manipulating, moving, or interacting with objects in a three-dimensional workspace without direct human contact. In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, manipulators are formally classified under the broader category of **Industrial Robots** as defined by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021) and the Robotics Industries Association (RIA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition Anchor**
> A manipulator is essentially a "mechanical arm" endowed with sensors (for perception), actuators (for motion), and a controller (for intelligence). The chain of links extends from a fixed **base frame** to an **end-effector** (gripper/tool), and its kinematic architecture defines its capability, accuracy, and reach.

### Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| **Link** | A rigid structural member connecting two adjacent joints. |
| **Joint** | A mechanical pairing that allows relative motion between two links. |
| **Base Frame** | The immobile reference coordinate system attached to the manipulator's mounting surface. |
| **End-Effector** | The terminal device (gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer) attached at the distal end of the manipulator. |
| **Tool Center Point (TCP)** | The precisely defined operational point of the end-effector, used for trajectory planning. |
| **Workspace** | The total volume of space that the TCP can physically reach. |
| **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** | The number of independent positional/orientational parameters required to fully specify the configuration of the manipulator in 3D space. |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Arm"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Intuition Box**
> Think of a robotic manipulator as a **mechanical human arm mounted on the ceiling/wall**. Your **shoulder**, **elbow**, and **wrist** correspond to **rotary joints (Revolute joints)**. Your **collarbone sliding forward/backward** corresponds to a **linear sliding joint (Prismatic joint)**. The **palm gripping a glass** is the **end-effector**. Just as your arm can position your hand anywhere on the table and orient it at different angles, a robotic manipulator achieves this through a precise arrangement of joints. The number of joints essentially determines how *dexterous* the arm is — more joints (typically 6 DOF) allow the hand to reach every point with every orientation, mimicking full human dexterity.

The conceptual mapping can be summarized as:

- **Skeleton (bones)** → Robot Links (rigid mechanical structures, usually aluminum, steel, or composites).
- **Muscles (actuators)** → Motors, Hydraulic cylinders, or Pneumatic pistons that drive the motion.
- **Nerves (sensors)** → Encoders, Force-Torque sensors, Vision systems providing feedback.
- **Brain (controller)** → Microcontroller / PLC / Industrial PC running the control algorithm.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

For KTU numerical problems, the following metrics are universally applied:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \ \text{m/s}^2$
> - **Industrial Robot Repeatability Range:** $\pm 0.02 \ \text{mm}$ to $\pm 0.5 \ \text{mm}$ (typical)
> - **Standard DOF Count:** A 6-DOF manipulator is required for *full spatial dexterity* (arbitrary position + arbitrary orientation in 3D).
> - **Reach (Working Envelope Radius):** Typically **0.5 m** to **3.5 m** for industrial arms.
> - **Payload:** Ranges from **3 kg** (light-pick-and-place) to **>1000 kg** (heavy foundry robots).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Robot Manipulators

Manipulators are classified in the KTU syllabus under **three principal taxonomies**. Mastering this classification is critical for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

### Taxonomy 1: Based on Kinematic Architecture

The kinematic structure — i.e., the sequence and type of joints — is the most academically significant classification.

| # | Type | Joint Sequence | Workspace Shape | Key Industry Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cartesian / Gantry (PPP)** | 3 Prismatic | Rectangular (cuboid) | CNC, Pick-and-place, 3D printing |
| 2 | **Cylindrical (RPP)** | Revolute + 2 Prismatic | Hollow Cylinder | Assembly, machine loading |
| 3 | **Spherical / Polar (RRP)** | 2 Revolute + Prismatic | Spherical sector | Welding, die-casting |
| 4 | **Articulated / Revolute (RRR)** | 3 Revolute | Sphere-like (complex) | Painting, welding, assembly |
| 5 | **SCARA (RRP)** | 2 Revolute + Prismatic | Cylindrical (horizontal) | PCB assembly, pick-and-place |
| 6 | **Parallel / Delta** | Multiple parallel linkages | Dome / flat zone | High-speed packaging |
| 7 | **Anthropomorphic** | Mimics human arm | Sphere-like | Service robots, research |

### Taxonomy 2: Based on Power Source / Actuation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Actuation choice directly determines **payload**, **speed**, and **precision**.

- **Hydraulic Manipulators:** Use incompressible fluid (oil). **Pros:** Very high payload (up to 1000+ kg), high force density. **Cons:** Leakage, noise, maintenance overhead. *Example: Palletizing robots in foundries.*
- **Pneumatic Manipulators:** Use compressed air. **Pros:** Clean, cheap, fast. **Cons:** Compressibility causes poor position accuracy (typically used in 2-3 DOF pick-and-place). *Example: Small assembly automation.*
- **Electric Manipulators:** Use servo/stepper motors with gear reducers. **Pros:** Excellent precision, repeatability, clean operation. **Cons:** Limited payload (usually <100 kg) compared to hydraulic. *Example: Most modern industrial robots (KUKA, ABB, FANUC).*

### Taxonomy 3: Based on Mechanical Linkage

- **Serial (Open-Chain) Manipulators:** Links are connected end-to-end in an open chain. The end-effector is the *terminal* link. *Example: Articulated 6-axis arm.*
- **Parallel (Closed-Chain) Manipulators:** Multiple serial chains connect the base to a common moving platform (end-effector). *Example: Delta robot, Stewart platform.* **Pros:** Higher stiffness, speed, accuracy. **Cons:** Limited workspace, complex kinematics.

---

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Mathematical Heart of Manipulation

### Theoretical Foundation

For a rigid body in **3-dimensional Euclidean space**, full spatial localization requires:
- **3 DOF** for position (translation along $x$, $y$, $z$)
- **3 DOF** for orientation (roll, pitch, yaw — rotations about $x$, $y$, $z$)

$$\text{Total DOF for full spatial manipulation} = 3 + 3 = 6$$

A manipulator with fewer than 6 DOF is **deficient** in some aspect (e.g., 5-DOF arm cannot achieve arbitrary orientation in 3D).

### Kutzbach-Grübler Criterion for DOF Calculation

For spatial (3D) mechanisms with closed loops, the general mobility equation is:

$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + f$$

Where:
- $M$ = Mobility (DOF) of the mechanism
- $n$ = Total number of links (including ground)
- $j_1$ = Number of lower-pair joints (1-DOF joints, e.g., revolute, prismatic)
- $j_2$ = Number of higher-pair joints (2-DOF joints, e.g., cam, gear)
- $f$ = Number of passive DOF (redundant/identity)

> [!NOTE]
> For **serial manipulators** (no closed loops), the formula simplifies to:
> $$M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i$$
> where $f_i$ is the DOF of each joint. A 6-revolute joint arm has $M = 6 \times 1 = 6$ DOF.

---

## 2.3 Joint Types — The Building Blocks

| Joint Symbol | Joint Name | Motion Type | DOF | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **R** | Revolute | Rotation about an axis | 1 | Shoulder of articulated arm |
| **P** | Prismatic | Linear translation along an axis | 1 | Gantry axis of Cartesian robot |
| **H** | Helical | Rotation + Translation combined | 1 | Lead screw, ball screw |
| **C** | Cylindrical | Rotation + Translation (independent) | 2 | (Rare in modern robots) |
| **U** | Universal | 2 rotations about perpendicular axes | 2 | Wrist joints |
| **S** | Spherical / Ball-and-Socket | 3 rotations about a point | 3 | Human shoulder (biological analogue) |

---

## 2.4 Workspace Definition and Formulas

### Workspace Classification

1. **Dexterous Workspace:** Subset of workspace where the TCP can reach a point with **all possible orientations**. For a 6-DOF arm, this is typically small or empty (often the wrist center).
2. **Reachable Workspace:** Subset of workspace where the TCP can reach a point with **at least one orientation**.
3. **Total Workspace:** Union of reachable + dexterous = full reachable volume.

### Analytical Formulas for Common Architectures

**Cartesian Robot (PPP):**
$$V_{\text{cart}} = 2L_x \times 2L_y \times 2L_z = 8 L_x L_y L_z$$
Where $L_x, L_y, L_z$ are the half-lengths of travel along each axis.

**Cylindrical Robot (RPP):**
$$V_{\text{cyl}} = \pi h (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2)$$
Where $h$ is vertical (prismatic) travel and $r_{\max}, r_{\min}$ are radial bounds.

**Spherical Robot (RRP):**
$$V_{\text{sph}} = \frac{2}{3}\pi h (r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3)$$

**Articulated Arm (RRR, simplified):**
The workspace is approximately bounded by two intersecting spheres (shoulder reach + elbow reach).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Numerical Tip:** When asked "Compare workspaces of Cartesian vs Spherical robot of same link lengths," compute both volumes using the formulas above and tabulate the results.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DOF for 3D positioning | $M = 3(\text{position}) + 3(\text{orientation})$ | Dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | Kutzbach-Grübler (spatial) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + f$ | Dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | Kutzbach-Grübler (planar) | $M = 2(n-1) - j_1 - j_2 + f$ | Dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | Cartesian Workspace | $V = 8 L_x L_y L_z$ | $\text{m}^3$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | Cylindrical Workspace | $V = \pi h (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2)$ | $\text{m}^3$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | Spherical Workspace | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi h (r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3)$ | $\text{m}^3$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | Joint DOF count (serial) | $M = \sum f_i$ | Dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | Required DOF for general task | $\geq 6$ (positioning + orienting arbitrary object) | Dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | DH Parameter Matrix | $A_i = \text{Rot}_z(\theta_i) \cdot \text{Trans}_z(d_i) \cdot \text{Trans}_x(a_i) \cdot \text{Rot}_x(\alpha_i)$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | Forward Kinematics | $T_n^0 = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$ | $4 \times 4$ matrix | ⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Matters in Industry**
> - **Automotive Welding Lines:** Articulated (6-DOF) robots with electric servo actuation dominate. *Example: FANUC R-2000iC series.*
> - **Semiconductor Fabrication:** SCARA and Cartesian robots are preferred for high-speed pick-and-place of microchips.
> - **Food Packaging:** Delta (parallel) robots for blister-pack loading at 100+ picks/minute.
> - **Space Manipulators:** Canadarm (CSA) — long-reach serial manipulator mounted on space shuttle, used for satellite retrieval.
> - **Surgical Robotics:** da Vinci system — 7-DOF serial manipulator for minimally invasive surgery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Kutzbach-Grübler Criterion for Spatial Mechanisms

We will now derive the generalized mobility formula used for analyzing the degrees of freedom of any spatial kinematic chain.

### Step 1: Define the System

Consider a spatial mechanism consisting of:
- $n$ total links, of which **one** is the ground (fixed reference)
- $j_1$ lower-pair joints (each providing 1 DOF of relative motion)
- $j_2$ higher-pair joints (each providing 2 DOF of relative motion)
- $f$ passive (redundant) degrees of freedom

The number of **moving links** is therefore:
$$n_{\text{moving}} = n - 1$$

### Step 2: Count the Total Available Degrees of Freedom

In 3D space, each rigid body (unconstrained) has **6 independent DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations).

Total unrestricted DOF of all moving links:
$$\text{Total DOF}_{\text{free}} = 6(n - 1)$$

### Step 3: Count the Degrees of Freedom Removed by Joint Constraints

Each lower-pair joint removes **5 DOF** (since it allows only 1 relative motion, the other 5 are constrained).

Each higher-pair joint removes **4 DOF** (since it allows 2 relative motions, the other 4 are constrained).

Total constraints removed:
$$\text{Total Constraints} = 5 j_1 + 4 j_2$$

### Step 4: Compute the Net Mobility

The net DOF (mobility) of the mechanism is:

$$M = \text{Total DOF}_{\text{free}} - \text{Total Constraints} + \text{Passive DOF}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:

$$M = 6(n - 1) - 5 j_1 - 4 j_2 + f$$

### Step 5: Simplify Using the Standard Convention

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, this is conventionally rewritten by expressing $j_2$ (higher-pair, 2 DOF) in terms of equivalent 1-DOF joints. Since 1 higher-pair joint = 2 lower-pair joints (both remove 5 DOF each, totaling 10, but the higher-pair allows 2 motions, so effectively $10 - 5 = 5$ removed, equivalent to 1 lower-pair... this can confuse). The cleaner canonical form taught in KTU is:

$$\boxed{M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2 + f}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Difference?** The two forms are equivalent when the joint classifications are interpreted correctly. The KTU-preferred form (with coefficients $3, 2, 1$) assumes links are counted with 3 DOF each in a planar sense, generalized to spatial. Always verify the version given in your KTU question paper.

### Step 6: Worked Example — A 6R Articulated Robot

Consider a 6-Revolute (6R) articulated robot arm:
- $n = 7$ (6 moving links + 1 ground)
- $j_1 = 6$ (all revolute joints)
- $j_2 = 0$
- $f = 0$

Applying the formula:
$$M = 3(7 - 1) - 2(6) - 0 + 0$$
$$M = 3(6) - 12$$
$$M = 18 - 12$$
$$M = 6$$

The 6R manipulator has exactly **6 DOF**, sufficient for full spatial dexterity. ✅

### Step 7: Worked Example — SCARA Robot (RRP)

The Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm (SCARA) has:
- $n = 4$ (3 moving links + 1 ground)
- $j_1 = 3$ (2 revolute + 1 prismatic, all 1-DOF joints)
- $j_2 = 0$
- $f = 0$

Applying the formula:
$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 0 + 0$$
$$M = 9 - 6$$
$$M = 3$$

The SCARA has **3 DOF** — sufficient for vertical pick-and-place on a horizontal plane. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Forward Kinematics via Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) Convention

The DH convention provides a systematic way to assign coordinate frames to each joint of a serial manipulator, enabling a homogeneous transformation matrix from the base to the end-effector.

### Step 1: Define the Four DH Parameters

For each joint $i$, four parameters describe the relative position/orientation of link $i$ with respect to link $i-1$:

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Joint angle | $\theta_i$ | Rotation about $z_{i-1}$ to align $x_{i-1}$ with $x_i$ |
| Link offset | $d_i$ | Translation along $z_{i-1}$ to align $x_{i-1}$ with $x_i$ |
| Link length | $a_i$ | Translation along $x_i$ to align $z_{i-1}$ with $z_i$ |
| Link twist | $\alpha_i$ | Rotation about $x_i$ to align $z_{i-1}$ with $z_i$ |

### Step 2: Construct the Individual Transformation Matrix

Each transformation $A_i$ is a composition of four elementary operations:

$$A_i = R_z(\theta_i) \cdot T_z(d_i) \cdot T_x(a_i) \cdot R_x(\alpha_i)$$

### Step 3: Expand the Matrix Form

Computing the product explicitly:

$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Overall End-Effector Pose

The complete transformation from base frame $\{0\}$ to end-effector frame $\{n\}$ is:

$$\boxed{T_n^0 = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdots A_n(\theta_n)}$$

This is a $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transformation matrix of the form:

$$T_n^0 = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & p_{3 \times 1} \\ 0_{1 \times 3} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $R$ is the orientation matrix (3×3 rotation) and $p$ is the position vector of the TCP relative to the base.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Robot Architectures

| Property | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) | SCARA (RRP) | Delta (Parallel) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rigidity** | Excellent | Good | Moderate | Moderate | Good | Excellent |
| **Accuracy** | High | High | Moderate | Moderate | High | Very High |
| **Reach** | Limited by gantry size | Medium | Medium | Large | Medium | Limited dome |
| **Speed** | Slow | Medium | Medium | Slow | Fast | Ultra-fast |
| **Payload** | Medium | Medium-Heavy | Heavy | Medium | Light | Light-Medium |
| **Complexity** | Low | Low-Medium | Medium | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Cost** | Low | Low | Medium | High | Medium | Very High |
| **Best For** | Pick-place, CNC | Machine tending | Die-cast | Welding, paint | PCB assembly | Packaging |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Cart-Cyl-Sph-Art-SCARA"* — recall the **R-R-R-P-P** rule (number of Revolute joints comes first, then Prismatic).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Degree of Freedom (DOF) in a robotic manipulator. Why is 6 DOF considered the standard for general-purpose manipulation? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robotic manipulator is defined as the **number of independent positional and orientational parameters** required to completely specify the configuration of its end-effector with respect to the base frame, in three-dimensional Cartesian space.

Mathematically:
$$\text{Total DOF} = \text{Position DOF} + \text{Orientation DOF} = 3 + 3 = 6$$

A 6-DOF manipulator is considered the universal standard because it can:
1. Position the TCP at any arbitrary point $(x, y, z)$ in 3D space (3 translational DOF).
2. Orient the end-effector at any arbitrary roll-pitch-yaw angle (3 rotational DOF).
3. Achieve full dexterity — equivalent to a human arm's reachability.

For tasks requiring less complexity (e.g., pick-and-place on a flat surface), 3 or 4 DOF manipulators (like SCARA) are economically preferred.

**Word Count:** ~95 words | **Marks:** 3 | **Bloom Level:** Remember/Understand

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between a serial manipulator and a parallel manipulator. Mention one industrial application of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Serial Manipulator | Parallel Manipulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Open kinematic chain | Closed kinematic chain (multiple legs) |
| **Links** | Many links connected end-to-end | Several legs connect base to moving platform |
| **Workspace** | Large, dexterous | Small, dome-shaped |
| **Stiffness** | Lower (joints stack up errors) | Very high (loads distributed) |
| **Accuracy** | Lower | Higher |
| **Speed** | Slower | Very fast |
| **Computation** | Simpler kinematics | Complex kinematics |
| **Example** | Articulated 6R arm (FANUC, ABB) | Delta robot, Stewart platform |
| **Application** | Automotive welding, painting | High-speed packaging (e.g., chocolates, blister packs) |

**Word Count:** ~90 words | **Marks:** 3 | **Bloom Level:** Understand

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Manipulator Classification and DOF Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Classify robot manipulators based on kinematic architecture with neat sketches. Compare their workspaces, rigidity, and typical applications. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Using the Kutzbach-Grübler criterion, calculate the degrees of freedom of (i) a 4-DOF SCARA robot, and (ii) a planar 3-R mechanism with one closed loop. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Classification Based on Kinematic Architecture:**

**1. Cartesian (PPP):** Three prismatic joints arranged mutually perpendicular.
- Workspace: Rectangular cuboid, $V = 8 L_x L_y L_z$
- Rigidity: Excellent (no moment-loading on joints)
- Application: CNC machines, 3D printers, pick-and-place

**2. Cylindrical (RPP):** One revolute joint at the base + two prismatic joints.
- Workspace: Hollow cylindrical volume, $V = \pi h (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2)$
- Rigidity: Good
- Application: Machine tending, foundry loading

**3. Spherical / Polar (RRP):** Two revolute joints + one prismatic (radial) joint.
- Workspace: Spherical sector, $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi h (r_{\max}^3 - r_{\min}^3)$
- Rigidity: Moderate (joints experience bending moments)
- Application: Welding, die-casting extraction

**4. Articulated / Revolute (RRR):** Three revolute joints — waist, shoulder, elbow.
- Workspace: Complex spherical-lobed volume
- Rigidity: Moderate (lowest among the four — cantilever loading)
- Application: Painting, arc welding, complex assembly

**5. SCARA (RRP):** Two parallel revolute joints for horizontal motion + one prismatic for vertical.
- Workspace: Flat cylindrical volume (horizontally compliant, vertically rigid)
- Application: PCB assembly, vertical pick-and-place

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**(i) SCARA Robot — 4 DOF:**

Parameters:
- $n = 5$ links (4 moving + 1 ground)
- $j_1 = 4$ (3 revolute + 1 prismatic — all 1-DOF lower pairs)
- $j_2 = 0$, $f = 0$

Applying the spatial Kutzbach-Grübler formula:
$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2 j_1 - j_2 + f$$
$$M = 3(5 - 1) - 2(4) - 0 + 0$$
$$M = 12 - 8$$
$$\boxed{M = 4 \ \text{DOF}}$$

**(ii) Planar 3R Closed-Loop Mechanism:**

For planar mechanisms, the Kutzbach-Grübler formula simplifies to:
$$M = 2(n - 1) - j_1 - j_2 + f$$

For a 3R planar mechanism (e.g., a parallelogram linkage or four-bar with one loop):
- Take a 4-bar linkage: $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$ revolute, $j_2 = 0$, $f = 0$
- $M = 2(4 - 1) - 4 - 0 + 0 = 6 - 4 = 2$ DOF (planar 2-DOF)

For a 5-bar planar mechanism with 5 revolute joints:
- $n = 5$, $j_1 = 5$, $j_2 = 0$
- $M = 2(5 - 1) - 5 = 8 - 5 = 3$ DOF

For an **8-bar planar Stephenson III mechanism** (a standard exam question):
- $n = 8$, $j_1 = 10$ (all revolute), $j_2 = 0$
- $M = 2(8 - 1) - 10 = 14 - 10 = 4$ DOF

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the ground link:** Always include the ground (fixed link) in $n$. A 4-bar linkage has $n = 4$, NOT 3.
> 2. **Planar vs Spatial confusion:** Planar formula has coefficient **2** in front of $(n-1)$; spatial formula has **3**. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks instantly.
> 3. **Passive DOF omission:** If the question specifies redundant rollers or idler gears, account for them in $f$.
> 4. **Units & Setup:** Always state the formula first, then list parameters, then substitute — partial credit is awarded even if the final number is wrong.

---

### Question B: Actuator Types and Forward Kinematics Foundation `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare hydraulic, pneumatic, and electric actuation systems for robot manipulators. Discuss their suitability for different industrial scenarios. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) parameter convention. Define the four DH parameters and write the general form of the homogeneous transformation matrix $A_i$. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

| Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | Electric (Servo) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Fluid** | Incompressible oil | Compressible air | Electricity (no fluid) |
| **Force/Power Density** | Very high (up to 30 kW/kg) | Medium | Medium |
| **Speed** | Medium | Very fast | Fast |
| **Precision** | Moderate | Poor (compressibility) | Excellent |
| **Position Control** | Difficult (servo-valve needed) | End-point or 2-3 point | Precise servo control |
| **Payload** | Up to 1000+ kg | < 50 kg typically | Up to 1000 kg (modern) |
| **Maintenance** | High (leakage) | Low | Low |
| **Cleanliness** | Poor (oil leaks) | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Cost** | High | Low | Medium |
| **Noise** | High | High (exhaust) | Low |
| **Suitable For** | Heavy forging, palletizing, foundry | Light packaging, food industry | Welding, painting, assembly, precision tasks |

**Industrial Suitability Scenarios:**
- **Hydraulic:** Used in heavy-payload industrial robots in automotive press shops, e.g., unloading hot billets from furnaces.
- **Pneumatic:** Used in low-cost, clean environments like electronic chip packaging, pharmaceutical sorting.
- **Electric:** Used in modern flexible manufacturing systems (FMS), surgical robotics, collaborative robots (cobots).

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) Convention:**

The DH convention, proposed by Jacques Denavit and Richard Hartenberg (1955), provides a **minimal 4-parameter representation** of the relative pose between consecutive links of a serial manipulator.

**The Four DH Parameters:**

For each joint $i$, define:

1. **$\theta_i$ (Joint angle):** The rotation about the previous $z$-axis ($z_{i-1}$) required to align $x_{i-1}$ with $x_i$. *Variable for revolute joints.*

2. **$d_i$ (Link offset):** The translation along $z_{i-1}$ required to align $x_{i-1}$ with $x_i$. *Variable for prismatic joints.*

3. **$a_i$ (Link length):** The translation along $x_i$ required to bring the origins of frames $i-1$ and $i$ to the common normal.

4. **$\alpha_i$ (Link twist):** The rotation about $x_i$ required to align $z_{i-1}$ with $z_i$.

**General Homogeneous Transformation Matrix:**

The individual link transformation is composed as:
$$A_i = R_z(\theta_i) \cdot T_z(d_i) \cdot T_x(a_i) \cdot R_x(\alpha_i)$$

Expanding explicitly:
$$A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Forward Kinematics Equation:**

The overall transformation from the base frame $\{0\}$ to the end-effector frame $\{n\}$ is obtained by successive multiplication:

$$\boxed{T_n^0 = A_1(\theta_1) \cdot A_2(\theta_2) \cdot A_3(\theta_3) \cdots A_n(\theta_n)}$$

This $4 \times 4$ matrix encodes both the **position** $p$ (last column) and **orientation** $R$ (top-left $3 \times 3$ block) of the TCP:

$$T_n^0 = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3 \times 3} & p_{3 \times 1} \\ 0 \ \ 0 \ \ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Mistakes in DH-Based Questions**
> 1. **Skipping the frame-assignment diagram:** Always draw the joint axes ($z_0, z_1, \ldots$) explicitly. Marks are allocated for the diagram, not just the formula.
> 2. **Sign errors in $\alpha_i$ and $\theta_i$:** Right-hand rule must be followed rigorously. The orientation of positive rotation must be consistent throughout.
> 3. **Matrix multiplication order:** $T_n^0 = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$, NOT $A_n A_{n-1} \cdots A_1$. Reversing the order silently changes the result.
> 4. **Forgetting the bottom row $[0 \ 0 \ 0 \ 1]$:** Marks deducted for an incomplete homogeneous matrix.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "The Manipulator Cheat Sheet"**

### 🔑 Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [x] A manipulator is a multi-DOF mechanical arm with **links**, **joints**, and an **end-effector**.
- [x] **6 DOF** = universal standard for arbitrary position + orientation in 3D.
- [x] Joint symbols: **R** (Revolute), **P** (Prismatic), **H** (Helical), **C** (Cylindrical), **U** (Universal), **S** (Spherical).
- [x] Five major architectures: **Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA (RRP)** + **Parallel (Delta)**.

### 🔑 Governing Equations (Must Memorize)
- [x] **Spatial DOF:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 + f$
- [x] **Planar DOF:** $M = 2(n-1) - j_1 - j_2 + f$
- [x] **Cartesian Workspace:** $V = 8 L_x L_y L_z$
- [x] **Cylindrical Workspace:** $V = \pi h (r_{\max}^2 - r_{\min}^2)$
- [x] **Forward Kinematics:** $T_n^0 = A_1 A_2 \cdots A_n$

### 🔑 Actuator Comparison (Must Remember)
- [x] **Hydraulic** → high payload, dirty, noisy, slow.
- [x] **Pneumatic** → light, clean, fast, imprecise.
- [x] **Electric** → precise, clean, flexible, modern industry standard.

### 🔑 DH Convention (Must Apply Correctly)
- [x] Four parameters: $\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i$.
- [x] Always draw joint axes ($z$-axes) and common normals ($x$-axes) before assigning parameters.
- [x] Matrix order: $R_z \to T_z \to T_x \to R_x$.
- [x] Multiply $A_i$ matrices **left-to-right** to get $T_n^0$.

### 🔑 Common Pitfalls (Must Avoid)
- [x] Forgetting to count the **ground link** in $n$.
- [x] Confusing **planar (coefficient 2)** vs **spatial (coefficient 3)** DOF formulas.
- [x] Mixing up **matrix multiplication order** in forward kinematics.
- [x] Writing the **homogeneous matrix** without the bottom row $[0 \ 0 \ 0 \ 1]$.
- [x] Treating SCARA as a fully articulated arm — SCARA is **horizontally articulated, vertically prismatic**.
