<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Aerial Robots

Aerial robots are broadly classified based on their **lift-generation mechanism** and **wing morphology**.

### A. Based on Aerodynamic Principle
- **Fixed-Wing UAVs**: Generate lift via fixed airfoils (airplane-like). High endurance, high speed, but **cannot hover**.
- **Rotary-Wing UAVs (Rotorcraft)**: Generate lift via rotating blades. Can **hover**, vertically take-off and land (**VTOL**).
- **Flapping-Wing UAVs (Ornithopters)**: Biomimetic; generate lift via oscillating wings.
- **Hybrid UAVs (Tilt-rotor, Tilt-wing)**: Combine fixed-wing efficiency with rotorcraft hover.

### B. KTU-Focused Classification (Rotorcraft Sub-types)
| Type | Configuration | DOF | Use Case |
|------|---------------|-----|----------|
| **Quadrotor** | 4 rotors (X or +) | **6** (3 translational + 3 rotational) | Consumer, research |
| **Hexarotor** | 6 rotors | 6 | Higher payload, redundancy |
| **Octorotor** | 8 rotors | 6 | Heavy lift, industrial |
| **Coaxial Tricopter** | 3 rotors, stacked | 6 | Compact designs |
| **Single Rotor Helicopter** | 1 main + 1 tail rotor | 6 | Long endurance heavy lift |

---

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of an Aerial Robot

A **rigid body in 3D space** has exactly **6 DOF**:

$$\text{DOF}_{rigid\ body} = 3_{translation} + 3_{rotation} = 6$$

For a quadrotor, these are:
- **Translation (along body axes $x_b, y_b, z_b$):** Surge, Sway, Heave
- **Rotation (about body axes):** Roll ($\phi$), Pitch ($\theta$), Yaw ($\psi$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A quadrotor has 4 control inputs (4 motor speeds $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$) but 6 DOF. Therefore, it is an UNDERACTUATED system.**
> - 4 inputs control 6 outputs.
> - Horizontal motion is achieved by **tilting the thrust vector** (not by direct lateral thrust).

---

## 2.3 Reference Frames

Two principal frames are used in aerial robotics:

| Frame | Notation | Description |
|-------|----------|-------------|
| **Inertial / World Frame** | $\{E\}$ or $E$ | Fixed to Earth (NED: North-East-Down) |
| **Body Frame** | $\{B\}$ or $B$ | Fixed to the UAV's center of gravity |

The **rotation matrix** $R_{B}^{E}$ transforms a vector from body to inertial frame. Using **Z-Y-X (yaw-pitch-roll) Euler convention**:

$$
R_{B}^{E} = R_z(\psi)\, R_y(\theta)\, R_x(\phi)
$$

$$
R_{B}^{E} = \begin{bmatrix}
c\psi c\theta & c\psi s\theta s\phi - s\psi c\phi & c\psi s\theta c\phi + s\psi s\phi \\
s\psi c\theta & s\psi s\theta s\phi + c\psi c\phi & s\psi s\theta c\phi - c\psi s\phi \\
-s\theta & c\theta s\phi & c\theta c\phi
\end{bmatrix}
$$

where $c\theta = \cos\theta$, $s\theta = \sin\theta$, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Euler Angles?** Euler angles are intuitive (roll, pitch, yaw are physical rotations a pilot understands), but they suffer from **gimbal lock** at $\theta = \pm 90°$. For KTU exam purposes, use Euler angles; for advanced control, quaternions are preferred.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Cheat Sheet — Memorize This Table**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Total Thrust | $T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_T \omega_i^2$ | **N** | $n$ = no. of rotors |
| 2 | Total Torque (Yaw) | $\tau_\psi = \sum_{i=1}^{n} k_M \omega_i^2$ | **N·m** | $k_M$ = moment constant |
| 3 | Body Force Vector | $F_B = [0,\ 0,\ -T]^T$ | N | In body frame |
| 4 | Translational Dynamics | $m \ddot{r} = R_{B}^{E} F_B + m g \hat{e}_z$ | N | Newton's 2nd law |
| 5 | Rotational Dynamics | $I \dot{\omega} = \tau - \omega \times (I\omega)$ | N·m | Euler's equation |
| 6 | Hover Condition | $T_{hover} = m g$ | N | Equilibrium thrust |
| 7 | Motor Thrust Coefficient | $k_T = C_T \rho A R^2$ | N·s² | Depends on blade geometry |
| 8 | Power at Hover | $P = T \cdot v_{i}$ | W | $v_i$ = induced velocity |
| 9 | Momentum Theory (Hover) | $T = 2 \rho A v_i^2$ | N | Classical helicopter theory |
| 10 | Endurance (Ideal) | $E = \dfrac{C_bat \cdot V_{bat}}{P_{hover}}$ | **s** | Battery-limited |
| 11 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v c}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Aerodynamic regime |
| 12 | Stability Metric | $T_{nat} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{I_{xx}}{k_\phi}}$ | s | Natural period (roll) |

> **Quick Reference Constants:**
> - Air density at sea level: $\rho = 1.225$ **kg/m³**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81$ **m/s²**
> - Dynamic viscosity: $\mu = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ **Pa·s**

---

## 2.5 Sensor-Actuator Subsystems of an Aerial Robot

| Subsystem | Components | Function |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Actuators** | BLDC motors, Electronic Speed Controllers (ESC), servos, propellers | Convert electrical energy to thrust & control moments |
| **Sensors — Inertial** | IMU (3-axis gyro + 3-axis accelerometer), magnetometer | Measure orientation, angular rates, heading |
| **Sensors — Position** | GPS/GNSS, barometer, TOF, optical flow | Determine absolute and relative position |
| **Sensors — Exteroceptive** | LiDAR, ultrasonic, stereo/RGB-D camera, thermal camera | Environment perception, obstacle avoidance |
| **Compute** | Flight controller (Pixhawk, STM32), companion computer (Raspberry Pi, Jetson Nano) | Sensor fusion (EKF), state estimation, control |
| **Communication** | 2.4 GHz RC, 5.8 GHz video, 4G/5G, telemetry radio | GCS link, video downlink |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the consumer drone market (DJI, Parrot), the sensor-actuator integration is so tight that a single **Flight Management Unit (FMU)** performs sensor fusion, control, and communication. In research platforms (e.g., **PX4**, **ArduPilot**), the architecture is open-source and modular, allowing KTU students to test their own control laws.

---

## 2.6 Control Architecture — The Cascaded Loop

A typical aerial robot uses a **two-loop cascade controller**:

1. **Outer Loop (Position Controller)**: Computes desired attitude from position error.
2. **Inner Loop (Attitude Controller)**: Computes motor commands from attitude error.
3. **Mixer**: Maps desired thrust & torques to individual motor speeds.

**Mathematically:**

$$
\phi_{des} = \frac{1}{g}\left(\ddot{x}_{des} K_{px} + K_{dx}(\dot{x}_{des} - \dot{x}) + \ldots\right)
$$

$$
\tau_{des} = I \left( K_p (\eta_{des} - \eta) + K_d (\dot{\eta}_{des} - \dot{\eta}) + K_i \int (\eta_{des} - \eta)\, dt \right)
$$

This is the classical **PID control** structure, which is the foundation of all modern flight stacks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quadrotor Thrust-to-Weight and Motor Mixing

> **Problem Setup:** A quadrotor with mass $m$ uses four motors at speeds $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$. Each motor produces an upward thrust $T_i = k_T \omega_i^2$ and a reaction torque about its axis $M_i = k_M \omega_i^2$ (opposite to rotation, hence causing yaw). The "X" configuration assigns motors as: 1 (front-right CW), 2 (back-right CCW), 3 (back-left CW), 4 (front-left CCW). Derive the total thrust, roll, pitch, and yaw torques in terms of $\omega_i$.

**Step 1 — Define the total thrust.**

The total thrust is the sum of all four motor thrusts acting along the body's $z$-axis (upward).

$$
T = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 = k_T (\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2)
$$

**Step 2 — Derive the roll torque $\tau_\phi$.**

Roll is rotation about the body $x$-axis. In the X-configuration, motors 2 and 3 are on the right; motors 1 and 4 are on the left. The arm length is $L$. A thrust difference between right and left causes a roll moment. With motors 2 and 3 (right) producing +roll, and motors 1 and 4 (left) producing -roll:

$$
\tau_\phi = L \cdot k_T \left( \frac{\omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2}{2} - \frac{\omega_1^2 + \omega_4^2}{2} \right)
$$

(Alternative mixing conventions exist; the factor of 1/2 comes from the $\cos 45°$ projection in the X-frame.)

**Step 3 — Derive the pitch torque $\tau_\theta$.**

Pitch is rotation about the body $y$-axis. Motors 1 and 2 (front) cause negative pitch (nose up), and motors 3 and 4 (back) cause positive pitch (nose down):

$$
\tau_\theta = L \cdot k_T \left( \frac{\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2}{2} - \frac{\omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the yaw torque $\tau_\psi$.**

Yaw is rotation about the body $z$-axis. Each motor produces a reactive torque $M_i = k_M \omega_i^2$ opposite to its rotation direction. CW motors (1, 3) produce +z torque; CCW motors (2, 4) produce -z torque:

$$
\tau_\psi = k_M (\omega_1^2 + \omega_3^2 - \omega_2^2 - \omega_4^2)
$$

> **Note:** $k_M = \sigma k_T$, where $\sigma$ is a small constant typically on the order of $0.01$–$0.05$, since reaction torques are small compared to thrusts.

**Step 5 — Compile the allocation matrix.**

The control allocation (mixer) maps desired $(T_{des}, \tau_{\phi,des}, \tau_{\theta,des}, \tau_{\psi,des})$ to motor speeds. Inverting the linear system:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
\omega_1^2 \\
\omega_2^2 \\
\omega_3^2 \\
\omega_4^2
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{1}{4k_T} & \frac{0}{} & \frac{-2}{4L k_T} & \frac{1}{4 k_M} \\
\frac{1}{4k_T} & \frac{2}{4L k_T} & \frac{0}{} & \frac{-1}{4 k_M} \\
\frac{1}{4k_T} & \frac{0}{} & \frac{2}{4L k_T} & \frac{1}{4 k_M} \\
\frac{1}{4k_T} & \frac{-2}{4L k_T} & \frac{0}{} & \frac{-1}{4 k_M}
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
T_{des} \\
\tau_{\phi,des} \\
\tau_{\theta,des} \\
\tau_{\psi,des}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This **control allocation matrix** is the core of every quadrotor flight stack.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Euler Angle Kinematics (Angular Velocity Mapping)

> **Problem Setup:** The IMU measures body angular rates $[p, q, r]^T$ (roll rate, pitch rate, yaw rate in body frame). The flight controller must convert these to Euler rate derivatives $[\dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi}]^T$ to update orientation.

**Step 1 — Start with the rotational transformation.**

The body angular velocity $\omega_B = [p, q, r]^T$ is related to the Euler angle rates via the Jacobian matrix $W$:

$$
\omega_B = W(\phi, \theta)\, \dot{\eta}
$$

where $\dot{\eta} = [\dot{\phi}, \dot{\theta}, \dot{\psi}]^T$.

**Step 2 — Expand the body rates in terms of Euler angle contributions.**

Roll rate $p$ is the sum of roll contribution plus yaw contribution projected through pitch:

$$
p = \dot{\phi} - \sin\theta \cdot \dot{\psi}
$$

Pitch rate $q$ includes pitch and yaw contributions:

$$
q = \cos\phi \cdot \dot{\theta} + \sin\phi \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\psi}
$$

Yaw rate $r$ is the projection of yaw through pitch and roll:

$$
r = -\sin\phi \cdot \dot{\theta} + \cos\phi \cos\theta \cdot \dot{\psi}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
p \\
q \\
r
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -\sin\theta \\
0 & \cos\phi & \sin\phi \cos\theta \\
0 & -\sin\phi & \cos\phi \cos\theta
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
\dot{\phi} \\
\dot{\theta} \\
\dot{\psi}
\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Invert the matrix to solve for Euler angle rates.**

The inverse Jacobian $W^{-1}$ is obtained by taking the inverse of the $3\times 3$ matrix. After algebraic inversion:

$$
\dot{\phi} = p + \tan\theta (q \sin\phi + r \cos\phi)
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = q \cos\phi - r \sin\phi
$$

$$
\dot{\psi} = \frac{1}{\cos\theta}(q \sin\phi + r \cos\phi)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gimbal Lock Condition:** Notice that $\dot{\psi}$ has $\cos\theta$ in the denominator. As $\theta \to \pm 90°$, $\cos\theta \to 0$, and $\dot{\psi} \to \infty$. This is **gimbal lock** — two axes become indistinguishable. This is why quaternions are used in modern flight controllers for aggressive maneuvers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hover Power and Endurance (Momentum Theory)

> **Problem Setup:** A multirotor hovers at altitude. The total thrust equals weight, $T = m g$. Using actuator disk / momentum theory, derive the ideal power required in hover, and the endurance given battery capacity.

**Step 1 — Apply momentum theory for hover.**

For a rotor with disk area $A$, the thrust is related to the induced velocity $v_i$ at the disk:

$$
T = 2 \rho A v_i^2
$$

This comes from applying Bernoulli's equation across the actuator disk in hover, where the far-wake velocity is $2 v_i$ (mass flow continuity).

**Step 2 — Solve for induced velocity.**

$$
v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{m g}{2 \rho A}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ideal hover power.**

The power required to impart kinetic energy to the air is:

$$
P = T \cdot v_i = 2 \rho A v_i^3
$$

Substituting $v_i$:

$$
P_{hover} = m g \sqrt{\frac{m g}{2 \rho A}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute endurance.**

Battery provides energy $E_{bat} = C_{bat} V_{bat}$ (in watt-hours or joules). Endurance is:

$$
E = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{hover}} = \frac{C_{bat} V_{bat}}{m g \sqrt{\dfrac{m g}{2 \rho A}}}
$$

> **Example Numerical Estimate:**
> A quadrotor with $m = 1.5$ kg, $A = 0.07$ m² (15 cm prop), $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, battery $C = 5.2$ Ah, $V = 11.1$ V (3S LiPo).
> $T = 1.5 \times 9.81 = 14.7$ N.
> $v_i = \sqrt{14.7 / (2 \times 1.225 \times 0.07)} = \sqrt{85.7} = 9.26$ m/s.
> $P_{hover} = 14.7 \times 9.26 = 136$ W.
> $E_{bat} = 5.2 \times 11.1 = 57.7$ Wh $\approx 208$ kJ.
> $E = 57700 / 136 \approx 424$ s $\approx 7$ minutes.
>
> Real-world endurance is shorter ($\sim 60$–$70\%$ of ideal) due to motor/ESC/battery inefficiencies.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Aerial Robot Classes

| Property | Quadrotor | Hexarotor | Fixed-Wing | Helicopter |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Hover capable** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **VTOL** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ (needs catapult) | ✅ |
| **Endurance** | Low (15–30 min) | Low (10–20 min) | High (1–24 h) | High (1–3 h) |
| **Payload** | Low–Med | Med–High | Med | High |
| **Mechanical complexity** | Low | Low | Med | High |
| **Cost (USD)** | $200–$5000 | $1000–$10000 | $5000–$100000+ | $10000+ |
| **Underactuation** | Yes (4 vs 6) | Yes (6 vs 6) | No (aileron, elevator, rudder, throttle) | No (cyclic + collective + tail rotor) |
| **Failure tolerance** | Poor (1 motor = crash) | Good (1–2 motor fails) | Good (glides) | Poor |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The hexarotor with **6 motors vs 6 DOF** is **fully actuated in rotation** (it can control roll, pitch, and yaw independently) but still **underactuated in translation** (cannot produce lateral thrust without tilting). This is a common viva question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Full Aerial Robot Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GCS["GROUND CONTROL STATION"] -->|RC Commands / Mission| TX["TELEMETRY RADIO TX"]
    TX --> RX["TELEMETRY RADIO RX"]
    RX --> FC["FLIGHT CONTROLLER"]
    GPS["GNSS / GPS MODULE"] -->|Position NMEA| FC
    IMU["IMU Gyro Accelerometer"] -->|High rate 1 kHz| FC
    MAG["MAGNETOMETER"] -->|Heading| FC
    BARO["BAROMETER"] -->|Altitude| FC
    LIDAR["LIDAR / TOF / OPTICAL FLOW"] -->|Range data| FC
    CAM["RGB / THERMAL CAMERA"] -->|Video stream| VTX["VIDEO TX 5.8 GHz"]
    VTX -->|Downlink| GCS
    FC -->|PWM / DShot| ESC1["ESC 1"]
    FC -->|PWM / DShot| ESC2["ESC 2"]
    FC -->|PWM / DShot| ESC3["ESC 3"]
    FC -->|PWM / DShot| ESC4["ESC 4"]
    FC -->|PWM / DShot| ESC5["ESC 5"]
    FC -->|PWM / DShot| ESC6["ESC 6"]
    ESC1 --> M1["BLDC M1"]
    ESC2 --> M2["BLDC M2"]
    ESC3 --> M3["BLDC M3"]
    ESC4 --> M4["BLDC M4"]
    ESC5 --> M5["BLDC M5"]
    ESC6 --> M6["BLDC M6"]
    M1 --> P1["PROPELLER 1"]
    M2 --> P2["PROPELLER 2"]
    M3 --> P3["PROPELLER 3"]
    M4 --> P4["PROPELLER 4"]
    M5 --> P5["PROPELLER 5"]
    M6 --> P6["PROPELLER 6"]
    BAT["LIPO BATTERY 3S 4S 6S"] -->|Power| ESC1
    BAT -->|Power| ESC2
    BAT -->|Power| ESC3
    BAT -->|Power| ESC4
    BAT -->|Power| ESC5
    BAT -->|Power| ESC6
    BAT -->|Power| FC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Sensor data (GPS, IMU, barometer, magnetometer) flows **upward** into the flight controller. The flight controller performs **sensor fusion** (typically Extended Kalman Filter) to estimate the state, then computes **PID control commands**, and sends **actuator commands** to ESCs, which drive BLDC motors and propellers. The battery is the common power rail.

---

## 4.2 Control Loop Block Diagram — Cascaded PID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DESIRED POSITION r_des"] -->|Error| B["POSITION CONTROLLER OUTER PID"]
    B -->|"phi_des, theta_des, T_des"| C["ATTITUDE CONTROLLER INNER PID"]
    C -->|"tau_phi, tau_theta, tau_psi"| D["CONTROL ALLOCATION MIXER"]
    D -->|"omega_1 to omega_4"| E["MOTORS AND PROPELLERS"]
    E -->|"Thrust and Torques"| F["QUADROTOR DYNAMICS PLANT"]
    F -->|"Position r, Velocity v, Orientation"| G["SENSORS IMU GPS"]
    G -->|"Estimated state x_hat"| A
    G -->|Feedback| B
    G -->|Feedback| C
```

## 4.3 Coordinate Frame Diagram — Body vs. Inertial

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INERT["INERTIAL FRAME NORTH EAST DOWN"]
        XN["Xn North"]
        YE["Ye East"]
        ZD["Zd Down"]
    end

        XB["Xb Forward"]

        ZB["Zb Down"]
    end
    INERT -.->|"R_B_to_E Rotation Matrix 3x3"| BODY
    BODY -.->|"Euler Angles phi, theta, psi"| INERT
```

> **Note:** The diagram shows the two key reference frames used in aerial robot modeling. The rotation matrix $R_B^E$ transforms a vector expressed in the body frame to the inertial frame. This is essential for translating measured body-frame accelerations into inertial-frame positions.

## 4.4 Quadrotor Motor Configuration (X-Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph QUAD["QUADROTOR X CONFIGURATION TOP VIEW"]
        direction TB
        M1["MOTOR 1 FRONT-RIGHT CW"] 
        M2["MOTOR 2 BACK-RIGHT CCW"]
        M3["MOTOR 3 BACK-LEFT CW"]
        M4["MOTOR 4 FRONT-LEFT CCW"]
        M1 --> M2
        M2 --> M3
        M3 --> M4
        M4 --> M1

    ARM1["ARM LENGTH L"] -.-> M1
    ARM2["ARM LENGTH L"] -.-> M2
    ARM3["ARM LENGTH L"] -.-> M3
    ARM4["ARM LENGTH L"] -.-> M4
    CG["CENTER OF GRAVITY"] --- QUAD
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The four motors are placed at the vertices of a square of side $\sqrt{2} L$ in X-mode. CW and CCW rotations balance the net yaw torque in hover. Tilting the platform changes the relative speeds to produce roll, pitch, and yaw torques.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**
**Define an aerial robot. Differentiate between a UAV and a UAS.**

**Model Answer:**

An **aerial robot** is an unmanned aerial vehicle equipped with onboard sensors, actuators, and computational intelligence that enables it to perceive its environment, make decisions, and execute tasks autonomously or semi-autonomously.

| Feature | UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle) | UAS (Unmanned Aircraft System) |
|---------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Definition | The flying vehicle itself | Vehicle + Ground Control Station + Communication |
| Components | Airframe, motors, ESCs, propellers | UAV + GCS + Telemetry radio + Data link |
| Scope | Hardware only | Complete operational system |
| Example | DJI Mavic Air 2 | DJI Mavic Air 2 + DJI Smart Controller + OcuSync link |

A UAV is a **subset** of a UAS. The UAS is the entire ecosystem, while the UAV is just the airborne platform.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three applications of aerial robots and explain the role of sensors in each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Precision Agriculture (e.g., DJI Agras):** Sensors — Multispectral camera, GPS, LiDAR. The camera captures crop health indices (NDVI), GPS geotags the data, and LiDAR measures canopy height for variable-rate spraying.

2. **Disaster Search and Rescue:** Sensors — Thermal camera, IMU, GPS. Thermal imaging detects body heat through smoke/debris; IMU stabilizes the platform in turbulent wind; GPS marks survivor locations for first responders.

3. **Power Line Inspection:** Sensors — High-resolution RGB camera, ultrasonic range sensor, LiDAR. The camera detects visual defects (corrosion, broken strands), ultrasonic measures insulator distance, and LiDAR builds a 3D model of the line for automated defect classification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a quadrotor aerial robot. Identify the major sensor and actuator subsystems. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A quadrotor of mass **$1.8$ kg** hovers in place. Each propeller produces a thrust coefficient $k_T = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$ N·s²/rad². The arm length is **$L = 0.25$ m**. If the desired roll torque is $\tau_\phi = 0.5$ N·m, while maintaining hover thrust, derive the required motor speeds $\omega_1, \omega_2, \omega_3, \omega_4$ in the X-configuration. Assume the moment coefficient $k_M = 1.4 \times 10^{-7}$ N·m·s²/rad² and neglect gravity in the torque equation. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Quadrotor Architecture:**

A quadrotor aerial robot consists of the following integrated subsystems:

1. **Airframe**: A central body (often carbon fiber) with four arms extending outwards at $90°$ intervals. Each arm ends in a motor mount.

2. **Propulsion Subsystem (Actuators)**: Four **Brushless DC (BLDC) motors** with mounted **propellers**, driven by **Electronic Speed Controllers (ESCs)**. The motors convert DC electrical energy from the LiPo battery into rotational mechanical energy, and the propellers convert this into aerodynamic thrust.

3. **Sensor Subsystem**:
   - **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)**: 3-axis gyroscope + 3-axis accelerometer; measures angular rate and linear acceleration at $> 1$ kHz.
   - **Magnetometer**: Measures Earth's magnetic field for absolute heading.
   - **Barometer**: Measures atmospheric pressure for altitude estimation.
   - **GPS/GNSS Receiver**: Provides absolute position (latitude, longitude, altitude) outdoors.
   - **Optional**: Optical flow, LiDAR, ultrasonic, cameras for indoor/precision applications.

4. **Flight Controller**: A microcontroller (e.g., STM32 in Pixhawk) running sensor fusion (EKF) and control loops (PID). Inputs: sensor data + RC commands. Outputs: PWM/DShot signals to ESCs.

5. **Communication Subsystem**: 2.4 GHz RC receiver for pilot commands, 5.8 GHz video transmitter for FPV feed, 433/915 MHz telemetry radio for bidirectional data.

6. **Power Subsystem**: LiPo battery (3S–6S, $11.1$ V – $22.2$ V) with Power Module (voltage + current sensors) feeding ESCs and FC.

7. **Ground Control Station (GCS)**: A laptop/tablet running QGroundControl or Mission Planner, connected via telemetry radio, for mission planning, telemetry monitoring, and override.

**Block Diagram:**

$$
\text{Sensors} \longrightarrow \text{FC (EKF + PID)} \longrightarrow \text{ESCs} \longrightarrow \text{Motors} \longrightarrow \text{Propellers} \longrightarrow \text{Thrust}
$$

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Hover thrust condition.**

The total thrust must balance weight:

$$
T = m g = 1.8 \times 9.81 = 17.658 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} k_T \omega_i^2 = T = 17.658 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Symmetric assumption for pure roll.**

For a pure roll torque without yaw or pitch, we impose:
- Yaw torque = 0: $\omega_1^2 + \omega_3^2 = \omega_2^2 + \omega_4^2$
- Pitch torque = 0: $\omega_1^2 + \omega_2^2 = \omega_3^2 + \omega_4^2$

These two conditions together imply:

$$
\omega_1 = \omega_2 = \omega_3 = \omega_4 = \omega_0 \text{ (no torque)}
$$

To introduce roll, increase motors 2, 3 (right side) by $\Delta$ and decrease motors 1, 4 (left side) by $\Delta$:

$$
\omega_1^2 = \omega_0^2 - \Delta,\quad \omega_2^2 = \omega_0^2 + \Delta,\quad \omega_3^2 = \omega_0^2 + \Delta,\quad \omega_4^2 = \omega_0^2 - \Delta
$$

**Step 3 — Apply thrust constraint.**

$$
k_T \left[ (\omega_0^2 - \Delta) + (\omega_0^2 + \Delta) + (\omega_0^2 + \Delta) + (\omega_0^2 - \Delta) \right] = 4 k_T \omega_0^2 = 17.658
$$

$$
\omega_0^2 = \frac{17.658}{4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{17.658}{4.8 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.679 \times 10^{6} \text{ rad}^2/\text{s}^2
$$

$$
\omega_0 = \sqrt{3.679 \times 10^{6}} = 1918 \text{ rad/s} = 18,316 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply roll torque constraint.**

The roll torque formula (from Section 3.1):

$$
\tau_\phi = L k_T \left( \frac{\omega_2^2 + \omega_3^2}{2} - \frac{\omega_1^2 + \omega_4^2}{2} \right) = L k_T \cdot 2 \Delta
$$

Wait, careful: in X-configuration with proper sign convention, the roll torque is:

$$
\tau_\phi = L k_T \left[(\omega_4^2 - \omega_2^2) + (\omega_3^2 - \omega_1^2)\right]/2
$$

Or with the simpler form (when CW/CCW are properly assigned and signs align):

$$
\tau_\phi = L k_T (2 \Delta) = 2 L k_T \Delta
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\Delta$.**

$$
\Delta = \frac{\tau_\phi}{2 L k_T} = \frac{0.5}{2 \times 0.25 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.5}{6 \times 10^{-7}} = 8.33 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute individual motor speeds.**

$$
\omega_1^2 = \omega_0^2 - \Delta = 3.679 \times 10^{6} - 0.833 \times 10^{6} = 2.846 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\omega_1 = 1687 \text{ rad/s} = 16{,}108 \text{ RPM}
$$

$$
\omega_2^2 = \omega_0^2 + \Delta = 3.679 \times 10^{6} + 0.833 \times 10^{6} = 4.512 \times 10^{6}
$$

$$
\omega_2 = 2124 \text{ rad/s} = 20{,}281 \text{ RPM}
$$

$$
\omega_3 = \omega_2 = 2124 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\omega_4 = \omega_1 = 1687 \text{ rad/s}
$$

**Verification of thrust:** $4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.679 \times 10^{6} = 17.66$ N ✓
**Verification of torque:** $0.25 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 8.33 \times 10^{5} = 0.5$ N·m ✓

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam - 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** Compare the four major classes of aerial robots (fixed-wing, single-rotor helicopter, multirotor, and flapping-wing). Discuss the advantages, disadvantages, and ideal use cases of each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using momentum theory, derive the ideal hover power and endurance of a multirotor UAV with the following specifications: $m = 2.0$ kg, total rotor disk area $A = 0.12$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, battery capacity $5.0$ Ah at nominal voltage $22.2$ V (6S LiPo). State any assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

| Class | Principle | Advantages | Disadvantages | Use Case |
|-------|-----------|------------|---------------|----------|
| **Fixed-Wing** | Lift via fixed airfoil forward motion | High endurance (1–24 h), high speed, long range, efficient cruise | Cannot hover, needs runway or catapult, mechanically complex control surfaces | Long-range surveillance, mapping, large-area agriculture |
| **Single-Rotor Helicopter** | Lift via single large main rotor + tail rotor for anti-torque | High payload, efficient hover, autorotation safety | Mechanically complex (swashplate, gearbox, tail rotor), high vibration, expensive | Heavy-lift industrial, long-endurance SAR |
| **Multirotor (Quad/Hexa/Octo)** | Lift via multiple smaller rotors | VTOL, hover, simple mechanics, high maneuverability, low cost | Short endurance (15–30 min), low efficiency, blade-tip speed limits | Inspection, delivery, cinematography, research |
| **Flapping-Wing (Ornithopter)** | Lift via oscillating wing (biomimetic) | Stealth, low Reynolds number flight, novel research | Extremely low efficiency, poor controllability, very short endurance, fragile | Research, micro-scale surveillance (DARPA Nano Hummingbird) |

> **Comparison Metric (Figure of Merit, $FM$):**
> $FM = P_{ideal} / P_{actual}$. Multirotors typically have $FM \approx 0.5$–$0.6$ (inefficient), while well-designed helicopters reach $FM \approx 0.75$–$0.85$.

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, undisturbed hover (no wind).
2. Ideal actuator disk (no swirl, uniform induced velocity).
3. Battery delivers nominal voltage throughout the flight.
4. No payload other than the multirotor frame.

**Step 1 — Hover thrust.**

$$
T_{hover} = m g = 2.0 \times 9.81 = 19.62 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Induced velocity at the disk.**

$$
v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T_{hover}}{2 \rho A}} = \sqrt{\frac{19.62}{2 \times 1.225 \times 0.12}} = \sqrt{\frac{19.62}{0.294}} = \sqrt{66.73} = 8.17 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Ideal hover power.**

$$
P_{hover} = T_{hover} \cdot v_i = 19.62 \times 8.17 = 160.3 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Battery energy.**

$$
E_{bat} = C \cdot V = 5.0 \times 22.2 = 111.0 \text{ Wh} = 111.0 \times 3600 = 399{,}600 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Ideal endurance.**

$$
t_{ideal} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{hover}} = \frac{399{,}600}{160.3} = 2493 \text{ s} \approx 41.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 6 — Realistic correction.**

Including $\sim 60$–$70\%$ system efficiency (motor, ESC, battery losses), real endurance is:

$$
t_{real} \approx 0.65 \times 41.5 \approx 27 \text{ min}
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 2 kg multirotor with 6S 5 Ah battery hovers for about **27 minutes** in practice, consistent with consumer/professional drone datasheets.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Aerial Robot Answers:**
> 1. **Mixing up DOF and control inputs.** A quadrotor has 4 inputs and 6 DOF — it is **underactuated**. Writing "fully actuated" loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Wrong reference frame.** Body-frame accelerations MUST be rotated via $R_B^E$ to be integrated in the inertial frame. Forgetting this in a dynamics derivation loses 3 marks.
> 3. **Sign errors in motor mixing.** CW and CCW motors have opposite reaction torques. Swapping them inverts the yaw command.
> 4. **Forgetting units.** Motor speeds must be in **rad/s** (not RPM) when used in SI equations. $1 \text{ RPM} = 2\pi / 60 \text{ rad/s}$.
> 5. **Confusing $k_T$ and $k_M$.** $k_T$ relates speed to thrust; $k_M$ relates speed to reaction torque. They are different constants with different units.
> 6. **Writing "drone" in formal exam answers.** Use "UAV" or "Aerial Robot" instead.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aerial Robots**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Aerial Robot = UAV + onboard sensors + autonomous decision-making.
- ⭐ **Taxonomy:** Fixed-wing, Rotary-wing (multirotor/helicopter), Flapping-wing, Hybrid.
- ⭐ **DOF:** Always 6 for a rigid body in 3D (3 translation + 3 rotation).
- ⭐ **Underactuation:** Quadrotor has 4 inputs vs 6 DOF → underactuated; achieves lateral motion by tilting.
- ⭐ **Reference Frames:** Inertial (NED world) and Body (rotates with UAV), connected by $R_B^E$.
- ⭐ **Hover Condition:** $T_{total} = m g$.
- ⭐ **Thrust formula:** $T_i = k_T \omega_i^2$; Yaw torque $M_i = k_M \omega_i^2$.
- ⭐ **PID Cascade:** Outer position loop → Inner attitude loop → Mixer → Motors.
- ⭐ **Critical Sensors:** IMU (attitude, $> 1$ kHz), GPS (position, $1$–$10$ Hz), Barometer (altitude), Magnetometer (heading), Camera/LiDAR (perception).
- ⭐ **Critical Actuators:** BLDC motors + ESCs + Propellers; the **only** source of force/torque in a multirotor.
- ⭐ **Momentum Theory:** $T = 2 \rho A v_i^2$; $P = T v_i$.
- ⭐ **Endurance Equation:** $t = E_{bat} / P_{hover}$.
- ⭐ **Gimbal Lock:** Occurs at $\theta = \pm 90°$ in Euler representation; use quaternions in advanced work.
- ⭐ **KTU Buzzwords to use in answers:** Underactuation, Sensor Fusion (EKF), VTOL, BVLOS, Control Allocation, MTOW.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Industrial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Robot (ISO 8373 / KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> An **automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose manipulator** programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications.

The definition contains **three non-negotiable criteria** a machine must satisfy to qualify as an industrial robot:

1. **Automatically Controlled** – It must operate without continuous human intervention once programmed.
2. **Re-programmable** – Its motion sequences can be altered without permanent physical modification (software-driven, not hard-wired).
3. **Multi-purpose** – It can perform a variety of tasks, not a single dedicated operation.
4. **Three or more Axes** – The minimum Degrees of Freedom (DOF) is three, although most industrial robots have 4–6 DOF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the distinction matters in KTU exams:** A numerically controlled (NC) milling machine, although automatically controlled, is **NOT** a robot because it is dedicated to one task and not easily re-programmable across diverse operations. A pick-and-place arm with only **2 axes** is technically a "mechanical manipulator," not a full industrial robot.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Arm" Mental Model

Imagine a factory worker's arm performing a repetitive spot-welding job on a car body 800 times per shift. Now imagine giving that arm:

- A **mechanical skeleton** with motorized joints (the *manipulator*).
- A **screwdriver socket** instead of a hand (the *end-effector*).
- A **brain in a cabinet** that remembers the welding path (the *controller*).
- **Nerves** that tell it where it is and what it touches (the *sensors*).
- A **power cord and hydraulic lines** feeding energy to the muscles (the *power supply*).

That is, in essence, an **industrial robot** — a programmable mechanical analogue of the human arm, designed to perform industrial tasks with endurance, precision, and without fatigue, boredom, or errors caused by human factors.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple memorization trick for KTU viva:** Remember the acronym **"A-R-M-P-C-S"** for the six anatomical components of any industrial robot:
> **A**ctuators, **R**obotic arm (manipulator), **M**echanical linkages, **P**ower supply, **C**ontroller, **S**ensors (End-effectors can be considered a 7th, "E").

## 1.3 Historical Context (Frequently Asked in KTU 2-Mark Questions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Brief Timeline of Industrial Robotics**
> - **1954:** George Devol files the first patent for a programmable manipulator (USA).
> - **1959:** Devol and Joseph Engelberger found **Unimation Inc.** — the first commercial robotics company. Engelberger is widely called the **"Father of Robotics."**
> - **1961:** The first Unimate robot is installed at a General Motors plant in New Jersey for die-casting.
> - **1969:** Victor Scheinman at Stanford builds the **Stanford Arm** (first electrically powered, articulated 6-DOF robot).
> - **1973:** KUKA builds the first **6-axis electromechanical robot (Famulus).**
> - **1978:** The **PUMA (Programmable Universal Machine for Assembly)** is born out of General Motors / Unimation.
> - **1980s onward:** Advent of SCARA, Delta, and collaborative robots (cobots).

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Performance Metrics (Memorize These!)

Any industrial robot is specified by a handful of **standard performance metrics** that appear in every KTU question paper:

| Metric | Definition | Typical Industrial Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Payload** | Maximum mass the robot can lift at rated speed | 3 kg – 1000 kg |
| **Reach** | Maximum distance from robot base to end-effector center | 0.5 m – 3.5 m |
| **Repeatability** | Ability to return to a pre-taught position under same conditions | ±0.02 mm to ±0.5 mm |
| **Accuracy** | Ability to reach an absolute commanded position in world coordinates | Lower than repeatability |
| **Resolution** | Smallest incremental motion the controller can command | Function of encoder bits |
| **Speed** | Maximum TCP (Tool Center Point) velocity | 1 m/s – 10 m/s |
| **DOF** | Number of independent positional/orientation variables | 3, 4, 5, or 6 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Accuracy** with **Repeatability**. A robot can be *highly repeatable but poorly accurate* (it always returns to the same wrong spot). Accuracy depends on the absolute calibration of the kinematic model; repeatability depends on the precision of the joint encoders and backlash.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomical Architecture of an Industrial Robot

An industrial robot system is composed of **four tightly coupled sub-systems**:

### 2.1.1 The Manipulator (Mechanical Arm)

The manipulator is the **mechanical structure** — a series of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints. It is the only part of the robot that physically moves in the workspace.

- **Links:** Rigid mechanical members (typically steel or aluminum castings).
- **Joints:** Two principal types:
  - **Prismatic (P / L):** Linear sliding motion. *d* is the joint variable.
  - **Revolute (R):** Rotary motion about a fixed axis. *θ* is the joint variable.
- **Wrist:** The terminal cluster of 2 or 3 joints that orients the end-effector.
- **Base:** The fixed mounting point that anchors the robot to the floor or rail.

### 2.1.2 The End-Effector

The end-effector is the **specialized tool mounted on the wrist** that interacts with the workpiece. Two broad families:

- **Grippers** (mechanical, vacuum, magnetic, adhesive) — for pick-and-place.
- **Tools** (welding torch, paint sprayer, drilling spindle, cutting laser) — for process operations.

### 2.1.3 The Controller

The **"brain"** of the robot. Modern controllers are multi-CPU industrial PCs running real-time operating systems (RTOS). They execute:

1. **Trajectory planning** (interpolation between taught points).
2. **Inverse kinematics** (computing joint angles from Cartesian targets).
3. **Servo loops** (PID control of each joint motor at 1–8 kHz).
4. **I/O handling** (communication with PLCs, vision systems, conveyors).
5. **Safety monitoring** (E-stops, zone limits, collision detection).

### 2.1.4 The Power Supply & Drive System

Converts mains power (AC 415 V / 50 Hz in India) into controlled energy for the actuators. Three drive technologies dominate industry:

- **Hydraulic drives** — high payload (> 100 kg), used in foundries and forging.
- **Pneumatic drives** — low cost, low payload, high speed; common in pick-and-place.
- **Electric drives** — servo motors (AC synchronous with absolute encoders). Dominant modern technology.

## 2.2 Robot Classification — The KTU-Critical Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 expects students to classify robots along FOUR independent axes.**

### 2.2.1 Classification by Coordinate System / Arm Geometry

This is the **most heavily tested** classification. There are **six canonical geometries**:

| Geometry | Coordinate System | Joint Sequence (Base → Wrist) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian / Rectilinear (PPP)** | X, Y, Z linear | P – P – P | Loading CNC, palletizing |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | θ, r, z | R – P – P | Assembly, machine tending |
| **Spherical / Polar (RRP)** | θ, φ, r | R – R – P | Welding, painting |
| **Articulated / Anarm (RRR)** | Joint angles | R – R – R (and wrist) | Welding, sealing, complex paths |
| **SCARA** (Selective Compliant Articulated Robot Arm) | X, Y, Z + θ | R – R – P | PCB assembly, pick-and-place |
| **Delta / Parallel** | Three parallel arms | Parallelograms | Ultra-high-speed packaging |

### 2.2.2 Classification by Control Method

- **Non-servo / Open-loop / Pick-and-place:** Limit switches stop motion; no feedback. Cheapest.
- **Servo / Closed-loop / Point-to-point:** Feedback from encoders; moves between taught points without controlling the path between them.
- **Servo / Continuous-path:** Controls the entire trajectory (every interpolation point), essential for painting, welding, and sealing.

### 2.2.3 Classification by Drive Technology

Hydraulic / Pneumatic / Electric (already discussed in §2.1.4).

### 2.2.4 Classification by Application

- **Assembly robots**
- **Welding robots** (spot, arc)
- **Material handling robots** (palletizing, machine tending)
- **Painting robots**
- **Inspection robots**

## 2.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Mobility Index

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DOF** = the number of independent positional and orientational parameters required to completely specify the configuration of the end-effector in 3D space.

In 3D space, a rigid body possesses a maximum of **6 DOF**:
- **3 positional DOF** (translation along X, Y, Z).
- **3 orientational DOF** (rotation about X — roll, Y — pitch, Z — yaw).

Industrial robots are categorized by DOF count:

- **3 DOF** — positioning only, no orientation control (rare in industry).
- **4 DOF** — typical SCARA (3 position + 1 rotation).
- **5 DOF** — common in palletizing, basic welding.
- **6 DOF** — universal; can place and orient the end-effector anywhere within the workspace.
- **7+ DOF** — research-grade and cobots, with redundancy for obstacle avoidance.

## 2.4 Work Envelope (Workspace)

The **work envelope** is the volume of space that the end-effector (specifically the Tool Center Point, TCP) can reach.

| Robot Geometry | Work Envelope Shape | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Cartesian | Rectangular box | $2a \times 2b \times 2c$ |
| Cylindrical | Hollow cylinder segment | radius *r*, height *h* |
| Spherical | Spherical sector | radius *r*, cone angle 2α |
| Articulated | Irregular lobed volume | Aproximated by spherical sector with dead zones |
| SCARA | Flat horizontal disc | limited vertical Z stroke |
| Delta | Dome / frustum | Fast, shallow workspace above base |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the consolidated cheat sheet — memorize it cold for the 14-mark derivations.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grübler's DOF formula (planar) | $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | *n* links, $j_1$ 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ 2-DOF joints |
| 2 | Grübler's DOF formula (spatial) | $F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | n links, $j_k$ joints with k DOF |
| 3 | Resolution of joint (n-bit encoder, gear ratio *N*) | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{2^n \cdot N}$ | Smallest controllable angular increment |
| 4 | Resolution of prismatic joint | $\Delta d = \dfrac{L_{stroke}}{2^n}$ | $L_{stroke}$ = total mechanical travel |
| 5 | Cartesian workspace volume | $V_C = 8\,a\,b\,c$ | Half-lengths *a, b, c* on each axis |
| 6 | Cylindrical workspace volume | $V_{Cy} = \pi\,r^2\,h$ | *r* = radial reach, *h* = vertical stroke |
| 7 | Spherical workspace volume | $V_S = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi\,r^3\,(1 - \cos\alpha)$ | *r* = reach, $\alpha$ = half cone angle |
| 8 | Repeatability definition | $R = \pm\,3\sigma_{positional\,error}$ | Three-sigma statistical band |
| 9 | Maximum Cartesian reach (spherical) | $R_{max} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ | Sum of arm link lengths |
| 10 | Pose error propagation | $\delta p = J(q)\,\delta q$ | *J* is the Jacobian matrix at configuration *q* |

## 2.6 Why Industrial Robots Matter — Real-World Utility

Industrial robots are the backbone of **Industry 4.0** and **Smart Manufacturing**:

- **Automotive sector:** A single BMW assembly line uses ~800 robots, with 99.97 % dimensional repeatability on the body-in-white.
- **Electronics:** Delta robots execute > 300 picks per minute in SMD placement.
- **Pharmaceutical:** ISO Class 5 cleanroom robots dispense and assemble sterile products.
- **Aerospace:** 8-axis hybrid robots drill and rivet fuselage panels with 0.1 mm tolerance.
- **ROI metric:** A typical 6-axis welding robot pays back its capital cost in **18–24 months** at two-shift operation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Grübler's Formula for Degrees of Freedom (Spatial Case)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most commonly asked 7-mark derivation** under Module 1.

### 3.1.1 Conceptual Foundation

A mechanism is a system of rigid links constrained by joints. We count:

- **Independent motions available** to the system **without** the joints (free-flight DOF).
- **Independent constraints** imposed by the joints.
- The remaining motions **are the mechanism's DOF.**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Unconstrained Rigid Bodies in 3D Space**

A single rigid body in 3D space has **6 degrees of freedom** (3 translations + 3 rotations).

**Step 2 — Total Free DOF of *n* Unconnected Links**

If the mechanism has *n* rigid links (including the ground/base), the total unconstrained DOF is:

$$
DOF_{free} = 6\,n
$$

**Step 3 — DOF Lost at Each Joint**

A *k*-DOF joint removes $6 - k$ degrees of freedom from the system. So a joint with $k$ DOF imposes $c_k = 6 - k$ constraints.

**Step 4 — Total Constraints**

If the mechanism has $j_k$ joints of type *k* (i.e., each joint permits *k* independent relative motions), the total number of constraints is:

$$
DOF_{lost} = \sum_{k=1}^{5} (6 - k)\,j_k
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract Ground Link**

One link is the **ground** (immobile base) and should not be counted as a free body, so the free DOF becomes $6(n - 1)$.

**Step 6 — Net DOF of the Mechanism**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= DOF_{free} - DOF_{lost} \\
F &= 6(n-1) - \sum_{k=1}^{5} (6 - k)\,j_k \\
F &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Grübler's formula (spatial version).**

### 3.1.3 Worked Example — A 6-DOF Articulated Robot

A standard 6R industrial robot has:

- *n* = 7 links (base + 6 moving links, including end-effector)
- $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints, each permitting 1 DOF

Substituting:

$$
F = 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 4(0) - 3(0) - 2(0) - 1(0)
$$

$$
F = 6 \times 6 - 30 = 36 - 30 = 6
$$

So the manipulator indeed has **6 DOF**, matching the desired result.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resolution of a Rotary Joint with Encoder

### 3.2.1 Setup

A revolute joint is driven through a **harmonic-drive (or RV) reducer** of ratio *N* : 1 (i.e., the motor turns *N* times for one joint revolution). The motor shaft carries an **absolute optical encoder** of *n* bits.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Counts per Motor Revolution**

A *n*-bit encoder produces:

$$
C = 2^n \text{ counts per motor revolution}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Increment per Count (at the Motor Shaft)**

$$
\Delta\theta_{motor} = \frac{360°}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Reduction at the Gear Train**

The gear train divides the motor's rotation by *N* to produce the joint's rotation:

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = \frac{\Delta\theta_{motor}}{N} = \frac{360°}{2^n \cdot N}
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to Radians (for KTU numerical problems)**

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = \frac{2\pi}{2^n \cdot N} \text{ radians}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example

A KUKA KR 16 robot joint uses a 17-bit absolute encoder (typical for high-end industrial servo) and a 100:1 harmonic drive. Calculate the joint resolution.

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = \frac{360°}{2^{17} \times 100}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = \frac{360°}{131072 \times 100} = \frac{360°}{13\,107\,200}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = 2.747 \times 10^{-5} \text{ degrees} = 0.099 \text{ arc-seconds}
$$

This is the **finest angular increment** the controller can command — and it is the foundation of the robot's sub-millimetre Cartesian repeatability.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Work Envelope Volume of a Spherical Robot

A spherical-coordinate robot has:

- A shoulder revolute joint rotating through angle $\phi_{max}$ (azimuth).
- An elbow revolute joint rotating through angle $\alpha$ (elevation).
- A prismatic extension of length *r* reaching out to *R*.

### 3.3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set Up the Spherical Sector**

The end-effector sweeps a **spherical sector** of radius *R*, with polar half-angle $\alpha$ and azimuthal sweep $\phi_{max}$.

**Step 2 — Volume Element in Spherical Coordinates**

In spherical coordinates $(R, \phi, \theta)$:

$$
dV = R^2 \sin\theta \, dR \, d\phi \, d\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_S &= \int_{0}^{R}\int_{0}^{\phi_{max}}\int_{0}^{\alpha} R^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \, dR \\
V_S &= \int_{0}^{R} R^2\, dR \cdot \int_{0}^{\phi_{max}} d\phi \cdot \int_{0}^{\alpha} \sin\theta\, d\theta \\
V_S &= \frac{R^3}{3} \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot \left[-\cos\theta\right]_{0}^{\alpha} \\
V_S &= \frac{R^3}{3} \cdot \phi_{max} \cdot (1 - \cos\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

If the robot rotates a full circle ($\phi_{max} = 2\pi$):

$$
V_S = \frac{2\pi R^3}{3}(1 - \cos\alpha)
$$

For a **half-sphere** ($\alpha = \pi/2$):

$$
V_S = \frac{2\pi R^3}{3} \approx 2.094\,R^3
$$

For a **full sphere** ($\alpha = \pi$):

$$
V_S = \frac{4\pi R^3}{3}
$$

This recovers the familiar formula for the volume of a sphere — a useful sanity check.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Robot Geometries vs. Application Suitability

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical matrix is a **direct KTU 2024 Part-B question template** — students should be able to reproduce it from memory.

| Property | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) | SCARA | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stiffness** | Very High | High | Medium | Medium-Low | High | High |
| **Reach-to-Footprint** | Poor | Medium | Good | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Payload** | High | Medium-High | Medium-High | Medium | Low-Medium | Low |
| **Speed** | Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | High | Very High |
| **Repeatability** | ±0.05 mm | ±0.05 mm | ±0.10 mm | ±0.10–0.30 mm | ±0.02 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| **Floor Space** | High | High | Medium | Low | Low | Low |
| **Programming Ease** | Easy | Medium | Medium | Hard | Medium | Hard |
| **Best For** | Heavy load transfer | Machine tending | Welding | Complex 3D paths | PCB assembly | High-speed packaging |
| **Cost Index** | 1× | 1.2× | 1.4× | 1.6× | 1.3× | 2.0× |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Repeatability vs. Accuracy Numerical Bound

A robot returns to a taught point *P* a total of *N* times. Let the deviations from *P* along the X-axis be $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N$.

**Standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma_x = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}
$$

**Repeatability** (per ISO 9283) is defined as **±3σ** of the positional scatter:

$$
R = \pm 3\sigma
$$

**Accuracy** is the deviation of the **mean** of the *N* measurements from the commanded position:

$$
A = |\bar{x} - x_{commanded}|
$$

This is **why a poorly calibrated robot can be highly repeatable** ($\sigma$ small) but inaccurate ($\bar{x}$ far from target). KTU 2024 expects students to compute both.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anatomical Block Diagram of an Industrial Robot System

<!--
Mermaid Safeguard Compliance:
- All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefix.
- No reserved keywords used as node IDs.
- No markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels.
-->
```mermaid
graph TD
    PS[POWER SUPPLY UNIT] --> CONV[Power Converter]
    PS --> HYD[Hydraulic Reservoir]
    CONV --> ACT[ACTUATOR BANK]
    HYD --> ACT
    ACT --> ARM[ROBOT ARM MANIPULATOR]
    SENS[SENSOR ARRAY] --> ARM
    SENS --> CONT[CONTROLLER]
    TCH[Teach Pendant] --> CONT
    PLC[Plant PLC] --> CONT
    CONT --> ACT
    CONT --> INV[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    INV --> ACT
    ARM --> WRIST[Robotic Wrist]
    WRIST --> EE[END EFFECTOR]
    EE --> WORK[Workpiece in Workspace]
    SAF[Safety Scanner Light Curtain] --> CONT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Power Supply Unit feeds both electrical (via converter) and hydraulic media. The Controller (the brain) receives high-level commands from a Teach Pendant or factory PLC, runs the inverse-kinematics solver to map Cartesian goals to joint angles, and closes the servo loop on the actuator bank using feedback from the sensor array. The Safety Scanner is a hard-wired, independent layer that can cut power at any moment.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of Industrial Robots

```mermaid
graph TD
    IR[INDUSTRIAL ROBOT]
    IR --> CG[By Arm Geometry]
    IR --> CM[By Control Method]
    IR --> DR[By Drive Technology]
    IR --> AP[By Application]

    CG --> CG1[Cartesian PPP]
    CG --> CG2[Cylindrical RPP]
    CG --> CG3[Spherical RRP]
    CG --> CG4[Articulated RRR]
    CG --> CG5[SCARA RRP]
    CG --> CG6[Delta Parallel]

    CM --> CM1[Non Servo Open Loop]
    CM --> CM2[Servo Point to Point]
    CM --> CM3[Servo Continuous Path]

    DR --> DR1[Hydraulic]
    DR --> DR2[Pneumatic]
    DR --> DR3[Electric Servo]

    AP --> AP1[Material Handling]
    AP --> AP2[Welding]
    AP --> AP3[Painting]
    AP --> AP4[Assembly]
    AP --> AP5[Inspection]
```

## 4.3 Work Envelope Comparison — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Cartesian Robot] -->|Rectangular Box| B1[Reach X times Y times Z]
    C[Cylindrical Robot] -->|Hollow Cylinder| B2[Pi times R squared times H]
    D[Spherical Robot] -->|Spherical Sector| B3[Two Pi R cubed over three]
    E[Articulated Robot] -->|Irregular Lobe| B4[Spherical with dead zones]
    F[SCARA Robot] -->|Flat Disc| B5[Cylindrical disc with limited Z]
    G[Delta Robot] -->|Dome| B6[Inverted frustum above base]

    B1 --> APPS[Application Mapping]
    B2 --> APPS
    B3 --> APPS
    B4 --> APPS
    B5 --> APPS
    B6 --> APPS

    APPS --> APP1[CNC Tending]
    APPS --> APP2[Machine Tending]
    APPS --> APP3[Welding Painting]
    APPS --> APP4[Complex 3D Paths]
    APPS --> APP5[PCB Assembly]
    APPS --> APP6[High Speed Packaging]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of a Robot Joint Servo Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    REF[Reference Joint Angle] --> SUM[Junction Summation]
    FBK[Encoder Feedback] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal]
    ERR --> PID[PID Controller]
    PID --> AMP[PWM Amplifier]
    AMP --> MTR[Servo Motor]
    MTR --> GBX[Gear Reducer]
    GBX --> JT[Robot Joint Movement]
    JT --> FBK
    JT --> ARM[Manipulator Link Motion]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key takeaway from the servo diagram:** The PID controller operates at 1–8 kHz. A 17-bit encoder at the motor shaft, combined with a 100:1 reducer, gives ~0.1 arc-second joint resolution. The closed-loop bandwidth ultimately determines the dynamic performance of the robot — the higher the bandwidth, the faster and more accurately the joint can track a moving reference.

## 4.5 Kinematic Joint Notation Legend (DH Convention Preview)

```mermaid
graph LR
    R[Revolute Joint Symbol Theta] --> M1[Rotation about Z]
    P[Prismatic Joint Symbol D] --> M2[Translation along Z]
    M1 --> AS[Assign link length a twist angle alpha]
    M2 --> AS
    AS --> DH[Denavit Hartenberg Frame]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The DH (Denavit–Hartenberg) parameterization is a Module 2 topic; this diagram previews the joint-notation vocabulary that students must already recognize to follow the kinematic chains in §2.2.1.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**  
Define an **Industrial Robot** as per ISO 8373. List any **four** key characteristics that distinguish it from a conventional NC machine tool.

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** An Industrial Robot is an automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be fixed or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications (ISO 8373:2021).
>
> **Distinguishing Characteristics (any four):**
> 1. **Re-programmability** — Robot programs can be altered via software; NC machines typically require re-coding of G-code and sometimes physical retooling.
> 2. **Multi-purpose versatility** — A single robot can weld, paint, pick, and assemble with end-effector changeover.
> 3. **Multi-axes articulation** — Industrial robots have ≥ 3 programmable axes; NC machines usually have only 2–3 (X, Y, Z) and one spindle.
> 4. **Continuous path / point-to-point programming flexibility** — Robots support both PTP and CP motion in 3D space; NC machines move along pre-defined linear/circular paths only.
> 5. **Open architectural integration** — Robots can be re-deployed on different tasks; NC machines are dedicated.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**  
Differentiate between **Repeatability** and **Accuracy** of an industrial robot, with a numerical example.

**Model Answer:**

> **Repeatability** is the ability of the robot to return to a pre-taught position under identical conditions. It is quantified as ±3σ of the position scatter over many cycles. It depends on joint encoder precision, backlash, and mechanical stiffness.
>
> **Accuracy** is the ability of the robot to reach an *absolute* commanded Cartesian position in world coordinates. It depends on the fidelity of the kinematic model (link lengths, joint offsets) and the calibration of the base frame.
>
> **Numerical Example:** Suppose a robot is commanded to move to the point (500.00 mm, 250.00 mm) in the base frame. It returns there 50 times, and the actual mean position is (500.40 mm, 250.25 mm) with a standard deviation of 0.03 mm.
> - **Accuracy** = $\vert\bar{x} - x_{cmd}\vert = \sqrt{0.40^2 + 0.25^2} = 0.47$ mm.
> - **Repeatability** = $\pm 3\sigma = \pm 0.09$ mm.
>
> Hence the robot is **highly repeatable** (±0.09 mm) but **poorly accurate** (0.47 mm error) — a classic symptom of an uncalibrated kinematic model.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024]**  
*(a)* Classify industrial robots **by arm geometry** with neat sketches. Discuss the **work envelope** and **typical applications** of each. **(7 marks)**  
*(b)* Derive **Grübler's formula** for the degrees of freedom of a spatial mechanism. Apply it to compute the DOF of a **6R articulated robot** and a **SCARA robot (RRP)**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification by Arm Geometry**

| Geometry | Joint Notation | Work Envelope Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (PPP) | P-P-P | Rectangular box $2a \times 2b \times 2c$ | CNC loading, palletizing, inspection |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | R-P-P | Hollow cylinder $\pi r^2 h$ | Machine tending, die casting |
| Spherical / Polar (RRP) | R-R-P | Spherical sector | Welding, painting, deburring |
| Articulated (RRR) | R-R-R | Lobed / spherical with dead zones | Complex 3D path welding, sealing |
| SCARA (RRP) | R-R-P | Flat disc + limited Z | PCB assembly, pick-and-place |
| Delta (parallel) | 3 parallelograms | Dome / frustum | Ultra-fast packaging |

*Neat sketch marks* — Students should draw each robot's joint axis orientation and a representative envelope using a 2-D projection.

**Part (b) — Grübler's Formula Derivation**

Refer to §3.1.2 above for the full derivation. Final result:

$$
F = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
$$

**Application 1 — 6R Articulated Robot:**

$$
n = 7, \quad j_1 = 6
$$

$$
F = 6(7-1) - 5(6) = 36 - 30 = 6 \text{ DOF}
$$

**Application 2 — SCARA (RRP) Robot:**

A SCARA has 3 moving links and 3 joints (2 revolute, 1 prismatic):

$$
n = 4, \quad j_1 = 3
$$

$$
F = 6(4-1) - 5(3) = 18 - 15 = 3 \text{ DOF}
$$

(Note: A SCARA is sometimes specified as 4-DOF if the wrist includes an additional rotation, but the canonical RRP gives 3 DOF.)

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
*(a)* Explain the **anatomy of an industrial robot** with a labeled block diagram. Describe the role of each of the **six major components**. **(7 marks)**  
*(b)* A revolute joint of an industrial robot uses a **20-bit absolute encoder** and a **harmonic-drive gear reducer of ratio 120:1**. Calculate the **joint angular resolution** in degrees and arc-seconds. If the link length is 350 mm, compute the resulting **linear resolution** at the end of the link. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Anatomy of an Industrial Robot**

Refer to the diagram in §4.1. The six major components and their roles:

1. **Manipulator (Arm):** The mechanical structure — a chain of links and joints that physically positions the end-effector. It is the only moving part of the robot.
2. **End-Effector:** The task-specific tool (gripper, welder, sprayer) mounted on the wrist.
3. **Actuators:** The "muscles" — servo motors, hydraulic cylinders, or pneumatic pistons that move the joints.
4. **Controller:** The "brain" — a real-time computer that runs the trajectory planner, inverse kinematics, and servo loops.
5. **Sensors:** Encoders, force/torque sensors, vision cameras, and proximity switches that provide feedback to the controller.
6. **Power Supply:** Converts mains electricity (and/or compressed air / hydraulic fluid) into regulated energy for the actuators.

**Part (b) — Resolution Calculation**

**Step 1 — Counts per Motor Revolution**

$$
C = 2^{20} = 1\,048\,576 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 2 — Joint Resolution in Degrees**

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = \frac{360°}{2^{20} \times 120}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = \frac{360°}{125\,829\,120}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = 2.861 \times 10^{-6} \text{ degrees}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Arc-Seconds**

$$
1° = 3600 \text{ arc-seconds}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = 2.861 \times 10^{-6} \times 3600
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = 0.0103 \text{ arc-seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear Resolution at End of 350 mm Link**

For a small angular increment, the linear resolution is:

$$
\Delta L = L \cdot \Delta\theta_{joint} \text{ (radians)}
$$

$$
\Delta\theta_{joint} = \frac{2.861 \times 10^{-6} \times \pi}{180} = 4.994 \times 10^{-8} \text{ rad}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 0.350 \times 4.994 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
\Delta L = 1.748 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 0.0175 \text{ μm}
$$

This sub-20-nanometre theoretical resolution is far smaller than the **mechanical repeatability** (~ ±0.05 mm in practice), confirming that the resolution is encoder-limited, not the dominant source of error.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Trap:** Forgetting to convert degrees → radians before computing $\Delta L = L \cdot \Delta\theta$. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Wrong Joint Count in Grübler's:** Including the end-effector as an extra link or forgetting to count the ground as a link. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 3. **Confusing Resolution with Repeatability:** Resolution is the *minimum increment*; repeatability is the *statistical returnability*. They are NOT the same number. Marks lost: 1.
> 4. **Omitting the *n − 1* term in Grübler's:** A very common error. Always write the full formula before substituting.
> 5. **Forgetting joint types in classification:** Listing 6 robots but failing to identify them as PPP, RPP, RRP, or RRR. Marks lost: 1.
> 6. **Not drawing a work envelope in classification answers:** Marks are explicitly reserved for a sketch — always include one, however rough.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Industrial Robots (Module 1)**

- **Definition (ISO 8373):** Automatically controlled, re-programmable, multi-purpose manipulator with ≥ 3 axes. Memorize the four-part definition word-for-word.
- **Father of Robotics:** Joseph F. Engelberger. Inventor of the first industrial robot: George Devol. First installed robot: Unimate, 1961, GM plant.
- **Six anatomical components:** Manipulator, end-effector, actuators, controller, sensors, power supply. Mnemonic: **"A-R-M-P-C-S"** or "**M**an, **E**nd, **A**ctuator, **C**ontroller, **S**ensor, **P**ower."
- **Six arm geometries:** Cartesian (PPP), Cylindrical (RPP), Spherical (RRP), Articulated (RRR), SCARA (RRP), Delta (parallel). Know joint notation, envelope, and application for each.
- **DOF in 3D:** Max 6 (3 translation + 3 rotation). Industrial robots typically have 4, 5, or 6 DOF.
- **Grübler's formula (spatial):** $F = 6(n-1) - \sum (6-k)j_k$. Special case: $F = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ (planar).
- **Performance metrics:** Payload, reach, repeatability (±3σ), accuracy, resolution, speed, DOF.
- **Resolution formula (rotary joint):** $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{360°}{2^n \cdot N}$. Always convert to radians for linear-resolution calculations.
- **Workspace volumes:** Cartesian $V = 8abc$, Cylindrical $V = \pi r^2 h$, Spherical $V = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi r^3(1-\cos\alpha)$.
- **Repeatability ≠ Accuracy:** Repeatability is statistical scatter (3σ); accuracy is mean offset from commanded target.
- **Three control methods:** Non-servo (limit-switch based), servo PTP (encoder feedback), servo continuous-path (full trajectory control).
- **Three drive technologies:** Hydraulic (heavy payload, low precision), Pneumatic (light, fast, low precision), Electric servo (dominant, high precision).
- **Work envelope shapes (one-line recall):** Box / Cylinder / Sphere / Lobed-sphere / Disc / Dome.
- **SCARA DOF:** Canonical RRP = 3 DOF; commercial 4-DOF versions add a wrist roll.
- **Delta robot:** Parallel kinematic chain; ultra-high-speed (> 300 picks/min) packaging; low payload (< 5 kg typical).
- **KTU 2024 hot topics:** Definitions, classification tables, Grübler's formula derivation, resolution numericals, repeatability vs accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Humanoids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Humanoids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HUMANOID ROBOT (KTU 2024 Standard Definition):**
> A *humanoid robot* is a **bipedal, anthropomorphic robotic system** whose physical architecture, kinematic structure, and behavioral patterns emulate the **morphology, biomechanics, and cognitive interaction** of the human body. It integrates multi-degree-of-freedom (multi-DOF) articulated limbs, a sensor-rich perception stack, and an actuator-driven actuation stack under a unified real-time control architecture.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: PCRAT205)**, a humanoid is studied not merely as a mechanical replica of a human, but as an **integrated mechatronic platform** where the synergy between **Sensors** (perception of the environment and internal state) and **Actuators** (execution of physical motion) defines the machine's class and capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Humanoids Matter in Robotics & AI Engineering:**
> Humanoid form-factors are designed for **human-centric environments** — doorways, staircases, tool handles, and vehicles — all of which are dimensioned for the human body. Therefore, a humanoid morphology provides the **highest universal compatibility** for service, healthcare, disaster-response, and industrial co-working applications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **chess-playing grandmaster who is also a marathon runner**. The grandmaster must:

- **See** the board clearly (perception = sensors).
- **Decide** a move (cognition = AI controller / processor).
- **Physically lift** a chess piece and run a marathon (execution = actuators).

A humanoid robot is exactly this — a **"complete athlete"**. It does not just *think* (which a desktop AI can do) and it does not just *push a payload* (which a wheeled AGV can do). It must **perceive, reason, and physically move in a complex, unstructured, three-dimensional human world** — all at the same time.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Nervous & Muscular System:**
> - **Sensors** in a humanoid = Human *senses* (vision cameras ≈ eyes; IMU ≈ inner-ear vestibular system; force/torque sensors ≈ skin pressure receptors; microphones ≈ ears).
> - **Actuators** in a humanoid = Human *muscles and joints* (electric servomotors ≈ muscle fibers; series-elastic actuators ≈ tendons; harmonic drives ≈ bone joints).
> - **Controller** = Human *brain and spinal cord*.

---

## 1.3 Key Classification Metrics of Humanoids

| Metric | Typical Range | Standard / Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Height | **0.5 m – 1.9 m** | From miniature humanoids (NAO ~0.58 m) to full-scale (ASIMO ~1.3 m, Optimus ~1.7 m) |
| Total Degrees of Freedom (DOF) | **20 – 50+** | Sum of independent rotational/translational joint variables |
| Number of Actuators | **20 – 60+** | Each active joint requires ≥1 actuator (often 2 for compliance) |
| Sensors Onboard | **10 – 70+** | Cameras, IMUs, encoders, force/torque, tactile, sonar/LiDAR |
| Locomotion Mode | **Bipedal walking** | Static or dynamic walking; running; stair climbing |
| Power Source | **Li-Ion / Li-Po battery** | 24 V – 48 V DC; autonomy 30 min – 4 hrs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Exam Highlight:**
> Whenever a humanoid question appears, examiners expect students to **explicitly mention the Sensor-Actuator-Controller triad**. A description that focuses only on the mechanical shell (plastic + metal) without referencing sensors/actuators will lose significant marks.

---

## 1.4 Historical & Evolutionary Milestones (Contextual Background)

| Year | Robot | Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| 1973 | **WABOT-1** (Waseda Univ.) | First full-scale anthropomorphic humanoid (gait + vision + conversation) |
| 2000 | **ASIMO** (Honda) | Stable dynamic walking using **ZMP-based** control |
| 2006 | **Nao** (SoftBank / Aldebaran) | Standardized research & education platform |
| 2013 | **Atlas** (Boston Dynamics) | Hydraulic humanoids; rough-terrain locomotion |
| 2016 | **Sophia** (Hanson Robotics) | Social humanoid; NLP-driven facial expression |
| 2022 | **Optimus** (Tesla) | Mass-production-oriented bipedal humanoid |
| 2024 | **Figure 01 / 02** | Foundation-model-driven general-purpose humanoids |

This timeline demonstrates the **progression from research curiosities → commercial service robots → AI-integrated general-purpose platforms**, which directly aligns with KTU 2024's NEP-2020 vision of interdisciplinary AI-engineering.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Anatomical Architecture of a Humanoid

A humanoid is decomposed into the following **sub-systems** — each sub-system has a dedicated sensor/actuator mapping that KTU examiners love to test.

### 2.1.1 Head Subsystem
- **Purpose:** Perception & social interaction.
- **Sensors:** Stereo RGB cameras (vision), MEMS microphones (hearing), IMU (head orientation tracking), depth sensors (structured-light / ToF).
- **Actuators:** Pan-tilt servos (neck DOF: yaw + pitch), facial-servo array (eye-blinks, lip movement for expression).
- **Typical DOF:** 2 (neck) + 6–12 (face).

### 2.1.2 Torso / Trunk Subsystem
- **Purpose:** Houses the main controller, battery, and acts as the central kinematic link.
- **Sensors:** Central IMU (full-body orientation), battery management sensors (voltage, current, temperature).
- **Actuators:** Waist joint (yaw rotation) — often 1–3 DOF.
- **Engineering insight:** The torso's **Center of Mass (CoM)** location is critical for balance calculations.

### 2.1.3 Arm Subsystem (x2)
- **Purpose:** Manipulation, gestures, interaction.
- **Sensors:** Joint encoders (position/velocity feedback), current sensors (torque estimation), tactile sensors in fingertips (grip force).
- **Actuators:** Brushless DC / servo motors with harmonic-drive reducers.
- **Typical DOF per arm:** **7 DOF** (shoulder: 3, elbow: 1, forearm: 1, wrist: 2) — matches the **7-DOF human arm** anthropomorphism.

### 2.1.4 Leg Subsystem (x2)
- **Purpose:** Bipedal locomotion and balance.
- **Sensors:** Joint encoders, **6-axis Force/Torque (F/T) sensors at the ankles**, foot contact sensors, foot-pressure-distribution sensors.
- **Actuators:** High-torque brushless motors with **series-elastic actuators (SEA)** for impact absorption.
- **Typical DOF per leg:** **6 DOF** (hip: 3, knee: 1, ankle: 2) — total 12 DOF for the lower body.

---

## 2.2 The Sensor–Actuator–Controller Triad (KTU High-Yield Concept)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Layer Control Architecture of a Humanoid:**

**Layer 1 — Perception (Sensors):**
Raw signals from cameras, IMUs, encoders, and F/T sensors are pre-processed (filtering, denoising, feature extraction).

**Layer 2 — Cognition (Controller / AI):**
A real-time controller (RTOS, e.g., Xenomai / PREEMPT-RT on Linux) computes the desired joint trajectories and stability metrics (ZMP, CoM). Modern humanoids inject **learned policies** (reinforcement learning, imitation learning) here.

**Layer 3 — Action (Actuators):**
Low-level motor controllers (current-mode PWM drivers) execute the joint torques through gear-reduced servo chains, often using **impedance / admittance control** for safe physical interaction.

---

## 2.3 Zero Moment Point (ZMP) — The Foundation of Bipedal Balance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Zero Moment Point (ZMP) Definition:**
> The ZMP is the specific point on the ground plane about which the **net moment of the ground-reaction-force distribution is zero**. For a humanoid to maintain **dynamic balance**, the ZMP must lie **strictly inside the support polygon** formed by the foot (or both feet during double-support phase).

The support polygon is the convex hull of all foot contact points in the horizontal plane.

**Stability criterion (KTU standard inequality):**

$$ \vec{p}_{ZMP} \in \text{Interior}\bigl(\text{SupportPolygon}\bigr) $$

If the ZMP exits this polygon, the robot will **tip over** — the classical "falling" failure mode.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!TIP]
> This table is the **minimum required** reference set for solving any humanoid-related KTU numerical problem.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Degrees of Freedom (planar) | $\text{DOF} = 3n - 2p - q$ | $n$ = links, $p$ = lower pairs, $q$ = higher pairs | dimensionless |
| 2 | DOF (spatial, Kutzbach) | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5p_1 - 4p_2 - 3p_3 - 2p_4 - p_5$ | $p_i$ = joints with $i$ DOF | dimensionless |
| 3 | ZMP X-coordinate (2D sagittal) | $x_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} m_i\bigl(\ddot{z}_i + g\bigr)x_i - \sum_{i} m_i\ddot{x}_i z_i}{\sum_{i} m_i\bigl(\ddot{z}_i + g\bigr)}$ | $m_i$ = link mass, $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$ | m |
| 4 | ZMP Y-coordinate (lateral) | $y_{ZMP} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} m_i\bigl(\ddot{z}_i + g\bigr)y_i - \sum_{i} m_i\ddot{y}_i z_i}{\sum_{i} m_i\bigl(\ddot{z}_i + g\bigr)}$ | lateral analogue of (3) | m |
| 5 | Center of Mass (CoM) | $\vec{r}_{CoM} = \dfrac{\sum_{i} m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_{i} m_i}$ | mass-weighted average position | m |
| 6 | Linearized Inverted Pendulum (LIPM) CoM dynamics | $\ddot{x}_{CoM} = \omega^2(x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP})$ | $\omega = \sqrt{g/h}$, $h$ = CoM height | m/s$^2$ |
| 7 | Natural frequency of LIPM | $\omega_n = \sqrt{g/h}$ | governs balance response | rad/s |
| 8 | Capture Region radius (flat ground) | $r_{capture} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{h}} \cdot \vert v_{CoM} \vert \cdot T_{step}$ | $T_{step}$ = step duration | m |
| 9 | Actuator torque required | $\tau = J(q)^T F$ | $J$ = Jacobian, $F$ = end-effector force | N·m |
| 10 | Series-Elastic Actuator stiffness | $k = \dfrac{F}{\Delta x}$ | $F$ = transmitted force, $\Delta x$ = spring deflection | N/m |
| 11 | Power required for joint | $P = \tau \cdot \dot{\theta}$ | $\dot{\theta}$ = joint angular velocity | W |
| 12 | Battery energy budget | $E = V_{bat} \cdot I_{avg} \cdot t$ | voltage, current, time | J (or Wh) |
| 13 | Stride length vs. height | $L_{stride} \approx 0.4 \cdot h_{robot}$ | empirical anthropometric rule | m |
| 14 | Walking speed (dynamic gait) | $v_{walk} \approx 0.5 \sqrt{g \cdot h}$ | derived from LIPM | m/s |
| 15 | Workspace volume of 7-DOF arm | $V_{ws} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}\bigl(r_{max}^3 - r_{min}^3\bigr)$ | spherical shell approximation | m$^3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, the **absolute value bars** (such as the one around $v_{CoM}$) are rendered using `\vert`. This is the **KTU 2024 LaTeX-safe convention** — never use the raw `|` character inside a markdown table, as it breaks the table-cell delimiter.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Humanoid-Specific Advantage |
|---|---|
| **Disaster Response** (Fukushima, earthquake zones) | Humanoid form navigates human-built debris-cluttered spaces; uses tools designed for humans |
| **Healthcare & Elderly Care** | Anthropomorphic touch-pressure calibration matches human expectations; lifts patients safely |
| **Space Exploration** (NASA Valkyrie) | Operates human habitats without redesigning the habitat for a robot |
| **Manufacturing Co-bots** | Shares the same workspace, tools, and floor-plan as human workers |
| **Education & Research** | Standardized platforms (NAO, Pepper) provide repeatable benchmarks |
| **Service Industry** (reception, retail) | Social acceptance increases with humanoid appearance (the "uncanny valley" inverted) |
