# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom of a Bipedal Humanoid Leg

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
A humanoid leg is modeled as a serial chain: **pelvis → hip (3 DOF ball joint) → thigh → knee (1 DOF hinge) → shank → ankle (2 DOF universal) → foot**.

We apply the **Kutzbach / Grübler criterion for spatial mechanisms**:

$$ \text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5p_1 - 4p_2 - 3p_3 - 2p_4 - p_5 $$

**Step 1 — Count the links.**
For one leg, treating the pelvis as the ground (fixed link), the moving links are: thigh, shank, foot. The ground itself is link 0. So moving links:

$$ n_{\text{moving}} = 3 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; n = 3 $$

**Step 2 — Identify the joints.**

| Joint | Type | DOF ($i$) | Count ($p_i$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hip | Ball-and-socket | 3 | $p_3 = 1$ |
| Knee | Hinge (revolute) | 1 | $p_1 = 1$ |
| Ankle | Universal (2-axis) | 2 | $p_2 = 1$ |

**Step 3 — Substitute into Kutzbach's formula.**

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} = 6(n-1) - 5p_1 - 4p_2 - 3p_3 $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} = 6(3-1) - 5(1) - 4(1) - 3(1) $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} = 12 - 5 - 4 - 3 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} = 0 \;\; \text{(apparent contradiction — recheck link count)}} $$

**Step 4 — Correct the link count.**
The pelvis itself is a **rigid floating base** (not ground). In spatial mechanism analysis, we treat the entire leg as a **floating serial chain**, which contributes its own **6 base DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). So we add:

$$ n = 4 \;\; \text{(pelvis, thigh, shank, foot)} $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} = 6(4-1) - 5(1) - 4(1) - 3(1) $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} = 18 - 5 - 4 - 3 = 6 $$

**Step 5 — Final answer with explanation.**

$$ \boxed{\text{DOF}_{\text{one leg}} = 6 \;\; \text{(3 hip + 1 knee + 2 ankle)} } $$

**Why the 0 appeared first:** When the pelvis is mistakenly treated as ground, the mechanism becomes a *closed loop* in the body frame, which artificially constrains motion. The KTU examiner tests this common error.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Total DOF of a Full Humanoid

A standard research humanoid (e.g., NAO, ASIMO class) has:

| Subsystem | DOF |
|---|---|
| Head (neck) | 2 |
| Each arm | 7 |
| Each hand (fingers) | 1 (grasp) |
| Waist | 1–3 |
| Each leg | 6 |
| Total | $2 + 2(7+1) + 3 + 2(6) = 31$ DOF |

**Step-by-step summation:**

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{total}} = \text{DOF}_{\text{head}} + 2 \cdot \text{DOF}_{\text{arm}} + 2 \cdot \text{DOF}_{\text{hand}} + \text{DOF}_{\text{waist}} + 2 \cdot \text{DOF}_{\text{leg}} $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{total}} = 2 + 2(7) + 2(1) + 3 + 2(6) $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{\text{total}} = 2 + 14 + 2 + 3 + 12 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{DOF}_{\text{total}} = 33 } $$

This is consistent with ASIMO (34 DOF) and NAO (25 DOF, lower because no articulated fingers and reduced waist).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ZMP for a Simplified 2-Link Inverted Pendulum (Sagittal Plane)

**Physical model:** A humanoid is reduced to a single **inverted pendulum** of total mass $m$ concentrated at the CoM, located at height $h$ above the ground. The CoM horizontal position is $x_{CoM}(t)$, and the ZMP is $x_{ZMP}(t)$.

**Step 1 — Free-body diagram forces.**
- Gravity $mg$ acts downward at the CoM.
- Ground-reaction force $F_{GRF}$ acts upward at the ZMP.

**Step 2 — Moment balance about the ZMP.**

Taking moments about the ZMP and setting the net moment to zero (definition of ZMP):

$$ \sum M_{ZMP} = m\bigl(\ddot{z}_{CoM} + g\bigr)\bigl(x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP}\bigr) - m\ddot{x}_{CoM}\, z_{CoM} = 0 $$

> Note: $z_{CoM} = h$ (constant CoM height in LIPM), and $\ddot{z}_{CoM} \approx 0$ (vertical CoM acceleration is negligible in standard LIPM).

**Step 3 — Simplify using $z_{CoM} = h$, $\ddot{z}_{CoM} = 0$:**

$$ m \cdot g \cdot (x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP}) - m \cdot \ddot{x}_{CoM} \cdot h = 0 $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x_{ZMP}$:**

$$ g(x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP}) = \ddot{x}_{CoM} \cdot h $$

$$ x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP} = \dfrac{h \cdot \ddot{x}_{CoM}}{g} $$

$$ x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \dfrac{h}{g}\, \ddot{x}_{CoM} $$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to the canonical LIPM form.**

Define $\omega^2 = g/h$. Then:

$$ \ddot{x}_{CoM} = \omega^2 \bigl(x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP}\bigr) $$

$$ \boxed{\ddot{x}_{CoM} = \dfrac{g}{h}\bigl(x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP}\bigr) } $$

**Engineering meaning:** This differential equation shows that if the ZMP is held at a constant point (e.g., under the support foot), the CoM behaves like an **unstable first-order-diverging mode** — it accelerates away from the ZMP exponentially. This is why **continuous ankle/hip control** is required to keep the humanoid upright during standing.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example: Stability check for a stationary humanoid.**
> A humanoid of total mass **$m = 50$ kg** stands with feet together. The CoM is at height **$h = 0.9$ m** above the ground, and the CoM is at the geometric center. The single support foot is a rectangle of length **$L = 0.25$ m** (toe-to-heel) and width **$W = 0.10$ m**. The robot is on flat ground ($g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$).

**Question:** Is the robot statically stable if it leans forward by **$\theta = 5^\circ$**? Compute the new ZMP and check if it lies inside the foot.

**Step 1 — Compute the new CoM horizontal offset.**
Initial CoM is centered over the foot. Leaning by $\theta$ displaces the CoM horizontally by:

$$ \Delta x = h \cdot \sin(\theta) $$

$$ \Delta x = 0.9 \cdot \sin(5^\circ) = 0.9 \cdot 0.0872 $$

$$ \Delta x = 0.0784 \text{ m} \approx 7.84 \text{ cm} $$

**Step 2 — CoM is now accelerating slightly as it tries to fall; but for a *static* stability check, $\ddot{x}_{CoM} \approx 0$.**

Therefore the ZMP coincides with the CoM projection:

$$ x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} $$

**Step 3 — Compute foot boundaries (sagittal direction).**
The foot length is $L = 0.25$ m, so the ZMP must satisfy:

$$ -\dfrac{L}{2} \leq x_{ZMP} \leq +\dfrac{L}{2} $$

$$ -0.125 \text{ m} \leq x_{ZMP} \leq +0.125 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4 — Substitute the displaced ZMP.**

$$ x_{ZMP} = 0.0784 \text{ m} $$

**Step 5 — Compare.**

$$ 0.0784 \text{ m} < 0.125 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark $$

**Conclusion:** The ZMP is **inside the support polygon**. The robot is **statically stable** at a $5^\circ$ forward lean.

**Step 6 — Compute the critical lean angle at which the robot tips.**

Set $x_{ZMP} = L/2$:

$$ h \cdot \sin(\theta_{crit}) = \dfrac{L}{2} $$

$$ \sin(\theta_{crit}) = \dfrac{L}{2h} = \dfrac{0.25}{2 \cdot 0.9} = 0.1389 $$

$$ \theta_{crit} = \arcsin(0.1389) \approx 7.98^\circ $$

**Interpretation:** The humanoid can lean up to **$\approx 8^\circ$** before its ZMP exits the foot and it topples forward. This explains why dynamic walking is mandatory — humanoids cannot stay balanced through *static* posture control alone beyond a few degrees.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Series-Elastic Actuator (SEA) Force Control

**Why SEAs are used in humanoids:** Direct-drive rigid actuators cannot absorb sudden impact forces (e.g., foot-strike during walking). An SEA inserts a **spring** of stiffness $k$ between the motor and the joint.

**Step 1 — Spring force equation.**

$$ F_{joint} = k \cdot \Delta x $$

where $\Delta x$ is the deflection of the spring (the difference between motor-side position and joint-side position).

**Step 2 — Force-control law.**
If a desired joint force $F_{des}$ is commanded, the controller adjusts $\Delta x$ such that:

$$ \Delta x_{des} = \dfrac{F_{des}}{k} $$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth limitation.**
The SEA's controllable bandwidth $\omega_{SEA}$ is limited by the natural frequency of the spring-mass system:

$$ \omega_{SEA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} $$

**Engineering trade-off:** A *soft* spring (low $k$) → better impact absorption but lower bandwidth. A *stiff* spring (high $k$) → better bandwidth but worse impact absorption. The KTU exam typically asks for the choice of $k$ that satisfies a given force-bandwidth specification.

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!TIP]
> The diagrams below are **Mermaid block-level functional architecture flows** — they show the *interactions* between sensor, controller, and actuator subsystems. They are designed to be rendered reliably across all Mermaid-compatible platforms (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian, Confluence).

## 4.1 Humanoid High-Level System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENV[Environment and Human Interaction Zone]
    PERC[Perception Layer - Sensor Stack]
    COG[Cognition Layer - Real Time Controller and AI Planner]
    ACT[Action Layer - Actuator Stack]
    FB[Feedback Loop - Closed Loop Stability]
    PWR[Power Subsystem - Li Ion Battery and BMS]

    ENV --> PERC
    PERC --> COG
    COG --> ACT
    ACT --> ENV
    ACT --> FB
    FB --> COG
    PWR --> PERC
    PWR --> COG
    PWR --> ACT
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **perception layer** (sensors) captures the environment and feeds it to the **cognition layer** (RT controller + AI).
- The **cognition layer** issues desired joint trajectories and stability commands to the **action layer** (actuators).
- A **closed feedback loop** returns sensor data to the controller, enabling balance corrections at **1 kHz** typical.
- The **power subsystem** supplies regulated DC voltage to all three layers and includes a Battery Management System (BMS) for safety.

---

## 4.2 Sensor Subsystem — Detailed Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAM1[Head Stereo RGB Camera - Vision]
    LID[LiDAR or Depth Sensor - 3D Mapping]
    IMU1[Head IMU - 6 Axis Orientation]
    MIC[MEMS Microphone Array - Hearing]
    IMU2[Torso Central IMU - Body Posture]
    FTS[Ankle 6 Axis Force Torque Sensor - GRF]
    ENC[Joint Encoders - Position and Velocity Feedback]
    CUR[Motor Current Sensors - Torque Estimation]
    TAC[Fingertip Tactile Sensors - Grip Force]
    BAT[Battery Voltage Current Temperature - Power State]

    CAM1 --> FUS[Sensor Fusion Unit - Extended Kalman Filter]
    LID --> FUS
    IMU1 --> FUS
    MIC --> ASR[Speech Recognition Pipeline]
    IMU2 --> FUS
    FTS --> ZMP[ZMP Calculator Module]
    ENC --> FUS
    CUR --> CTRL[Joint Servo Controller]
    TAC --> GRP[Grasp Controller]
    BAT --> BMS[Battery Management System]

    FUS --> CTRL
    ZMP --> CTRL
    ASR --> HRI[Human Robot Interaction Module]
    GRP --> CTRL
    BMS --> PWRM[Power Monitor and Safety Cutoff]
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Two sensor clusters** feed the same fusion unit: exteroceptive (cameras, LiDAR, mic) and proprioceptive (IMU, encoders, F/T).
- The **ZMP calculator** is a dedicated software module that takes the ankle F/T reading and the CoM estimate to compute the current balance point in real time.
- **Tactile sensors** route to a separate *grasp controller* because hand-control loops require much higher frequency (~1 kHz) than walking control (~200 Hz).

---

## 4.3 Actuator Subsystem — Detailed Functional Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CTRL[Central Motion Controller - RTOS]
    LEG[Leg Joint Servo Drivers - 12 Channels]
    ARM[Arm Joint Servo Drivers - 14 Channels]
    HEAD[Head Pan Tilt Drivers - 2 Channels]
    WAIST[Waist Yaw Driver - 1 Channel]
    BLDC1[Brushless DC Motors with Harmonic Drives]
    BLDC2[Brushless DC Motors with Cycloidal Reducers]
    SEA1[Series Elastic Actuators - Lower Body]
    HYD[Hydraulic Actuators - Heavy Duty Humanoids]

    CTRL --> LEG
    CTRL --> ARM
    CTRL --> HEAD
    CTRL --> WAIST
    LEG --> SEA1
    LEG --> BLDC1
    LEG --> HYD
    ARM --> BLDC2
    HEAD --> BLDC2
    WAIST --> BLDC1
```

**Interpretation:**
- Modern humanoids use a **mix of actuator technologies**:
  - **BLDC + harmonic drive** for arms (high precision, low back-drivability).
  - **Series-Elastic Actuators (SEA)** for legs (compliance, impact tolerance).
  - **Hydraulic** for high-payload humanoids like Atlas (force density).
- The **central motion controller** runs a real-time operating system (RTOS) and dispatches joint commands at the rate required for stability (typically 1 kHz for balance, 200 Hz for gait planning).

---

## 4.4 Control Hierarchy — Three-Layer Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Layer 1 - Strategic Planner - Mission and Task Level]
    L2[Layer 2 - Tactical Gait and Motion Planner - Footstep and ZMP Planner]
    L3[Layer 3 - Reactive Joint Servo Layer - Impedance and Torque Control]
    SENS[Sensor Data Stream - 1 kHz]
    ACT[Actuator Command Stream - 1 kHz]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    SENS --> L3
    L3 --> ACT
    SENS --> L2
    SENS --> L1
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Layer 1 (slowest, ~1–10 Hz):** Decides *where to go* (navigation, task sequencing).
- **Layer 2 (medium, ~100–200 Hz):** Decides *how to step* (ZMP trajectory, footstep locations, CoM reference).
- **Layer 3 (fastest, ~1 kHz):** Decides *how much torque* at each joint, using impedance/admittance control to track the references from Layer 2.
- The **sensor stream** feeds all three layers, with higher-frequency data going to lower layers.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]`
**Define a humanoid robot. List any four sensors and four actuators commonly used in a humanoid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribution shown):**

A **humanoid robot** is a bipedal, anthropomorphic robotic system that emulates the **human form, biomechanics, and behavior** through an integrated sensor-actuator-controller architecture.

*Four sensors (2 marks):*
1. **Stereo RGB cameras** — vision-based perception of the environment.
2. **Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU)** — measures linear acceleration and angular velocity for posture estimation.
3. **6-axis Force/Torque (F/T) sensor at the ankle** — measures ground-reaction forces for ZMP computation.
4. **Joint encoders** — provide position/velocity feedback for closed-loop servo control.

*Four actuators (1 mark):*
1. **Brushless DC motors with harmonic drives** — primary joint actuators in arms.
2. **Series-Elastic Actuators (SEA)** — compliant actuators in legs for impact absorption.
3. **Servo motors with cycloidal reducers** — used in compact joints such as wrists and fingers.
4. **Hydraulic actuators** — used in heavy-duty humanoids like Atlas for high force density.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Module 1]`
**What is the Zero Moment Point (ZMP)? State the stability criterion for a humanoid.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribution shown):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the specific point on the ground (or support surface) about which the **net moment of the ground-reaction-force distribution is zero**. In other words, it is the point where the resultant ground-reaction force produces no horizontal moment about the contact surface.

**Stability criterion (1 mark):**
For a humanoid to maintain dynamic balance, the ZMP must lie **strictly inside the convex support polygon** (the foot, or the convex hull of both feet during double-support). If the ZMP exits this polygon, the robot will tip over.

$$ \vec{p}_{ZMP} \in \text{Interior}\bigl(\text{SupportPolygon}\bigr) $$

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. The student must answer **one full question** (either Q.A or Q.B). Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer control architecture** of a humanoid robot. Clearly state the function of each layer, the typical operating frequency, and the sensor/actuator interfaces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A humanoid stands on a single foot modeled as a rectangle of length $L = 0.30$ m and width $W = 0.12$ m. Its total mass is $m = 60$ kg, and its CoM is at height $h = 0.95$ m above the ground. The CoM is currently displaced **forward by $d = 0.05$ m** from the ankle joint, and the horizontal acceleration of the CoM is $\ddot{x}_{CoM} = 0.3$ m/s$^2$ (forward). Take $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$.

Determine:
(i) The ZMP position $x_{ZMP}$ relative to the ankle. **(3 marks)**
(ii) Whether the robot is **statically + dynamically stable** (i.e., is the ZMP inside the foot?). **(2 marks)**
(iii) The **critical forward lean angle** beyond which the robot will tip forward during quasi-static motion. **(2 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Three-Layer Control Architecture (7 marks)

**Layer 1 — Strategic / Task Planner (≈1–10 Hz).** *(2 marks)*
- **Function:** High-level mission planning, task sequencing, navigation, human-interaction dialogues.
- **Examples:** Path planning, SLAM, "go to kitchen and pick up cup" decomposition.
- **Implementation:** AI planner running on a high-level CPU/GPU.
- **Inputs:** Mission goals, global map, human commands (speech/text).
- **Outputs:** Task-level references to Layer 2 (e.g., "walk to coordinates $(x,y)$," "pick object with left hand").

**Layer 2 — Tactical / Gait & Motion Planner (≈100–200 Hz).** *(2 marks)*
- **Function:** Converts task references into **whole-body motion**: footstep locations, CoM trajectory, ZMP reference, arm trajectories.
- **Key algorithm:** Preview Control over ZMP, Model Predictive Control (MPC) on a Linear Inverted Pendulum Model.
- **Outputs:** Joint-space references (desired $q$, $\dot{q}$, $\ddot{q}$) to Layer 3.
- **Sensors used:** CoM estimate from IMU fusion, foot contact from F/T sensors, environment map from cameras/LiDAR.

**Layer 3 — Reactive / Servo Layer (≈1 kHz).** *(2 marks)*
- **Function:** Low-level closed-loop torque control at each joint. Uses **impedance control** or **admittance control** to track the joint references from Layer 2 while regulating interaction forces.
- **Implementation:** FPGA or dedicated DSP running current-mode PWM at 20–50 kHz, with outer position/torque loops at 1 kHz.
- **Sensors used:** Joint encoders, motor current sensors, F/T sensors.
- **Actuators driven:** BLDC motors, SEAs, hydraulic valves.

**Block diagram (1 mark):** The student should sketch a three-block vertical stack with bidirectional arrows showing sensor feedback entering each layer and command flow going downward. (Refer to **Diagram 4.4** in Section 4 of these notes.)

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical ZMP Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L = 0.30$ m, $W = 0.12$ m
- $m = 60$ kg, $h = 0.95$ m
- $d = x_{CoM} = 0.05$ m
- $\ddot{x}_{CoM} = 0.3$ m/s$^2$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s$^2$

**Step 1 (i) — ZMP calculation.** *(3 marks)*

Using the LIPM ZMP formula:

$$ x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \dfrac{h}{g}\,\ddot{x}_{CoM} $$

$$ x_{ZMP} = 0.05 - \dfrac{0.95}{9.81} \cdot 0.3 $$

$$ x_{ZMP} = 0.05 - 0.0968 \cdot 0.3 $$

$$ x_{ZMP} = 0.05 - 0.0291 $$

$$ \boxed{x_{ZMP} = 0.0209 \text{ m} \approx 2.09 \text{ cm forward of the ankle} } $$

**Step 2 (ii) — Stability check.** *(2 marks)*

The ZMP must lie within $\pm L/2 = \pm 0.15$ m (sagittal direction).

$$ \vert x_{ZMP} \vert = 0.0209 \text{ m} < 0.15 \text{ m} \;\;\checkmark $$

**Conclusion:** The ZMP is **well inside the support polygon**, so the robot is **dynamically stable** at this instant.

**Step 3 (iii) — Critical lean angle.** *(2 marks)*

At the tipping limit, $x_{ZMP} = L/2 = 0.15$ m. Setting this equal to the LIPM ZMP expression (assuming $\ddot{x}_{CoM} \to 0$ at the quasi-static tipping point):

$$ x_{CoM,crit} = \dfrac{L}{2} $$

$$ h \cdot \sin(\theta_{crit}) = \dfrac{L}{2} $$

$$ \sin(\theta_{crit}) = \dfrac{0.30}{2 \cdot 0.95} = \dfrac{0.30}{1.90} = 0.1579 $$

$$ \boxed{\theta_{crit} = \arcsin(0.1579) \approx 9.09^\circ } $$

**Conclusion:** The robot will tip forward quasi-statically if it leans beyond approximately **$9.1^\circ$**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the **Series-Elastic Actuator (SEA)** concept used in humanoid legs. Derive the relationship between spring stiffness $k$ and force-controllable bandwidth. Why is SEA preferred over rigid actuators for bipedal locomotion? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **total degrees of freedom** of a humanoid with the following joint configuration:
- Head: 2 DOF (neck pan + tilt)
- Each arm: 7 DOF (shoulder 3 + elbow 1 + forearm 1 + wrist 2)
- Each hand: 1 DOF (single-axis grasp)
- Waist: 3 DOF (yaw + roll + pitch)
- Each leg: 6 DOF (hip 3 + knee 1 + ankle 2)

Also compute, using the Kutzbach criterion, the DOF of a **single leg treated as a floating serial chain** with $n = 4$ moving links and joints: hip (3-DOF ball), knee (1-DOF hinge), ankle (2-DOF universal). Verify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Series-Elastic Actuator (7 marks)

**Concept (2 marks):**
A Series-Elastic Actuator (SEA) inserts a **compliant element (spring)** between the motor and the joint output. The motor's torque is transmitted through the spring before reaching the load. This decouples the motor's high-impedance characteristics from the joint's low-impedance (safe) behavior, enabling **accurate force control** and **passive impact absorption**.

**Force–deflection relationship (1 mark):**

$$ F_{joint} = k \cdot \Delta x $$

where $\Delta x = x_{motor} - x_{joint}$ is the spring deflection.

**Force control law (1 mark):**
To command a desired joint force $F_{des}$:

$$ \Delta x_{des} = \dfrac{F_{des}}{k} $$

The controller modulates motor position to achieve $\Delta x_{des}$, which implicitly sets the joint force.

**Bandwidth derivation (2 marks):**
The SEA behaves as a spring–mass system. The motor inertia and the load mass $m_{eff}$, together with stiffness $k$, form a second-order system with natural frequency:

$$ \omega_{SEA} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} $$

The **maximum controllable bandwidth** is approximately equal to $\omega_{SEA}$ (crossover typically set at $\omega_{SEA}/3$ for stability margin). Hence:

$$ f_{bw} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m_{eff}}} $$

**Why SEA for bipedal locomotion (1 mark):**
- **Impact absorption** during foot strike (forces up to 5× body weight for ~50 ms).
- **Accurate force control** enables safe physical interaction (human contact, uneven terrain).
- **Energy storage** in the spring improves locomotion efficiency (passive elastic recoil).
- **Back-drivability** prevents the rigid-environment damage common with stiff actuators.

---

#### Part (b) — DOF Computation (7 marks)

**Total DOF of humanoid (4 marks):**

$$ \text{DOF}_{total} = \text{DOF}_{head} + 2 \cdot \text{DOF}_{arm} + 2 \cdot \text{DOF}_{hand} + \text{DOF}_{waist} + 2 \cdot \text{DOF}_{leg} $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{total} = 2 + 2(7) + 2(1) + 3 + 2(6) $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{total} = 2 + 14 + 2 + 3 + 12 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{DOF}_{total} = 33 } $$

**Single-leg DOF via Kutzbach (3 marks):**

$$ \text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5p_1 - 4p_2 - 3p_3 $$

- $n = 4$ (pelvis, thigh, shank, foot — pelvis treated as a moving link since the leg is a floating chain)
- $p_1 = 1$ (knee, 1-DOF)
- $p_2 = 1$ (ankle, 2-DOF)
- $p_3 = 1$ (hip, 3-DOF)

$$ \text{DOF}_{leg} = 6(4-1) - 5(1) - 4(1) - 3(1) $$

$$ \text{DOF}_{leg} = 18 - 5 - 4 - 3 $$

$$ \boxed{\text{DOF}_{leg} = 6 } $$

**Verification (counted manually):** Hip (3) + Knee (1) + Ankle (2) = **6 DOF** ✓. The Kutzbach result matches the explicit count.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the floating base in Kutzbach analysis:** A common error is treating the pelvis as ground, which gives an apparent DOF of 0. Always clarify whether the chain is *floating* or *grounded*.
> 2. **Confusing ZMP with CoM:** Students often write "the CoM must be inside the support polygon." This is **wrong** — the *ZMP* must be inside, not the CoM. The CoM can (and does) lie outside the support polygon during dynamic walking.
> 3. **Using `g = 10` m/s²:** The KTU exam typically specifies `g = 9.81` m/s². Using `g = 10` may incur a 0.5–1 mark deduction if the answer is sensitive to the value.
> 4. **Omitting units in the final answer:** ZMP and CoM must carry units of meters; joint torques in N·m; angular velocities in rad/s. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 5. **Mixing ZMP and CoP:** The Center of Pressure (CoP) is the actual pressure-distribution centroid; the ZMP is the point where the moment is zero. On a flat rigid surface, ZMP = CoP. On a non-rigid or small-contact surface, they may differ.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist as your final-revision sheet one hour before the KTU exam.**

- [ ] **Humanoid** = bipedal, anthropomorphic, sensor-actuator integrated robotic system.
- [ ] Standard DOF distribution: Head 2 + Arms 2×7 + Hands 2×1 + Waist 3 + Legs 2×6 ≈ **33 DOF** (typical research humanoid).
- [ ] **ZMP definition:** the point on the ground where the ground-reaction-force moment is zero.
- [ ] **Stability criterion:** ZMP ∈ Interior(Support Polygon). If ZMP exits → robot falls.
- [ ] **ZMP formula (LIPM, sagittal):** $x_{ZMP} = x_{CoM} - \dfrac{h}{g}\ddot{x}_{CoM}$.
- [ ] **LIPM CoM dynamics:** $\ddot{x}_{CoM} = \dfrac{g}{h}(x_{CoM} - x_{ZMP})$ — a *diverging* system requiring active control.
- [ ] **Critical tip angle** for a humanoid of CoM height $h$ on a foot of length $L$: $\theta_{crit} = \arcsin\bigl(\dfrac{L}{2h}\bigr)$.
- [ ] **Kutzbach criterion (spatial):** $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5p_1 - 4p_2 - 3p_3 - 2p_4 - p_5$.
- [ ] Always treat a leg/arm as a **floating serial chain** when computing DOF.
- [ ] **SEA (Series-Elastic Actuator):** $F = k\Delta x$; bandwidth $\omega = \sqrt{k/m_{eff}}$; preferred for legged locomotion due to impact absorption.
- [ ] **Three-layer control:** Strategic (1–10 Hz) → Tactical gait planner (100–200 Hz) → Reactive servo (1 kHz).
- [ ] **Key sensors:** IMU, F/T sensor at ankle, joint encoders, stereo cameras, depth/LiDAR, tactile fingertips.
- [ ] **Key actuators:** BLDC + harmonic drive (arms), SEA (legs), hydraulic (heavy-duty, e.g., Atlas).
- [ ] **Power source:** Li-Ion battery 24–48 V DC; autonomy 30 min – 4 hrs.
- [ ] Famous humanoids: **ASIMO (Honda), Atlas (Boston Dynamics), NAO (SoftBank), Optimus (Tesla), Sophia (Hanson), Valkyrie (NASA).**
- [ ] **Uncanny valley** — a sociological phenomenon relevant to humanoid facial design (Mori, 1970).
- [ ] In markdown/KTU exam answers, **always include units** in numerical final results.
- [ ] Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value bars in LaTeX tables — never use the raw `|` character.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Section 5 — Topic Recap Complete.**
> This concludes the comprehensive KTU 2024 Scheme study notes for **Humanoids** from **Module 1** of **PCRAT205 — Sensors and Actuators for Robotics**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cobots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cobots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cobot** (short for **Collaborative Robot**) is a robot specifically engineered to physically interact with humans in a **shared, defined workspace**, where the human and the robot perform tasks in a coordinated or parallel manner without the need for traditional physical safety barriers (fencing, light curtains, or cages).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A cobot is a robotic system designed for human–robot collaboration (HRC) that complies with the operational modalities defined in **ISO 10218-1/2** and **ISO/TS 15066**, integrating inherent safety, force/torque sensing, and speed/position monitoring to ensure that contact, if it occurs, remains within biomechanical tolerance limits.

The term was originally coined in **1996** by Professors **Edward Colgate and Michael Peshkin** at Northwestern University, USA, derived from the concept of a "computerized coaxing device." Modern cobots are governed by:

- **ISO 10218-1:2011** – Robots and robotic devices – Safety requirements for industrial robots (Part 1: Robots).
- **ISO 10218-2:2011** – Part 2: Robot systems and integration.
- **ISO/TS 15066:2016** – Collaborative robots – Technical specification (defines biomechanical limits).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The fundamental paradigm shift of a cobot is that the human is treated as a **co-worker, not an obstruction**. Therefore, the robot must *yield* safely to the human and never inflict harm exceeding the **biomechanical injury thresholds** defined in ISO/TS 15066.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **trained horse pulling a cart alongside a farmer walking beside it**. The horse is powerful (motorized) but is trained to:
1. Match the farmer's pace,
2. Stop instantly if the farmer touches the reins or applies pressure, and
3. Never make sudden jerks that could harm the farmer.

A **traditional industrial robot** is more like a **mechanical bull inside a cage** — extremely fast, strong, and unsafe for direct contact. A **cobot** is the **trained horse** — it has high strength for its task, but is built with **rounded contours, force-sensing skin, and software-defined safety zones** that slow or stop motion the moment a human enters its envelope.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Hook:** *"Cobot = Caged Robot's Calm Cousin."*

## 1.3 Key Differentiating Metrics

| Parameter | Traditional Industrial Robot | Cobot (Collaborative Robot) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Workspace Isolation | Fully fenced | Shared with humans |
| Maximum Payload | Up to **1000+ kg** | Typically **3–35 kg** |
| Maximum TCP Speed | Up to **10 m/s** | Limited to **≤ 250 mm/s** (during contact operation) |
| Stopping Time | **100–500 ms** | **< 50 ms** (Safety-rated monitored stop) |
| Safety Strategy | Physical barriers | Software + sensor based |
| Programming | Expert-level (text-based) | Lead-through teaching / hand-guiding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **250 mm/s** rule is a benchmark — not a hard universal law — but it is referenced in ISO/TS 15066 as a quasi-static contact threshold for the hand/finger region when transient energies are bounded. Always check the latest standard for updates.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Modes of Collaboration (ISO/TS 15066)

A cobot can operate in any one of **four** collaborative operation modalities. Understanding these is essential for both KTU module exams and the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)**
   - The robot ceases all motion when a human enters the collaborative workspace.
   - Used for **loading/unloading** tasks or when the human is in close proximity.
   - Stop category: **Stop Category 0, 1, or 2** (per IEC 60204-1).

2. **Hand-Guiding (HG)**
   - The operator physically grasps the end-effector (or a dedicated handle) and guides the robot.
   - Requires an **enabling device** (3-position hold-to-run button) and direct force feedback.
   - Speed is proportional to the operator's applied force.

3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)**
   - The robot's speed is dynamically modulated based on the **separation distance** between the robot and the human.
   - As the human approaches, the robot slows; if distance < protective separation distance → robot stops.

4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)**
   - The robot is intrinsically designed so that **contact forces and pressures** cannot exceed biomechanical limits.
   - Achieved via **compliant joints, force/torque sensors, soft skin, and current/torque control loops**.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently frame a 14-mark question as: *"Explain any two collaborative operation modalities with suitable diagrams."* Be ready to draw or describe the **SSM and PFL** in detail.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every governing equation you may need for a numerical problem on cobots.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Standard Reference |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S_p = v_h \cdot T_r + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s} + S_h$ | Protective separation distance | ISO/TS 15066 (SSM) |
| 2 | $T_r = T_{sense} + T_{react} + T_{stop}$ | Total robot reaction time | SSM derivation |
| 3 | $S_h = v_r \cdot T_r + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s}$ | Hugging distance (minimum) | ISO/TS 15066 |
| 4 | $KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Kinetic energy of moving mass | PFL energy bound |
| 5 | $KE \le E_{limit}$ | Maximum allowable kinetic energy | PFL constraint |
| 6 | $F_{max} = m a_{max}$ | Maximum contact force (Newton's 2nd law) | PFL force bound |
| 7 | $F_{qs} = p_{max} \cdot A_c$ | Quasi-static contact force (pressure × area) | Biomechanical limit |
| 8 | $v_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{limit}}{m}}$ | Max allowable TCP velocity | Inverting KE |
| 9 | $a_s = \dfrac{F_{brake}}{m}$ | Robot deceleration (brake force / mass) | Stopping dynamics |
| 10 | $\tau = I \alpha$ | Joint torque (inertia × angular acceleration) | Compliant joint design |

Where the typical symbol notation is:

- $v_h$ = velocity of the human operator (m/s), typically **1.6 m/s** (walking speed).
- $v_r$ = velocity of the robot TCP (m/s).
- $T_r$ = total robot reaction time (s).
- $T_{sense}$ = sensor detection latency (s).
- $T_{react}$ = controller reaction latency (s).
- $T_{stop}$ = mechanical braking time (s).
- $a_s$ = robot deceleration during emergency stop (m/s²).
- $S_h$ = protective distance (hugging distance) (m).
- $m$ = effective moving mass (kg).
- $p_{max}$ = maximum allowable body-region pressure (N/m² or Pa).
- $A_c$ = contact patch area (m²).
- $E_{limit}$ = biomechanical energy limit (J).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing absolute values or norm operators inside the KTU table, use the LaTeX command `\vert` instead of a literal pipe character `\vert x \vert`. This prevents markdown table-breaking and is a best-practice for KTU LaTeX submissions.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cobots dominate the **flexible manufacturing, medical assistance, and SME (Small & Medium Enterprise) automation** sectors because they:
- Eliminate the cost of **cages and light curtains** (~30–60% of cell cost).
- Allow **rapid redeployment** in a few hours (vs. days for industrial robots).
- Enable **tactile human–robot interaction** in surgical (e.g., da Vinci) and rehabilitation robotics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Hugging (Protective Separation) Distance for SSM

The **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** mode requires the robot to maintain a minimum safe distance from the human operator. We derive the governing equation in five rigorous steps.

### Step 1 — Identify the contributing distance terms

The minimum safe separation distance $S_p$ must account for **how far the human can move toward the robot** in the time the robot takes to perceive, decide, and stop. Symbolically:

$$
S_p = S_{human\ approach} + S_{robot\ stop} + S_{safety\ margin}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the human's approach distance

If the human approaches at a constant walking speed $v_h$ (standardized as **1.6 m/s** for a brisk walk) over the robot's total reaction time $T_r$, the human's approach distance is simply:

$$
S_{human\ approach} = v_h \cdot T_r
$$

### Step 3 — Express the robot's stopping distance

Assuming the robot's TCP is moving at velocity $v_r$ when the human is detected, the robot applies a deceleration $a_s$ (braking acceleration) to come to rest. Using the kinematic equation of motion $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 a_s d$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$
0 = v_r^2 - 2 a_s \cdot S_{robot\ stop}
$$

Solving for $S_{robot\ stop}$:

$$
S_{robot\ stop} = \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s}
$$

### Step 4 — Decompose the total reaction time

The robot's reaction time is the sum of three latencies: the time taken for the **safety sensor to detect** the human, the **controller to compute** the stop command, and the **brake to engage**.

$$
T_r = T_{sense} + T_{react} + T_{stop}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine all terms into the final SSM equation

Adding a final **safety margin** $S_h$ (often taken as $\ge 0$, but in some implementations equals the robot's stop distance as a redundancy), we obtain the standard ISO/TS 15066 form:

$$
S_p = v_h \cdot T_r + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s} + S_h
$$

Substituting $T_r$ from Step 4 into Step 2:

$$
S_p = v_h \cdot \left( T_{sense} + T_{react} + T_{stop} \right) + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s} + S_h
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** As $T_r \to 0$ (faster sensors) and $a_s \to \infty$ (better brakes), the robot can operate at higher $v_r$ without violating safety — this is why **collaborative robots are equipped with sub-millisecond capacitive or vision-based proximity sensors**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Allowable TCP Velocity from PFL Constraints

In **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** mode, the contact energy must be bounded to prevent injury. We derive the maximum allowable TCP velocity.

### Step 1 — Define the kinetic energy bound

The translational kinetic energy of the moving TCP (and its effective payload) is:

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

The biomechanical injury threshold $E_{limit}$ for a given body region (e.g., hand, chest) is given by ISO/TS 15066 tables. The safety constraint is:

$$
KE \le E_{limit}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the inequality

$$
\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \le E_{limit}
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for v

Multiply both sides by 2:

$$
m v^2 \le 2 E_{limit}
$$

Divide by $m$:

$$
v^2 \le \frac{2 E_{limit}}{m}
$$

Take the positive square root (velocity is a magnitude):

$$
v \le \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{limit}}{m}}
$$

Therefore the **maximum allowable TCP velocity** is:

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{limit}}{m}}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A cobot's effective moving mass is $m = 10$ kg. Per ISO/TS 15066, the allowable transient contact energy for the **hand/finger region** is $E_{limit} = 0.49$ J. Find the maximum TCP velocity for PFL operation.

**Solution:**

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 0.49}{10}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.98}{10}} = \sqrt{0.098}
$$

$$
v_{max} \approx 0.313\ \text{m/s} = 313\ \text{mm/s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice that $v_{max} \approx 313$ mm/s is **above the 250 mm/s benchmark**, but the actual limit depends on body region. For the **chest/torso region**, $E_{limit}$ is higher, so $v_{max}$ can be larger. Always refer to the specific body-region table in ISO/TS 15066.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: SSM vs PFL

| Feature | Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) | Power and Force Limiting (PFL) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Core Variable | Separation distance $S_p$ | Contact energy $E_{limit}$ |
| Primary Sensor | Vision, LiDAR, capacitive proximity | Force/Torque sensor, current sensing |
| Robot Speed | Dynamic, distance-dependent | Capped, intrinsically bounded |
| Workspace Size | Large (robot can move fast when human is far) | Constrained (robot is always slow) |
| Safety Strategy | Reactive (responds to human presence) | Inherent (cannot exceed limits) |
| Typical Use Case | Heavy payload assistance | Light assembly, polishing, screw-driving |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Cobot Safety System

The figure below maps the closed-loop data flow of a typical collaborative robot's safety and control architecture. Each block represents a hardware or software subsystem, and the arrows represent the direction of data and actuation signals.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Operator] --> B[Safety Zone Sensors\nVision / LiDAR / Capacitive]
    A --> C[Force / Torque Sensor\nat End Effector]
    B --> D[Safety PLC\nISO 13849 PLd]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Robot Controller\nVelocity and Trajectory Planner]
    E --> F[Servo Drives\nand Joint Motors]
    F --> G[Robot Manipulator\nCobot Arm]
    G --> H[Collaborative Workspace\nShared with Human]
    H --> A
    F --> C
    D --> I[Safety Stop\nEstop Relay]
    I --> G
    E --> J[Hand Guiding\nInput Device]
    J --> E
```

### 4.2 Block-by-Block Interpretation (for KTU 14-mark answers)

1. **Block A — Human Operator:** The coworker whose presence triggers the safety chain. Treated as a dynamic variable, not a static obstacle.
2. **Block B — Safety Zone Sensors:** Vision cameras (e.g., Intel RealSense), LiDAR, or capacitive skins detect the human's relative position. Output: a stream of (x, y, z) coordinates per unit time.
3. **Block C — Force/Torque Sensor:** Mounted at the wrist or in each joint, measures the contact wrench. Output: 6-DOF force vector $\mathbf{F} = (F_x, F_y, F_z, \tau_x, \tau_y, \tau_z)$.
4. **Block D — Safety PLC:** Runs **STO (Safe Torque Off)**, **SLS (Safely Limited Speed)**, and **SMM (Safe Motion Monitoring)** functions. Conforms to **ISO 13849-1 PL d** or higher.
5. **Block E — Robot Controller:** Receives safety-validated trajectory and computes joint commands. The trajectory planner implements the **SSM** law derived in Section 3.1.
6. **Block F — Servo Drives and Joint Motors:** Actuate the arm. Each drive receives a torque command $\tau_i$ that respects the **PFL** energy bound derived in Section 3.2.
7. **Block G — Robot Manipulator:** The 6- or 7-DOF cobot arm with **compliant joints** (series-elastic actuators or torque-controlled modules).
8. **Block H — Collaborative Workspace:** The shared cell where the human and robot interact. The robot is designed with **rounded edges, no pinch points, and soft covers**.
9. **Block I — Safety Stop / Estop Relay:** Hardware-level cutoff that removes power to the drives in < 5 ms. Independent of software.
10. **Block J — Hand-Guiding Input Device:** A 3-position enabling switch + force handle; feeds operator-applied force back into Block E.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When drawing a block diagram in your exam, use **labeled arrows** (e.g., "Position data", "Force feedback", "Joint torque command"). Examiners reward clear signal-flow labeling with 1–2 bonus marks.

## 4.3 Application-Level State Machine (Collaboration Mode Switcher)

The following state diagram shows how a cobot transitions between the four ISO/TS 15066 modes based on operator presence.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SRMS
    SRMS --> HG: Operator grips handle
    HG --> SSM: Handle released
    SSMS_State: SSM
    SRMS --> SSMS_State: Operator detected
    SSMS_State --> PFL: Human within 500 mm
    PFL --> SRMS: Operator leaves workspace
    SSMS_State --> HG: Operator grips handle
    PFL --> [*]: Emergency stop triggered
    SRMS --> [*]: Emergency stop triggered
    HG --> [*]: Emergency stop triggered
    SSMS_State --> [*]: Emergency stop triggered
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For exam answers, you can redraw the state diagram using a simple **flowchart with rounded rectangles**. Mermaid is a tool for your digital notes — your handwriting on the answer sheet is what scores marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a cobot. List the four collaborative operation modes defined in ISO/TS 15066.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question Paper, Module 1)]** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **cobot (collaborative robot)** is a robotic system engineered to share its workspace with a human operator, performing tasks in a coordinated or parallel manner without the need for conventional physical safety barriers. It complies with **ISO 10218-1/2** and **ISO/TS 15066**, ensuring that any incidental contact remains within biomechanical injury thresholds.

The four collaborative operation modes per ISO/TS 15066 are:

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS)** — robot halts all motion upon human entry.
2. **Hand-Guiding (HG)** — operator physically directs the robot's motion.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM)** — robot speed scales with the human's separation distance.
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** — robot's mechanical power and contact forces are intrinsically bounded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking pattern:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four modes (0.5 each).

---

### Q2. State any three key differences between a traditional industrial robot and a cobot.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Traditional Industrial Robot | Cobot |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Operates inside a **fenced safety cell** | Operates in a **shared, open workspace** |
| 2 | High payload (**up to 1000+ kg**) and high TCP speed | Limited payload (**3–35 kg**), TCP speed capped near **250 mm/s** in PFL |
| 3 | Safety via **physical interlocks and light curtains** | Safety via **inherent design (PFL) + sensor-based SSM/SRMS** |
| 4 | Expert-level text programming | **Lead-through / hand-guiding** teaching |
| 5 | Higher risk of severe injury on contact | Inherently designed to stay within **biomechanical limits** |

*(Any three rows from the table for full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Cobot Questions:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse **SRMS** with **PFL**. SRMS is a *stop*, PFL is *continuous constrained motion*.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Forgetting to include the **$T_{sense} + T_{react} + T_{stop}$ decomposition** of the total reaction time costs 2 marks.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Writing "**250 mm/s** is a hard law" — it is a *biomechanical guideline* for finger/hand contact, not an absolute. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this over-generalization.
> - **Pitfall 4:** For SSM numericals, units must be **converted to SI before substitution** (e.g., mm/s → m/s). Most students lose 1 mark to unit errors.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1, Question 1(a)/(b) (Adapted)]**

#### (a) Explain the four collaborative operation modes defined in ISO/TS 15066 with suitable diagrams. Mention at least two safety-rated sensor technologies for each mode. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The four collaborative operation modes are:

**1. Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS) — 2 marks**
- The robot halts all motion the moment a human enters the **collaborative workspace**. The robot remains in a stop state (Stop Category 0, 1, or 2 per IEC 60204-1) until the human exits.
- **Sensors used:** Safety-rated laser scanners (SICK microScan3), safety mats, and light curtains with muting logic.
- **Application:** Loading/unloading parts into a CNC machine while the operator is present.

**2. Hand-Guiding (HG) — 2 marks**
- The operator physically grasps the end-effector (or a dedicated handle) and steers the robot. The robot's motion direction and velocity are proportional to the operator's applied force, measured via a 6-axis F/T sensor.
- **Sensors used:** 6-DOF force/torque sensor (e.g., ATI Mini45), enabling switch (3-position hold-to-run), and encoder feedback.
- **Application:** Teaching new trajectories by demonstration; precision assembly where the operator provides fine corrections.

**3. Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM) — 2 marks**
- The robot's TCP speed is dynamically scaled based on the **separation distance** to the operator. The governing equation is:
  $$S_p = v_h \cdot T_r + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s} + S_h$$
- **Sensors used:** Depth cameras (Intel RealSense D435), LiDAR (Velodyne VLP-16), and capacitive proximity sensors mounted on the arm.
- **Application:** Large workspace cells where the robot performs heavy-payload transfers while maintaining a safe bubble around the human.

**4. Power and Force Limiting (PFL) — 1 mark**
- The robot's mechanical design (compliant joints, current/torque limits) ensures that any **contact force and pressure** cannot exceed ISO/TS 15066 biomechanical limits.
- **Sensors used:** Joint torque sensors, current sensing in each servo drive, and soft capacitive skin.
- **Application:** Polishing, deburring, screw-driving, and direct hand-off of parts to the operator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark allocation:** 1 mark for the mode name, 0.5 mark for the working principle, 0.5 mark for the sensor type. Bonus 0.5 mark for an application example.

#### (b) Derive the protective separation distance $S_p$ for Speed and Separation Monitoring. A cobot has a TCP speed of 1.0 m/s, a braking deceleration of 5 m/s², a sensor latency of 25 ms, a controller reaction time of 15 ms, and a mechanical braking time of 30 ms. The human operator approaches at 1.6 m/s. Compute $S_p$ (neglect the safety margin $S_h$). *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Derivation — 3 marks:**

From kinematics, the human's approach distance during the robot's reaction time $T_r$ is:

$$
S_{human} = v_h \cdot T_r
$$

The robot's stopping distance, derived from $v_f^2 = v_r^2 - 2 a_s d$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$
S_{robot} = \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s}
$$

The total reaction time is decomposed as:

$$
T_r = T_{sense} + T_{react} + T_{stop}
$$

Summing the two distances and adding the safety margin $S_h$:

$$
S_p = v_h \cdot T_r + \frac{v_r^2}{2 a_s} + S_h
$$

**Numerical Evaluation — 4 marks:**

Given:
- $v_r = 1.0$ m/s
- $a_s = 5$ m/s²
- $T_{sense} = 25$ ms $= 0.025$ s
- $T_{react} = 15$ ms $= 0.015$ s
- $T_{stop} = 30$ ms $= 0.030$ s
- $v_h = 1.6$ m/s
- $S_h = 0$ (neglected)

Step 1 — Total reaction time:

$$
T_r = 0.025 + 0.015 + 0.030 = 0.070\ \text{s}
$$

Step 2 — Human approach distance:

$$
S_{human} = 1.6 \times 0.070 = 0.112\ \text{m}
$$

Step 3 — Robot stopping distance:

$$
S_{robot} = \frac{(1.0)^2}{2 \times 5} = \frac{1.0}{10} = 0.10\ \text{m}
$$

Step 4 — Total protective separation distance:

$$
S_p = 0.112 + 0.10 + 0 = 0.212\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_p = 0.212\ \text{m} = 212\ \text{mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's note:** The robot must stop completely (or reduce speed below the safe limit) before the human comes closer than 212 mm. In practice, an additional **250–500 mm safety margin** is added for unexpected sensor failures.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Question 2(a)/(b) (Adapted)]**

#### (a) What is Power and Force Limiting (PFL)? Derive the maximum allowable TCP velocity for a cobot operating in PFL mode. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition — 2 marks:**

**Power and Force Limiting (PFL)** is a collaborative operation mode in which the robot's mechanical design and control software ensure that any contact with a human produces forces and pressures that remain within the **biomechanical injury thresholds** specified in ISO/TS 15066. Unlike SSM (which is reactive), PFL is **inherent** — the robot cannot, under any fault-free condition, exceed the bounded contact energy.

**Derivation — 3 marks:**

The translational kinetic energy of the effective moving mass is:

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^2
$$

Applying the biomechanical safety constraint:

$$
KE \le E_{limit}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \le E_{limit}
$$

Multiplying both sides by 2 and dividing by $m$:

$$
v^2 \le \frac{2 E_{limit}}{m}
$$

Taking the positive square root (velocity is non-negative):

$$
\boxed{v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 E_{limit}}{m}}}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation — 2 marks:**

- As the **payload mass** $m$ increases, the **maximum velocity** must **decrease** to keep the kinetic energy bounded.
- This is why cobots have a **trade-off curve** between payload and speed, often published by manufacturers as a "PFL performance envelope."

#### (b) A 6-DOF cobot has an effective moving mass of 8 kg (TCP + payload). The biomechanical energy limit for the hand/finger region is 0.49 J. *(7 marks)*

**(i)** Calculate the maximum TCP velocity for PFL operation. *(3 marks)*

**(ii)** If the payload is doubled to 16 kg, what is the new maximum velocity? *(2 marks)*

**(iii)** What design strategy would you recommend to allow a higher velocity while still respecting the PFL constraint? *(2 marks)*

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum velocity at 8 kg — 3 marks:**

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 0.49}{8}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.98}{8}} = \sqrt{0.1225}
$$

$$
v_{max} = 0.35\ \text{m/s} = 350\ \text{mm/s}
$$

**(ii) Maximum velocity at 16 kg — 2 marks:**

$$
v_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot 0.49}{16}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.98}{16}} = \sqrt{0.06125}
$$

$$
v_{max} = 0.2475\ \text{m/s} \approx 247.5\ \text{mm/s}
$$

**(iii) Design strategies to allow higher velocity — 2 marks:**

- **Reduce the effective moving mass** $m$ by using **lighter linkages** (carbon-fiber arms, hollow rotary actuators).
- **Increase the deceleration $a_s$** of the emergency stop, so the robot can stop within a shorter distance and the human approach time $T_r$ becomes less critical.
- **Use a softer body-region contact zone** (e.g., design the contact pad to engage the palm or forearm, which has a higher $E_{limit}$ than the fingertip).
- **Implement a hybrid SSM + PFL scheme:** Operate in PFL when the human is far, and switch to SSM (with adaptive speed scaling) when the human is within 500 mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's observation:** Many students wrongly believe that increasing the payload increases the safety margin. In fact, **higher payload → lower $v_{max}$** for the same $E_{limit}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this on the day before the exam!**

- **Cobot = Collaborative Robot** — shares workspace with humans without cages.
- **Governing standards:** ISO 10218-1, ISO 10218-2, ISO/TS 15066, ISO 13849-1 PL d.
- **Four collaborative modes:** SRMS, HG, SSM, PFL — be ready to **name, define, and contrast** them.
- **SSM governing equation:** $S_p = v_h \cdot T_r + \dfrac{v_r^2}{2 a_s} + S_h$.
- **Total reaction time:** $T_r = T_{sense} + T_{react} + T_{stop}$.
- **PFL maximum velocity:** $v_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{limit}}{m}}$.
- **Biomechanical energy reference:** Hand/finger $\approx 0.49$ J, but **always refer to the ISO/TS 15066 table** for the specific body region.
- **Standard human walking speed** used in SSM calculations: $v_h = 1.6$ m/s.
- **Benchmark TCP speed** for PFL: $\le 250$ mm/s (for hand contact, not a hard law).
- **Sensors used in cobots:** 6-DOF F/T sensor, depth camera, LiDAR, capacitive proximity skin, current sensing, joint torque sensor.
- **Hardware safety features:** STO (Safe Torque Off), SLS (Safely Limited Speed), SMM (Safe Motion Monitoring) — all on the Safety PLC, not the motion controller.
- **Cobot ≠ weaker industrial robot.** A cobot is *designed* for safe contact, not just slowed down.
- **Common exam traps:** Don't confuse SRMS with PFL; don't say "250 mm/s is the law"; always decompose $T_r$ into three terms; always convert units to SI before substitution.
- **Real-world examples to cite in answers:** Universal Robots UR5e, FANUC CRX-10iA, ABB YuMi, KUKA LBR iiwa, Techman TM5.
- **Biomechanical limit logic:** Energy ∝ velocity², so halving the velocity reduces contact energy by a factor of 4.
- **Hybrid architectures:** Many modern cobots combine **SSM (outer loop) + PFL (inner loop)** for maximum flexibility and minimum risk.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked *"Differentiate SSM and PFL,"* do **not** write vague lines like *"SSM uses sensors and PFL uses limits."* Be specific: SSM uses **vision/LiDAR-derived distance** to scale speed dynamically; PFL uses **joint torque/current limits + F/T sensors** to bound contact energy. Specificity = marks.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Autonomous Robots and Swarm Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Autonomous Robots and Swarm Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Autonomous Robots — Formal KTU Definition

An **Autonomous Robot** is a mechatronic system that can perform intended tasks and accomplish its mission objectives in unstructured environments **without continuous human intervention or guidance**, by intelligently perceiving its surroundings through on-board sensors, reasoning about the perceived data, planning suitable action sequences, and executing the chosen actions via actuators while continuously adapting to dynamic changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> *An autonomous robot possesses sufficient onboard computational intelligence, sensory capability, and actuation to operate independently in partially known, unknown, or dynamically changing environments, with the ability to make goal-directed decisions in real time.*

### 1.2 Swarm Robots — Formal KTU Definition

A **Swarm Robotic System** is a multi-robot system composed of a large number (typically **10 to 1000+**) of simple, physically embodied, homogeneous (or near-homogeneous) robots that interact **locally** with one another and with their environment to collectively achieve a global objective that no individual robot can accomplish alone, exhibiting **emergent behavior** and **swarm intelligence** principles derived from biological systems such as ant colonies, bee hives, and bird flocks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Swarm Robotics (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **Decentralization** — No single robot holds global authority.
> 2. **Local Sensing & Communication** — Robots interact only with near neighbors.
> 3. **Emergent Global Behavior** — Complex group behavior arises from simple individual rules.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

#### Autonomous Robot Analogy — "The Self-Driving Car"
Imagine you are sitting in a **self-driving Tesla** on a busy Indian road in Kochi during the monsoon. You tell the car, *"Take me to the airport."* You do not touch the steering wheel, brake, or accelerator. The car:
- **Sees** the road using cameras, LiDAR, and RADAR (Perception).
- **Thinks** "There is a pedestrian crossing; I should slow down" (Reasoning/Planning).
- **Acts** by adjusting steering and brake pressure (Actuation).

That is an **autonomous robot** — it perceives, decides, and acts on its own, adapting to real-world surprises.

#### Swarm Robot Analogy — "The Army of Ants"
Now imagine you are watching a **colony of ants** carry a large breadcrumb back to their nest. No single ant knows the full path. No ant is the "leader." Yet through tiny pheromone trails and local interactions (ant touches ant), the colony moves the crumb efficiently. If you remove 30% of the ants, the rest still complete the task. That is **swarm robotics** — many dumb, cheap, simple units cooperating locally to produce brilliant collective behavior.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Performance Indicators (KTU Board-Favorite)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Definition |
|--------|--------|---------------|------------|
| Degree of Autonomy | $\mathcal{A}$ | 0 to 5 (SAE Levels) | Level of independence from human control |
| Swarm Size | $N$ | 10 – 1000+ robots | Number of agents in the swarm |
| Local Communication Range | $r_c$ | 1 – 10 m | Maximum distance for inter-robot communication |
| Sensing Range | $r_s$ | 0.5 – 5 m | Maximum distance at which a robot perceives neighbors |
| Scalability | $\mathcal{S}$ | High (Swarm), Low (Single) | Ability to maintain performance as $N$ grows |
| Fault Tolerance | $\mathcal{F}$ | High (Swarm), Low (Single) | Continued operation after individual failures |

### 1.5 Levels of Robot Autonomy (As per KTU / SAE J3016)

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Levels of Driving/Robot Autonomy — Memorize for KTU:**
> - **Level 0** — No Automation (Human does everything)
> - **Level 1** — Driver Assistance (e.g., cruise control)
> - **Level 2** — Partial Automation (e.g., lane keep + adaptive cruise)
> - **Level 3** — Conditional Automation (Robot decides, human monitors)
> - **Level 4** — High Automation (Robot handles all, geofenced)
> - **Level 5** — Full Automation (Robot handles all, any condition)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of an Autonomous Robot

The internal architecture of an autonomous robot follows the canonical **Sense → Plan → Act (SPA)** paradigm, modernly extended to the **Sense → Plan → Act → Observe → Re-Plan** loop (closed-loop autonomy).

#### Layer 1: Perception Layer (Sensors)
- **Proprioceptive Sensors** — Encoders, IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit), current sensors, force/torque sensors.
- **Exteroceptive Sensors** — Cameras, LiDAR, ultrasonic sensors, GPS, infrared.
- Function: Converts physical phenomena into digital signals representing the internal state and the external world.

#### Layer 2: Cognition / Reasoning Layer
- **Localization** — Determining the robot's pose $(x, y, \theta)$ in a known map using algorithms like EKF (Extended Kalman Filter) or Particle Filters.
- **Mapping** — Building or updating a representation of the environment (occupancy grid, feature map).
- **Path Planning** — Computing a collision-free trajectory from start to goal (A*, Dijkstra, RRT, RRT*).
- **Decision Making** — Behavior selection, state machines, fuzzy logic, or reinforcement learning.

#### Layer 3: Actuation Layer (Actuators)
- DC motors, servo motors, stepper motors, BLDC motors, pneumatic/hydraulic cylinders.
- Function: Converts electrical/hydraulic control signals into physical motion.

#### Layer 4: Control Layer
- **Low-Level Control** — PID controllers, velocity/position regulators (inner loop, runs at **1 kHz**).
- **High-Level Control** — Trajectory tracking, behavioral arbitration (outer loop, runs at **10–100 Hz**).

### 2.2 Mathematical Modeling of an Autonomous Robot

A wheeled autonomous mobile robot in a 2D plane is typically modeled by its **pose vector** and **kinematic equations**.

**Pose Vector:**
$$P = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ \theta \end{bmatrix}$$

where $x, y$ are planar coordinates and $\theta$ is the heading angle.

**Differential Drive Kinematic Model:**
$$\dot{P} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

where $v$ is the linear velocity and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

### 2.3 Swarm Robot Properties (KTU High-Yield Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Five Canonical Properties of Swarm Robotics Systems (Must-Know):**
> 1. **Autonomy** — Each robot decides locally without central control.
> 2. **Locality** — Interactions occur only within a small neighborhood $r_c$.
> 3. **Homogeneity** — All robots have identical hardware/software (typically).
> 4. **Scalability** — Performance is robust to changes in $N$ (from 10 to 1000).
> 5. **Fault Tolerance** — No single point of failure; loss of $k$ robots still yields acceptable performance.

### 2.4 Swarm Intelligence Algorithms (Bio-Inspired Foundation)

| Algorithm | Biological Inspiration | Application in Swarm Robots | KTU Relevance |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------------|---------------|
| Ant Colony Optimization (ACO) | Ant pheromone trails | Path planning, exploration | Very High |
| Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) | Bird flocking / fish schooling | Collective foraging, formation | Very High |
| Bee Colony Algorithm (ABC) | Honeybee waggle dance | Task allocation, coverage | High |
| Firefly Algorithm | Firefly light flashing | Synchronization, coverage | Medium |
| Flocking (Reynolds Rules) | Boids (separation, alignment, cohesion) | Formation control | Very High |

### 2.5 Reynolds' Three Rules of Flocking (KTU Board Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **Reynolds (1987) Boids Rules:**
> 1. **Separation** — Steer to avoid crowding neighbors (collision avoidance).
> 2. **Alignment** — Steer towards the average heading of local neighbors.
> 3. **Cohesion** — Steer towards the average position of local neighbors.

Mathematical formulation of the steering force for robot $i$:

$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{sep}} = -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \vert^2}$$

$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{ali}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j - \vec{v}_i$$

$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{coh}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Table

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---------|--------------------|----------------|-------|
| Robot Pose Update (Euler) | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Forward kinematic integration | m, s |
| Robot Pose Update (Heading) | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Angular update | rad, s |
| Communication Adjacency | $a_{ij} = 1$ if $\Vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \Vert \leq r_c$ | Binary neighbor matrix | dimensionless |
| Pheromone Update (ACO) | $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \Delta \tau_{ij}$ | Evaporation + deposit | concentration |
| Reynolds Separation | $\vec{F}_i^{\text{sep}} = -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\Vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \Vert^2}$ | Avoidance force | N (or unit) |
| Reynolds Alignment | $\vec{F}_i^{\text{ali}} = \bar{\vec{v}}_{\mathcal{N}} - \vec{v}_i$ | Velocity matching | m/s |
| Reynolds Cohesion | $\vec{F}_i^{\text{coh}} = \bar{\vec{r}}_{\mathcal{N}} - \vec{r}_i$ | Centering force | m |
| Swarm Coverage Density | $\rho_{\text{sw}} = \frac{N \cdot A_{\text{robot}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | Coverage ratio | unitless |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Robots**: Self-driving cars (Waymo, Tesla), warehouse AMRs (Amazon Kiva), Mars rovers (Perseverance, Curiosity), surgical robots (Da Vinci).
- **Swarm Robots**: Agricultural pollination drones, search-and-rescue swarms after earthquakes, military reconnaissance swarms, nano-medical swarms for targeted drug delivery, environmental monitoring of forests and oceans.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Differential Drive Odometry Update Equations

This derivation is **extremely high-yield for KTU exams** as it directly links kinematics to autonomous robot motion.

**Step 1 — Define the wheel parameters.**
Let the robot have two wheels (left and right) with radii $r_L$ and $r_R$, separated by a wheelbase $L$. The angular velocities of the wheels are $\omega_L$ and $\omega_R$.

**Step 2 — Compute linear velocities of each wheel.**
The left wheel produces a linear velocity $v_L = r_L \omega_L$ and the right wheel produces $v_R = r_R \omega_R$.

**Step 3 — Compute robot's linear and angular velocity in body frame.**

The linear velocity of the robot's center is the average of the two wheel velocities:
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{r_R \omega_R + r_L \omega_L}{2}$$

The angular velocity is the difference divided by the wheelbase:
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{r_R \omega_R - r_L \omega_L}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Project body-frame velocities into world frame.**
Using the rotation matrix $R(\theta)$:
$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \\ \dot{\theta} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 \\ \sin\theta & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} v \\ \omega \end{bmatrix}$$

This expands to:
$$\dot{x} = v \cos\theta$$
$$\dot{y} = v \sin\theta$$
$$\dot{\theta} = \omega$$

**Step 5 — Discretize using Euler integration with timestep $\Delta t$.**
This produces the **odometry update equations**, which are the cornerstone of dead-reckoning in mobile robots:
$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \cdot \Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 6 — Final consolidated form.**

$$\begin{aligned} x_{k+1} &= x_k + \frac{\Delta t}{2}(r_R \omega_R + r_L \omega_L)\cos\theta_k \\ y_{k+1} &= y_k + \frac{\Delta t}{2}(r_R \omega_R + r_L \omega_L)\sin\theta_k \\ \theta_{k+1} &= \theta_k + \frac{\Delta t}{L}(r_R \omega_R - r_L \omega_L) \end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** This is the exact update loop the robot executes every $\Delta t$ (typically 10–50 ms) to estimate its new pose from wheel encoders. KTU loves asking students to "compute the new pose after one timestep."

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A differential drive robot with $r_L = r_R = 0.05$ m and $L = 0.3$ m starts at $P_0 = (0, 0, 0)$. Wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 10$ rad/s, $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s for $\Delta t = 0.1$ s. Find the new pose.

**Step 1 — Compute wheel linear velocities.**
$$v_L = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = 0.05 \times 12 = 0.6 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute robot's linear and angular velocity.**
$$v = \frac{0.6 + 0.5}{2} = 0.55 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\omega = \frac{0.6 - 0.5}{0.3} = 0.333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Update pose using Euler integration.**
$$x_1 = 0 + 0.55 \times \cos(0) \times 0.1 = 0.055 \text{ m}$$
$$y_1 = 0 + 0.55 \times \sin(0) \times 0.1 = 0 \text{ m}$$
$$\theta_1 = 0 + 0.333 \times 0.1 = 0.0333 \text{ rad} \approx 1.91^\circ$$

**Final Answer:** $P_1 = (0.055, 0, 0.0333)$ — the robot moves forward **5.5 cm** and rotates **1.91° counterclockwise**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Autonomous vs. Swarm Robots

| Parameter | Autonomous Robot | Swarm Robotic System |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Number of robots $N$ | 1 (typically) | 10 – 1000+ |
| Intelligence | High (powerful on-board computer) | Low (microcontroller per unit) |
| Cost per unit | High (\$10,000 – \$200,000) | Low (\$50 – \$500) |
| Failure impact | Mission fails if robot fails | Mission continues; swarm is fault-tolerant |
| Central control | May have central planner or none | Strictly no central control |
| Communication | Often global (Wi-Fi, 4G/5G) | Strictly local ($r_c$ small) |
| Sensing | Expensive sensors (LiDAR, stereo cam) | Cheap sensors (IR, bump sensors) |
| Decision making | Global planning (A*, D* Lite) | Reactive rules / local consensus |
| Example | Mars Perseverance Rover | Kilobots, Crazyflie drones, e-puck |
| Application domain | Industrial, space, defense, medical | Agriculture, search-rescue, surveillance |
| Complexity of coordination | Low (single agent) | High (multi-agent emergent behavior) |
| Scalability | Poor | Excellent |
| Energy requirement | High (kWh) | Low (mWh per robot) |

### 3.4 Pheromone Update Derivation (ACO in Swarm Robotics)

The **Ant Colony Optimization** algorithm is foundational to swarm foraging tasks. The pheromone update rule is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Define pheromone intensity.** Let $\tau_{ij}(t)$ be the pheromone on edge $(i,j)$ at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Apply evaporation.** All pheromones decay with rate $\rho \in (0,1)$:
$$\tau_{ij}^{\text{evap}}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply deposit.** Each ant $k$ that traversed $(i,j)$ deposits $\Delta \tau_{ij}^{(k)}$:
$$\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \Delta \tau_{ij}^{(k)}$$

**Step 4 — Define individual deposit.**
$$\Delta \tau_{ij}^{(k)} = \begin{cases} \dfrac{Q}{L_k} & \text{if ant } k \text{ traversed } (i,j) \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $L_k$ is the total path length of ant $k$ and $Q$ is a tunable constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Shorter paths get reinforced more (since $1/L_k$ is larger), which is the **positive feedback mechanism** that drives emergent shortest-path behavior in a swarm.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Autonomous Robot Architecture (Sense-Plan-Act Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ENV[Environment] -->|stimuli| SENS[Sensors]
    SENS -->|raw data| PERC[Perception Layer]
    PERC -->|localized pose| LOC[Localization Module]
    PERC -->|map update| MAP[Mapping Module]
    LOC -->|pose estimate| PLAN[Path Planning Module]
    MAP -->|occupancy grid| PLAN
    PLAN -->|trajectory| CTRL[Motion Controller PID]
    CTRL -->|motor commands| ACT[Actuators Motors]
    ACT -->|physical motion| ENV
    PLAN -->|mission status| DEC[Decision Maker]
    DEC -->|re-plan trigger| PLAN
    SENS -.->|feedback| CTRL
```

**Interpretation:** This closed-loop diagram illustrates the cyclical data flow inside an autonomous robot. The sensors perceive the environment, the perception layer fuses data into a localized map, the planner computes a trajectory, the controller generates motor commands, the actuators move the robot, and the cycle repeats. The dashed feedback line from sensors to controller represents inner-loop regulation (high-frequency feedback control).

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Swarm Robot Interaction Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Robot 1] ---|local comm| R2[Robot 2]
    R1 ---|local comm| R3[Robot 3]
    R2 ---|local comm| R4[Robot 4]
    R3 ---|local comm| R4
    R4 ---|local comm| R5[Robot 5]
    R3 ---|local comm| R6[Robot 6]
    R5 ---|local comm| R6
    R6 ---|local comm| R7[Robot 7]
    R1 -.->|out of range| R7
    R2 -.->|out of range| R5
    style R1 fill:#ffe082
    style R2 fill:#ffe082
    style R3 fill:#ffe082
    style R4 fill:#ffe082
    style R5 fill:#ffe082
    style R6 fill:#ffe082
    style R7 fill:#ffe082
```

**Interpretation:** Each robot (yellow node) only communicates with neighbors within its local communication radius $r_c$. Robot 1 and Robot 7 are out of range (dotted line) and do **not** directly exchange information. This is the **locality property** central to swarm robotics. Information propagates across the swarm through multi-hop relay, not through a central hub.

### 4.3 Comparison Diagram: Centralized vs. Decentralized Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CENTRAL[Centralized Control - Autonomous Robot]
        CP[Central Planner] --> R1A[Robot 1]
        CP --> R2A[Robot 2]
        CP --> R3A[Robot 3]
        CP --> R4A[Robot 4]
    end
    subgraph SWARM[Decentralized Control - Swarm Robots]
        R1B[Robot 1] ---|local| R2B[Robot 2]
        R2B ---|local| R3B[Robot 3]
        R3B ---|local| R4B[Robot 4]
        R4B ---|local| R1B
        R1B ---|local| R3B
    end
    style CP fill:#ef9a9a
    style CENTRAL fill:#ffebee
    style SWARM fill:#e8f5e9
```

**Interpretation:** In a centralized autonomous system, a single planner commands all robots (red, single point of failure). In a decentralized swarm, every robot is both an actor and a peer, communicating only locally to produce emergent group behavior (green, fault-tolerant). KTU students must clearly distinguish these architectures in theory answers.

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Reynolds Flocking Control for Swarm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NBR[Neighbor Detection] -->|neighbor set N| SEP[Separation Force Calc]
    NBR -->|neighbor set N| ALI[Alignment Force Calc]
    NBR -->|neighbor set N| COH[Cohesion Force Calc]
    SEP --> SUM[Vector Sum]
    ALI --> SUM
    COH --> SUM
    SUM --> VEL[Velocity Command]
    VEL --> MOT[Motor Driver]
    MOT --> WH[Wheel / Rotor]
    WH --> ROB[Robot i Motion]
    ROB -.-> NBR
```

**Interpretation:** The flocking algorithm continuously detects local neighbors within sensing range, computes the three Reynolds forces (separation, alignment, cohesion), sums them into a net steering vector, converts it into a velocity command, and drives the motors. The robot moves, its neighbors change, the loop repeats — producing emergent flocking, schooling, or herding behavior at the swarm level.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, June 2024 Style]**
*Define an autonomous robot. List the three fundamental components that enable its autonomy.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
An **autonomous robot** is an intelligent mechatronic system capable of performing its assigned tasks in unstructured or dynamic environments **without continuous human supervision**, by perceiving its surroundings through sensors, reasoning about the perceived data, and executing actions through actuators to achieve its mission goals.

**Three Fundamental Components (2 marks — 1 mark for naming + 1 mark for explanation):**

1. **Perception System (Sensors):** Provides awareness of both internal state (IMU, encoders) and external environment (cameras, LiDAR, GPS). Converts physical signals into digital information.

2. **Cognition / Decision System (Controller + Software):** Processes sensor data using algorithms for localization, mapping, path planning, and decision making. Computes the next desired action.

3. **Actuation System (Actuators):** Converts controller commands into physical motion (motors, drivers, mechanical linkages) that actually moves the robot and changes the world.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
*What is a swarm robot? State any four properties of a swarm robotic system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A **swarm robot** is a member of a large group of simple, homogeneous, physically embodied robots that cooperate through **local interactions** to collectively accomplish a global task that no individual robot could complete alone, exhibiting **emergent behavior** inspired by biological swarms.

**Four Properties (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Decentralization** — No central controller; each robot makes autonomous local decisions.
2. **Locality** — Each robot senses and communicates only with nearby neighbors within range $r_c$.
3. **Scalability** — The swarm can grow from 10 to 1000 robots without redesigning control laws.
4. **Fault Tolerance** — Loss of individual robots does not collapse the mission; the swarm self-heals.

(Additional valid properties: Homogeneity, Simplicity per unit, Emergent global behavior.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Autonomous Robot Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Sense-Plan-Act (SPA) architecture** of an autonomous mobile robot. Describe the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A differential drive mobile robot has wheel radius $r = 0.04$ m and wheelbase $L = 0.3$ m. The robot starts at pose $P_0 = (0, 0, 0)$. The left and right wheel angular velocities are $\omega_L = 8$ rad/s and $\omega_R = 12$ rad/s respectively. Compute the new pose after one timestep of $\Delta t = 0.1$ s using the Euler odometry update equations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the SPA architecture diagram. (Student should draw a labelled flowchart with Sensors → Perception → Planning → Control → Actuators → Environment feedback loop.)

**Explanation of Each Layer (4 marks — 1 mark per layer):**

1. **Sense Layer (1 mark):** Consists of onboard sensors — encoders, IMU, LiDAR, cameras, GPS. The robot uses them to measure its own state and detect obstacles, landmarks, and other agents in the environment.

2. **Plan Layer (1 mark):** Receives processed sensor data, performs **localization** (estimating pose $x, y, \theta$), **mapping** (building/updating a map), and **path planning** (computing a collision-free trajectory from start to goal using A*, RRT, or D* algorithms).
