3. **Act Layer (1 mark):** Receives the planned trajectory from the planner and converts it into low-level motor commands using **PID controllers** running at 1 kHz. The commands are sent to motors, gears, and wheels that physically move the robot.

4. **Feedback Loop / Closed-Loop Note (1 mark):** Modern implementations extend SPA to **Sense-Plan-Act-Observe-Replan (SPARO)** — after acting, the robot re-observes, and if the world has changed unexpectedly, it replans. This is the essence of true autonomy.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Compute linear wheel velocities:**
$$v_L = r \cdot \omega_L = 0.04 \times 8 = 0.32 \text{ m/s}$$
$$v_R = r \cdot \omega_R = 0.04 \times 12 = 0.48 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute robot linear and angular velocity:**
$$v = \frac{v_R + v_L}{2} = \frac{0.48 + 0.32}{2} = 0.40 \text{ m/s}$$
$$\omega = \frac{v_R - v_L}{L} = \frac{0.48 - 0.32}{0.3} = 0.5333 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply Euler odometry update for x:**
$$x_1 = x_0 + v \cos\theta_0 \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.40 \times \cos(0) \times 0.1 = 0.04 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Apply Euler odometry update for y:**
$$y_1 = y_0 + v \sin\theta_0 \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.40 \times \sin(0) \times 0.1 = 0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Apply Euler odometry update for heading:**
$$\theta_1 = \theta_0 + \omega \cdot \Delta t = 0 + 0.5333 \times 0.1 = 0.05333 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Convert and state final answer:**
Converting radians to degrees: $\theta_1 = 0.05333 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 3.056^\circ$.

$$\boxed{P_1 = (0.04 \text{ m}, \; 0 \text{ m}, \; 0.0533 \text{ rad} \approx 3.06^\circ)}$$

**Physical Interpretation (1 mark):** The robot moves **forward by 4 cm** and **rotates counter-clockwise by ~3.06°** because the right wheel is faster than the left wheel, causing a leftward curve.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Swarm Robot Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme Style]**

**(a)** Define **swarm robotics**. Explain the **five canonical properties** of a swarm robotic system with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Reynolds' Boids rules** (separation, alignment, cohesion) used in swarm flocking. Write the mathematical formulation of each rule and explain how the combination produces emergent flocking behavior. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **swarm robotic system** is a multi-robot system comprising a large number $N$ (typically 10 to 1000+) of simple, physically embodied, homogeneous robots that interact **locally** with one another and with their environment using simple rules, producing **emergent global behavior** that achieves a mission impossible for any single robot, inspired by biological swarms such as ant colonies, bee hives, and bird flocks.

**Five Canonical Properties (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Autonomy of Individuals (1 mark):** Each robot is fully self-contained with its own power, computation, sensing, and actuation. No external operator controls it. *Example:* Each Kilobot runs its own microcontroller.

2. **Decentralized Control (1 mark):** There is no central planner or leader. Decisions are made locally by each robot based on its own sensor data. *Example:* Foraging ants have no "queen ant controller" for food search.

3. **Locality of Sensing and Communication (1 mark):** Robots only sense and communicate with neighbors within radius $r_c$. No global information is available. *Example:* A flocking drone only knows about drones within 5 meters.

4. **Scalability (1 mark):** The same control algorithm works whether $N = 10$ or $N = 1000$ without modification. *Example:* The same pheromone-following rule works for 50 or 5000 ants.

5. **Fault Tolerance (1 mark):** Failure of any subset $k \ll N$ of robots does not stop the mission; the swarm gracefully degrades and self-heals. *Example:* Losing 20% of search-and-rescue drones still completes the search.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Reynolds' Boids Background (1 mark):**
Proposed by Craig Reynolds in 1987, the Boids model simulates flocking behavior in birds, fish, and herding animals using three local rules — Separation, Alignment, and Cohesion. Each rule is computed based only on the neighbors within sensing range $r_s$.

**Mathematical Formulation (3 marks — 1 mark per rule):**

Let $\mathcal{N}_i$ be the set of neighbors of robot $i$ within range $r_s$, $\vec{r}_i$ be the position of robot $i$, and $\vec{v}_i$ be its velocity.

**1. Separation Rule (1 mark):** Each robot steers away from neighbors that are too close, avoiding collision.
$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{sep}} = -\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \frac{\vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j}{\Vert \vec{r}_i - \vec{r}_j \Vert^2}$$

**2. Alignment Rule (1 mark):** Each robot steers to match the average velocity of its neighbors.
$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{ali}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{v}_j - \vec{v}_i$$

**3. Cohesion Rule (1 mark):** Each robot steers toward the centroid (average position) of its neighbors.
$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{coh}} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{N}_i \vert} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_i} \vec{r}_j - \vec{r}_i$$

**Net Steering Force and Emergent Behavior (3 marks):**

**Net Force (1 mark):** The total steering force on robot $i$ is the weighted sum of the three rules:
$$\vec{F}_i^{\text{total}} = w_1 \vec{F}_i^{\text{sep}} + w_2 \vec{F}_i^{\text{ali}} + w_3 \vec{F}_i^{\text{coh}}$$

where $w_1, w_2, w_3$ are tunable weights (e.g., $w_1 = 1.5$, $w_2 = 1.0$, $w_3 = 1.0$).

**Velocity Update (1 mark):** The new velocity is computed and clamped to a maximum speed $v_{\max}$:
$$\vec{v}_i^{\text{new}} = \text{clamp}\left( \vec{v}_i + \vec{F}_i^{\text{total}} \cdot \Delta t, \; v_{\max} \right)$$

**Emergent Flocking (1 mark):** When all three rules are simultaneously applied to every robot, **no robot explicitly knows** the global flock shape, yet the swarm **emerges** into a coordinated, collision-free, directionally-aligned group moving as a single fluid-like entity — the hallmark of **emergent intelligence**. This collective order arises purely from local interactions, demonstrating the power of swarm robotics.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> - Students commonly **lose 2–3 marks** by writing the Reynolds rules **without the mathematical equation**. The KTU 2024 scheme mandates formulae wherever possible — verbal description alone is insufficient.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** by confusing the sign of the **separation force** (it must be **negative** to push away).
> - For the differential drive numerical, students **lose 1–2 marks** by forgetting to convert $\omega \cdot \Delta t$ into **radians** correctly, or by writing the answer in mixed units.
> - **Never** write "autonomous" and "swarm" interchangeably — KTU strictly differentiates **single autonomous agent** from **multi-agent decentralized swarm**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Autonomous Robots & Swarm Robots**

**Autonomous Robots:**
- ☐ Definition: Mechatronic system operating independently without continuous human control.
- ☐ Core architecture: **Sense → Plan → Act** (SPA), extended to **SPARO** (Sense-Plan-Act-Observe-Replan).
- ☐ Six SAE levels of autonomy: **0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5**.
- ☐ Pose vector: $P = [x, y, \theta]^T$.
- ☐ Differential drive odometry: $x_{k+1} = x_k + v \cos\theta_k \Delta t$, $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega \Delta t$.
- ☐ Examples: Mars Perseverance, self-driving cars, Da Vinci surgical robot, Amazon Kiva warehouse bots.

**Swarm Robots:**
- ☐ Definition: Large group ($N = 10$ to $1000$+) of simple, homogeneous robots with **local interactions** and **emergent behavior**.
- ☐ Five canonical properties: **Autonomy, Decentralization, Locality, Scalability, Fault Tolerance**.
- ☐ Biological inspiration: ants (ACO), bees (ABC), birds (PSO/Boids), fireflies.
- ☐ Reynolds' three rules: **Separation, Alignment, Cohesion**.
- ☐ Pheromone update: $\tau_{ij}(t+1) = (1-\rho)\tau_{ij}(t) + \sum \Delta \tau_{ij}^{(k)}$.
- ☐ Examples: Kilobots (Harvard), Crazyflie drones, e-puck robots, agricultural drone swarms.

**Key Differences to Memorize:**
- ☐ Autonomous = **single agent**, may be centralized, expensive, high intelligence.
- ☐ Swarm = **multi-agent**, strictly decentralized, cheap per unit, low intelligence, emergent global behavior.
- ☐ Swarm has **no single point of failure**; autonomous robot does.
- ☐ Swarm is **highly scalable**; autonomous robot design is not.

**Common KTU Keywords (use these in answers to score bonus):**
- ☐ "Emergent behavior," "local interaction," "decentralized control," "stigmergy" (indirect communication via environment), "homogeneity," "scalability," "fault tolerance," "bio-inspired," "swarm intelligence."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robotics Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Robotics Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Robotics Applications** refers to the systematic deployment of autonomous or semi-autonomous robotic systems—integrating sensors, actuators, end-effectors, controllers, and software intelligence—to execute specific, often repetitive or hazardous, tasks across industrial, medical, service, defense, agricultural, space, and underwater domains. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the term denotes the **functional utilization of mechatronic architectures** (perception–decision–action loops) in real-world engineering environments to extend human capability, improve precision, enhance safety, and reduce operational cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A *robotic application* is not merely a robot performing a task; it is a **closed-loop engineered system** where sensor data, actuator response, and control algorithms synergize to achieve a defined functional outcome with measurable performance metrics (accuracy, repeatability, payload, cycle time).

The fundamental architecture of any robotic application can be abstracted into the following canonical triad:

1. **Perception Layer** — Sensors (vision, force, proximity, IMU, LIDAR).
2. **Decision Layer** — Microcontroller / PLC / Embedded AI processor.
3. **Actuation Layer** — Motors, pneumatics, hydraulics, smart material actuators.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **chefs kitchen in a 5-star hotel**. The chef (human) tastes, smells, and *senses* the food (perception). Based on the recipe knowledge, the chef *decides* how much salt to add (decision). The hand then *acts* by sprinkling salt precisely (actuation). Now, replace the chef with a **robotic chef**: cameras and chemical sensors (electronic nose) become the perception, an AI algorithm in a processor becomes the decision-maker, and a precision stepper motor controlling a shaker becomes the actuator. This is, in essence, every robotic application—be it a Mars rover scooping soil or a Da Vinci surgical robot suturing tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 PCRAT205 Module 1 emphasizes the *cross-domain* application of sensor-actuator fusion. Students must understand that the **same kinematic structure** (e.g., a 6-DOF arm) can be re-tasked for vastly different applications simply by changing end-effectors and perception stacks.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Robotics Applications — Macro View

The International Federation of Robotics (IFR) and KTU 2024 curriculum broadly classify robotic applications into the following **eight principal domains**:

| Sl. No. | Application Domain | Primary Function | Typical Sensor Stack | Typical Actuator Stack |
|---------|-------------------|------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Industrial Manufacturing | Welding, painting, assembly, pick-and-place | Force/torque, vision, proximity | Servo motors, pneumatic cylinders |
| 2 | Medical & Surgical | Minimally invasive surgery, rehabilitation | Force feedback, vision, EMG | DC motors, piezoelectric actuators |
| 3 | Service & Domestic | Cleaning, hospitality, elderly care | LIDAR, ultrasonic, IR, cameras | Wheeled drives, robotic arms |
| 4 | Space Exploration | Planetary rovers, satellite servicing | Vision, IMU, radiation sensors | Brushless DC, harmonic drives |
| 5 | Defense & Security | Surveillance, bomb disposal, UAVs | Thermal cameras, radar, GPS | BLDC, internal combustion (UAVs) |
| 6 | Agricultural | Harvesting, spraying, weeding | Multispectral vision, GPS, soil sensors | Hydraulic arms, BLDC motors |
| 7 | Underwater / Marine | ROVs, pipeline inspection, oceanography | Sonar, pressure sensors, depth sensors | Thrusters (BLDC), hydraulic claws |
| 8 | Humanoids & Research | Human-robot interaction, locomotion studies | Full IMU suite, tactile skin, vision | Series elastic actuators, BLDCs |

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

The following metrics are **universally evaluated** in any robotic application assessment:

- **Payload (kg):** Maximum load the robot can manipulate.
- **Reach / Workspace (m):** Maximum distance from robot base to end-effector.
- **Repeatability (mm):** ±0.02 mm (high precision); defined as the deviation when returning to a taught position.
- **Accuracy (mm):** Deviation between commanded and actual position.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Number of independent joints (typically 6 for full spatial manipulation).
- **Cycle Time (s):** Time to complete one full task cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** A modern automotive welding robot (e.g., KUKA KR 1000) achieves **±0.05 mm repeatability** with a **payload of 1000 kg**, demonstrating extreme industrial-grade precision.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Robotics Application Pipeline — Theoretical Decomposition

Every robotic application, irrespective of its domain, follows a **canonical closed-loop pipeline**. KTU examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions around this pipeline.

### Step 1 — Task Specification (High-Level Goal)
The user defines the application goal: *"Pick a ripe apple from a branch without bruising it."* This translates into a quantifiable specification: target object geometry, acceptable force threshold, success rate percentage.

### Step 2 — Perception (Sensor Layer Activation)
Sensors acquire raw environmental data. The signal-conditioning circuit filters noise. Analog-to-Digital Converters (ADCs) digitize the signal. The processor runs feature-extraction algorithms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** In KTU's outcome-based framework, the *accuracy of perception* directly bounds the *accuracy of action*. Garbage-In-Garbage-Out (GIGO) principle.

### Step 3 — Decision (Algorithmic Processing)
The controller (PID, MPC, fuzzy logic, neural network) computes desired actuator commands. Control laws such as:

$$ u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} $$

where $e(t)$ is the error between desired and actual state, and $K_p, K_i, K_d$ are tuning gains. This PID law is the **workhorse** of 90% of industrial robotic applications.

### Step 4 — Actuation (Physical Execution)
Actuators convert electrical/pneumatic/hydraulic energy into mechanical motion. For servo motors, the torque equation is:

$$ \tau = K_t \cdot I $$

where $K_t$ (Nm/A) is the torque constant and $I$ (A) is the armature current.

### Step 5 — Feedback Loop Closure
The encoder or resolver measures actual position. The comparator subtracts it from the setpoint. The cycle repeats at frequencies of **1 kHz to 20 kHz** in modern industrial controllers.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Top Application Domains

### 2.2.1 Industrial Manufacturing Robots

Industrial applications dominate the global robotics market (~**45%** of all installed robots). The major sub-categories are:

- **Welding Robots:** Typically 6-DOF articulated arms with arc-welding or spot-welding torches. Sensors: seam-tracking vision systems, arc current sensors.
- **Painting Robots:** Require explosion-proof (ATEX-certified) designs due to volatile solvents. Use pneumatic atomizers.
- **Assembly Robots:** SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm) configurations dominate for high-speed PCB assembly; **cycle times reach 0.3 seconds per pick-and-place**.
- **Material Handling:** Palletizing robots handle loads up to **1500 kg** with 4-DOF.

### 2.2.2 Medical & Surgical Robots

The **Da Vinci Surgical System** (Intuitive Surgical) is the gold standard. Key specifications:

- **Master–Slave Teleoperation Architecture**
- **10x magnification 3D HD vision**
- **7 degrees of freedom** end-effectors (EndoWrist instruments)
- **Force scaling:** 1:5 (surgeon moves 5 cm, instrument moves 1 cm) for tremor elimination.

Rehabilitation robotics (e.g., **Lokomat** for gait therapy) uses **series elastic actuators (SEAs)** that store energy in a spring to provide compliant force control—critical for safe human interaction.

### 2.2.3 Space Robotics

Mars rovers (**Perseverance, Curiosity**) must operate in:
- Temperatures: **−130 °C to +30 °C**
- Communication delay: **4 to 24 minutes** (one-way to Earth)
- Gravity: **3.71 m/s²** (vs Earth's 9.81 m/s²)

This necessitates **full autonomy** with hazard avoidance algorithms and **radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs)** for power.

### 2.2.4 Autonomous Vehicles (Mobile Robots)

Self-driving cars employ a **sensor fusion hierarchy**:
- LIDAR (10 Hz, 360° scan)
- Radar (77 GHz, 200 m range)
- Cameras (semantic segmentation)
- GPS + IMU (localization)
- Ultrasonic sensors (parking)

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** The following formulas are *guaranteed high-yield* for ESE and internal assessments.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Units |
|---|---------|---------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | PID Control Law | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_p$: proportional gain; $K_i$: integral; $K_d$: derivative; $e(t)$: error | $u$ varies (V, N, Nm) |
| 2 | Motor Torque | $\tau = K_t \cdot I$ | $K_t$: torque constant; $I$: current | $\tau$ in Nm |
| 3 | Motor Back-EMF | $V_{emf} = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$: back-EMF constant; $\omega$: angular velocity | V |
| 4 | Forward Kinematics (2R planar) | $\begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$ | $L_1, L_2$: link lengths; $\theta_1, \theta_2$: joint angles | m, rad |
| 5 | Jacobian (velocity) | $\dot{x} = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$ | $J$: Jacobian matrix | varies |
| 6 | Static Joint Torque | $\tau = J^T F$ | $F$: end-effector force; $J^T$: Jacobian transpose | Nm |
| 7 | Repeatability vs Accuracy | $R = \pm \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n}}$ | $x_i$: position attempts; $\bar{x}$: mean | mm |
| 8 | Hydraulic Pressure | $P = \frac{F}{A}$ | $F$: force; $A$: piston area | Pa or bar |
| 9 | Power Transmission | $P_{out} = \tau \cdot \omega$ | $\tau$: torque; $\omega$: angular velocity | W |
| 10 | Sensor Resolution | $\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Full Scale}}{2^n}$ | $n$: number of ADC bits | LSB |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing ROI:** A single automotive welding robot costs ~$150,000 but operates continuously for **100,000+ hours**, yielding a payback period of 1.5–2 years.
- **Surgical Precision:** Robotic surgery reduces surgeon hand tremor to **< 50 μm**, vastly improving microsurgical outcomes.
- **Space Economics:** A single Mars rover mission costs **$2.7 billion**; autonomous capability reduces operational risk dramatically.
- **Agricultural Yield:** Autonomous tractors and drones reduce herbicide usage by **up to 77%** through precision spraying.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Kinematics of a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator (Analytical Foundation)

This is a **favorite KTU 14-mark derivation**. We derive the end-effector position as a function of joint angles.

**Given:** A 2-revolute planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

**Step 1 — Transform from base to joint 1 frame (pure rotation + translation along $x$):**

$$
T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_1 & -\sin\theta_1 & L_1\cos\theta_1 \\ \sin\theta_1 & \cos\theta_1 & L_1\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Transform from joint 1 to joint 2 frame (the second link extends along its local $x$-axis after joint angle $\theta_2$):**

$$
T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_2 & -\sin\theta_2 & L_2\cos\theta_2 \\ \sin\theta_2 & \cos\theta_2 & L_2\sin\theta_2 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the overall homogeneous transformation $T = T_1 \cdot T_2$:**

$$
T = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Extract end-effector position $(x, y)$ from the translation column of $T$:**

$$
x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

**Textual Explanation of the Logic:**
The position $(x, y)$ is the vector sum of link 1's tip (which is at distance $L_1$ from origin at angle $\theta_1$) and link 2's tip (which extends an additional $L_2$ at the combined angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$). This vector addition in Cartesian coordinates yields the closed-form forward kinematics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jacobian Matrix for the 2-DOF Planar Arm

The Jacobian $J(\theta)$ maps joint-space velocities to Cartesian-space velocities:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = J(\theta) \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $x$ with respect to time:**

$$
\dot{x} = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \cdot (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $y$ with respect to time:**

$$
\dot{y} = L_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \cdot (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into matrix form:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Textual Explanation:**
Each row of the Jacobian represents how the Cartesian velocity (x or y direction) depends on each joint velocity. The off-diagonal element of the second column depends only on link 2's geometry, while the first column accumulates both link contributions—physically intuitive, as joint 1 motion moves both links, but joint 2 motion moves only link 2.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Force–Torque Relationship

For a robot in static equilibrium, the relationship between end-effector force $F = (F_x, F_y)^T$ and joint torques $\tau = (\tau_1, \tau_2)^T$ is given by the principle of virtual work:

$$
\tau = J^T F
$$

**Step 1 — Take transpose of $J$:**

$$
J^T = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ -L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the relation:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ -L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Application:**
This is fundamental in **force-controlled robotic applications** such as polishing, deburring, and robotic surgery—where the controller must compute joint torques to apply a specific end-effector force on a workpiece without exceeding safety thresholds.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Repeatability Calculation

**Problem:** A pick-and-place robot attempts to place an electronic chip at the same target 10 times. The recorded positions (in mm from target center) are: 0.02, −0.01, 0.03, −0.02, 0.01, 0.02, −0.01, 0.03, 0.00, 0.02. Calculate the repeatability.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean position:**

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{0.02 - 0.01 + 0.03 - 0.02 + 0.01 + 0.02 - 0.01 + 0.03 + 0.00 + 0.02}{10}
$$

$$
\bar{x} = \frac{0.09}{10} = 0.009 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute squared deviations:**

$$
(x_i - \bar{x})^2 : (0.011)^2, (-0.019)^2, (0.021)^2, (-0.029)^2, (0.001)^2, (0.011)^2, (-0.019)^2, (0.021)^2, (-0.009)^2, (0.011)^2
$$

$$
= 0.000121, 0.000361, 0.000441, 0.000841, 0.000001, 0.000121, 0.000361, 0.000441, 0.000081, 0.000121
$$

**Step 3 — Sum and divide by $n$:**

$$
\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 0.00289
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \frac{0.00289}{10} = 0.000289 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Standard deviation:**

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{0.000289} \approx 0.017 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Repeatability definition (3σ):**

$$
R = \pm 3\sigma \approx \pm 0.051 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion:** This robot has a repeatability of approximately **±0.05 mm**, which is typical of high-precision assembly robots used in semiconductor manufacturing.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Torque Required for a Robotic Arm

**Problem:** A 2-DOF arm with $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.3$ m, currently at $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, must apply an end-effector force $F = (10 \text{ N}, 0 \text{ N})$ along the x-axis. Compute the joint torques.

**Step 1 — Convert angles to radians:**
$\theta_1 = \pi/6 \approx 0.5236$ rad, $\theta_2 = \pi/4 \approx 0.7854$ rad.

**Step 2 — Compute trigonometric values:**
$\sin\theta_1 = 0.5$, $\cos\theta_1 = 0.866$, $\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) = \sin(75°) = 0.966$, $\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) = \cos(75°) = 0.259$.

**Step 3 — Substitute into $J^T$:**

$$
J^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5(0.5) - 0.3(0.966) & 0.5(0.866) + 0.3(0.259) \\ -0.3(0.966) & 0.3(0.259) \end{bmatrix}
$$

$$
J^T = \begin{bmatrix} -0.540 & 0.511 \\ -0.290 & 0.078 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $F = (10, 0)^T$:**

$$
\tau_1 = -0.540 \cdot 10 + 0.511 \cdot 0 = -5.40 \text{ Nm}
$$

$$
\tau_2 = -0.290 \cdot 10 + 0.078 \cdot 0 = -2.90 \text{ Nm}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:** Negative torque indicates the joint motor must actively oppose the applied force (braking mode) to maintain static equilibrium. The magnitudes (5.40 Nm and 2.90 Nm) are within the safe operating range of a standard 100 W servo motor.

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Robotic Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Goal / Setpoint] --> B[Controller / Processor]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner]
    C --> D[Actuator Driver]
    D --> E[Mechanical Actuator]
    E --> F[End Effector / Tool]
    F --> G[Workpiece / Environment]
    G --> H[Physical Feedback]
    H --> I[Sensor Suite]
    I --> J[ADC / Digitizer]
    J --> K[State Estimator / Filter]
    K --> L[Error Computation]
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This closed-loop block diagram represents the **universal control architecture** applicable to every robotic system—from a welding arm to a Mars rover. The outer loop (B→C→D→E→F→G→H→I→J→K→L→B) is the perception-decision-action cycle. The inner loop (B→C→D→E) is the actuator command pathway.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]

**Q: Differentiate between accuracy and repeatability in the context of robotic manipulators. Why is repeatability more critical than accuracy in most industrial applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**Accuracy** is the deviation between the *commanded (desired) position* and the *actual mean position* reached by the robot over multiple attempts. It quantifies how close the robot gets to an absolute target.

**Repeatability** is the deviation between *successive attempts* to reach the *same taught position*, measured typically as ±3σ (three standard deviations). It quantifies the consistency of the robot.

In most industrial applications (e.g., automotive welding, PCB assembly), the robot is **taught** a reference point in its own coordinate frame and then commanded to return to it repeatedly. Since the reference is internal rather than absolute, repeatability is the dominant performance metric. A typical industrial 6-DOF robot has **repeatability of ±0.02 mm** but **accuracy of ±1 mm**—yet the former determines production yield.

| Aspect | Accuracy | Repeatability |
|--------|----------|---------------|
| Definition | Deviation from absolute target | Deviation between successive attempts |
| Typical Value | ±1 mm | ±0.02 mm |
| Criticality | Low for taught applications | High for taught applications |
| Higher Cost Impact? | No | Yes |

---

**Q: List and briefly explain any three distinct industrial applications of robots. State the sensor and actuator used in each.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Arc Welding:** Used to join metal parts in automotive manufacturing. Sensor: seam-tracking vision camera + arc current sensor. Actuator: 6-DOF servo-driven articulated arm with welding torch end-effector.

**2. Spray Painting:** Used for uniform coating on car bodies. Sensor: explosion-proof proximity sensors for safety. Actuator: 6-DOF hydraulic arm with pneumatic atomizer.

**3. Pick-and-Place Assembly:** Used for PCB component placement. Sensor: vision system for component orientation detection. Actuator: SCARA robot with 4-DOF and vacuum gripper.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Each application must be tied to a specific sensor-actuator pair. Generic statements like "robots use sensors" lose marks.
