## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define an eddy current proximity sensor. State TWO of its primary industrial applications in robotics. (3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Examiner-Standard):**

An **eddy current proximity sensor** is a non-contact, solid-state inductive transducer that detects the presence and proximity of an electrically conductive target by sensing changes in the impedance of an excitation coil caused by eddy currents induced in the target via high-frequency electromagnetic induction.

**Two robotics applications:**
1. **Tool presence verification** in a robotic end-effector changer (e.g., confirming a drill bit is clamped before machining starts).
2. **Shaft position / runout monitoring** on a robot-driven rotary joint to enable closed-loop position control and bearing health diagnostics.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
*State the principle of operation of an eddy current proximity sensor. Why is it unable to detect non-conductive materials? (3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** The sensor drives a high-frequency alternating current through a coil, producing an alternating magnetic field. When a conductive target enters the field, **Faraday's Law** induces circulating **eddy currents** in the target. These currents generate a counter-flux (**Lenz's Law**) that alters the coil's impedance, which is then detected.

**Why it cannot detect non-conductors:** Eddy current formation requires free charge carriers (conduction electrons or ions) to circulate within the target. Non-conductive materials (plastics, wood, ceramics) lack sufficient free charge carriers, so no significant eddy currents are induced, no counter-flux develops, and the coil impedance remains unchanged — yielding no detectable signal.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **skin depth** of an electromagnetic wave penetrating a good conductor. Explain how the operating frequency of an eddy current sensor is chosen based on target properties. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an eddy current proximity sensor. Compare its performance with a capacitive proximity sensor. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Derivation of skin depth** (as derived in Section 3.1 above — key steps summarized):

Starting from Maxwell's equations in a conductor with no displacement current, we obtain the **electromagnetic diffusion equation**:

$$
\nabla^2 \vec{H} = j\omega \mu \sigma \vec{H}
$$

For a 1-D plane wave propagating into the conductor in the $+z$ direction, assuming solution of the form $H(z) = H_0 e^{-\alpha z} e^{-j\beta z}$ and substituting into the diffusion equation:

$$
\alpha^2 - \beta^2 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad 2\alpha\beta = \omega\mu\sigma
$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
\alpha = \beta = \sqrt{\frac{\omega\mu\sigma}{2}}
$$

The skin depth $\delta$ is defined as the depth at which the amplitude falls to $1/e$ of its surface value:

$$
\boxed{\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\omega\mu\sigma}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f \mu\sigma}}}
$$

**Frequency selection rationale:**

For a target of thickness $t$:
- If $t < \delta$: eddy currents are not fully developed → sensitivity is poor.
- If $t \approx 2\delta$ to $3\delta$: optimum operating condition → maximum sensitivity.
- If $t > 3\delta$: bulk regime → sensitivity independent of $t$ (the desirable case for general-purpose sensors).

Therefore, the operating frequency is chosen so that the target is effectively a *semi-infinite* conductor (i.e., $\delta$ is much smaller than $t$). For typical industrial sensors, this places $f$ in the **100 kHz to 2 MHz** band.

**Numerical example (already shown in Section 3.2):** For aluminum with $\delta = 0.2\ \text{mm}$, the optimum $f \approx 181\ \text{kHz}$, which lies within the standard band.

(7 marks awarded for: 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for frequency selection reasoning, 2 marks for numerical example.)

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Block Diagram** (reproduced from Section 4.1 above — students should draw this in the answer sheet):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[DC Supply] --> B1[HF Oscillator]
    B1 --> C1[Probe Coil]
    C1 --> D1[AC Magnetic Field]
    D1 --> E1[Conductive Target Eddy Currents]
    E1 --> F1[Coil Impedance Change]
    F1 --> G1[Detector]
    G1 --> H1[Amplifier]
    H1 --> I1[Output]
```

**Working Explanation (Step-by-Step):**

1. The high-frequency oscillator generates a sinusoidal current, typically at **100 kHz – 2 MHz**.
2. This current flows through the probe coil, producing an alternating magnetic flux that radiates outward from the probe face.
3. When a conductive target enters the field, eddy currents are induced in it (Faraday's law).
4. The eddy currents generate a counter-flux (Lenz's law), reducing the net flux linking the primary coil.
5. This reduces the coil's inductance $L$ and increases its effective resistance $R$ (the eddy-current loop loads the primary).
6. The detector circuit — either an amplitude demodulator (envelope detector) or a frequency discriminator — converts the impedance change into a voltage.
7. The output stage produces either an analog voltage (0–10 V) proportional to distance, or a binary switching signal (NPN/PNP open-collector output) when the target crosses a threshold.

**Comparison Table — Eddy Current vs. Capacitive Proximity Sensor:**

| Parameter | Eddy Current Sensor | Capacitive Proximity Sensor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Detects | Conductive metals only | Both metals and non-metals (plastic, wood, liquid) |
| Operating principle | Electromagnetic induction (Faraday) | Change in capacitance |
| Sensing range | 0.5–15 mm | 1–40 mm |
| Output | Analog or switching | Analog or switching |
| Target material sensitivity | Strongly affected (target factor 0.4–1.0) | Less sensitive to material |
| Insensitive to dust, oil, water | Yes | Less so (dielectric constant matters) |
| Typical application | Metal part detection, position | Liquid level, plastic part detection |
| Cost | Moderate | Low to moderate |

(7 marks awarded for: 3 marks for working with diagram, 4 marks for comprehensive comparison.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of an eddy current proximity sensor with a labeled schematic. Discuss the role of skin depth in determining sensor performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An eddy current sensor is to be designed to detect an aluminum target ($\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7\ \text{S/m}$, $\mu_r = 1$, thickness = 5 mm) at a frequency of 500 kHz. Calculate: (i) the skin depth, (ii) whether the target is "effectively thick" for eddy-current purposes, and (iii) the ratio of skin depth at 500 kHz to that at 2 MHz. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Construction of an Eddy Current Proximity Sensor:**

The sensor consists of the following physical and electronic components:

1. **Probe Assembly:** A ferrite-cored inductor coil wound with many turns of fine enameled copper wire, encapsulated in a rugged polymer (e.g., PPS, epoxy) or metal housing. The ferrite core concentrates the field, increasing the effective range.
2. **Oscillator Circuit:** A high-frequency oscillator (Colpitts, Hartley, or crystal-stabilized) generating the excitation current. Frequency is typically 100 kHz to 2 MHz.
3. **Detector/Discriminator:** An envelope detector (diode + RC filter) for amplitude-based designs, or a frequency discriminator for FM designs.
4. **Signal Conditioner:** Includes an AC amplifier, rectifier, comparator, and linearization circuit.
5. **Output Stage:** Open-collector NPN or PNP transistor, push-pull driver, analog voltage output (0–10 V), 4–20 mA current loop, or IO-Link digital interface.
6. **Housing and Cable:** Threaded cylindrical metal/plastic body (M8, M12, M18, M30 are industry-standard sizes) with shielded cable for EMC immunity.

**Labeled Schematic (text-representation):**

```
   [Oscillator]----+---[Probe Coil L_p]---+
        |          |                      |
        |        [C_tune]              [Target] 
        |          |                    (eddy
        +----[Detector]<--[Rectifier]<--- currents)
                 |
              [Amplifier]
                 |
              [Output]
```

**Role of Skin Depth in Sensor Performance:**

- **Determines effective target depth:** Eddy currents flow primarily within one skin depth of the surface. A target must be at least $3\delta$ thick to behave as a "bulk" conductor.
- **Sets operating frequency:** Higher $f$ → smaller $\delta$ → field is more surface-localized → sharper spatial resolution but lower penetration.
- **Influences sensitivity:** Smaller $\delta$ concentrates currents, increasing the load on the probe coil → higher signal change per unit distance → better sensitivity.
- **Material discrimination:** Different metals have different $\delta$ at the same $f$, enabling qualitative material identification.

(7 marks awarded for: 3 marks for construction, 2 marks for working, 2 marks for skin-depth role.)

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — *Numerical Problem*

**Given:**
- Material: aluminum
- $\sigma = 3.5 \times 10^7\ \text{S/m}$
- $\mu_r = 1 \Rightarrow \mu = \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
- $t = 5\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $f_1 = 500\ \text{kHz} = 5 \times 10^5\ \text{Hz}$
- $f_2 = 2\ \text{MHz} = 2 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}$

**(i) Skin depth at 500 kHz:**

$$
\delta_1 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi f_1 \mu \sigma}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\delta_1 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 5 \times 10^5 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 3.5 \times 10^7}}
$$

Computing denominator step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \times 5 \times 10^5 &= 1.5708 \times 10^6 \\
\times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} &= 1.5708 \times 10^6 \times 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} = 1.974 \\
\times 3.5 \times 10^7 &= 6.908 \times 10^7
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta_1 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{6.908 \times 10^7}} = \sqrt{1.4477 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1.203 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\delta_1 \approx 0.120\ \text{mm}}
$$

**(ii) Is the target "effectively thick"?**

The criterion for a bulk (effectively thick) target is $t \geq 3\delta$:

$$
3\delta_1 = 3 \times 0.120 = 0.360\ \text{mm}
$$

Since target thickness $t = 5\ \text{mm} \gg 0.360\ \text{mm}$:

$$
\boxed{t \gg 3\delta_1 \Rightarrow \text{Target is "effectively thick" — sensor operates in the bulk regime.}}
$$

This is the desired condition for stable, reproducible sensing.

**(iii) Ratio of skin depths at 500 kHz and 2 MHz:**

Since $\delta \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}}$:

$$
\frac{\delta_1}{\delta_2} = \sqrt{\frac{f_2}{f_1}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^5}} = \sqrt{4} = 2
$$

$$
\boxed{\frac{\delta_1}{\delta_2} = 2}
$$

Equivalently, $\delta_2 = \delta_1 / 2 = 0.060\ \text{mm}$.

**Interpretation:** Doubling the frequency quarters the skin depth, concentrating eddy currents into a thinner surface layer — useful for high-resolution surface scanning but reducing penetration.

(7 marks awarded for: 2 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 3 marks for part (iii).)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Eddy Current Sensor Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing inductive proximity sensor with eddy current sensor** — they share a principle but are NOT synonymous. The inductive switch is a *switching-only, factory-floor* device; the eddy current sensor is its *precision, analog-output* cousin. Examiners specifically deduct for this conflation.
> 2. **Forgetting units in skin-depth problems** — always convert $f$ to Hz (not kHz or MHz) and $t$ to meters (not mm) before substituting. A common error is dropping the $\pi$ factor.
> 3. **Stating "eddy currents heat the target"** — true physically, but heating is a *side effect*, not the sensing mechanism. The sensor detects the *impedance change* caused by eddy currents, not the temperature rise.
> 4. **Claiming the sensor detects all metals equally** — it does not. The *target factor* derating (see Section 2.4) is a high-value viva point.
> 5. **Misapplying Lenz's Law** — the eddy-current flux *opposes the change* in applied flux, not the applied flux itself. Examiners are alert to this wording.
> 6. **Missing the $3\delta$ bulk criterion** — many students answer frequency questions without specifying whether the target is in the thin or thick regime.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core Principle:** High-frequency AC magnetic field → eddy currents in conductive target → impedance change in probe coil.
- **Foundational Laws:** Faraday's Law (induces EMF) and Lenz's Law (opposing flux).
- **Operating Frequency:** 100 kHz – 2 MHz (chosen for $\delta \ll$ target thickness).
- **Skin Depth Formula:** $\delta = \sqrt{1/(\pi f \mu \sigma)}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- **Detects:** Only conductive metals. Al, Cu, brass, Fe all work; non-metals do not.
- **Key Derating:** Non-ferrous targets reduce sensing range by 0.4× to 0.5× vs. mild steel.
- **Sensing Range:** Typically 0.5–15 mm; precision probes reach 0.05–2 mm.
- **Resolution:** Up to 10 nm in laboratory-grade displacement probes.
- **Non-Contact:** No mechanical wear, suitable for high-cycle-rate applications.
- **Block Diagram Stages:** Oscillator → Probe → Detector → Amplifier → Output.
- **Target Factor $F_t$:** Mild steel = 1.0 reference; aluminum ≈ 0.45; copper ≈ 0.40.
- **Bulk Criterion:** Target effectively thick if $t \geq 3\delta$.
- **Frequency vs. Skin Depth:** $\delta \propto 1/\sqrt{f}$ — doubling $f$ quarters $\delta$.
- **Advantages:** Non-contact, high accuracy, immune to dirt/oil/water (non-metallic contaminants), high switching frequency, long life.
- **Disadvantages:** Detects only metals; range derated by target material; affected by temperature.
- **Robotic Applications:** Tool presence, shaft position, vibration monitoring, gear-tooth counting, end-effector verification.
- **Comparison Hook:** Inductive switch = binary; eddy current = analog/precision; both use Faraday/Lenz.
- **Material Identification Bonus:** Different $\delta$ for different metals enables *qualitative material discrimination* — a frequent viva question.
- **Mathematical Safety:** When writing skin depth in tables, use $\dfrac{1}{}$ (LaTeX fraction), never the vertical pipe `|`.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Proximity Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inductive Proximity Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inductive Proximity Sensor (IPS):** A non-contact, solid-state electronic sensor that detects the presence (or absence) of a metallic target within a defined sensing range by exploiting the principles of **electromagnetic induction** and **eddy-current losses**. The sensor is part of the broader family of *electromagnetic proximity sensors* covered under **IEC 60947-5-2**, and is classified as a *passive transducer* because it does not require physical contact or a mechanical linkage to detect the target.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, an inductive proximity sensor is fundamentally a **Limit Switch Replacement Device** in industrial automation, used extensively in robotic end-effectors, conveyor systems, and assembly lines. It converts a mechanical/physical quantity (target position or distance) into a measurable change in electrical impedance.

### 1.2 Working Principle — The Magnetic "Damping" Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine a swing oscillating back and forth. If a heavy person stands close to the swing, the air resistance increases, and the swing slows down dramatically. An inductive proximity sensor works almost identically:
>
> - The **swing** = the sensor's internal **LC oscillator circuit** producing a high-frequency alternating magnetic field.
> - The **heavy person** = the **metallic target** that approaches the sensor face.
> - The **slowing down** = the **damping** of the oscillator's amplitude because of induced **eddy currents** in the target.
> - The **sensor's decision** = the trigger circuit detects this amplitude drop and switches the output transistor ON or OFF.

### 1.3 Core Physical Phenomena

The sensor relies on two interrelated electromagnetic phenomena:

1. **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** — A time-varying magnetic flux through a conducting loop induces an electromotive force (EMF) in the loop.
2. **Lenz's Law** — The direction of the induced current opposes the change in flux that produced it, generating *eddy currents* and dissipating energy as heat in the target.

### 1.4 Key Standardised Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Frequency (typical) | $f$ | **100 kHz to 1 MHz** |
| Sensing Range | $S_r$ | **0.8 mm to 100 mm** (depending on sensor size) |
| Standard Switching Distance | $S_n$ | **mm** (rated at nominal conditions) |
| Real Switching Distance | $S_r$ | **0.9 × $S_n$** (within ±10% of $S_n$) |
| Hysteresis | $H$ | **1% to 15% of $S_r$** |
| Operating Temperature | $T_{op}$ | **-25 °C to +85 °C** (industrial) |
| Supply Voltage | $V_s$ | **10–30 V DC** (3-wire type) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A KTU examiner will always expect you to remember that the *real* switching distance $S_r$ is approximately **0.9 × $S_n$**, where $S_n$ is the *nominal* switching distance declared by the manufacturer under ideal conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Architecture

An inductive proximity sensor is built from four functional sub-blocks:

1. **Coil / Inductor ($L$)** — Wound on a ferrite core (often potted in epoxy resin). The coil is positioned at the active face of the sensor. Typical inductance values are in the range of **10 μH to 10 mH**.
2. **Oscillator Circuit** — A Colpitts or Hartley-type LC oscillator driving the coil at a high frequency to generate an alternating magnetic field.
3. **Detector / Demodulator Stage** — A peak detector or rectifier circuit that monitors the oscillator's amplitude or the impedance change.
4. **Output Switching Stage** — A transistor (BJT, MOSFET, or Darlington pair) that drives the load. Common configurations are NPN (sourcing) or PNP (sinking) in 3-wire, or 2-wire AC types.

### 2.2 Operating Sequence — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WHY each step happens — the physics behind it:**

- **Step 1 — No Target Present:** The oscillator freely sustains a stable high-frequency alternating current in the coil. The magnetic field radiates outward, but no conductive object is close enough to absorb energy. The detector senses *high* amplitude and keeps the output transistor in its *de-energised* state (for a "normally open" sensor).

- **Step 2 — Target Approaches:** As a metallic (preferably ferrous) target enters the magnetic field, the alternating flux $\phi(t) = \phi_0 \sin(\omega t)$ cuts through the target's conductive body.

- **Step 3 — Eddy Currents are Induced:** Per Faraday's Law, an EMF $\varepsilon = -d\phi/dt$ is induced in the target, driving circular *eddy currents*. These currents create a *secondary* magnetic field that opposes the primary field (Lenz's Law).

- **Step 4 — Damping of Oscillator:** The opposing field reduces the net flux linking the sensor coil, effectively *increasing the load* on the oscillator. This raises the losses and the effective series resistance of the tank circuit.

- **Step 5 — Threshold Detection:** The amplitude of the LC tank voltage drops below a pre-set threshold. The detector circuitry flips state, energising the output transistor — the sensor output turns ON (or OFF, depending on NO/NC logic).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are *must-know* for KTU examinations. Memorise the units and the underlying assumptions.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$ | Inductance of the sensor coil | $N$ = turns, $\mu$ = core permeability, $A$ = core cross-section, $l$ = magnetic path length |
| 2 | $f_{osc} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | Natural frequency of the LC oscillator | Determines the operating frequency of the sensor |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = -N \dfrac{d\phi}{dt}$ | Induced EMF (Faraday's Law) | Drives eddy currents in the target |
| 4 | $Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$ | Total impedance of tank circuit | $X_L = 2\pi f L$, $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ |
| 5 | $S_r \approx 0.9 \times S_n$ | Real switching distance | Per IEC 60947-5-2 |
| 6 | $H\,\% = \dfrac{S_{r,\text{release}} - S_{r,\text{operate}}}{S_r} \times 100$ | Hysteresis as a percentage of $S_r$ | Indicates the deadband around $S_r$ |
| 7 | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^2$ | Energy stored in the inductor's field | Energy lost to eddy currents in the target |
| 8 | $P_{eddy} \propto B^2 f^2 t^2 / \rho$ | Power dissipated by eddy currents | $B$ = flux density, $t$ = target thickness, $\rho$ = resistivity |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Alert:** Do **not** confuse the symbols $S_n$ (nominal switching distance, manufacturer rating) and $S_r$ (real switching distance, achieved in practice). KTU questions frequently ask students to compute one given the other.

### 2.4 Target Material — Correction Factor

Not all metals produce the same eddy-current response. The *correction factor* (CF) quantifies the reduction in sensing range for a non-standard material relative to a standard reference target (mild steel Fe 360, square, 1 mm thick, side length equal to the diameter of the active face).

| Material | Typical Correction Factor |
|---|---|
| Mild Steel (Fe 360) — *Reference* | **1.00** |
| Stainless Steel (V2A) | **0.6 – 0.85** |
| Brass | **0.35 – 0.50** |
| Aluminium | **0.30 – 0.45** |
| Copper | **0.25 – 0.40** |

> The *effective* switching distance for a non-ferrous target is $S_{r,\text{eff}} = CF \times S_r$.

### 2.5 Shielded vs Unshielded Sensors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constructional Sub-types — frequently asked in KTU:**

- **Shielded (Flush Mountable):** The ferrite core is encapsulated by a metal ring that *concentrates* the field axially. The sensor can be installed *flush* with a metal support without false triggering. The sensing range is shorter.
- **Unshielded (Non-Flush):** The ferrite core protrudes; the magnetic field radiates radially. Gives a *longer* sensing range (typically **1.5× to 2×** that of an equivalent shielded sensor), but requires a free zone (no metal) around the housing.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Inductive proximity sensors are indispensable in:
- **Robotics:** End-effector position feedback, gripper jaw closure detection, joint-limit homing.
- **Automotive Assembly:** Detecting piston position, gear-tooth counting (with custom targets).
- **Conveyor Automation:** Counting metal cans, detecting object presence on production lines.
- **CNC Machining:** Tool-breakage detection, spindle-speed verification.
- **Heavy Machinery:** Position feedback in hydraulic cylinders via metal flag targets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LC Oscillator Operating Frequency

> [!NOTE]
> The sensor's behaviour is governed by the natural resonance of its LC tank. Deriving the resonant frequency is a high-yield topic for KTU Module 2 derivations.

**Given:** A parallel LC tank with inductance $L$ and capacitance $C$.

**Step 1 — Write the differential equation for the tank voltage.** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop, the current $i(t)$ through the inductor equals the current through the capacitor with opposite sign:

$$v(t) = L \frac{di(t)}{dt}, \quad i_C(t) = C \frac{dv(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Set the loop current to zero (LC resonance condition).** The current circulating in the closed LC loop has the form $i(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t + \phi)$. Substituting into the inductor equation:

$$v(t) = L \frac{d}{dt}\left[I_0 \cos(\omega t + \phi)\right] = -\omega L I_0 \sin(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the capacitor relation.** The current through the capacitor must equal the inductor current:

$$i_C(t) = C \frac{dv(t)}{dt} = -C \omega^2 L I_0 \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

**Step 4 — For a self-sustaining oscillation, equate magnitudes.** Setting $\vert i_C \vert = \vert i \vert$:

$$C \omega^2 L I_0 = I_0$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the angular frequency $\omega$:**

$$\omega^2 = \frac{1}{LC}$$

$$\omega = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to ordinary frequency in Hertz.** Using $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$f = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The sensor is engineered to oscillate at **100 kHz – 1 MHz** by choosing $L$ and $C$ accordingly. When a target approaches, the *effective* inductance of the coil changes (the ferrite's apparent permeability is modified by eddy-current reaction), shifting $f$ and/or dropping the amplitude. Either shift is detected by the trigger circuit.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Hysteresis Relation

> [!NOTE]
> Hysteresis is essential to prevent *chattering* (rapid ON/OFF cycling) when a target dwells exactly at the switching threshold.

**Given:** A target approaching the sensor face along the axial direction.

**Step 1 — Define the operate point.** The target is detected (output switches) at distance $S_{r,\text{operate}}$, with $S_{r,\text{operate}} < S_r$ (the target is *closer* than the nominal range when switching occurs).

**Step 2 — Define the release point.** As the target recedes, the output releases at $S_{r,\text{release}}$, with $S_{r,\text{release}} > S_r$.

**Step 3 — The hysteresis band $H$ is the difference between release and operate distances:**

$$H = S_{r,\text{release}} - S_{r,\text{operate}}$$

**Step 4 — Express $H$ as a percentage of the real switching distance $S_r$:**

$$H\,\% = \frac{S_{r,\text{release}} - S_{r,\text{operate}}}{S_r} \times 100$$

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A sensor has $S_n = 10$ mm, and the operate distance is measured as 8.5 mm while the release distance is 9.5 mm. Compute the hysteresis percentage.

**Solution:**

$$S_r = 0.9 \times S_n = 0.9 \times 10 = 9\ \text{mm}$$

$$H\,\% = \frac{9.5 - 8.5}{9} \times 100 = \frac{1.0}{9} \times 100 \approx 11.11\,\%$$

> This falls within the **1%–15%** industrial range, confirming the sensor meets IEC standards.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Target Material on Sensing Range

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a KTU-favourite. Exam questions often ask: "Will the sensor detect a brass target at the same distance as a steel target?"

Let the *rated* range for mild steel be $S_{r,\text{steel}}$. The effective range for any other material is:

$$S_{r,\text{eff}} = CF_{\text{material}} \times S_{r,\text{steel}}$$

**Sample Computation:** $S_n = 8$ mm nominal for steel. What is the effective range for aluminium ($CF \approx 0.4$)?

$$S_{r,\text{steel}} = 0.9 \times 8 = 7.2\ \text{mm}$$

$$S_{r,\text{Al}} = 0.4 \times 7.2 = 2.88\ \text{mm}$$

> **Engineering Verdict:** A robotic cell designed to detect steel gears at 7 mm will *miss* aluminium gears placed at the same location. The fixture must either (a) move the aluminium target closer, (b) choose a sensor with higher $S_n$, or (c) use a *capacitive* or *magnetic* sensor instead.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Inductive Reactance

> [!NOTE]
> Useful when explaining why the oscillator frequency choice matters for eddy-current generation.

The inductive reactance of the sensor coil at the operating frequency $f$ is:

$$X_L = 2 \pi f L$$

The capacitive reactance of the tank capacitor is:

$$X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}$$

The total impedance of a series RLC circuit is:

$$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + (X_L - X_C)^2}$$

At resonance, $X_L = X_C$, so $Z = R$ (minimum impedance, maximum current). When a target introduces eddy-current losses, the effective $R$ rises, the tank $Q$-factor drops, and the oscillation amplitude decreases — the physical signature the detector circuit senses.

$$Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

A *high-Q* oscillator (low $R$, sharp resonance) gives better sensitivity but a smaller effective range; a *low-Q* oscillator gives longer range but poorer discrimination.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Inductive Proximity Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is a **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** showing the signal flow inside a typical 3-wire DC inductive proximity sensor. The eddy-current path (from coil through the target back as a reaction field) is shown explicitly.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS[Power Supply 10 to 30 V DC] --> OSC[LC Oscillator 100 kHz to 1 MHz]
    OSC --> COIL[Sensor Coil with Ferrite Core]
    COIL -. "Alternating Magnetic Field" .-> TGT[Metallic Target]
    TGT -. "Eddy Currents Induce Reaction Field" .-> COIL
    COIL --> DET[Peak Detector and Threshold Comparator]
    DET --> AMP[Signal Amplifier and Debounce]
    AMP --> OUT[Output Transistor NPN or PNP]
    OUT --> LOAD[External Load Relay or PLC Input]
    OUT --> LED[Output Status LED Indicator]
    style PS fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style OSC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style COIL fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style DET fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style AMP fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6f42c1
    style OUT fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **Power Supply** energises the oscillator and the output stage.
- The **LC Oscillator** drives high-frequency current through the **Coil**, creating an alternating magnetic field at the sensor face.
- The **Target** (a ferrous or non-ferrous metal object) intercepts this field. Eddy currents induced in the target draw energy from the field and create a reaction field.
- The **Detector** senses the drop in the tank's oscillation amplitude. When amplitude falls below a threshold, the comparator flips.
- The **Amplifier/Debounce** circuit cleans up the signal and prevents rapid retriggering.
- The **Output Transistor** is finally switched, energising (or de-energising) the external **Load**.

---

### 4.2 Shielded vs Unshielded Sensor — Field Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below contrasts the magnetic field geometry for shielded (flush) versus unshielded (non-flush) inductive proximity sensors.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph US["UNSHIELDED NON FLUSH SENSOR"]
        direction TB
        U1[Active Face] --> U2[Field Lines Radiate Axially and Radially]
        U2 --> U3[Free Zone Required Around Housing]
        U2 --> U4[Longer Sensing Range Approx 1.5x to 2x]
    end
    subgraph SS["SHIELDED FLUSH SENSOR"]
        direction TB
        S1[Metal Ring Encapsulates Coil] --> S2[Field Lines Confined Axially]
        S2 --> S3[Can Be Mounted Flush in Metal]
        S2 --> S4[Shorter Sensing Range but Better Side Stability]
    end
    style US fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style SS fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

**Engineering Insight:** A KTU question may give you a robot cell drawing and ask which sensor to choose. The deciding rule is:
- If the sensor must be **embedded in metal** → choose *shielded*.
- If **maximum range** is the priority and the surroundings are *non-metallic* → choose *unshielded*.

---

### 4.3 Signal Flow During Target Detection — Time-Domain View

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram explains the cause-and-effect chain during detection, emphasising the *temporal* relationship between eddy-current loss and the output state.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sensor Coil
    participant O as LC Oscillator
    participant T as Metallic Target
    participant D as Detector Threshold
    participant Out as Output Transistor
    Note over S,O: Idle State High Amplitude
    O->>S: Drive Coil at f osc
    S-->>O: Tank Voltage Stable
    Note over T: Target Enters Sensing Zone
    T->>S: Absorbs Energy via Eddy Currents
    S-->>O: Tank Voltage Amplitude Drops
    O->>D: Signal Below Threshold
    D->>Out: Trip Comparator
    Out->>Out: Switch Output State NO to Closed
    Note over Out: Output Energised
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Define an inductive proximity sensor. State the principle on which it operates.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> An **inductive proximity sensor** is a non-contact electronic sensor used to detect the presence of metallic objects within a defined sensing range without physical contact. *(1 mark)*

> It operates on the principle of **electromagnetic induction and eddy-current losses**: a high-frequency LC oscillator drives a coil to produce an alternating magnetic field. When a metallic target enters this field, eddy currents are induced in the target, which oppose the primary field and damp the oscillator amplitude. *(2 marks)*

> A detector circuit senses this amplitude drop and switches the output transistor, producing a binary (ON/OFF) signal indicating target presence.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Repeated Pattern]**
**Differentiate between shielded and unshielded inductive proximity sensors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> This is a *direct 3-mark question* in KTU. Use a comparison table in the exam.

| Parameter | Shielded (Flush) | Unshielded (Non-Flush) |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Coil surrounded by a metal ring that confines the field axially | Coil protrudes; field radiates axially and radially |
| Mounting | Can be flush-mounted in metal | Requires a free zone of clear space around the housing |
| Sensing Range | Shorter | Longer (typically **1.5×–2×** of shielded) |
| Side Stability | Excellent (no false triggers from nearby metal) | Susceptible to false triggers from adjacent metal |

*(3 marks distributed across the four parameters.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an inductive proximity sensor. Discuss the role of the LC oscillator and the detector circuit. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the following performance parameters of an inductive proximity sensor: (i) Nominal switching distance $S_n$, (ii) Real switching distance $S_r$, (iii) Hysteresis. A sensor has $S_n = 12$ mm. If the operate and release distances are measured as 10.2 mm and 11.4 mm respectively, compute the percentage hysteresis. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

The inductive proximity sensor consists of four functional blocks: *(1 mark for block diagram)*

1. **Coil with Ferrite Core:** The active element that generates and receives the magnetic field. Wound with $N$ turns, inductance $L = N^2 \mu A / l$, where $\mu$ is the core permeability, $A$ the cross-section, and $l$ the magnetic path length. *(1 mark)*

2. **LC Oscillator (Colpitts or Hartley type):** Sustains a high-frequency alternating current ($f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$) through the coil, generating an alternating magnetic field at the sensor face. Typical operating frequencies are 100 kHz – 1 MHz. *(1 mark)*

3. **Detector / Demodulator:** A peak detector or rectifier monitors the tank voltage amplitude. In the *no-target* condition, the amplitude is high; when a metallic target enters and eddy-current losses are introduced, the amplitude drops below a preset threshold. *(1 mark)*

4. **Output Switching Stage:** A transistor (BJT, MOSFET, or Darlington pair) that drives the external load. Configurations include NPN (sourcing), PNP (sinking), or 2-wire AC types. *(1 mark)*

**Working sequence (2 marks for the explanation):**

- *No target:* Oscillator is in steady state, amplitude is high, output transistor is OFF (for NO type).
- *Target approaches:* Alternating flux $\phi = \phi_0 \sin(\omega t)$ cuts through the target, inducing an EMF $\varepsilon = -N \, d\phi / dt$.
- This EMF drives **eddy currents** in the target. The eddy currents, per **Lenz's Law**, generate a magnetic field opposing the primary field, effectively *loading* the oscillator.
- The tank amplitude drops, the detector's threshold is crossed, and the output transistor switches.

---

**(b) Performance Parameters and Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **$S_n$ (Nominal Switching Distance):** The manufacturer's rated switching distance under ideal conditions (standard target: 1 mm thick mild-steel Fe 360 square, side = sensor face diameter). *(1 mark)*
- **$S_r$ (Real Switching Distance):** The actual distance at which switching occurs in a real installation, accounting for manufacturing tolerances, supply voltage, and ambient temperature. Per IEC 60947-5-2, $S_r \approx 0.9 \times S_n$, and is specified within ±10%. *(1 mark)*
- **Hysteresis $H$:** The difference between the release distance $S_{r,\text{release}}$ and the operate distance $S_{r,\text{operate}}$, expressed as a percentage of $S_r$. It creates a *deadband* that prevents output chattering when the target dwells near the switching threshold. *(1 mark)*

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $S_n = 12$ mm, $S_{r,\text{operate}} = 10.2$ mm, $S_{r,\text{release}} = 11.4$ mm.

**Step 1:** Compute the real switching distance.

$$S_r = 0.9 \times S_n = 0.9 \times 12 = 10.8\ \text{mm}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the hysteresis formula.

$$H\,\% = \frac{S_{r,\text{release}} - S_{r,\text{operate}}}{S_r} \times 100$$

$$H\,\% = \frac{11.4 - 10.2}{10.8} \times 100 = \frac{1.2}{10.8} \times 100$$

$$H\,\% \approx 11.11\,\%$$

**Step 3:** Comment on the result. *(1 mark)*

> This value lies within the standard industrial range of **1%–15%**, confirming the sensor meets IEC 60947-5-2 specifications. The hysteresis band of 1.2 mm provides a robust deadband against vibration-induced chattering in robotic end-effectors.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the factors that affect the sensing range of an inductive proximity sensor. Explain the *correction factor* concept for non-ferrous targets with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the expression for the resonant frequency of the LC oscillator used in an inductive proximity sensor. A sensor uses a coil of $L = 470\ \mu H$ and a tank capacitor of $C = 4.7\ nF$. Compute its operating frequency. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Factors Affecting Sensing Range & Correction Factor (7 Marks):**

The sensing range of an inductive proximity sensor is influenced by: *(1 mark for the list)*

1. **Target Material:** Ferrous metals (e.g., mild steel) produce the strongest eddy-current response because of their high magnetic permeability. Non-ferrous metals (aluminium, copper, brass) generate weaker eddy currents, reducing the effective range. *(1 mark)*
2. **Target Size and Shape:** The standard target is a 1 mm thick, square steel plate with side length equal to the sensor's active-face diameter. Smaller targets produce a weaker signal. *(1 mark)*
3. **Target Thickness:** Very thin targets (< 0.5 mm) reduce the eddy-current loop area, decreasing range. Plates thicker than 1 mm show little further improvement. *(1 mark)*
4. **Mounting (Shielded vs Unshielded):** Unshielded sensors give 1.5×–2× the range of shielded equivalents, but the surroundings must be non-metallic. *(1 mark)*
5. **Operating Temperature and Supply Voltage:** Extreme temperatures alter the ferrite's permeability, and supply voltage variations affect the oscillator amplitude. Both factors can shift the switching point. *(1 mark)*

**Correction Factor (1 mark):**

> The correction factor $CF$ is a dimensionless multiplier that quantifies the reduction in sensing range for a material *other than mild steel*. It is defined as:

$$CF = \frac{S_{r,\text{material}}}{S_{r,\text{steel}}}$$

**Example:** *(1 mark)*

> A sensor with $S_n = 10$ mm (steel) and a *brass* target ($CF = 0.45$) will have an effective sensing range of:
>
> $$S_{r,\text{brass}} = 0.45 \times (0.9 \times 10) = 0.45 \times 9 = 4.05\ \text{mm}$$

> Thus, the brass target must approach to within **4.05 mm** to be detected, compared to **9 mm** for a steel target.

---

**(b) Derivation and Numerical (7 Marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.1 for the full derivation.)*

> Starting from the LC loop equations:
>
> $$L \frac{di}{dt} = v(t), \quad C \frac{dv}{dt} = -i(t)$$
>
> Substituting a trial solution $i(t) = I_0 \cos(\omega t)$:
>
> $$\frac{d^2 i}{dt^2} + \frac{1}{LC} i = 0$$
>
> The natural (resonant) angular frequency is:
>
> $$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$$
>
> Converting to ordinary frequency:
>
> $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Numerical (3 marks):**

Given: $L = 470\ \mu H = 470 \times 10^{-6}\ H$, $C = 4.7\ nF = 4.7 \times 10^{-9}\ F$.

**Step 1:** Compute $LC$.

$$LC = 470 \times 10^{-6} \times 4.7 \times 10^{-9} = 2209 \times 10^{-15} = 2.209 \times 10^{-12}\ H \cdot F$$

**Step 2:** Compute the square root.

$$\sqrt{LC} = \sqrt{2.209 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.4863 \times 10^{-6}\ s$$

**Step 3:** Compute $f_0$.

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.4863 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{9.3376 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_0 \approx 107,094\ Hz \approx 107.1\ kHz$$

> This lies within the typical operating range of **100 kHz – 1 MHz**, confirming the design is consistent with industrial inductive-sensor specifications.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost KTU students marks every semester:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $S_n$ and $S_r$:** Many students interchange these two terms, losing the **definition mark** instantly. Always state: "$S_n$ = nominal (manufacturer-rated); $S_r$ = real (achieved in practice), $S_r \approx 0.9 S_n$." *(1 mark lost in nearly every paper.)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** In the LC frequency derivation, students plug in $L$ in mH and $C$ in $\mu F$ without converting to SI (H and F). The result is off by **6 orders of magnitude**. *(Lose all numerical marks.)*
>
> 3. **Omitting the role of the standard target:** When asked about correction factors, students often write "brass has a lower range" without mentioning the *standard target* (1 mm thick Fe 360 square) as the reference. *(Lose 1 mark.)*
>
> 4. **No block diagram in Part B:** The KTU valuation key **explicitly awards 1 mark for a labelled block diagram** in construction-type questions. Always draw one — even a rough sketch with a pen earns the mark.
>
> 5. **Writing "inductive sensors detect all metals equally":** This is *factually wrong*. Non-ferrous metals have a *significantly* reduced range. Examiners dock marks for this misconception.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Inductive Proximity Sensor**

- ☐ **Definition:** Non-contact, solid-state sensor using **electromagnetic induction** and **eddy currents** to detect metallic targets.
- ☐ **Operating Frequency:** **100 kHz to 1 MHz** (LC oscillator-driven).
- ☐ **Operating Sequence:** Coil energised → magnetic field → target enters → eddy currents induced → oscillator damped → detector threshold crossed → output switches.
- ☐ **Key Formula — Resonant Frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.
- ☐ **Key Formula — Inductance:** $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu A}{l}$.
- ☐ **Key Formula — Hysteresis:** $H\,\% = \dfrac{S_{r,\text{release}} - S_{r,\text{operate}}}{S_r} \times 100$.
- ☐ **Real vs Nominal Range:** $S_r \approx 0.9 \times S_n$ (per IEC 60947-5-2).
- ☐ **Standard Target:** 1 mm thick mild steel Fe 360, square, side = sensor face diameter.
- ☐ **Correction Factor (CF):** Multiplier for non-ferrous targets. Example: $CF_{\text{Al}} \approx 0.4$, $CF_{\text{Cu}} \approx 0.3$.
- ☐ **Shielded vs Unshielded:** Shielded = flush mountable, shorter range; Unshielded = 1.5×–2× range, requires free zone.
- ☐ **Hysteresis Range:** 1%–15% of $S_r$ (industrial standard).
- ☐ **Output Types:** 3-wire DC (NPN/PNP), 2-wire AC, 4-wire complementary.
- ☐ **Lenz's Law:** Induced eddy currents create a magnetic field that *opposes* the original — the basis of the damping effect.
- ☐ **Cannot Detect:** Non-metals (plastic, wood, liquids) — for those, use *capacitive* sensors instead.
- ☐ **Industrial Applications:** Robotic gripper position feedback, conveyor object counting, piston-position detection, CNC tool-breakage sensing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacitive Proximity Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Capacitive Proximity Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Capacitive Proximity Sensor (CPS)** is a non-contact, solid-state electronic transducer that detects the presence, absence, or position of a target object by sensing variations in the **electrical capacitance** of a detection zone formed between an active electrode (sensor face) and a reference electrode (ground or target itself).

These sensors are categorized under the broader family of **non-contact displacement transducers** and operate on the fundamental principle that any object — **conductive (metallic) or non-conductive (plastic, wood, liquid, granulate, human body)** — entering the electrostatic field of the sensor face alters its effective dielectric properties, thereby producing a measurable change in capacitance.

The standard operational symbol for a capacitive proximity sensor in circuit schematics is a two-plate capacitor symbol, often paired with an oscillator/amplifier block, as per **IEC 60617** standards adopted by KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** Unlike **inductive proximity sensors** (which detect ONLY ferromagnetic/metallic targets), capacitive sensors can detect **virtually any material** because the disturbance mechanism is *dielectric perturbation*, not eddy current induction. This makes them indispensable in **food processing, pharmaceutical, and packaging robotics**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hidden Water Tank" Model

Imagine you have two metal plates held a fixed distance apart, connected to a small battery. Initially, the system stores a certain amount of electric charge. Now, bring your **hand** (non-metallic) close to the plates — *without touching them*. You will notice that the plates now seem to "store" more charge, even though your hand is not a conductor in the traditional sense.

**Why does this happen?**
Your hand is made of water and organic tissue, which has a **dielectric constant (εᵣ ≈ 40–80)** far greater than air (εᵣ ≈ 1). When your hand enters the electric field between the plates, the air (dielectric) is partially replaced by your hand's tissue, increasing the total stored charge at the same voltage. This increase in stored charge is exactly an **increase in capacitance**.

A capacitive proximity sensor does precisely this — it continuously monitors the capacitance of its sensing zone and triggers an output when the capacitance crosses a **preset switching threshold**.

> **Real-world intuition:** A capacitive sensor is essentially the industrial cousin of the touchscreen on your smartphone. Both rely on the human body (or any dielectric) disturbing a known electrostatic field.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **must-memorize** for KTU 2024 board examinations:

- **Permittivity of free space (ε₀):** $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- **Relative permittivity of air (εᵣ,air):** **1.0006 ≈ 1**
- **Relative permittivity of water (εᵣ,water):** **≈ 80**
- **Relative permittivity of human body tissue (εᵣ,tissue):** **40–80**
- **Relative permittivity of common plastics (PVC, PE):** **2–4**
- **Relative permittivity of glass:** **5–10**
- **Relative permittivity of oil:** **2–3**
- **Standard sensing range (Sₙ) for cylindrical CPS:** **1 mm to 50 mm** (typically)
- **Switching frequency:** **10 Hz to 100 Hz** (slower than inductive sensors)
- **Operating temperature range:** **-25°C to +85°C** (industrial grade)

> [!NOTE]
> The **sensing range** of a capacitive sensor is highly dependent on the dielectric constant of the target. Manufacturers specify the rated range using a **standard target (a grounded metal plate)**, but the effective range for low-dielectric materials can drop to **10–30%** of the rated value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The working of a capacitive proximity sensor can be broken into **five sequential functional stages**:

1. **Electrostatic Field Generation:** A high-frequency alternating electric field is generated between the **active electrode** (sensor face) and a **reference electrode** (often the housing ground or the target itself). This is achieved using an **RC oscillator** (typically a Colpitts or RC relaxation oscillator).

2. **Dielectric Perturbation:** When a target object (any material) enters the fringing electric field, it acts as a new dielectric medium. Because every material has a unique **relative permittivity (εᵣ)**, the effective capacitance of the system changes.

3. **Capacitance-to-Frequency Conversion:** The change in capacitance alters the oscillation frequency of the internal LC/RC tank circuit. The frequency deviation $\Delta f$ is proportional to the change in capacitance $\Delta C$.

4. **Signal Conditioning & Rectification:** The frequency-modulated signal is fed to a **rectifier and envelope detector**, which converts it to a slowly-varying DC voltage proportional to the target distance.

5. **Threshold Comparison & Switching:** A **Schmitt trigger comparator** evaluates the rectified voltage against a preset reference (adjustable via the sensor's sensitivity potentiometer). When the threshold is crossed, the output stage (transistor, MOSFET, or relay) switches state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** The sensor's output is **bistable** — it has only two states: ON (target detected) and OFF (no target). For continuous distance measurement, a **linear analog output (0–10 V or 4–20 mA) variant** is required.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting the Sensing Range

The effective sensing range of a capacitive sensor is influenced by **four primary factors**, all of which are regularly tested in KTU examinations:

- **Target Material (εᵣ):** Higher dielectric constant ⇒ greater range. A grounded metal target gives maximum range because it acts as a perfect mirror for electric field lines.
- **Target Size:** Targets smaller than the sensor face reduce the effective range significantly.
- **Mounting Geometry:**
  - **Flush mounting:** Sensor face is level with metal surround → reduced range (~60% of rated).
  - **Non-flush mounting:** Sensor face protrudes → full rated range.
- **Environmental Conditions:** Humidity, dust, condensation, and temperature drift can all cause false triggering due to changes in the dielectric constant of the surrounding air.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | $C$: capacitance, $\varepsilon_0$: $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_r$: relative permittivity, $A$: plate area, $d$: plate separation | F, m², m |
| $\Delta C = C_0 (\varepsilon_{r,\text{target}} - 1)$ | Change in capacitance due to target entry | $C_0$: baseline (air) capacitance | F |
| $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonant frequency of LC tank | $L$: inductance, $C$: capacitance | Hz, H, F |
| $S = \dfrac{\Delta C / C}{\Delta x}$ | Sensitivity of capacitive displacement sensor | $\Delta x$: change in distance | dimensionless per m |
| $V_{out} = K \cdot \Delta C$ | Linear output (analog variant) | $K$: transducer constant | V/F |
| $\tau = R_s C_x$ | Time constant of charge/discharge | $R_s$: sense resistance, $C_x$: sense capacitance | s |
| $S_n^{\text{eff}} = S_n \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{target}}}$ | Effective sensing range for a given material | $S_n$: rated range (with grounded metal target) | mm |
| $V_{bal} = 0$ | Balance condition of AC Wheatstone bridge | All four impedances matched | V |
| $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C}{C}$ | Output of AC bridge for small $\Delta C$ | $V_{in}$: excitation voltage | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Hallucination Note:** The formula $S_n^{\text{eff}} = S_n \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,\text{target}}}$ is an **engineering approximation** (not a strict physical law) used by manufacturers like **Sick AG** and **Omron** in their datasheets. It is valid for $\varepsilon_{r,\text{target}} \geq 1$ and is a standard exam-friendly form.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Capacitive proximity sensors are extensively deployed in the following **KTU-relevant** industrial and robotics applications:

- **Liquid level detection** in non-metallic tanks (plastic, glass) — critical in chemical and food industries.
- **Object detection on conveyor belts** for packaging lines — works for paper, cardboard, plastic, glass.
- **Gripper feedback** in robotic pick-and-place systems where the target material is unknown.
- **Sheet-feed monitoring** in printing and paper-handling machinery.
- **Presence detection of granular materials** (grain, sugar, salt, plastic pellets) in silos.
- **Touchless human-machine interfaces (HMI)** in elevators, kiosks, and biomedical devices.
- **Thickness measurement** in sheet-metal rolling mills (using differential capacitive arrangement).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important for robotics?** In a **robotic grasping application**, the end-effector must be able to detect the part regardless of its material (metal, plastic, wood, ceramic). An inductive sensor would fail on plastic — but a capacitive sensor handles *all* materials uniformly (with sensitivity adjustment).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance of a Parallel-Plate Sensor Head

We begin with **Gauss's Law** in its integral form applied to a parallel-plate geometry with plate area $A$ and separation $d$, where the dielectric is homogeneous with permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$.

**Step 1 — Surface Charge Density:**
Assume a charge $+Q$ on the upper plate and $-Q$ on the lower plate. The surface charge density is:

$$\sigma = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Electric Field Between the Plates:**
By Gauss's Law, the uniform electric field between the plates (ignoring fringing) is:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

**Step 3 — Potential Difference:**
The potential difference between the plates is the line integral of the electric field across the separation $d$:

$$V = \int_0^d E \, dx = E \cdot d = \frac{Q \, d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Definition of Capacitance:**
By definition, $C = \dfrac{Q}{V}$. Substituting the expression for $V$:

$$C = \frac{Q}{\dfrac{Q \, d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Therefore, the parallel-plate capacitance is:

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Charge $Q$ appears on both numerator and denominator and cancels out, leaving only the geometric ($A$, $d$) and material ($\varepsilon_r$) terms — this is the fundamental result used for **all** capacitive sensor analysis.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Change in Capacitance When a Target Enters the Field

Consider a capacitive sensor with active electrode area $A$ and nominal sensing distance $d_0$ (in air). When a target material with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,t}$ enters the fringing field region, the **effective permittivity** of the medium changes.

**Step 1 — Baseline Capacitance (No Target, Air Only):**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot 1 \cdot A}{d_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0}$$

**Step 2 — Capacitance with Target Present:**
Using a series combination model (air gap + target material), where the target occupies a fraction $\alpha$ of the effective gap:

$$C_{\text{target}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{(1-\alpha) d_0 + \dfrac{\alpha d_0}{\varepsilon_{r,t}}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplification for Full Coverage ($\alpha \to 1$):**
When the target fully covers the field (target in contact, $\alpha = 1$):

$$C_{\text{target}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,t} A}{d_0}$$

**Step 4 — Net Change in Capacitance:**

$$\Delta C = C_{\text{target}} - C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Delta C = C_0 \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This is the **master equation** for capacitive sensor analysis. It tells us that the change in capacitance is directly proportional to $(\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$. For a metal target, the boundary condition $\varepsilon_{r,t} \to \infty$ (perfect conductor) makes $\Delta C$ very large — which is why grounded metal targets give the **maximum** sensing range.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output of the AC Bridge Detector

A common readout circuit is the **AC Wheatstone bridge**. The four arms consist of capacitors $C_1$, $C_2$, $C_3$, and the sensing capacitor $C_x = C_0 + \Delta C$.

**Step 1 — Bridge Balance Condition (No Target):**
When $C_x = C_0$ and the bridge is balanced:

$$\frac{C_1}{C_2} = \frac{C_3}{C_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Off-Balance Condition (Target Present):**
The output voltage for an AC bridge with excitation $V_{in}$ is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left( \frac{C_x}{C_1 + C_x} - \frac{C_3}{C_2 + C_3} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Linearization for Small $\Delta C$:**
Let $C_x = C_0 + \Delta C$ and assume $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C_0$ for a symmetric bridge. Expanding to first order in $\Delta C / C_0$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{C_0}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the Expression for $\Delta C$:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{C_0 \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)}{C_0} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This shows the output voltage is **independent of the physical geometry** of the sensor (the $A$ and $d_0$ terms cancel out) and depends only on the dielectric constant of the target and the excitation voltage. This is why capacitive sensors are exceptionally good at **material discrimination** — the output directly reveals $\varepsilon_{r,t}$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frequency Shift in the LC Oscillator

The sensor's capacitance $C_x = C_0 + \Delta C$ forms the tank circuit of a Colpitts oscillator with fixed inductance $L$.

**Step 1 — Resonant Frequency (No Target):**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_0}}$$

**Step 2 — Resonant Frequency (With Target):**

$$f_x = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L (C_0 + \Delta C)}}$$

**Step 3 — Frequency Shift Calculation:**

$$\Delta f = f_0 - f_x = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L}} \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{C_0}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{C_0 + \Delta C}} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Linearized Frequency Shift (for $\Delta C \ll C_0$):**

$$\Delta f \approx \frac{f_0}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{C_0}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\Delta f \approx \frac{f_0}{2} \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{f_0}{2} \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The frequency shift is **directly proportional** to the change in capacitance. This frequency-modulated signal is easier to transmit over long cable runs (immune to noise pickup) and is the standard readout method in industrial capacitive sensors.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Functional Dependence | Proportional to $\varepsilon_{r,t}$? | Used in |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacitance change | $\Delta C$ | $\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1$ | Linear | All capacitive sensors |
| Bridge output voltage | $V_{out}$ | $\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1$ | Linear | Analog-output CPS |
| Frequency shift | $\Delta f$ | $\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1$ | Linear | Oscillator-based CPS |
| Effective range | $S_n^{\text{eff}}$ | $\sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,t}}$ | Sub-linear | Switching CPS |
| Charge stored | $Q$ | $\varepsilon_{r,t}$ | Linear | Touchscreen-type sensing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow (Block Diagram of a Capacitive Proximity Sensor)

This Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete signal flow** from the electrostatic field to the digital output stage, as covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillator Circuit RC Relaxation Type] --> B[Active Electrode Sensor Face]
    B --> C[Electrostatic Fringing Field Detection Zone]
    C --> D{Target Object Enters Field}
    D -- Yes --> E[Change in Dielectric Medium]
    D -- No --> F[Reference Capacitance Maintained]
    E --> G[Capacitance Change Delta C]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Rectifier and Envelope Detector]
    H --> I[Schmitt Trigger Comparator]
    J[Threshold Set by Sensitivity Potentiometer] --> I
    I --> K[Output Stage Transistor or MOSFET]
    K --> L[Digital Output ON or OFF Signal to PLC or Robot Controller]
    L --> M[Robot Actuator Response Gripper Action or Stop Command]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The oscillator drives the active electrode, creating a fringing field. When a target enters, the capacitance changes, which is rectified, compared to a threshold, and converted into a switching action at the output — which is then read by the robot's controller (PLC/microcontroller) to trigger an actuator (e.g., gripper close, conveyor stop).

---

## 4.2 Operating Principle Schematic (Sensor Face Geometry)

This diagram shows the physical arrangement of the electrodes, the fringing field, and the target interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENSOR_HEAD[Capacitive Sensor Head Cross Section]
        E1[Active Electrode at Sensor Face]
        E2[Reference Electrode Housing Ground]
        F1[Fringing Electric Field Lines]
        E1 --- F1
        F1 --- E2
    end
    subgraph DETECTION_ZONE[Detection Zone Outside Sensor]
        T[Target Object Any Material]
        F1 -. Perturbs .-> T
    end
    T -- Causes Delta C --> OSC[RC Oscillator Frequency Modulation]
    OSC --> PROC[Signal Processor Comparator]
    PROC --> OUT[Output Switch]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The active and reference electrodes form the two "plates" of the sensor capacitor. The fringing field extends outward into the detection zone. Any target material entering this zone perturbs the field, changing capacitance. The oscillator detects this as a frequency shift, which the processor converts to a switching action.

---

## 4.3 AC Bridge Readout Circuit Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Excitation Source Vin at 100 kHz to 1 MHz] --> B[Capacitor C1 Reference]
    A --> C[Capacitor Cx Sensing Element with Target]
    B --> D[Junction Point A]
    C --> E[Junction Point B]
    D --> F[Differential Amplifier]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Rectifier and Filter]
    G --> H[Schmitt Trigger with Hysteresis]
    H --> I[Output Transistor Switching Stage]
    I --> J[Digital Output to Controller]
    K[Balance Capacitor C3] --> E
    L[Balance Capacitor C2] --> D
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the AC Wheatstone bridge readout. The bridge is balanced when no target is present. When the target enters, $C_x$ changes, unbalancing the bridge and producing a non-zero $V_{out}$, which is amplified, rectified, and fed to a Schmitt trigger for clean switching.

---

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Material Detection Capability Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Object Enters Detection Zone] --> B{Is the Object Electrically Conductive Metal?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Inductive Sensor Works AND Capacitive Sensor Works]
    B -- No --> D{Is the Object Non Conductive Plastic Wood Glass Liquid?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Only Capacitive Sensor Can Detect]
    D -- No --> F[Check Dielectric Constant Greater than 1]
    F -- Yes --> E
    F -- No --> G[No Standard Proximity Sensor Will Detect]
    C --> H[Output Signal Triggered]
    E --> H
    G --> I[Consider Vision or Ultrasonic Sensor]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a high-yield KTU exam diagram — it summarizes the comparative advantage of capacitive sensors over inductive sensors, which is a frequently asked concept question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
