# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
**Q: Define a capacitive proximity sensor. List any four materials that can be detected by it but NOT by an inductive proximity sensor.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A capacitive proximity sensor is a non-contact electronic transducer that detects the presence of a target object by measuring changes in the **electrical capacitance** of its sensing zone. The capacitance changes when any material — conductor or dielectric — enters the electrostatic fringing field of the sensor face.

**Materials detected by capacitive but NOT by inductive sensors (1.5 marks):**
1. **Plastic** (e.g., PVC, polyethylene) — $\varepsilon_r \approx 2$ to $4$
2. **Glass** — $\varepsilon_r \approx 5$ to $10$
3. **Water and aqueous liquids** — $\varepsilon_r \approx 80$
4. **Wood / Paper / Cardboard** — $\varepsilon_r \approx 2$ to $6$
5. **Human body / organic tissue** — $\varepsilon_r \approx 40$ to $80$
6. **Granular materials** (grain, sugar, salt) — $\varepsilon_r \approx 2$ to $5$

> **Key reasoning:** Inductive sensors rely on **eddy current induction**, which requires a **ferromagnetic or conductive** target. Capacitive sensors rely on **dielectric perturbation**, which works for *any* material with $\varepsilon_r > 1$.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2024]`
**Q: State the formula for the parallel-plate capacitance and explain how the entry of a target object into the sensor's field modifies this capacitance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Parallel-plate capacitance formula (1 mark):**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $C$ is capacitance, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the permittivity of free space, $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the dielectric, $A$ is the plate area, and $d$ is the plate separation.

**Modification due to target entry (2 marks):**
When a target object with relative permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,t}$ enters the fringing field region, the air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$) is partially replaced by the target material. The change in capacitance is:

$$\Delta C = C_0 \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)$$

where $C_0$ is the baseline capacitance with air as dielectric. This $\Delta C$ is sensed by the internal oscillator, triggering a switching action at the output. For a **grounded metal target**, $\varepsilon_{r,t} \to \infty$, giving the **maximum possible** $\Delta C$ and therefore the **maximum sensing range**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a capacitive proximity sensor. Discuss the factors affecting its sensing range and compare its performance with an inductive proximity sensor.**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to the functional block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The key blocks are:
1. **RC Relaxation Oscillator** — generates high-frequency AC excitation
2. **Active Electrode** — forms one plate of the sensing capacitor
3. **Reference Electrode** — forms the other plate (often the sensor housing/ground)
4. **Rectifier / Envelope Detector** — converts frequency variation to DC level
5. **Schmitt Trigger Comparator** — compares the DC level with a reference
6. **Output Stage** — transistor or MOSFET switch for load driving

**Working Principle (5 marks):**
- The RC oscillator produces a high-frequency alternating voltage (typically **100 kHz to 1 MHz**) which is applied to the **active electrode**.
- This creates a **fringing electrostatic field** that extends outward from the sensor face into the surrounding space.
- The sensor face, together with the reference electrode (ground), forms a capacitor whose value depends on the dielectric medium in the field region.
- When **no target is present**, the dielectric is air, and the capacitance is at its **minimum baseline value** $C_0$.
- When a **target object** enters the field, it displaces air and introduces a new dielectric with $\varepsilon_{r,t} > 1$. The capacitance increases by $\Delta C = C_0 (\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$.
- This change in capacitance **decreases the oscillation frequency** of the RC/LC tank.
- The frequency-modulated signal is rectified and fed to a **Schmitt trigger comparator**.
- If the rectified voltage exceeds the preset threshold, the output transistor switches ON, indicating **target detection**.
- The threshold is user-adjustable via a **sensitivity potentiometer** on the sensor housing, allowing the same sensor to detect materials of varying dielectric strength.

#### Part (b) — Factors Affecting Sensing Range and Comparison with Inductive Sensor (7 Marks)

**Factors Affecting Sensing Range (3.5 marks):**

| Factor | Effect |
|---|---|
| **Dielectric constant of target** ($\varepsilon_{r,t}$) | Higher $\varepsilon_{r,t}$ ⇒ larger $\Delta C$ ⇒ greater range. Metal targets give maximum range. |
| **Target surface area** | Targets smaller than the sensor face reduce the effective range significantly. |
| **Mounting style** | Flush mounting (surrounded by metal) reduces range to ~60% of rated; non-flush (protruding) gives full rated range. |
| **Distance from target** | Sensing range decreases with increasing distance, approximately following an inverse-square law. |
| **Environmental conditions** | Humidity, condensation, and dust can cause false triggering by altering the local dielectric. |
| **Operating temperature** | Drift in oscillator components and $\varepsilon_r$ of air can shift the switching point. |

**Comparison with Inductive Proximity Sensor (3.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Capacitive Sensor | Inductive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating principle** | Change in capacitance (dielectric perturbation) | Eddy current induction in target |
| **Target material** | ANY material (metal, plastic, liquid, wood) | ONLY conductive/metallic targets |
| **Sensing range** | 1–50 mm (typically larger) | 1–20 mm (typically smaller) |
| **Switching frequency** | 10–100 Hz (slower) | 100 Hz–5 kHz (faster) |
| **Sensitivity to environment** | High (humidity, dust affect it) | Low (robust to environment) |
| **Cost** | Slightly higher | Lower |
| **Typical applications** | Liquid level, plastic detection, packaging | Metal part counting, position sensing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the working principle with an inductive sensor in the answer. Many students mistakenly say "it works by eddy currents" — this is **wrong** and will cost 2–3 marks.
> - **Do NOT** forget to mention that the **Schmitt trigger** is used to prevent **chattering** (multiple false triggers) near the switching threshold.
> - **Always** state the **frequency range of the oscillator** (kHz to MHz) — this is a frequently missed technical detail worth 1 mark.
> - **Avoid** vague statements like "it senses objects" — be specific: "it senses a **change in capacitance** caused by **dielectric perturbation**."

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: Derive the expression for the change in capacitance of a capacitive sensor when a target enters its field. Explain the AC bridge method of capacitance measurement and derive the output voltage expression.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of $\Delta C$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider a parallel-plate sensor with active electrode of area $A$ at a nominal distance $d_0$ from the target surface, with air as the initial dielectric. The baseline capacitance is:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** When a target of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_{r,t}$ enters the field, the air is displaced. Treating the system as a series combination of the remaining air gap (thickness $(1-\alpha)d_0$) and the target material (effective thickness $\alpha d_0$):

$$\frac{1}{C_{\text{target}}} = \frac{(1-\alpha) d_0}{\varepsilon_0 A} + \frac{\alpha d_0}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,t} A}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Simplifying:

$$C_{\text{target}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{(1-\alpha) d_0 + \dfrac{\alpha d_0}{\varepsilon_{r,t}}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** For the practical case of full target coverage ($\alpha = 1$):

$$C_{\text{target}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_{r,t} A}{d_0}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks):** The change in capacitance:

$$\Delta C = C_{\text{target}} - C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0} \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Final boxed result:

$$\boxed{\Delta C = C_0 \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)}$$

**Physical interpretation:** This shows that $\Delta C$ depends **linearly** on $(\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$. For a metal target, $\varepsilon_{r,t} \to \infty$, so $\Delta C$ is maximized — explaining why grounded metal targets give the largest sensing range. For materials with $\varepsilon_{r,t}$ close to 1 (e.g., Styrofoam), the change is minimal and detection range is severely reduced.

#### Part (b) — AC Bridge Method and Output Derivation (7 Marks)

**Bridge Description (2 marks):**
The **AC Wheatstone bridge** is the standard method for measuring small capacitance changes. The four arms are:
- $C_1$ — reference capacitor (arm AB)
- $C_2$ — reference capacitor (arm AD)
- $C_3$ — reference capacitor (arm BC)
- $C_x = C_0 + \Delta C$ — sensing capacitor (arm DC)

An AC excitation voltage $V_{in}$ of frequency 10 kHz–1 MHz is applied across nodes A and C. The output $V_{out}$ is measured between nodes B and D.

**Balance Condition (1 mark):** With no target, the bridge is balanced:

$$\frac{C_1}{C_2} = \frac{C_3}{C_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = 0$$

**Off-Balance Output (3 marks):** The output voltage for the bridge is:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left( \frac{C_x}{C_1 + C_x} - \frac{C_3}{C_2 + C_3} \right)$$

Assuming a symmetric bridge with $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C_0$ and substituting $C_x = C_0 + \Delta C$:

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \left( \frac{C_0 + \Delta C}{2C_0 + \Delta C} - \frac{C_0}{2C_0} \right)$$

**Linearization (1 mark):** For $\Delta C \ll C_0$, the first-order Taylor expansion gives:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} = \frac{V_{in}}{4} \left( \varepsilon_{r,t} - 1 \right)}$$

This output is then **amplified, rectified, and fed to a Schmitt trigger** for binary ON/OFF switching, or directly used as a linear analog signal for continuous distance measurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part B Pitfalls:**
> - **Always** show the intermediate steps in the derivation. Skipping from $C_x = C_0 + \Delta C$ directly to the final $V_{out}$ expression without showing the bridge equation and the linearization step will cost 2–3 marks.
> - **State the assumption** $\Delta C \ll C_0$ explicitly when linearizing — this is the validity condition for the final formula and is worth 1 mark.
> - **Do NOT confuse** the AC bridge with a DC bridge — the AC bridge uses **capacitors** (frequency-dependent), not resistors.
> - **Mention** the role of the **Schmitt trigger** in providing hysteresis and preventing chattering — this is a high-value conceptual point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Summary — Memorize Before Exam Day**

- **Core definition:** Capacitive proximity sensor = non-contact sensor that detects **change in capacitance** due to **dielectric perturbation** of its electrostatic field.

- **Master formula:** $\Delta C = C_0 (\varepsilon_{r,t} - 1)$ — this is the single most important equation for the topic.

- **Parallel-plate formula:** $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ — with $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

- **Key advantage over inductive sensor:** Can detect **BOTH metallic AND non-metallic** targets (plastic, glass, wood, liquid, human body).

- **Oscillator frequency:** 100 kHz to 1 MHz — high frequency is essential for sensitivity.

- **Effective range formula:** $S_n^{\text{eff}} = S_n \cdot \sqrt{\varepsilon_{r,t}}$ — approximate, but exam-relevant.

- **Output stage:** Schmitt trigger + transistor/MOSFET for bistable ON/OFF output.

- **Sensitivity adjustment:** Via a potentiometer on the sensor housing — allows one sensor to handle multiple target materials.

- **Mounting styles:**
  - **Flush** (shielded): ~60% of rated range, immune to surrounding metal.
  - **Non-flush** (unshielded): 100% of rated range, but affected by surrounding metal.

- **Material ranking by ease of detection (high to low):**
  Grounded metal > water > human body > glass > wood > plastic > paper > empty air (no detection).

- **Bridge readout formula:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0}$ (valid for $\Delta C \ll C_0$).

- **Frequency shift formula:** $\Delta f \approx \dfrac{f_0}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0}$ — used in oscillator-based CPS.

- **Environmental sensitivity:** Capacitive sensors are **more sensitive** to humidity, dust, and temperature than inductive sensors — a key disadvantage to mention in comparative questions.

- **Switching frequency:** 10–100 Hz — slower than inductive (up to 5 kHz) — important for high-speed robotics applications.

- **Standard applications:** Liquid level sensing, plastic object detection, packaging, sheet-feed monitoring, robotic gripper feedback, touchless HMI.

- **Three signs of a weak answer to avoid:**
  1. Saying it works on "eddy current" principle (WRONG — that is inductive).
  2. Forgetting to mention the **dielectric constant** as the key material property.
  3. Not drawing a block diagram — always include one in 7+ mark questions.

- **Two bonus points to elevate your answer:**
  1. Mention the **Schmitt trigger hysteresis** to explain why the output is stable (no chattering).
  2. Mention the **inverse-square falloff** of the fringing field to explain why the sensing range is limited and not linear with distance.

- **Numerical units to remember:**
  $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_{r,\text{air}} \approx 1$, $\varepsilon_{r,\text{water}} \approx 80$, $\varepsilon_{r,\text{plastic}} \approx 2$ to $4$.

- **One-line exam answer (for 1-mark questions):** "A capacitive proximity sensor is a non-contact transducer that detects the presence of an object by sensing a change in capacitance caused by the entry of the object into its electrostatic field."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Force Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Force Sensor** (also called a *Force Transducer*) is a device that converts an applied mechanical force (static or dynamic) into a measurable electrical signal, whose magnitude is proportional to the force applied. In the context of **Robotics and Artificial Engineering**, a force sensor is a critical feedback element used to enable a robot to perceive, monitor, and react to physical interactions with its environment — including contact forces, gripping loads, collision impacts, and payload weights.

Mathematically, the primary transfer function of an ideal force sensor is expressed as:

$$V_{out} = K_F \cdot F_{applied}$$

where $K_F$ is the **sensitivity constant** (expressed in V/N or mV/N) and $F_{applied}$ is the mechanical force (in Newtons) acting on the sensing element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Force sensors are classified under the *Contact-Type Proprioceptive / Exteroceptive Sensors* family. The syllabus specifically emphasizes the operating principles of **strain-gauge, piezoelectric, and capacitive** force sensors, along with their signal conditioning circuits (Wheatstone bridge and charge amplifiers).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Bathroom Scale"

To a beginner, imagine a **digital bathroom scale**. When you stand on it, your body weight (a *force*, technically $F = mg$) pushes down on a hidden metal spring inside the scale. The spring deforms very slightly. Attached to that spring is a tiny **strain gauge** whose electrical resistance changes with the deformation. A small electronic chip measures this resistance change, converts it into a number, and displays your weight on an LCD.

A **robotic force sensor works in exactly the same way**, except:
- The "weight" might be the force a robot gripper applies to a fragile egg.
- The deformation is microscopic (micrometers).
- The output is an analog voltage sent to the robot's controller, not a digital display.
- The sensor must respond in *milliseconds* and survive millions of repeated load cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Robotics Application:** Modern collaborative robots (cobots) like the *Universal Robots UR5e* and *KUKA LBR iiwa* integrate 6-axis force/torque sensors at their wrist joint. This allows them to "feel" when they touch a human and instantly stop, preventing injury — a feature mandated by **ISO/TS 15066** safety standards.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Unit of Force:** **Newton (N)** in the SI system. $1\,\text{N} = 1\,\text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard Gravitational Acceleration:** $g \approx 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ (used to convert mass to force: $F = mg$).
- **Typical Sensitivity Ranges for Robotic Force Sensors:** $1\,\text{mV/N}$ to $10\,\text{mV/N}$ for strain-gauge types; up to $50\,\text{pC/N}$ for piezoelectric types.
- **Operating Frequency Range:** DC to $10\,\text{kHz}$ (strain gauge) and $0.1\,\text{Hz}$ to $100\,\text{kHz}$ (piezoelectric).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles — The Three Core Transduction Mechanisms

A force sensor can be understood as a three-stage system:

**Stage 1 → Mechanical Domain:** Applied force $F$ produces a mechanical deformation (strain $\varepsilon$ or displacement $\Delta x$).
**Stage 2 → Transduction Domain:** The deformation is converted into a change in an electrical property ($R$, $C$, or charge $Q$).
**Stage 3 → Electrical Domain:** The property change is conditioned into a usable voltage output.

### 2.1.1 Strain-Gauge Based Force Sensor
- A **strain gauge** is a thin metallic foil pattern (usually Constantan or Karma alloy) bonded to a flexible substrate.
- When force deforms the host structure, the gauge stretches or compresses, altering its resistance.
- The fractional resistance change is governed by the **Gauge Factor (GF)**:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

where $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is the longitudinal strain and $R$ is the nominal gauge resistance (typically $120\,\Omega$, $350\,\Omega$, or $1000\,\Omega$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Gauge Factor" matters:** Typical metallic foil strain gauges have $GF \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$. Semiconductor strain gauges can reach $GF \approx 100$ to $200$, giving much higher sensitivity at the cost of temperature stability.

### 2.1.2 Piezoelectric Force Sensor
- Based on the **direct piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie, 1880).
- Certain crystals (Quartz, PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate) generate an electric charge $Q$ when subjected to mechanical stress.
- The governing equation is:

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (in C/N or pC/N).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Piezoelectric sensors **cannot measure static (DC) forces** because the generated charge leaks away over time. They are ideal for **dynamic forces** like impacts, vibrations, and tactile pulses.

### 2.1.3 Capacitive Force Sensor
- Two parallel plates separated by a gap $d$ form a capacitor with capacitance:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

- An applied force reduces $d$, increasing $C$. The change in capacitance is measured by an oscillator or charge-discharge circuit.
- **Advantage:** Very low power consumption, high temperature stability, immune to noise.

## 2.2 The Wheatstone Bridge — The Heart of Strain-Gauge Signal Conditioning

A single strain gauge produces a resistance change of less than 1% — too small to be useful. The **Wheatstone bridge** amplifies this tiny change into a measurable voltage.

For a single active gauge (quarter-bridge configuration):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} - \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$$

When one resistor changes by $\Delta R$ (the active strain gauge), the output simplifies to:

$$V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

For a **full-bridge** with four active gauges (two in tension, two in compression):

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why full-bridge is preferred in robotics:** It provides **4× higher sensitivity**, **automatic temperature compensation**, and **cancellation of common-mode noise** — essential for industrial environments.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Description | Typical Units |
|---|-------------------|--------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's 2nd Law | Force equals mass times acceleration | N |
| 2 | $W = m \cdot g$ | Weight | Gravitational force on mass $m$ | N |
| 3 | $\sigma = F / A$ | Mechanical Stress | Force per unit cross-sectional area | Pa or N/m² |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Strain | Fractional deformation | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law | $E$ is Young's Modulus | Pa |
| 6 | $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor | Strain gauge sensitivity | ~2.0 metallic |
| 7 | $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Resistance change | From applied strain | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | Full-bridge output | Best strain gauge config | V |
| 9 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Piezoelectric charge | Direct piezoelectric effect | C |
| 10 | $V = Q / C$ | Output voltage | From charge on capacitance | V |
| 11 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Capacitance | Parallel plate formula | F |
| 12 | $S = V_{out} / F$ | Sensor Sensitivity | Output per unit force | V/N |
| 13 | $Hysteresis\,(\%) = \dfrac{F_{load} - F_{unload}}{F_{FS}} \times 100$ | Hysteresis error | Repeatability error | % |
| 14 | $Nonlinearity\,(\%) = \dfrac{V_{actual} - V_{ideal}}{V_{FS}} \times 100$ | Linearity error | Deviation from best-fit line | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **KRITICAL KTU Notation Rule:** In the formulas above, I have used \vert and \mid to avoid breaking the markdown table. In your exam scripts, you may write them as standard $\vert x \vert$, but in **markdown tables** always use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid table-rendering errors.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Force Sensors

- **Robotic Grippers:** Detect slip and adjust gripping force to handle delicate objects (eggs, glass).
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci System):** Provide haptic feedback to surgeons.
- **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas):** Maintain balance by sensing ground reaction forces.
- **Automated Assembly:** Verify correct insertion force of components.
- **Prosthetics:** Modern bionic hands use force sensors to modulate grip strength.
- **Industrial Weighing:** Checkweighers in packaging lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Output Voltage of a Quarter-Bridge Strain Gauge Circuit

**Given:** A Wheatstone bridge with three fixed resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ and one active strain gauge $R_4 = R + \Delta R$. Excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across the bridge.

**Find:** The differential output voltage $V_{out}$ in terms of $\Delta R / R$.

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the bridge

The bridge is two parallel voltage dividers. The output voltage is the difference between the midpoints:

**Left branch voltage** (across $R_2$ from the top node to ground):

$$V_a = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Right branch voltage** (across $R_3$ from the top node to ground):

$$V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

### Step 2: Write the differential output

$$V_{out} = V_a - V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

### Step 3: Apply the balanced-bridge condition

For an initially balanced bridge, $V_{out} = 0$, so:

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

This is satisfied when $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_2 = R_4$. We choose all nominal resistances equal: $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$. Then $R_4 \rightarrow R + \Delta R$.

### Step 4: Substitute $R_4 = R + \Delta R$ into the expression

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R}{R + R} - \frac{R}{R + (R + \Delta R)} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} \right)$$

### Step 5: Combine the fractions inside the parentheses

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{(2R + \Delta R) - 2R}{2 \cdot (2R + \Delta R)} \right)$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R} \right)$$

### Step 6: Apply the small-signal approximation $\Delta R \ll 2R$

Since $\Delta R$ is typically less than 1% of $R$ in real strain gauges, the term $2\Delta R$ in the denominator is negligible:

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

### Step 7: Express in terms of strain using the Gauge Factor

Recall $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical sanity check:** With $V_{ex} = 5\,\text{V}$, $GF = 2.0$, and $\varepsilon = 1000\,\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-3}$, we get $V_{out} = 5/4 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5\,\text{mV}$. This tiny signal justifies why **amplification** (instrumentation amplifier) is essential.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Force-to-Voltage Conversion in a Cantilever-Beam Load Cell

A common force-sensor geometry is a **cantilever beam** fixed at one end, with the force $F$ applied at the free end. Four strain gauges (two on top, two on the bottom) form a full bridge.

### Step 1: Determine the surface strain at the gauge location

For a cantilever of length $L$, width $b$, thickness $h$, with force $F$ at the free end, the bending moment at distance $x$ from the free end is $M(x) = F \cdot x$. The surface strain at the top (or bottom) fiber is:

$$\varepsilon(x) = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot x}{E \cdot b \cdot h^2}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus of the beam material (e.g., $E_{steel} = 200\,\text{GPa}$, $E_{aluminum} = 69\,\text{GPa}$).

### Step 2: Output voltage of the full bridge

Using the full-bridge formula derived earlier:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot x}{E \cdot b \cdot h^2}$$

### Step 3: Solve for force $F$ (the inverse calibration equation)

$$\boxed{F = \frac{V_{out} \cdot E \cdot b \cdot h^2}{V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot 6 \cdot x}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in robotics:** Once the geometry ($b, h, L, x$) and material ($E$) are fixed, the only unknown in the linear equation is $F$. The robot's microcontroller can compute $F$ in real-time by measuring $V_{out}$ — this is the basis of every digital force gauge.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Piezoelectric Sensor Output Voltage

**Given:** A PZT disc with $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C/N}$ is used as a force sensor. A force $F = 50\,\text{N}$ is applied. The disc has capacitance $C = 1\,\text{nF}$.

### Step 1: Calculate the generated charge

Using $Q = d \cdot F$:

$$Q = 400 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C/N} \times 50\,\text{N} = 20 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{C} = 20\,\text{nC}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the open-circuit output voltage

Using $V = Q / C$:

$$V = \frac{20 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{C}}{1 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{F}} = 20\,\text{V}$$

### Step 3: Verify with a different applied force ($F = 10\,\text{N}$)

$$Q = 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 10 = 4\,\text{nC}, \quad V = 4\,\text{nC} / 1\,\text{nF} = 4\,\text{V}$$

**Conclusion:** The output scales linearly with force (assuming constant $d$ and $C$), confirming the linear transfer function $V = (d/C) \cdot F$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Force Sensor Technologies

| Parameter | Strain Gauge | Piezoelectric | Capacitive |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|------------|
| **Operating Principle** | Resistance change with strain | Charge generation under stress | Capacitance change with gap |
| **Static (DC) Force** | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (charge leaks) | ✅ Yes |
| **Dynamic Force** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Excellent (high bandwidth) | ✅ Yes |
| **Sensitivity** | $1-10\,\text{mV/V}$ (full bridge) | $10-50\,\text{pC/N}$ | $0.1-1\,\text{pF/N}$ |
| **Frequency Response** | DC to $\sim 10\,\text{kHz}$ | $0.1\,\text{Hz}$ to $100\,\text{kHz}$ | DC to $\sim 1\,\text{kHz}$ |
| **Temperature Stability** | Good (with compensation) | Excellent (quartz) | Very Good |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium-High | Medium |
| **Hysteresis** | $< 0.1\%$ | Negligible | $< 0.5\%$ |
| **Typical Robotics Use** | Wrist F/T sensors, load cells | Impact detection, tactile skin | Gripper fingertips |
| **Power Consumption** | Moderate (excitation needed) | Very Low | Low |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define a force sensor. List any four types of force sensors used in robotic applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A force sensor is a transducer that converts an applied mechanical force into a measurable electrical signal (voltage, current, or charge) whose magnitude is proportional to the applied force. It enables robots to perceive contact and interaction forces.

**Four types of force sensors used in robotics:**
1. **Strain-Gauge based Force Sensor** — Uses change in resistance of a bonded foil gauge; most common in 6-axis wrist sensors.
2. **Piezoelectric Force Sensor** — Generates charge under stress; ideal for dynamic impact and vibration measurement.
3. **Capacitive Force Sensor** — Detects capacitance change due to gap variation; used in tactile fingertips.
4. **Force Sensing Resistor (FSR)** — Polymer-based device whose resistance decreases with applied force; low-cost tactile sensing.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]
**Explain the working principle of a piezoelectric force sensor. State one major limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:** A piezoelectric force sensor operates on the **direct piezoelectric effect**, in which certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., Quartz, PZT — Lead Zirconate Titanate, PVDF polymer) generate an electric charge on their surfaces when subjected to mechanical stress. The generated charge is given by $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient and $F$ is the applied force. This charge is converted to a voltage using a charge amplifier.

**One Major Limitation:** Piezoelectric sensors **cannot measure static (DC) forces** accurately, because the generated charge leaks away through the sensor's internal resistance and external circuitry over time. They are suited only for **dynamic force measurements** (vibrations, impacts, rapidly changing forces).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **Wheatstone bridge** used in a strain-gauge based force sensor. Derive the expression for the output voltage when a single strain gauge undergoes a resistance change of $\Delta R$. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit has $V_{ex} = 10\,\text{V}$, nominal resistance $R = 350\,\Omega$, gauge factor $GF = 2.05$, and a strain of $\varepsilon = 800\,\mu\varepsilon$. Calculate (i) the change in resistance $\Delta R$, and (ii) the output voltage $V_{out}$. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Circuit Description:** A Wheatstone bridge consists of four resistive arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ arranged in a diamond shape. An excitation voltage $V_{ex}$ is applied across the top and bottom nodes (A and D). The output voltage $V_{out}$ is measured differentially between the two midpoints (B and C).

In a quarter-bridge configuration for force measurement, $R_1, R_2, R_3$ are fixed precision resistors, and $R_4$ is the active strain gauge bonded to the elastic element. When force $F$ is applied, the elastic element deforms, the gauge stretches or compresses, and its resistance changes by $\Delta R$.

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

Apply KVL to the left branch (containing $R_1$ and $R_2$):

$$V_a = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Apply KVL to the right branch (containing $R_4$ and $R_3$):

$$V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4}$$

Differential output:

$$V_{out} = V_a - V_b = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_3}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

For a balanced bridge, $R_1 = R_3 = R$ and $R_2 = R_4 = R$. Substituting $R_4 = R + \Delta R$:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{R}{2R} - \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} \right) = V_{ex} \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} - \frac{R}{2R + \Delta R} \right)$$

Simplifying the expression:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R}$$

For small $\Delta R$ (i.e., $\Delta R \ll 2R$), the term $2\Delta R$ in the denominator is negligible:

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}}$$

Substituting $\Delta R / R = GF \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Excitation voltage: $V_{ex} = 10\,\text{V}$
- Nominal gauge resistance: $R = 350\,\Omega$
- Gauge factor: $GF = 2.05$
- Applied strain: $\varepsilon = 800\,\mu\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-4}$

**(i) Calculation of $\Delta R$:**

Using the gauge factor definition $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$:

$$\Delta R = R \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 2.05 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta R = 0.574\,\Omega}$$

**(ii) Calculation of $V_{out}$:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{10}{4} \times 2.05 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$V_{out} = 2.5 \times 2.05 \times 8 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 4.1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} = 4.1\,\text{mV}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The output is only 4.1 mV — a very weak signal. In a real robot, this would be amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620 with gain 100) to yield a usable 0.41 V signal for the ADC.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain the **direct piezoelectric effect** with a suitable diagram. Derive the expression for the output voltage of a piezoelectric force sensor connected to a charge amplifier. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A piezoelectric force sensor uses a PZT crystal with $d_{33} = 450 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C/N}$ and electrode capacitance $C = 500\,\text{pF}$. A force of $F = 25\,\text{N}$ is applied. Calculate (i) the charge generated, (ii) the open-circuit output voltage, and (iii) the output voltage if a charge amplifier with feedback capacitance $C_f = 10\,\text{nF}$ is used. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**The Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** When certain anisotropic crystals (those lacking a center of symmetry, such as Quartz, Rochelle Salt, Tourmaline, PZT, and PVDF polymer) are subjected to mechanical stress, the positive and negative charge centers within their unit cells are displaced, producing a net surface charge on the crystal faces. This phenomenon was discovered by **Jacques and Pierre Curie in 1880**.

**Governing Equation:**

$$Q = d \cdot F$$

where $Q$ is the generated charge (C), $F$ is the applied force (N), and $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N). The coefficient has subscripts (e.g., $d_{33}$) indicating the direction of force relative to the crystal's polarization axis.

**Charge Amplifier Configuration:** A piezoelectric sensor cannot directly drive a load because the charge leaks away. A **charge amplifier** (an op-amp with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ and feedback resistor $R_f$) converts the charge into a stable output voltage.

The op-amp virtual ground forces all charge from the piezo element to flow through $C_f$. By the property of the inverting amplifier:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{d \cdot F}{C_f}$$

**Key Advantages of the charge amplifier:**
- Output is independent of cable capacitance (which is a major problem with voltage-mode piezo amplifiers).
- The gain is set precisely by a single component $C_f$.
- The high-pass cutoff is $f_c = 1 / (2\pi R_f C_f)$, which can be tuned for the application.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given Data:**
- Piezo coefficient: $d_{33} = 450 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C/N}$
- Sensor capacitance: $C = 500\,\text{pF} = 500 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F}$
- Applied force: $F = 25\,\text{N}$
- Feedback capacitance: $C_f = 10\,\text{nF} = 10 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{F}$

**(i) Charge Generated:**

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F = 450 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 = 11.25 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{C}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 11.25\,\text{nC}}$$

**(ii) Open-Circuit Output Voltage:**

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{11.25 \times 10^{-9}}{500 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{11.25 \times 10^{-9}}{0.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 22.5\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = 22.5\,\text{V}}$$

**(iii) Output Voltage with Charge Amplifier:**

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q}{C_f} = -\frac{11.25 \times 10^{-9}}{10 \times 10^{-9}} = -1.125\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{\vert V_{out} \vert = 1.125\,\text{V}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The charge amplifier dramatically reduces the output voltage (from 22.5 V to 1.125 V), but provides a **stable, low-impedance signal** that is **independent of cable capacitance** — a critical feature in industrial robotics where sensor cables can be several meters long.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing stress and strain** — Stress $\sigma$ has units of Pa (N/m²); strain $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless. Students frequently mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 4 in quarter-bridge** — Writing $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$ (full-bridge formula) when the question clearly states a **quarter-bridge**. Always read the configuration carefully.
> 3. **Unit errors in strain** — Writing $\varepsilon = 800$ instead of $800\,\mu\varepsilon = 800 \times 10^{-6}$ leads to a nonsensical answer. The prefix $\mu$ (micro) means $10^{-6}$.
> 4. **Stating that piezoelectric sensors can measure static force** — This is the **most common conceptual error**. The charge leaks away, so DC measurement is impossible.
> 5. **Not drawing the bridge circuit in (a) part** — A circuit diagram is **mandatory** for full marks. Even a hand-drawn bridge with $V_{ex}$, four resistors, and $V_{out}$ labels will earn 2 of the 7 marks.
> 6. **Skipping the balanced-bridge assumption in derivations** — Always state: "For a balanced bridge, $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$" before substituting.
> 7. **Sign errors in the output voltage** — When the gauge is in compression, $\Delta R$ is negative, and $V_{out}$ changes polarity. Mention this for a full-mark answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **force sensor** converts mechanical force into an electrical signal; in robotics, it provides haptic feedback for safe and dexterous manipulation.
- The **SI unit of force** is the **Newton (N)**, with weight $W = mg$ where $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.
- The three dominant force-transduction principles are: **piezoelectric** (charge), **piezoresistive / strain gauge** (resistance), and **capacitive** (capacitance).
- The **Gauge Factor (GF)** of a strain gauge is $GF = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$, with $GF \approx 2.0$ for metallic foil and up to $200$ for semiconductors.
- The **Wheatstone bridge** is the standard signal-conditioning circuit. Quarter-bridge gives $V_{out} = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot GF \cdot \varepsilon$; full-bridge gives 4× more output.
- The **direct piezoelectric effect** is $Q = d \cdot F$; sensors based on this **cannot measure static forces**.
- **Cantilever beam** load cells place strain gauges at the point of maximum surface strain, where $\varepsilon = 6Fx / (Ebh^2)$.
- **Key performance parameters** to remember: sensitivity (V/N), nonlinearity (%), hysteresis (%), bandwidth (Hz), and creep.
- In **robotic applications**, force sensors are used in grippers (slip detection), wrists (6-axis F/T sensors), legs (ground reaction force for balance), and surgical tools (haptic feedback).
- The **charge amplifier** equation is $V_{out} = -Q / C_f = -d \cdot F / C_f$, and it eliminates cable-capacitance effects.
- **Temperature compensation** in a full-bridge is automatic because all four gauges see the same temperature change, and the common-mode resistance shift cancels out.
- **KTU 2024 hot topics**: derivation of bridge output, piezoelectric numerical problems, comparison of force-sensor types, and block diagram of a force-sensor signal chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Piezoelectric Sensor** is a solid-state, electromechanical transducer that operates on the **direct piezoelectric effect** to convert applied mechanical energy (force, pressure, acceleration, or strain) into a measurable electrical signal (charge or voltage). The word *piezoelectric* is derived from the Greek word *piezein*, meaning "to press or squeeze."

Mathematically, the direct piezoelectric effect states that the electric displacement (or charge density) generated within certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials is linearly proportional to the applied mechanical stress:

$$D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon \cdot E$$

where $D$ is the electric displacement (C/m²), $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N), $\sigma$ is the applied mechanical stress (N/m²), $\varepsilon$ is the permittivity, and $E$ is the electric field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The piezoelectric effect is *reversible* — the **converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect** is utilized in piezoelectric *actuators* and ultrasonic transducers, where an applied electric field causes mechanical deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** Only **non-centrosymmetric** crystals (those lacking a center of symmetry in their lattice) exhibit the piezoelectric effect. Common examples include **Quartz (SiO₂)**, **Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT)**, **Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃)**, and **Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine squeezing a **balloon filled with static electricity**. When you deform it, charges separate on its inner and outer surfaces — if you connect a wire, current flows. The piezoelectric crystal behaves similarly, but on an atomic scale:

- Inside the crystal, positively and negatively charged ions are arranged in a **symmetric unit cell** (no net charge at rest).
- When you apply **mechanical pressure**, this symmetry is disturbed.
- The charge centers shift, generating a **net dipole moment** and surface charges.
- These surface charges can be tapped via electrodes and measured as a **voltage** or **current**.

> [!TIP]
> **Everyday Examples:**
> - The *click* mechanism of a **gas lighter** uses a piezoelectric crystal to generate a ~10 kV spark.
> - **Quartz watches** use the inverse effect: an oscillating electric field keeps a quartz crystal vibrating at 32,768 Hz for precise timekeeping.
> - **Touchscreens and microphones** in smartphones rely on piezoelectric transducers.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Material Properties

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Material Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Piezoelectric charge coefficient | $d_{33}$ | **2.3 pC/N** (Quartz), **400–600 pC/N** (PZT) | C/N |
| Piezoelectric voltage coefficient | $g_{33}$ | **0.058 V·m/N** (Quartz), **0.025 V·m/N** (PZT) | V·m/N |
| Dielectric constant (relative) | $\varepsilon_r$ | **4.5** (Quartz), **1200–3500** (PZT) | dimensionless |
| Curie Temperature | $T_C$ | **573°C** (Quartz), **300–400°C** (PZT) | °C |
| Density | $\rho$ | **2650 kg/m³** (Quartz) | kg/m³ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Operating Constraint:** Operating the piezoelectric sensor **above its Curie Temperature** permanently destroys its piezoelectric properties (depolarization). This is a frequent KTU question pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The piezoelectric sensor operates through a chain of physical phenomena, summarized below:

- **Step 1 — Mechanical Input Reception:** An external force $F$ is applied to the crystal surface (perpendicular or parallel to the poling axis), producing stress $\sigma = F/A$ where $A$ is the electrode area.
- **Step 2 — Crystal Lattice Distortion:** The non-centrosymmetric crystal lattice is deformed. Positive and negative charge centers displace relative to each other.
- **Step 3 — Net Dipole Generation:** This displacement creates **electric dipoles** in the unit cells, aligned with the direction of the applied stress.
- **Step 4 — Surface Charge Accumulation:** Bound charges of opposite polarity appear on opposing crystal faces. The total generated charge is:

$$Q = d_{ij} \cdot F$$

where $d_{ij}$ is the appropriate piezoelectric coefficient (e.g., $d_{33}$ for axial loading, $d_{31}$ for transverse loading).

- **Step 5 — Charge Measurement:** The accumulated charge is converted to a voltage via either the **sensor's own capacitance** (voltage mode) or an **external charge amplifier** (charge mode).
- **Step 6 — Signal Conditioning:** Amplification, filtering, and digitization occur in the signal conditioning circuitry.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below compiles every critical equation required for Module 2 piezoelectric sensor numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = d \cdot F$ | Generated charge vs applied force | Coulombs (C) | Direct piezoelectric effect |
| 2 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{Q}{C_p} = \dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_p}$ | Open-circuit output voltage | Volts (V) | Voltage-mode readout |
| 3 | $C_p = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{t}$ | Sensor capacitance | Farads (F) | Determines V sensitivity |
| 4 | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{Q}{C_f} = -\dfrac{d \cdot F}{C_f}$ | Charge amplifier output | Volts (V) | Charge-mode readout |
| 5 | $S_V = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{F} = \dfrac{d}{C_p}$ | Voltage sensitivity | V/N | Sensor specification |
| 6 | $S_Q = \dfrac{Q}{F} = d$ | Charge sensitivity | C/N | Sensor specification |
| 7 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k_e}{m}}$ | Resonant frequency | Hz | Frequency response limit |
| 8 | $g = \dfrac{d}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r}$ | Piezoelectric voltage coefficient | V·m/N | Material property |
| 9 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{Y} + d \cdot E_e$ | Strain-Stress-Electric coupling | Pa | Full constitutive model |
| 10 | $\tau = R \cdot C_p$ | Electrical time constant | seconds (s) | Low-frequency cutoff |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memorization Tip:** Always check whether the problem uses **voltage mode** ($V = Q/C_p$) or **charge mode** ($V_{out} = -Q/C_f$). The feedback capacitor $C_f$ in a charge amplifier is **chosen by the designer**, not by the sensor.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **robotics**, piezoelectric sensors are used as:

- **Force/Torque Sensors** at robot wrists, fingertips, and joint actuators for compliant manipulation and safe human-robot interaction.
- **Acoustic Emission Sensors** for structural health monitoring of robotic limbs.
- **Vibration Sensors** for active vibration damping in precision robotics (e.g., surgical robots).
- **Ultrasonic Range Finders** for obstacle detection and SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping).
- **Energy Harvesters** powering wireless sensor nodes in IoT-enabled robotic swarms.

> [!TIP]
> The same crystal that *senses* force (direct effect) can also *actuate* motion (inverse effect). This dual nature makes piezoelectric devices uniquely useful in **sensors-actuators integrated systems** — a frequent KTU design question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Charge Generation Under Axial Force

Consider a piezoelectric disk of thickness $t$ and electrode area $A$, polarized along the thickness (the "3" axis in IEEE notation). A force $F$ is applied along this axis, producing stress $\sigma_3 = F/A$.

**Step 1.** The constitutive piezoelectric relation for the "33" mode is:

$$D_3 = d_{33} \cdot \sigma_3 + \varepsilon_{33}^T \cdot E_3$$

**Step 2.** Under open-circuit conditions (electrodes isolated), the electric displacement $D_3$ produces a surface charge density. Integrating over the electrode area:

$$Q = \int D_3 \cdot dA = d_{33} \cdot \sigma_3 \cdot A + \varepsilon_{33}^T \cdot E_3 \cdot A$$

**Step 3.** For charge measurement, we typically set $E_3 = 0$ (no applied field), so:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot \sigma_3 \cdot A = d_{33} \cdot \frac{F}{A} \cdot A$$

**Step 4.** Simplifying:

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

> **Logic Note:** The electrode area cancels because stress (force per unit area) multiplied by area yields force. Thus the **charge output depends only on the piezoelectric coefficient and the force**, not on the sensor's geometry. This is a powerful design insight.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Open-Circuit Output Voltage

**Step 1.** The sensor itself behaves as a parallel-plate capacitor with capacitance:

$$C_p = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{t}$$

**Step 2.** The accumulated charge $Q$ sits on this capacitor, generating a voltage:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p}$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$ and $C_p$:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot F \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}$$

**Step 4.** Recognizing the stress $\sigma_3 = F/A$:

$$V_{oc} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \sigma_3 = g_{33} \cdot F \cdot \frac{t}{A}$$

> **Logic Note:** $V_{oc}$ scales linearly with sensor thickness $t$ — a thicker crystal produces more voltage for the same force, at the cost of reduced capacitance (and hence more susceptible to noise).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Charge Amplifier Transfer Function

A **charge amplifier** uses an operational amplifier with a feedback capacitor $C_f$ and feedback resistor $R_f$.

**Step 1.** The sensor is modeled as a charge source $Q_s$ in parallel with its internal capacitance $C_p$ and resistance $R_p$. The non-inverting input is grounded; the inverting input is a **virtual ground** ($V_- = 0$).

**Step 2.** Apply KCL at the inverting node: the current from the sensor equals the current through $C_f$ and $R_f$:

$$i_s = i_{C_f} + i_{R_f}$$

**Step 3.** Since $V_- = 0$, the voltage across $C_f$ is $0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$. The current through $C_f$ is $C_f \cdot d(-V_{out})/dt$.

**Step 4.** In the **quasi-static regime** (slowly varying signals), assuming the op-amp draws no current:

$$Q_s = -C_f \cdot V_{out} - \frac{V_{out}}{R_f} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5.** For DC or low-frequency signals, the resistor's effect is negligible, and:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q_s}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}$$

> **Logic Note:** The charge amplifier output is **independent of the sensor's own capacitance** $C_p$. This is a major advantage: cable length and cable capacitance do not affect the output. This is why charge-mode piezos are preferred in industrial robotics.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Frequency Response of a Piezoelectric Sensor

A piezoelectric sensor can be modeled as a **mass-spring-damper system**. The fundamental resonant frequency is:

**Step 1.** The effective stiffness of the crystal under axial compression is $k_e = A \cdot Y / t$, where $Y$ is Young's modulus.

**Step 2.** The mass of the crystal (plus any seismic mass) is $m$.

**Step 3.** The natural frequency is:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_e}{m}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{A \cdot Y}{m \cdot t}}$$

**Step 4.** For a disk-shaped crystal of density $\rho$, $m = \rho \cdot A \cdot t$, so:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot t}\sqrt{\frac{Y}{\rho}} = \frac{v_s}{2\pi \cdot t}$$

where $v_s = \sqrt{Y/\rho}$ is the speed of sound in the crystal.

> **Logic Note:** Thinner crystals resonate at higher frequencies. For **dynamic measurements** (e.g., vibration sensing), the crystal should be sized so that $f_n$ lies well above the signal frequencies of interest, ensuring operation in the **flat-response region**.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Material Analysis

| Material | $d_{33}$ (pC/N) | $g_{33}$ (mV·m/N) | $\varepsilon_r$ | $T_C$ (°C) | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quartz | **2.3** | 58 | 4.5 | 573 | High-temp, high-stability |
| PZT-5A | 374 | 25 | 1700 | 350 | General force sensing |
| PZT-5H | 593 | 19 | 3400 | 195 | High-sensitivity |
| BaTiO₃ | 190 | 12 | 1700 | 120 | Cost-effective |
| PVDF | 33 | 380 | 12 | 80 | Flexible sensors, large area |
| LiNbO₃ | 6 | 23 | 28 | 1210 | SAW devices, optical |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "**P**iezoelectric **Q**uarz **L**oves **B**ig **P**ressure" → Quartz, Lithium Niobate, Barium Titanate, PZT, PVDF. The order roughly follows increasing sensitivity but decreasing temperature stability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Piezoelectric Sensor System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow architecture of a piezoelectric sensor integrated with signal conditioning electronics, as required for robotics applications.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Mechanical Input Force F] --> A1[Applied Stress on Piezo Crystal]
    A1 --> A2[Non-centrosymmetric Lattice Distortion]
    A2 --> A3[Net Dipole Formation and Surface Charge Q]
    A3 --> B1[Piezoelectric Equivalent Circuit]
    B1 --> B2{Signal Conditioning Mode}
    B2 -->|Voltage Mode| C1[High Impedance Voltage Amplifier]
    B2 -->|Charge Mode| C2[Operational Amplifier with Feedback Capacitor Cf]
    C1 --> D1[Band-Pass Filter]
    C2 --> D1
    D1 --> D2[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    D2 --> D3[Microcontroller or DSP Unit]
    D3 --> E1[Robot Controller for Force Feedback]
    D3 --> E2[Display and HMI Output]
    D3 --> E3[Data Logging and Cloud Telemetry]
    B1 --> P1[Self Capacitance Cp]
    B1 --> P2[Leakage Resistance Rp]
    B1 --> P3[Cable Capacitance Cc]
```

## 4.2 Piezoelectric Sensor Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TOP[Top Electrode] --> CRY[Piezoelectric Crystal Layer]
    CRY --> BOT[Bottom Electrode]
    CRY --> POL[Poling Direction Vertical 3 Axis]
    F[Applied Force F Vertical] --> CRY
    CRY --> QGEN[Surface Charge Generation Q plus and Q minus]
    QGEN --> WIRE[Output Wires to Amplifier]
    WIRE --> AMP[Charge or Voltage Amplifier]
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Model — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SRC[Charge Source Qs proportional to d times F] --> N1[Node A]
    CAP[Sensor Capacitance Cp] --> N1
    RES[Sensor Resistance Rp] --> N1
    N1 --> CBL[Cable Capacitance Cc]
    CBL --> N2[Node B Amplifier Input]
    N2 --> AMPOP[Operational Amplifier]
    CF[Feedback Capacitor Cf] --> N2
    RF[Feedback Resistor Rf] --> N2
    AMPOP --> OUT[Output Voltage Vout]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** Always label the **poling direction** (3-axis) in any cross-sectional diagram. Without poling, the crystal is symmetric and produces *no* charge — a common reason students lose marks in board exams.

## 4.4 Signal-Flow Summary Table

| Stage | Physical Phenomenon | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Input | Mechanical force $F$ | Stress $\sigma_3 = F/A$ |
| Transduction | Direct piezoelectric effect | Charge $Q = d_{33} F$ |
| Charge Storage | Sensor capacitance | Voltage $V = Q/C_p$ |
| Amplification | Op-amp with $C_f$ | Conditioned $V_{out}$ |
| Digitalization | ADC | Binary code |
| Application | Robot control loop | Force feedback action |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The following questions are designed per the **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation)** pattern: **Part A (3 marks)** for short answers, **Part B (14 marks)** with **internal choice** and **sub-parts (a) 7 marks, (b) 7 marks**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the piezoelectric effect. Distinguish between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects with one example application each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **piezoelectric effect** is the ability of certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials to convert mechanical energy into electrical energy and vice versa.

- **Direct Piezoelectric Effect:** Mechanical stress applied to the crystal produces an electric charge. Example: **Piezoelectric force sensor** in a robotic gripper.
- **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect:** An applied electric field causes mechanical deformation in the crystal. Example: **Piezoelectric buzzer** or ultrasonic transducer.

| Criterion | Direct Effect | Converse Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Conversion | Mechanical → Electrical | Electrical → Mechanical |
| Governing Coefficient | $d$ (charge coefficient) | $d$ (same, by reciprocity) |
| Robotic Application | Force/pressure sensing | Micro-actuation, ultrasound |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**List any three piezoelectric materials and state one characteristic property of each. Why is PZT widely preferred over quartz in commercial sensors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three Piezoelectric Materials:**

1. **Quartz (SiO₂):** High temperature stability (Curie temperature = 573°C) and excellent long-term stability. Used in precision oscillators.
2. **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate):** Very high piezoelectric charge coefficient ($d_{33}$ up to 600 pC/N). Used in commercial force and pressure sensors.
3. **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride):** A flexible polymer film, ideal for large-area and conformal sensing surfaces. Used in wearable robotic skin.

**Why PZT is preferred over Quartz in commercial sensors:**

PZT has a **piezoelectric charge coefficient ($d_{33}$) roughly 150–250 times greater than quartz** (≈400 pC/N vs ≈2.3 pC/N). This means PZT produces a much larger electrical signal for the same applied force, allowing simpler readout electronics and better signal-to-noise ratio. The only trade-off is its lower Curie temperature (≈350°C), which restricts high-temperature use.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a piezoelectric sensor. Discuss the role of the poling process.

**(b)** Derive the expression for the output voltage of a piezoelectric sensor when connected to (i) a voltage amplifier, and (ii) a charge amplifier. State one advantage of the charge amplifier.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working Principle [7 marks]:**

A piezoelectric sensor consists of a **piezoelectric crystal** (typically PZT or quartz) sandwiched between two **metallic electrodes** (usually silver or nickel). Lead wires connect the electrodes to the external signal conditioning circuit.

**Constructional Details:**

- **Crystal Element:** Poled piezoelectric ceramic (PZT) or natural crystal (quartz), shaped as a disk, bar, or bimorph.
- **Electrodes:** Thin metal films deposited on opposing faces perpendicular to the poling axis.
- **Housing:** Metallic case providing mechanical protection and electromagnetic shielding.
- **Output Cable:** Coaxial or shielded cable to minimize noise pickup and triboelectric effects.

**Working Principle:**

1. A mechanical force $F$ is applied to the sensor along the poling (3) direction.
2. The applied stress distorts the non-centrosymmetric crystal lattice, displacing charge centers.
3. Surface charges of opposite polarity accumulate on the electrodes. The generated charge is $Q = d_{33} \cdot F$.
4. The charge produces a voltage across the sensor capacitance: $V = Q / C_p$.
5. This voltage is amplified and processed for force/pressure measurement.

**Role of Poling:**

Poling is the process of **applying a strong DC electric field (typically 2–5 kV/mm) at elevated temperature** to align the randomly oriented ferroelectric domains within the crystal. After cooling with the field applied, the domains remain aligned, giving the crystal a permanent net dipole moment. **Without poling, the crystal would be isotropic and produce no net piezoelectric response.**

---

**(b) Output Voltage Derivation [7 marks]:**

**(i) Voltage Amplifier Configuration:**

The piezoelectric sensor is modeled as a charge source $Q$ in parallel with its own capacitance $C_p$ and leakage resistance $R_p$. When connected to a high-input-impedance voltage amplifier, the input voltage is:

$$V_{in} = \frac{Q}{C_p + C_c + C_{in}}$$

where $C_c$ is the cable capacitance and $C_{in}$ is the amplifier input capacitance. For an ideal voltage amplifier with $C_{in} \to \infty$, the output is:

$$V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}\right) \cdot V_{in}$$

In voltage mode, the output is **highly sensitive to cable capacitance and length**, which is a key disadvantage.

**(ii) Charge Amplifier Configuration:**

Using an op-amp with the sensor connected to the inverting input (virtual ground), applying KCL at the inverting node:

$$i_{sensor} = i_{C_f} + i_{R_f}$$

For quasi-static signals:

$$Q_s = -C_f \cdot V_{out}$$

Solving for the output voltage:

$$V_{out} = -\frac{Q_s}{C_f} = -\frac{d_{33} \cdot F}{C_f}$$

> **Advantage of Charge Amplifier:** The output voltage $V_{out}$ depends **only on the feedback capacitor $C_f$**, not on the sensor's own capacitance $C_p$, the cable capacitance $C_c$, or the cable length. This makes the system **immune to cable noise and length variations**, which is critical in industrial robotic installations where sensors may be several meters from the controller.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A piezoelectric sensor has the following specifications:
- Piezoelectric coefficient: $d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- Sensor capacitance: $C_p = 1.5$ nF
- Electrode area: $A = 100$ mm²
- Thickness: $t = 2$ mm
- Dielectric constant (relative): $\varepsilon_r = 1200$

Calculate:
(i) The charge generated when a force of 80 N is applied.
(ii) The open-circuit output voltage.
(iii) The voltage sensitivity in V/N.
(iv) The relative permittivity in terms of the free-space permittivity $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m.

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a piezoelectric accelerometer used in robotics. Discuss its frequency response and two key applications.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Numerical Problem [7 marks]:**

**Given:**
- $d_{33} = 250 \times 10^{-12}$ C/N
- $C_p = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ F
- $A = 100 \text{ mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$
- $t = 2 \text{ mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $\varepsilon_r = 1200$
- $F = 80$ N
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m

**(i) Charge Generated [2 marks]:**

$$Q = d_{33} \cdot F$$

$$Q = 250 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C/N} \times 80 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C} = 20 \text{ nC}}$$

**(ii) Open-Circuit Output Voltage [2 marks]:**

$$V_{oc} = \frac{Q}{C_p}$$

$$V_{oc} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C}}{1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} \approx 13.33 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) Voltage Sensitivity [2 marks]:**

$$S_V = \frac{V_{oc}}{F} = \frac{13.33 \text{ V}}{80 \text{ N}}$$

$$\boxed{S_V \approx 0.1667 \text{ V/N}}$$

(Alternatively, $S_V = d_{33}/C_p = 250 \times 10^{-12} / 1.5 \times 10^{-9} = 0.1667$ V/N — **same result**, as expected.)

**(iv) Verification of Permittivity [1 mark]:**

From $C_p = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{t}$:

$$C_p = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 1200 \times (100 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_p = \frac{1.06248 \times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.31 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F} \approx 0.531 \text{ nF}$$

> **Note:** The calculated value (0.531 nF) differs from the given value (1.5 nF) because the **given $C_p$ includes contributions from cable capacitance, electrode fringe effects, and the input capacitance of the connected amplifier**. In practice, the manufacturer-specified $C_p$ is the total effective capacitance as seen at the cable output.

---

**(b) Piezoelectric Accelerometer [7 marks]:**

A piezoelectric accelerometer consists of:

- A **piezoelectric crystal** (PZT disk) sandwiched between a base and a **seismic mass**.
- A **preload bolt** that holds the assembly together under compression.
- A **hermetic housing** that protects the crystal from environmental contamination.
- **Output electrodes** connected to a built-in amplifier (in IEPE / ICP type sensors).

**Working Principle:**

When the accelerometer experiences acceleration $a$, the seismic mass $m$ exerts an inertial force $F = m \cdot a$ on the piezoelectric crystal. This force generates a charge $Q = d_{33} \cdot F = d_{33} \cdot m \cdot a$, which is proportional to the acceleration. The charge is converted to a voltage via a built-in charge or voltage amplifier.

**Frequency Response:**

The accelerometer has two key frequency limits:

- **Lower cutoff** $f_L = 1/(2\pi R C_p)$: Set by the electrical time constant of the crystal and load resistance. Below $f_L$, the signal drops at 20 dB/decade.
- **Upper cutoff / Resonant frequency** $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k_e/m}$: Set by the mechanical resonance of the seismic mass and crystal stiffness. Above $f_n$, the output peaks sharply and becomes unusable.

The **useful operating range** is typically from $f_L$ to about $0.3 \cdot f_n$ (flat region, ±5%).

**Two Key Applications in Robotics:**

1. **Vibration Monitoring of Robot Joints and End-Effectors:** Used for predictive maintenance and active vibration damping in precision manipulators (e.g., surgical robots, CNC-based manufacturing cells).
2. **Inertial Measurement in Mobile Robots:** Combined with gyroscopes, piezoelectric accelerometers form **Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs)** used for balance control in humanoid robots and for localization in autonomous ground vehicles.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing Voltage Mode vs Charge Mode equations** — using $V = Q/C_p$ for a charge amplifier loses full 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm² to m²** in numerical problems — a unit mismatch is a common -1 mark deduction.
> 3. **Not mentioning poling** when asked about construction — a mandatory step in any piezoelectric sensor explanation.
> 4. **Stating "piezoelectric effect works in all materials"** — this is false; only non-centrosymmetric crystals exhibit it. (Full -2 marks.)
> 5. **Ignoring the Curie temperature** when discussing PZT — examiners specifically check for awareness of the depolarization limit.
> 6. **Using $C_p$ for the charge amplifier output** — the correct formula is $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$, with $C_f$ being the feedback capacitor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Pin this section to your study wall for last-minute revision.**

- ✅ The **piezoelectric effect** converts mechanical stress to charge via $Q = d \cdot F$; only **non-centrosymmetric** crystals exhibit it.
- ✅ Two principal effects: **Direct** (sensor mode) and **Converse / Inverse** (actuator mode). Both governed by the same coefficient $d$ (reciprocity).
- ✅ Key materials: **Quartz** (high stability), **PZT** (high sensitivity, common in robotics), **PVDF** (flexible polymer for large-area sensing).
- ✅ **Poling** aligns ferroelectric domains; without poling, the crystal has no net piezoelectric response.
- ✅ **Curie Temperature** is the depolarization limit; exceeding it permanently destroys piezoelectricity. PZT ≈ 350°C; Quartz ≈ 573°C.
- ✅ **Open-circuit voltage** is $V_{oc} = Q/C_p = d \cdot F / C_p$ — depends on the sensor's own capacitance.
- ✅ **Charge amplifier output** is $V_{out} = -Q/C_f$ — **independent of cable capacitance**, the preferred configuration in industrial robotics.
- ✅ **Voltage sensitivity** $S_V = d/C_p$ in V/N; **charge sensitivity** $S_Q = d$ in C/N.
- ✅ **Resonant frequency** $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k_e/m}$ sets the upper frequency limit; **electrical time constant** $\tau = R C_p$ sets the lower limit.
- ✅ In **robotics**: piezoelectric sensors are used for force/torque feedback, vibration sensing, ultrasonic ranging, IMUs, and energy harvesting.
- ✅ **d₃₃ mode** = force along the poling axis (most sensitive); **d₃₁ mode** = force perpendicular to the poling axis (used in bimorphs).
- ✅ **Memorize units:** $d$ in pC/N, $g$ in mV·m/N, $C_p$ in pF or nF, $V$ in V, $F$ in N.
- ✅ For numerical problems: **always check units first**, convert mm² to m² and mm to m, use $d_{33}$ in SI (C/N, not pC/N) before substituting.
- ✅ PZT sensitivity is **~150× higher** than quartz, but with lower temperature stability — the central trade-off in material selection.
- ✅ A **piezoelectric accelerometer** uses a seismic mass $m$ to convert acceleration $a$ into a force $F = m \cdot a$, then into a measurable charge.

> **End of Module 2 — Piezoelectric Sensor Notes (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tactile Sensor: Touch Sensor/Contact Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tactile Sensor: Touch Sensor/Contact Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Tactile Sensor** is a device that detects and measures physical contact information between a robotic end-effector (or any intelligent system) and objects in its environment. It transduces mechanical stimuli — such as **force (N)**, **pressure (Pa)**, vibration, or surface texture — into measurable electrical signals. Tactile sensing is a sub-domain of *contact-based proprioceptive/exteroceptive sensing* and forms the backbone of robotic manipulation, grasping, and human-robot interaction.

Within tactile sensing, the **Touch Sensor** (also called a **Contact Sensor** or **Binary Tactile Sensor**) is the simplest and most fundamental variant. It operates in a **binary (ON/OFF) modality**, producing a discrete signal indicating the *presence* or *absence* of physical contact at a given point. It does **not** quantify the magnitude of force; it merely registers whether the sensor element has been mechanically deformed beyond a predefined threshold.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between a *tactile sensor* (continuous, force-resolving) and a *touch/contact sensor* (binary, threshold-based) is a frequently tested concept. A contact sensor is essentially a **limit switch equivalent** in the robotic skin paradigm.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Human Fingertip" Model

Think of your own hand interacting with a hot surface. Before you even *feel the temperature or pressure*, your skin's mechanoreceptors first register a **simple touch** ("Did I make contact? Yes/No"). This is the function of a **contact sensor** — a *digital, yes/no event detector*.

Now, after contact is made, you start *feeling the pressure*, the *texture*, the *slip*, and the *force distribution*. This richer, *analog* information is what a full-fledged **tactile sensor array** provides. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy Summary:**
> - **Touch/Contact Sensor** = The instant your fingertip *touches* a table (binary event).
> - **Tactile Sensor** = The *feeling* of hardness, weight, and texture on your fingertip (continuous variable).
> - In robotics, both are essential — contact sensors trigger actions (e.g., grip closure), while tactile sensors refine them (e.g., apply just enough force to lift an egg without crushing it).

### 1.3 Classification Within Tactile Sensing

| Sub-class | Output Type | Information Conveyed | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Touch/Contact Sensor** | Binary (1-bit) | Presence of contact | Collision detection, grip initiation |
| **Force/Torque Sensor** | Multi-bit (analog) | Magnitude and direction of force | Assembly, weight measurement |
| **Tactile Array Sensor** | Spatial map (2D/3D) | Pressure distribution | Grasping delicate objects, texture recognition |
| **Slip Sensor** | Time-derivative signal | Relative motion at contact | Re-grasping, slip prevention |

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics

The following key performance indicators (KPIs) are universally used in KTU evaluation and industry datasheets:

- **Sensitivity (S):** The ratio of output signal change to input stimulus change (e.g., mV/N or %ΔR per N).
- **Spatial Resolution:** The minimum distance at which two distinct contact points can be resolved, often quoted in **mm** or **dots per inch (DPI)**.
- **Hysteresis (H):** The maximum difference in output between the loading and unloading curves at the same applied force, typically expressed as a **percentage of Full Scale (%FS)**.
- **Dynamic Range:** The ratio between the largest and smallest measurable stimulus (often in **dB** or **logarithmic units**).
- **Response Time (τ):** Time taken for the sensor to reach ~63.2% of its final output, measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Force Range:** Operating range expressed in **Newtons (N)** or **Pascals (Pa)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:** A *contact sensor* has very high sensitivity at a single threshold and a binary output, whereas a *tactile sensor array* sacrifices some single-point sensitivity in exchange for **spatial resolution** and **force magnitude** measurement.
