This is why photoresistors use materials with **trap states** (impurities) that artificially extend $\tau$ from nanoseconds to milliseconds. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Photocurrent Through a Photoresistor **Problem Statement:** Find the photocurrent $I_{ph}$ flowing through a photoresistor with dimensions $L \times W \times d$ under illumination. ### Step 1 — Recall the Drift Current Equation The current density in a semiconductor is: $$J = \sigma E = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p) E$$ ### Step 2 — Add Photogenerated Carriers The total current density under illumination becomes: $$J_{total} = q[(n_0 + \Delta n)\mu_n + (p_0 + \Delta p)\mu_p] E$$ ### Step 3 — Subtract Dark Current The photocurrent density is the difference: $$J_{ph} = q(\Delta n \mu_n + \Delta p \mu_p) E$$ ### Step 4 — Convert to Total Current Multiply by the cross-sectional area $A = W \cdot d$: $$I_{ph} = J_{ph} \cdot A = q(\Delta n \mu_n + \Delta p \mu_p) E \cdot A$$ ### Step 5 — Apply Ohm's Law Since $E = V/L$, where $V$ is the applied bias: $$I_{ph} = q(\Delta n \mu_n + \Delta p \mu_p) \frac{V \cdot A}{L}$$ ### Step 6 — Substitute $\Delta n = \eta \Phi \alpha \tau$ $$\boxed{I_{ph} = q \eta \Phi \alpha \tau (\mu_n + \mu_p) \frac{V \cdot A}{L}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Notice the **linear dependence on applied bias voltage $V$**. This is unique to photoconductive devices (unlike photovoltaic devices like solar cells, which have $I_{ph}$ independent of $V$). This allows photoresistors to be used in high-gain applications. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem: LDR Voltage Divider **Given:** - Supply voltage $V_{cc} = 5$ V - Fixed resistor $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ - LDR resistance: $R_{LDR} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$ in dark, $500 \text{ }\Omega$ in bright light - Output is taken across $R_L$ **Find:** $V_{out}$ in both conditions. ### Step 1 — Dark Condition ($R_{LDR} = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$) Using the voltage divider rule: $$V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{LDR}}$$ $$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 50{,}000} = 5 \cdot \frac{10}{60} = 0.833 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 2 — Bright Condition ($R_{LDR} = 500 \text{ }\Omega$) $$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000 + 500} = 5 \cdot \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}500} = 4.762 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 3 — Interpretation - **Dark → Output ≈ 0.83 V** (Logic LOW) - **Bright → Output ≈ 4.76 V** (Logic HIGH) > [!TIP] > This is the **fundamental "Light-Activated Switch"** used in automatic streetlights and night lamps — a guaranteed question in KTU exams! # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Physical Construction Schematic (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph substrate["CERAMIC SUBSTRATE (Base)"] direction LR contact1["INTERDIGITATED ELECTRODE 1
Tin Oxide or Gold Film"] contact2["INTERDIGITATED ELECTRODE 2
Tin Oxide or Gold Film"] active["CADMIUM SULFIDE ACTIVE FILM
Zigzag Pattern for Max Area
Eg = 2.42 eV"] end photon1["INCIDENT PHOTON
Energy greater than Eg"] photon2["GENERATED ELECTRON-HOLE PAIR
EHP"] photon3["CHARGE COLLECTION
at Electrodes"] substrate --> photon1 photon1 --> photon2 photon2 --> photon3 ``` ## 4.2 Working Principle Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["STEP 1
PHOTON INCIDENCE
on LDR Surface"] --> B["STEP 2
ENERGY COMPARISON
Eph greater than Eg?"] B -- YES --> C["STEP 3
ELECTRON-HOLE PAIR
Generation in CdS"] B -- NO --> D["PHOTON TRANSMITTED
No Effect"] C --> E["STEP 4
CARRIER MOBILITY
Deltan increases"] E --> F["STEP 5
CONDUCTIVITY RISE
Delta sigma increases"] F --> G["STEP 6
RESISTANCE DROPS
R decreases"] G --> H["STEP 7
OUTPUT SIGNAL
Voltage or Current Change"] ``` ## 4.3 Voltage Divider Circuit Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD VCC["Vcc = 5V DC Supply"] --> R1["FIXED RESISTOR RL
10 kohm"] R1 --> NodeA["JUNCTION NODE
Vout Point"] NodeA --> LDR["LDR / PHOTORESISTOR
R varies with light"] LDR --> GND1["GROUND 0V"] NodeA --> Output["Vout TO ADC or
Comparator Input"] subgraph LightResponse["LIGHT RESPONSE BEHAVIOR"] direction LR DARK["DARK CONDITION
R-LDR = 50 kohm
Vout = 0.83V LOW"] BRIGHT["BRIGHT CONDITION
R-LDR = 500 ohm
Vout = 4.76V HIGH"] end ``` ## 4.4 Photoresistor vs Other Light Sensors (Comparison) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph LIGHTSENSORS["PHOTONIC SENSORS CLASSIFICATION"] direction TB quantum["QUANTUM SENSORS
Based on Photon-Electron Interaction"] thermal["THERMAL SENSORS
Based on Heat Absorption"] quantum --> photoconductive["PHOTOCONDUCTIVE LDR
CdS CdSe"] quantum --> photovoltaic["PHOTOVOLTAIC
Photodiode Solar Cell"] quantum --> photoemissive["PHOTOEMISSIVE
Phototube Photomultiplier"] thermal --> bolometer["BOLOMETER
Thermopile Pyroelectric"] end photoconductive --> P1["ADVANTAGE
Low Cost Simple
High Sensitivity Visible"] photoconductive --> P2["DISADVANTAGE
Slow Response ms
Non Linear Gamma"] ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern – 2 Mark Conceptual + 1 Mark Diagram]** ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **Define a photoresistive sensor. State TWO advantages and ONE disadvantage of using a CdS photoresistor.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A photoresistive sensor (Light Dependent Resistor, LDR) is a passive semiconductor device whose electrical resistance decreases when exposed to incident light, based on the principle of **photoconductivity**. **Two Advantages:** 1. **Low cost and simple interfacing** — no external bias amplifier needed; works with a basic voltage divider. 2. **High sensitivity in the visible spectrum** — peak response near 550 nm matches human eye perception. **One Disadvantage:** 1. **Slow response time** (typically 10–100 ms) due to long recombination lifetimes, making it unsuitable for high-frequency or pulsed light applications. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for advantages + 1 mark for disadvantage)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Write the expression for the cut-off wavelength of a photoresistor. Calculate $\lambda_c$ for a CdSe photoresistor with $E_g = 1.74$ eV.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The cut-off wavelength is the maximum wavelength of light that can generate an electron-hole pair: $$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} \text{ μm}$$ For CdSe with $E_g = 1.74$ eV: $$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{1.74} = 0.712 \text{ μm} = 712 \text{ nm}$$ This falls in the **red region** of the visible spectrum, confirming CdSe is suited for red/near-IR light detection. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Pattern: Either-Or Choice, 7 + 7 Marks Sub-Parts]** --- ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Choice A]** **Q.A. (a)** Explain the **working principle of a photoresistive sensor** with a neat block diagram. Derive the equation for **cut-off wavelength** and discuss its significance. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Working Principle (3 Marks):** A photoresistive sensor operates on the **photoconductive effect**. When photons of energy $E_{ph} \geq E_g$ strike the active semiconductor material (typically CdS), electron-hole pairs are generated within the bulk. These photogenerated carriers increase the conductivity ($\sigma = \sigma_{dark} + \Delta\sigma_{photo}$), thereby reducing the resistance. The change in resistance is converted into a voltage signal using a voltage divider circuit. **2. Cut-off Wavelength Derivation (3 Marks):** From Planck's relation and the threshold condition $E_{ph} = E_g$: $$\frac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g \implies \lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} \text{ μm}$$ **3. Significance (1 Mark):** - Defines the **spectral detection range** of the sensor. - For CdS, $\lambda_c = 512$ nm, making it ideal for **visible light sensing**. - Photons with $\lambda > \lambda_c$ are **not absorbed** and pass through the material without effect. **4. Block Diagram (included in SECTION 4.1 above — 0 additional marks here, but critical for full marks).** --- **Q.A. (b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **LDR-based automatic street light controller**. Design the voltage divider for the following specifications: $V_{cc} = 12$ V, dark resistance $R_D = 500$ kΩ, light resistance $R_L = 1$ kΩ. Choose $R_{fixed}$ such that the comparator switches at $V_{ref} = 6$ V. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Circuit Operation (3 Marks):** The LDR is connected in a **voltage divider** with a fixed resistor $R_{fixed}$. The junction voltage $V_{out}$ drives a **comparator** (e.g., LM358) referenced at $V_{ref} = 6$ V: - In **darkness**: $R_{LDR}$ is high → $V_{out}$ < $V_{ref}$ → Comparator output **HIGH** → Relay **ON** → Street light **glows**. - In **brightness**: $R_{LDR}$ is low → $V_{out}$ > $V_{ref}$ → Comparator output **LOW** → Relay **OFF** → Street light **OFF**. **2. Design Calculation (4 Marks):** At the switching threshold, $V_{out} = V_{ref}$: $$V_{ref} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_{LDR}}$$ In dark, $R_{LDR} = R_D = 500$ kΩ. The threshold occurs when the LDR is at a transition value $R_t$: $$6 = 12 \cdot \frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_t}$$ $$\frac{R_{fixed}}{R_{fixed} + R_t} = 0.5 \implies R_t = R_{fixed}$$ Choosing $R_{fixed} = 10$ kΩ gives a switching transition at $R_{LDR} = 10$ kΩ, which is well between the dark and light values — providing a **noise-immune hysteresis band**. --- ### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Module 3, Choice B — Alternative]** **Q.B. (a)** Compare **photoconductive, photovoltaic, and photoemissive** sensors. Derive the expression for the **change in conductivity** $\Delta\sigma$ of a photoresistor under illumination. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **1. Comparison Table (3 Marks):** | Feature | Photoconductive (LDR) | Photovoltaic (Photodiode) | Photoemissive (Phototube) | |---|---|---|---| | **Principle** | Bulk EHP generation | Junction photo-EMF | Electron emission from surface | | **Power Source** | Requires external bias | Self-powered (zero bias) | Requires high voltage | | **Response Time** | Slow (ms) | Fast (ns–μs) | Very fast (ns) | | **Sensitivity** | High (visible) | Moderate | Very high (with PMT) | | **Example Material** | CdS, CdSe | Silicon, GaAs | Cs-Sb, Ag-O-Cs | **2. Derivation of $\Delta\sigma$ (4 Marks):** The total conductivity is the sum of dark and photo-induced conductivities: $$\sigma_{total} = q(n_0 \mu_n + p_0 \mu_p) + q(\Delta n \mu_n + \Delta p \mu_p)$$ The **change in conductivity** is: $$\Delta\sigma = \sigma_{total} - \sigma_{dark} = q(\Delta n \mu_n + \Delta p \mu_p)$$ Since each absorbed photon generates one electron and one hole ($\Delta n = \Delta p = g\tau$): $$\boxed{\Delta\sigma = q \cdot g \cdot \tau \cdot (\mu_n + \mu_p)}$$ --- **Q.B. (b)** An LDR has the following characteristics: $R_{1\text{Lux}} = 100$ kΩ and $\gamma = 0.8$. Calculate its resistance at **(i)** 10 Lux and **(ii)** 1000 Lux. If this LDR is used in a voltage divider with $R_{fixed} = 5$ kΩ and $V_{cc} = 5$ V, find $V_{out}$ in both cases. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The resistance vs. illuminance relation is $R = R_0 \cdot (L_x)^{-\gamma}$. **Part (i) — At 10 Lux (3 Marks):** $$R_{10} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot (10)^{-0.8}$$ $$R_{10} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot 0.1585 = 15.85 \text{ k}\Omega$$ Voltage divider output: $$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{5}{5 + 15.85} = 5 \cdot \frac{5}{20.85} = 1.20 \text{ V}$$ **Part (ii) — At 1000 Lux (4 Marks):** $$R_{1000} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot (1000)^{-0.8}$$ $$R_{1000} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega \cdot 3.98 \times 10^{-3} = 0.398 \text{ k}\Omega = 398 \text{ }\Omega$$ Voltage divider output: $$V_{out} = 5 \cdot \frac{5000}{5000 + 398} = 5 \cdot 0.9263 = 4.63 \text{ V}$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** > Students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** in two specific ways: > 1. **Forgetting the negative exponent** — Writing $R = R_0 \cdot (L_x)^{\gamma}$ instead of $R = R_0 \cdot (L_x)^{-\gamma}$. Remember: **resistance DECREASES** as light INCREASES. > 2. **Ignoring the units of Lux in the formula** — The value of $R_0$ is always at **1 Lux reference**. If the problem says "$R_{10Lux} = X$", you must first extract $R_0$ or use a different formula form. > 3. **Mixing up voltage divider numerator** — In LDR circuits, $V_{out}$ is typically taken **across the fixed resistor**, not across the LDR. Read the circuit description carefully! --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🎯 > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — Photoresistive Sensors** - ✅ **Definition:** LDR / Photoresistor / Photoconductor = passive component whose **resistance DECREASES** with light. - ✅ **Core Principle:** Photoconductive effect — **bulk** generation of electron-hole pairs when $E_{ph} \geq E_g$. - ✅ **Key Formula 1 (Photon Energy):** $E_{ph} = \frac{1.24}{\lambda(\mu m)} \text{ eV}$ - ✅ **Key Formula 2 (Cut-off Wavelength):** $\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} \text{ μm}$ - ✅ **Key Formula 3 (Conductivity Change):** $\Delta\sigma = q(\mu_n \Delta n + \mu_p \Delta p)$ - ✅ **Key Formula 4 (Resistance vs. Lux):** $R = R_0 \cdot (L_x)^{-\gamma}$, where $\gamma \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ for CdS. - ✅ **Standard Material:** **Cadmium Sulfide (CdS)** with $E_g = 2.42$ eV (peak at 550 nm). - ✅ **Construction Feature:** **Zigzag / interdigitated electrode pattern** to maximize surface area. - ✅ **Two Types:** **Intrinsic** (pure semiconductor, higher $E_g$) and **Extrinsic** (doped, lower effective $E_g$). - ✅ **Standard Circuit:** **Voltage divider** with a fixed resistor; output across fixed resistor. - ✅ **Key Applications:** Automatic street lights, camera exposure control, security alarms, solar trackers, light meters in robotics (obstacle detection via light intensity). - ✅ **Major Limitation:** **Slow response** (10–100 ms) and **non-linear R-Lux curve** — the single biggest reason photodiodes are preferred for high-speed applications. - ✅ **Threshold Energy Check:** If $E_{ph} < E_g$ → no absorption → no photoconductivity → sensor is "blind" to that wavelength.

topics.title: Photodiodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Photodiodes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **photodiode** is a two-terminal semiconductor optoelectronic device built around a PN junction that converts incident optical (light) energy into measurable electrical current through the **internal photoelectric effect**. It is operated predominantly under **reverse bias** (photoconductive mode) to achieve a linear, fast, and intensity-proportional photocurrent response, making it one of the most fundamental light-sensing transducers in robotics vision systems, LIDAR, and optical encoders.

### The Photoelectric Effect — Foundation
The photoelectric effect, discovered by **Heinrich Hertz (1887)** and explained by **Einstein (1905)**, states that when photons of sufficient energy strike a material, they liberate electrons. In a photodiode, this liberation occurs inside the **depletion region** of a PN junction, where the built-in electric field efficiently separates the photo-generated carriers before they recombine.

### Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Overview
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Digital Rain Gauge for Light**
> Imagine a smart rain gauge designed to count every single raindrop. It has a funnel that is always "open and waiting" (reverse bias), and the moment a drop falls in, it sends an electrical pulse to a counter. A photodiode works exactly the same way:
> - **Raindrops** = Photons (particles of light)
> - **Funnel (always open)** = Reverse-biased depletion region
> - **Counter pulses** = Photocurrent pulses
> - **Drop intensity** = Light intensity (luminous flux per unit area)
>
> The faster it rains (more intense light), the faster the counter clicks (more photocurrent). You can count drops (measure light intensity) accurately and almost instantaneously.

### Essential Physical Constants
The following fundamental constants govern photodiode physics and **must be memorized** for KTU numerical problems:

- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J} \cdot \text{s}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}$
- **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{C}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \ \text{mV}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Module 3, photodiodes are grouped with other optical sensors (phototransistors, photoresistors/LDRs). Examiners frequently ask students to **compare** these devices. Always remember: photodiodes are **fastest, most linear**, and have the **widest spectral response** among common photodetectors.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a PN Junction Photodiode
A photodiode is constructed by forming a PN junction in a semiconductor such as **Silicon (Si)**, **Germanium (Ge)**, **Indium Gallium Arsenide (InGaAs)**, or **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**. The active sensing region is the **depletion region** at the metallurgical junction. A small transparent window on the package allows light to reach the junction. To maximize light absorption, the **p-layer is made very thin** (typically 1–3 µm) compared to the n-substrate.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Working Mechanism

1. **Photon Incidence:** Light of wavelength $\lambda$ strikes the thin p-layer and penetrates toward the depletion region.
2. **Photon Absorption Condition:** Each photon must have energy greater than or equal to the semiconductor's **bandgap energy** $E_g$:

$$E_{ph} = hf = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g$$

3. **Carrier Generation:** Each successfully absorbed photon transfers its energy to a valence electron, creating **one electron-hole pair (EHP)**.
4. **Carrier Separation:** The **built-in electric field** $E_0$ (and external reverse bias) sweeps electrons toward the n-region and holes toward the p-region within picoseconds.
5. **Photocurrent Flow:** This directed movement of charge constitutes a current — the **photocurrent $I_{ph}$** — which flows through the external load resistor $R_L$.
6. **Proportional Response:** The magnitude of $I_{ph}$ is **linearly proportional** to the incident optical power $P_{opt}$, enabling precise optical measurements.

### 2.3 Two Operating Modes

| Mode | Bias Condition | Application | Speed | Linearity |
|------|---------------|-------------|-------|-----------|
| **Photovoltaic Mode** | Zero bias ($V = 0$) | Solar cells, light meters | Slow | Non-linear |
| **Photoconductive Mode** | Reverse bias ($V < 0$) | Optical communication, robotics sensors | Very fast | Highly linear |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** In KTU exam answers, when asked about "mode of operation of photodiode as a sensor," always default to **photoconductive mode with reverse bias** because it offers (i) wider depletion region for better absorption, (ii) reduced junction capacitance → faster response, and (iii) linear I-P characteristics.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following table verbatim** — these equations appear in nearly every KTU numerical or theoretical question on photodiodes.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E_{ph} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a single photon | Joules (J) or eV |
| 2 | $\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g \ (\text{eV})} \ \mu\text{m}$ | Cutoff wavelength (longest detectable $\lambda$) | meters (m) |
| 3 | $\eta = \frac{\text{electrons collected/sec}}{\text{photons incident/sec}}$ | Quantum efficiency (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | Responsivity | A/W |
| 5 | $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$ | Photocurrent equation | Amperes (A) |
| 6 | $I_{total} = I_{ph} + I_{dark}$ | Total device current | Amperes (A) |
| 7 | $I_{dark} = I_S \left(e^{qV/kT} - 1\right)$ | Shockley diode equation (dark current) | Amperes (A) |
| 8 | $f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_L C_j}$ | Bandwidth (response speed) | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | $\text{NEP} = \frac{\sqrt{\langle i_n^2 \rangle}}{R}$ | Noise Equivalent Power | W/$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ |
| 10 | $D^{*} = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}}$ | Specific detectivity | cm·$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$/W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all KTU derivations, absolute value notations such as $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid V \mid$ must be used **inside math blocks** (not as markdown table separators) to preserve table integrity. Always express magnitude as $\vert V_R \vert$ or $\mid V_R \mid$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
Photodiodes form the optical sensing backbone of countless robotic and industrial systems:
- **Robotic vision systems** (CMOS/CCD image sensors contain millions of photodiodes)
- **LIDAR** (Light Detection And Ranging) for autonomous vehicles
- **Optical encoders** for precise motor shaft position feedback
- **Barcode scanners** in logistics automation
- **Smoke detectors** and **sun-tracking solar arrays**
- **Biomedical pulse oximeters** and **spectrophotometers**
- **Fiber-optic communication receivers** (typically InGaAs PIN photodiodes)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Responsivity $R$ from First Principles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 3. Be prepared to derive this from scratch in any university examination.

**Step 1: Define the incident optical power.**
Let $N_p$ photons per second be incident on the photodiode active area, each carrying energy $E_{ph}$. The total incident optical power is therefore:

$$P_{opt} = N_p \cdot E_{ph} = N_p \cdot \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

This step follows directly from the definition of power as energy per unit time.

**Step 2: Introduce quantum efficiency $\eta$.**
By definition, quantum efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of carriers (electrons) actually collected by the external circuit to the total number of photons incident on the device:

$$\eta = \frac{N_e}{N_p}$$

where $N_e$ is the number of electrons collected per second. Solving for $N_e$:

$$N_e = \eta \cdot N_p$$

The quantum efficiency accounts for losses due to surface reflection, incomplete absorption, and carrier recombination.

**Step 3: Convert electron count to electrical current.**
Since each electron carries an elementary charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, the photocurrent is simply charge per unit time:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot N_e = q \cdot \eta \cdot N_p$$

This is the fundamental current equation, derived using $I = \frac{dq}{dt}$.

**Step 4: Express $N_p$ in terms of $P_{opt}$.**
Rearranging the equation from Step 1:

$$N_p = \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 5: Substitute back into the photocurrent equation.**

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 6: Rearrange to isolate responsivity.**
By definition, **responsivity** $R$ is the photocurrent produced per unit incident optical power:

$$R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Responsivity is **directly proportional to wavelength** $\lambda$ and quantum efficiency $\eta$. Longer wavelengths (lower energy photons) produce more current per watt because more photons are present per joule of optical energy. However, this holds only up to the cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$ where $E_{ph} = E_g$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Problem Style

**Problem:** A silicon photodiode has a quantum efficiency of **75%** at a wavelength of **850 nm**. If the incident optical power is **10 µW**, calculate:
1. The responsivity of the device.
2. The resulting photocurrent.
3. The number of photons incident per second.

**Given Data:**
- $\eta = 0.75$
- $\lambda = 850 \ \text{nm} = 850 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}$
- $P_{opt} = 10 \ \mu\text{W} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{W}$
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J} \cdot \text{s}$
- $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \ \text{C}$

**Solution Part (a): Responsivity**

$$R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc} = \frac{0.75 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (850 \times 10^{-9})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^8)}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$0.75 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 850 \times 10^{-9} = 1.0213 \times 10^{-25} \ \text{C} \cdot \text{m}$$

Computing the denominator:

$$6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \ \text{J} \cdot \text{m}$$

Therefore:

$$R = \frac{1.0213 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 0.5137 \ \text{A/W}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.514 \ \text{A/W}}$$

**Solution Part (b): Photocurrent**

$$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = 0.514 \ \text{A/W} \times 10 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{I_{ph} = 5.14 \ \mu\text{A}}$$

**Solution Part (c): Number of incident photons per second**

From $P_{opt} = N_p \cdot \frac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$$N_p = \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{hc} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 850 \times 10^{-9}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$N_p = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-12}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 4.276 \times 10^{13} \ \text{photons/sec}$$

$$\boxed{N_p \approx 4.28 \times 10^{13} \ \text{photons/s}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Cutoff Wavelength $\lambda_c$

The longest wavelength that a photodiode can detect is set by the bandgap energy $E_g$. Setting $E_{ph} = E_g$:

$$\frac{hc}{\lambda_c} = E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}$$

Converting $E_g$ to eV and $hc$ to eV·µm gives the **handy KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{\lambda_c \ (\mu\text{m}) = \frac{1.24}{E_g \ (\text{eV})}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Silicon:** $E_g = 1.12$ eV → $\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{1.12} \approx 1.11 \ \mu\text{m}$ (1100 nm). This is why Si photodiodes cannot detect infrared beyond 1.1 µm.
> **For Germanium:** $E_g = 0.67$ eV → $\lambda_c \approx 1.85 \ \mu\text{m}$ (extended IR range).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Performance Trade-offs in Photodiode Operation

| Parameter | Effect of Increasing Reverse Bias $V_R$ | Engineering Trade-off |
|-----------|----------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Depletion width $W$ | Increases (depletion region widens) | Better absorption, higher quantum efficiency |
| Junction capacitance $C_j$ | Decreases (since $C_j \propto 1/W$) | **Faster response time**, higher bandwidth |
| Dark current $I_{dark}$ | Increases (exponentially with $V_R$) | **Higher noise floor**, lower SNR |
| Breakdown voltage | Approaches limit | Risk of permanent device damage |
| Photocurrent $I_{ph}$ | Marginally increases (saturation) | Diminishing returns beyond a few volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to justify "why reverse bias is used," structure your answer around these **four points**: (1) wide depletion region → higher quantum efficiency, (2) reduced junction capacitance → faster response, (3) linear I-P characteristics, (4) low dark current operation (with optimal $V_R$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Photodiode Operational Block Diagram (Light → Current Conversion)

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1["Incident Light Photons of Wavelength lambda"]
    node2["Photon Energy greater than or equal to Bandgap Eg"]
    node3{"Photon reaches Depletion Region"}
    node4["Photon absorbed - Electron Hole Pair Generated"]
    node5["Built in Electric Field separates the carriers"]
    node6["Electrons swept toward N region"]
    node7["Holes swept toward P region"]
    node8["Photocurrent Iph flows in external circuit"]
    node9["Load Resistor RL converts current to Voltage Vout"]
    node10["Signal Conditioning Amplifier Stage"]
    node11["Analog to Digital Converter ADC"]
    node12["Microcontroller for Robot Decision Making"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12
```

### 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model of a Photodiode

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Optical Input Popt in Watts"]
    nodeB["Current Source Iph equals R times Popt"]
    nodeC["Ideal Diode D with Shockley Equation"]
    nodeD["Series Resistance Rs typically 10 to 50 ohms"]
    nodeE["Junction Capacitance Cj depends on V_R"]
    nodeF["Dark Current Source I_dark"]
    nodeG["Output Terminal Anode"]
    nodeH["Output Terminal Cathode"]
    nodeI["Load Resistor R_L external"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeH
```

### 4.3 I-V Characteristics Under Varying Illumination (Block-Level Functional Plot Description)

```mermaid
graph TD
    axisX["X axis: Reverse Bias Voltage V_R increasing to the right"]
    axisY["Y axis: Photocurrent I_ph downward into Quadrant III for reverse bias"]
    curve1["Curve 1: Dark condition I equals I_dark only small constant"]
    curve2["Curve 2: Low illumination P1 produces I_ph1"]
    curve3["Curve 3: Medium illumination P2 produces I_ph2"]
    curve4["Curve 4: High illumination P3 produces I_ph3"]
    noteA["All curves nearly horizontal in reverse bias: linear photo response"]
    noteB["In forward bias: standard diode exponential I-V characteristic"]

    axisX --- axisY
    axisX --> curve1
    axisX --> curve2
    axisX --> curve3
    axisX --> curve4
    curve1 --- noteA
    curve2 --- noteA
    curve3 --- noteA
    curve4 --- noteA
    axisX --> noteB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Drawing Tip:** When drawing I-V characteristics in your answer sheet, the **fourth quadrant** (positive $V_R$ plotted right, negative $I$ plotted down) is the **operational region** for a photodiode. Mark the **dark current line** first, then draw parallel horizontal lines below it for increasing illumination levels. Label the axes clearly: "$I \ (\mu\text{A})$" on the Y-axis (downward) and "$V_R \ (\text{V})$" on the X-axis (rightward).

### 4.4 Functional Block Architecture: Photodiode in a Robotic Sensor Module

```mermaid
graph TD
    stage1["Stage 1: Optical Front End Photodiode + Optical Filter"]
    stage2["Stage 2: Transimpedance Amplifier TIA converts I_ph to V_out"]
    stage3["Stage 3: Band Pass Filter removes low and high frequency noise"]
    stage4["Stage 4: Comparator or ADC for signal digitization"]
    stage5["Stage 5: Embedded Processor estimates light intensity distance or position"]
    stage6["Stage 6: Robotic Actuator Response Motor Servo or Gripper"]

    stage1 --> stage2
    stage2 --> stage3
    stage3 --> stage4
    stage4 --> stage5
    stage5 --> stage6
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a photodiode. Mention any two advantages of operating a photodiode in reverse bias.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A photodiode is a semiconductor PN junction device that converts incident light energy into electrical current through the photoelectric effect. When photons with energy $\geq E_g$ strike the depletion region, electron-hole pairs are generated and separated by the built-in electric field, producing a photocurrent proportional to light intensity.
>
> **Two advantages of reverse bias operation:**
> 1. **Wide depletion region** → higher quantum efficiency due to greater absorption volume.
> 2. **Reduced junction capacitance** → faster response time, suitable for high-frequency optical communication and high-speed robotic sensing.
>
> *(Examiner's note: Any two valid points from {linear I-P response, low dark current, high bandwidth} accepted.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is meant by "responsivity" of a photodiode? State its SI unit.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> Responsivity $R$ is defined as the ratio of the photocurrent $I_{ph}$ generated by the photodiode to the incident optical power $P_{opt}$ on its active area:
>
> $$R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$$
>
> **SI Unit:** Ampere per Watt (A/W).
>
> *(Examiner's note: A typical silicon photodiode has $R \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$ A/W in the visible-to-near-IR range.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

> **Question A [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working principle of a PN junction photodiode operated in photoconductive mode. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for responsivity of a photodiode in terms of quantum efficiency and wavelength. A silicon photodiode with $\eta = 0.80$ is illuminated by 5 µW of light at 900 nm. Calculate the photocurrent. (7 marks)
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks):**
> - **Construction (3 marks):** A photodiode consists of a PN junction formed in a semiconductor (Si/Ge/InGaAs). The p-layer is intentionally made very thin ($\sim$1–3 µm) to allow light penetration. A transparent glass window on the package admits light. Two ohmic contacts — anode (p-side) and cathode (n-side) — provide electrical terminals. A reverse bias voltage $V_R$ is applied externally.
> - **Working Principle (4 marks):** When photons of energy $E_{ph} = hc/\lambda \geq E_g$ strike the depletion region, each absorbed photon creates one electron-hole pair. The built-in electric field (augmented by $V_R$) immediately separates the carriers: electrons drift toward the n-region and holes toward the p-region. This directed motion constitutes the **photocurrent $I_{ph}$**, which flows through the external load $R_L$ producing an output voltage $V_{out} = I_{ph} \cdot R_L$. The current is **linearly proportional** to the incident optical power, making the photodiode an excellent quantitative light sensor.
>
> **Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical (7 marks):**
>
> **Derivation (4 marks):** [Present the full derivation from Section 3.1 step-by-step, leading to]
>
> $$R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}$$
>
> **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**
> - $\eta = 0.80$, $\lambda = 900 \ \text{nm} = 900 \times 10^{-9} \ \text{m}$, $P_{opt} = 5 \ \mu\text{W} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{W}$
> - $R = \frac{0.80 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}$
> - $R = \frac{1.1534 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.5802 \ \text{A/W}$
> - $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = 0.5802 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}$
> - $\boxed{I_{ph} = 2.90 \ \mu\text{A}}$

---

> **Question B [Alternative Choice – KTU Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Draw the I-V characteristics of a photodiode under dark and illuminated conditions. Explain each region. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the key performance parameters of a photodiode: responsivity, quantum efficiency, dark current, and response time. State the typical range of values for a silicon photodiode. (7 marks)
>
> **Model Solution:**
>
> **Part (a) — I-V Characteristics (7 marks):**
> - **Axes (1 mark):** X-axis = Forward bias $V_F$ to the right, reverse bias $V_R$ to the left. Y-axis = Current $I$, with forward current up and photocurrent (negative) down.
> - **Dark Curve (2 marks):** In darkness, the photodiode behaves as an ordinary PN junction diode — forward current rises exponentially for $V > 0$ (Shockley equation), and a small reverse saturation current $I_{dark} \approx I_S$ (typically nA to µA) flows for $V < 0$.
> - **Illuminated Curves (3 marks):** Under illumination, the I-V curve shifts **downward** in the fourth quadrant. Each curve corresponds to a specific illumination level $P_1 < P_2 < P_3$. The shift downward equals $-I_{ph}$, where $I_{ph}$ is proportional to optical power. The curves are nearly **horizontal** in reverse bias (constant current region), demonstrating the linear I-P response. The intersection with the voltage axis gives the **open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$** (relevant for photovoltaic/solar cell mode).
> - **First and Third Quadrants (1 mark):** First quadrant: forward conduction; Third quadrant: photoconductive operation with linear photocurrent.
>
> **Part (b) — Performance Parameters (7 marks):**
>
> | Parameter | Definition | Typical Si Value |
> |-----------|-----------|------------------|
> | **Responsivity $R$** | $I_{ph}/P_{opt}$; photocurrent per unit optical power | $0.4$ – $0.6$ A/W (at peak $\lambda$) |
> | **Quantum Efficiency $\eta$** | Ratio of electrons collected to photons incident | $60\%$ – $90\%$ (wavelength dependent) |
> | **Dark Current $I_{dark}$** | Reverse leakage current with no illumination | $1$ nA – $100$ nA (at $V_R = 10$ V, 25°C) |
> | **Response Time $\tau_r$** | Time to reach 63% of final photocurrent (limited by $C_j$ and carrier transit time) | $1$ ns – $500$ ns |
>
> **Engineering Significance (1 mark):** Higher $R$ and $\eta$ indicate better photon-to-electron conversion. Lower $I_{dark}$ improves signal-to-noise ratio. Faster $\tau_r$ enables high-bandwidth applications like LIDAR and optical fiber receivers.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks typical):** Students forget to convert **nm → m** when computing $R$ via $R = \eta q \lambda / hc$. A wavelength of 900 entered as 900 instead of $900 \times 10^{-9}$ makes your answer wrong by **9 orders of magnitude**.
>
> 2. **Confusing Photodiode with Phototransistor (–1 mark):** Photodiodes have **two terminals** and output **current directly proportional to light**; phototransistors have **three terminals** and provide **internal current amplification** ($\beta$). Do not interchange them.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Cutoff Wavelength (–1 mark):** Photodiodes cannot detect photons with $\lambda > \lambda_c$. If a question states "incident IR at 1500 nm" with a Si photodiode ($E_g = 1.12$ eV, $\lambda_c \approx 1100$ nm), there will be **zero photocurrent**. Always verify $\lambda < \lambda_c$ first.
>
> 4. **Wrong Mode Identification (–1 mark):** When a question says "photodiode used in a smoke detector," the answer is **photoconductive mode with reverse bias**, not photovoltaic. Photovoltaic mode is for **energy harvesting** (solar cells).
>
> 5. **Missing the Word "Reverse" in Bias Description (–1 mark):** Always write "operated under **reverse bias**" — never just "operated under bias." The polarity matters.
>
> 6. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Sub-Question (–2 marks):** Construction or I-V questions **always require a labelled diagram**. A neat, well-labelled circuit with arrows showing light entry, current direction, and bias polarity earns full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Photodiodes (Module 3)**

- ☐ A photodiode is a **reverse-biased PN junction** that converts light into current via the **internal photoelectric effect**.
- ☐ It operates primarily in **photoconductive mode** (reverse bias) for sensor applications.
- ☐ The fundamental condition for photon absorption is $hf = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g$ (bandgap energy).
- ☐ **Cutoff wavelength:** $\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g \ (\text{eV})} \ \mu\text{m}$.
- ☐ **Photocurrent:** $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$, where $R$ is responsivity in A/W.
- ☐ **Responsivity formula:** $R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}$ — directly proportional to $\lambda$ and $\eta$.
- ☐ **Quantum efficiency** $\eta$ = (electrons collected) / (photons incident) — dimensionless, typically $0.6$ – $0.9$ for Si.
- ☐ **Dark current** $I_{dark}$ is the small reverse leakage current with no illumination; it sets the **noise floor**.
- ☐ **Response time** depends on junction capacitance: $\tau_r \propto R_L C_j$; reduces with increasing reverse bias.
- ☐ **I-V characteristics** show downward-shifted horizontal lines in Quadrant III for increasing illumination.
- ☐ **Photodiode vs. LDR:** Photodiodes are **faster, more linear**, and have a defined spectral peak; LDRs (CdS cells) are slow and non-linear.
- ☐ **Photodiode vs. Phototransistor:** Photodiode → 2 terminals, no internal gain; Phototransistor → 3 terminals, current gain $\beta$, slower.
- ☐ **Key applications in robotics:** Line-following sensors, proximity sensors, optical encoders, LIDAR receivers, solar-powered autonomous systems.
- ☐ **Common materials:** Si (visible–near IR, $\lambda_c = 1.1$ µm), Ge (near–mid IR, $\lambda_c = 1.85$ µm), InGaAs (fiber-optic, $\lambda_c = 1.7$ µm).
- ☐ **Constants to memorize:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $hc = 1.24$ eV·µm (very useful shortcut).
- ☐ **For numerical problems:** Always convert $\lambda$ to meters, $P_{opt}$ to watts, and report $I_{ph}$ in A (or µA/nA for readability).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phototransistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Phototransistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Phototransistor** is a semiconductor optoelectronic device that converts incident optical (light) radiation into an electrical current by exploiting the photosensitive nature of a reverse-biased p–n junction. Structurally, it is a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) in which the base–collector junction is exposed to incident light, and the photo-generated carriers are internally amplified by the transistor's normal current gain ($\beta$ or $h_{fe}$). In essence, the base current of a conventional BJT is replaced (or assisted) by the photocurrent generated within the depletion region, producing a much larger, light-modulated collector current.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Reference):**  
> *"A phototransistor is a light-sensitive transistor in which the base current is generated by the absorption of photons in the base–collector depletion region. The device integrates the function of a photodiode and a transistor amplifier in a single monolithic structure, providing internal current gain."*

The two most common configurations used in KTU-style problems are:

- **Two-terminal phototransistor** — base lead is floating; the device is operated between collector and emitter only.
- **Three-terminal phototransistor** — base lead is accessible and can be externally biased to adjust sensitivity, response time, and dark current.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap (transistor)** and a **funnel collecting rainwater (photodiode)**. In a normal transistor, someone must manually press a lever to start the flow of water — the harder you press, the more water comes out. In a **phototransistor**, sunlight itself presses the lever: the brighter the light, the harder the lever gets pushed, and the more current flows from collector to emitter.

So you can think of the phototransistor as a **"light-controlled amplifier."** The photons (tiny particles of light) play the role of an invisible hand turning the knob of an electrical faucet.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard physical quantities govern the behaviour of every phototransistor:

- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Wavelength of peak sensitivity (typical Si phototransistor):** $\lambda \approx 800\text{–}900\ \text{nm}$
- **Typical current gain (common-emitter):** $h_{fe} \approx 100\text{–}1000$
- **Rise time ($t_r$):** typically **2 – 10 µs** (much slower than photodiodes)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the phototransistor slower than a photodiode?**  
> Because the large base–collector capacitance must charge through the relatively high base resistance, and carrier transit time across the base width is finite. This is a common KTU MCQ/Fill-in topic.

## 1.4 Why Phototransistors Matter in Robotics

In robotics, phototransistors are used as low-cost, robust, ambient-light sensors for:

- Line-following robots (IR reflectance sensing).
- Object detection and proximity measurement.
- Optical encoders (wheel rotation counting).
- Sunlight / ambient light intensity estimation.
- End-of-travel limit detection (light-beam break sensors).

They are preferred over photodiodes when **internal amplification** is required without an external op-amp, making the interface to a microcontroller's ADC simpler.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Device Structure (NPN Si Phototransistor)

A typical silicon **NPN phototransistor** has:

- A large **n-type collector** region at the bottom.
- A thin **p-type base** region in the middle.
- A small **n-type emitter** region at the top.
- A transparent window on top allowing photons to reach the **base–collector depletion region**.
- An optional base contact pad for the three-terminal version.

The photon absorption is intentionally maximised in the **base–collector (B–C) junction**, which is reverse-biased during normal operation. This gives a wide depletion region for efficient carrier generation.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a phototransistor can be broken down into **four sequential steps**:

1. **Photon Absorption:**  
   Photons of energy $E = h\nu \ge E_g$ (bandgap energy of silicon, $E_g \approx 1.12\ \text{eV}$) strike the B–C depletion region and are absorbed by valence electrons.

2. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation:**  
   Each absorbed photon creates **one electron–hole pair**. The electron is swept into the n-collector and the hole into the p-base by the strong reverse-bias electric field.

3. **Base Current Equivalent:**  
   This flow of holes into the base is mathematically equivalent to a base current $I_{ph}$ injected into the transistor.

4. **Transistor Amplification:**  
   The normal BJT current gain amplifies this photo-generated base current by a factor of $(1 + h_{fe})$, giving a large collector current:
   $$I_C = (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot I_{ph} + I_{CEO(dark)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The phototransistor is essentially a photodiode (B–C junction) feeding its photocurrent into the base of a built-in amplifier (the rest of the BJT). The integration provides $\sim 100\text{–}1000\times$ internal gain.

## 2.3 Two-Terminal vs Three-Terminal Operation

| Feature | Two-terminal (Base floating) | Three-terminal (Base accessible) |
|---|---|---|
| External components | None (simplest) | Base resistor $R_B$ required |
| Sensitivity control | None | Adjustable via $R_B$ |
| Dark current | Higher | Lower (since $I_{CEO}$ can be nulled) |
| Response speed | Slower (Miller effect) | Faster (lower $R_B$ reduces RC) |
| Noise | Higher | Lower |
| KTU exam preference | Most common in syllabus | Asked for sensitivity/bandwidth tradeoff |

## 2.4 V–I Characteristics and Operating Regions

The output characteristic of a phototransistor plots collector current $I_C$ against collector–emitter voltage $V_{CE}$, **with light intensity $E$ (lx or mW/cm²) as the parameter family**, instead of base current as in a normal BJT.

Three regions appear:

- **Cut-off region:** $V_{CE} < V_{CE(sat)}$ → device acts as a switch; minimal current.
- **Active region:** $V_{CE} > V_{CE(sat)}$ → $I_C \approx (1+h_{fe}) I_{ph}$, nearly independent of $V_{CE}$.
- **Saturation region:** $V_{CE} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$ → used in switching applications (digital optocouplers, line sensors).

## 2.5 Spectral Response

The **spectral responsivity** $R(\lambda)$ describes how the output current varies with the wavelength of incident light. For silicon, the response peaks near **800–900 nm** (near-infrared), falls off in the visible spectrum, and cuts off sharply near **1100 nm** (the bandgap limit of Si).

For maximum sensitivity to typical IR LEDs used in robotics (e.g., 850 nm, 940 nm), silicon phototransistors are an excellent match.

## 2.6 Response Time and Bandwidth

The dominant pole is given by the RC time constant of the base–emitter capacitance $C_{BE}$ and the base input resistance. The approximate **−3 dB bandwidth** is:

$$f_{3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot R_B \cdot C_{BE}}$$

For a two-terminal phototransistor, $R_B$ is replaced by the dynamic emitter–base resistance, which is large — hence the low bandwidth ($\sim 100\ \text{kHz}$ to 1 MHz).

## 2.7 Noise Performance

Main noise sources:

- **Shot noise** in the photocurrent: $i_{n,shot} = \sqrt{2q I_C \, \Delta f}$
- **Thermal (Johnson) noise** in the base resistance: $e_{n,th} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R_B \, \Delta f}$
- **1/f (flicker) noise** at low frequencies (negligible above ~10 kHz).

The **Noise Equivalent Power (NEP)** and **Detectivity ($D^*$)** are the standard figures of merit but are usually only relevant for advanced phototransistors in low-light applications — rarely asked in KTU B.Tech exams.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{ph} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \Phi \cdot A$ | Photocurrent from incident photon flux | A |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$ | Spectral responsivity | A/W |
| 3 | $\eta = \dfrac{h \cdot c \cdot R}{q \cdot \lambda}$ | Quantum efficiency (0 < $\eta$ < 1) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $I_C = (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot I_{ph}$ | Collector current (no dark current) | A |
| 5 | $I_C = I_{CEO} + (1+h_{fe}) I_{ph}$ | Total collector current | A |
| 6 | $V_{CE(out)} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L$ | Output voltage across load resistor | V |
| 7 | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_B C_{BE}}$ | Approximate bandwidth | Hz |
| 8 | $i_{n,shot} = \sqrt{2q I_C \, \Delta f}$ | Shot noise current | A/√Hz |
| 9 | $E_g = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{\lambda_c}$ | Cut-off wavelength condition | J ↔ m |
| 10 | $\text{Sensitivity} \propto h_{fe}$ | More gain → more sensitive but slower | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU answer sheet, **always state units explicitly** for $I_{ph}$, $R$, and $\lambda$. A numerical answer without units is a guaranteed ½–1 mark deduction as per KTU valuation key.

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phototransistors are the workhorses of low-cost robotics because they:

- Need **no external amplifier** for moderate light levels.
- Interface directly to **microcontroller GPIO** (digital) or **ADC** (analog).
- Tolerate mechanical vibration and wide temperature ranges.
- Cost < ₹15 in Indian markets (e.g., L14G1, BPW77N).
- Can detect modulated IR (e.g., TV remote at 38 kHz) when combined with a bandpass filter IC (TSOP1738).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section walks through every important analytical expression a KTU 2024 student is expected to derive, justify, or apply in the End Semester Examination.

## 3.1 Derivation of Photocurrent from Incident Optical Power

**Given:**  
- Incident optical power on active area: $P_{opt}$ (in watts).  
- Photon energy: $E_{photon} = h \cdot \nu = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$.  
- Quantum efficiency: $\eta$ (number of e–h pairs collected per incident photon).  
- Active detector area: $A$ (in m²).  
- Photon flux (number of photons per second per unit area): $\Phi$ (in photons·m⁻²·s⁻¹).

**Step 1 — Number of photons per second striking the detector:**  
The total optical power $P_{opt}$ is distributed over photon energy $E_{photon}$, so the number of photons arriving per second is:

$$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

*Logic:* Power = energy/second, energy per photon = $h \nu$. So dividing gives the photon count rate.

**Step 2 — Effective number of e–h pairs generated per second:**  
Only a fraction $\eta$ of the incident photons successfully create collectable electron–hole pairs:

$$N_{e-h} = \eta \cdot N_{ph} = \eta \cdot \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to current:**  
Each collected electron contributes a charge $q$ per second of current. Therefore:

$$I_{ph} = q \cdot N_{e-h} = q \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{I_{ph} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{opt}}{h \cdot c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most fundamental equation** of any photon-to-current conversion device. Memorize it in this exact form. KTU examiners often present it in slightly rearranged forms — practice both.

## 3.2 Derivation of Spectral Responsivity

**Definition of responsivity $R$:** the photocurrent produced per unit of incident optical power:

$$R \equiv \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the $I_{ph}$ expression derived above:**

$$R = \frac{1}{P_{opt}} \cdot \left[ \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{opt}}{h \cdot c} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $P_{opt}$ from numerator and denominator:**

$$R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c} \quad \left[\frac{\text{A}}{\text{W}}\right]}$$

**Practical observation:** At $\lambda = 900\ \text{nm}$ and $\eta = 0.8$ for Si:

$$R = \frac{0.8 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}} \approx 0.58\ \text{A/W}$$

A phototransistor with $h_{fe} = 200$ therefore gives an **effective responsivity** of:

$$R_{eff} = (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot R \approx 201 \times 0.58 \approx 116.6\ \text{A/W}$$

This shows why a phototransistor can directly drive logic-level circuits from weak optical signals — a property unmatchable by a bare photodiode.

## 3.3 Derivation of Quantum Efficiency from Measured Responsivity

**Step 1 — Start from the responsivity formula:**

$$R = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

**Step 2 — Solve algebraically for $\eta$:**

$$\eta = \frac{R \cdot h \cdot c}{q \cdot \lambda}$$

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{R \cdot h \cdot c}{q \cdot \lambda}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> $\eta$ is dimensionless and must lie between **0 and 1**. If a calculation yields $\eta > 1$, an error has been made — most likely the wavelength was entered in the wrong units (m vs nm) or $h$ was taken as $6.626 \times 10^{-27}$ instead of $10^{-34}$.

## 3.4 Derivation of Collector Current in Active Mode

**Step 1 — Recall BJT current relations in active mode:**  
For a bipolar junction transistor in forward-active operation:

$$I_C = h_{fe} \cdot I_B$$

$$I_E = I_C + I_B = (1 + h_{fe}) I_B$$

**Step 2 — In a phototransistor, $I_B$ is replaced by the photocurrent $I_{ph}$:**  
The holes generated in the base–collector depletion region and collected in the base act as the base current drive:

$$I_B^{photo} = I_{ph}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the active-mode current equation:**

$$I_C = (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot I_{ph}$$

**Step 4 — Add the dark current $I_{CEO}$ (collector-emitter current with base open and zero light):**

$$I_{CEO} = (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot I_{CBO}$$

where $I_{CBO}$ is the reverse leakage of the B–C junction.

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{I_C = I_{CEO} + (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot I_{ph}}$$

## 3.5 Cut-off Wavelength Derivation

**Step 1 — Condition for photon absorption:** The photon energy must be at least equal to the semiconductor bandgap:

$$h \cdot \nu \ge E_g$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\nu = c / \lambda$:**

$$\frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda} \ge E_g$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum wavelength (cut-off):**

$$\lambda_c = \frac{h \cdot c}{E_g}$$

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\lambda_c = \frac{h \cdot c}{E_g}}$$

**Numerical example (Silicon):** $E_g(\text{Si}) = 1.12\ \text{eV} = 1.12 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}{1.12 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.107\ \mu\text{m} = 1107\ \text{nm}$$

This matches the experimentally observed Si phototransistor cut-off near 1100 nm.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Photodiode vs Phototransistor

| Parameter | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|---|---|---|
| Internal gain | None (unity) | $(1+h_{fe})$ — typically 100 to 1000 |
| Responsivity | $\eta q \lambda / hc$ | $(1+h_{fe}) \cdot \eta q \lambda / hc$ |
| Response time | ns to µs | µs to tens of µs |
| Dark current | Very low | Higher (amplified $I_{CBO}$) |
| Linearity | Excellent (linear) | Poor (depends on $h_{fe}$ vs $I_C$) |
| Output current | µA range | mA range |
| External circuitry | Needs transimpedance amplifier | Direct interface to logic/ADC |
| KTU 2024 application | Precision instruments | Robotics, switching, encoders |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU Part B 14-mark questions, comparing photodiodes and phototransistors is a recurring sub-question (typically 7 marks). Use this matrix and add 2–3 sentences of contextual reasoning for full marks.

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A Si phototransistor with $h_{fe} = 150$ and quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.7$ is illuminated by an LED of wavelength 850 nm, optical power 50 µW. The load resistor is $R_L = 2\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $V_{CC} = 5\ \text{V}$. Calculate: (a) the photocurrent in the B–C junction, (b) the collector current, and (c) the output voltage.

**Solution:**

**(a) Photocurrent in the B–C junction:**

$$I_{ph} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{opt}}{h \cdot c}$$

$$I_{ph} = \frac{0.7 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 850 \times 10^{-9} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$I_{ph} = \frac{0.7 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 4.25 \times 10^{-11}}{1.987 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$I_{ph} = \frac{4.765 \times 10^{-30}}{1.987 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 2.398 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A} \approx 23.98\ \mu\text{A}$$

**(b) Collector current (neglecting dark current):**

$$I_C = (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot I_{ph} = 151 \times 23.98\ \mu\text{A} \approx 3.621\ \text{mA}$$

**(c) Output voltage across the load resistor:**

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L = 5 - (3.621 \times 10^{-3}) \times (2 \times 10^{3})$$

$$V_{out} = 5 - 7.242 = -2.242\ \text{V}$$

The negative result indicates the load is too small (or the LED power too high) and the phototransistor is **saturated**. We need $I_C R_L < V_{CC}$ for linear operation, i.e., choose $R_L \ge V_{CC} / I_C \approx 1.38\ \text{k}\Omega$ minimum.

This kind of "saturated vs linear" check is a classic KTU follow-up.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Physical Structure of an NPN Phototransistor (Block Architecture Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the layered semiconductor structure and the photon absorption path. Because actual cross-sectional drawings are difficult to render in ASCII/Mermaid, we instead present a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that captures the same physical and electrical relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Light Source / IR LED] --> B[Transparent Glass Window]
    B --> C[Emitter Region - n type Si - Top Contact]
    C --> D[Base Region - p type Si - Thin Layer]
    D --> E[Base-Collector Depletion Region - Photon Absorption Zone]
    E --> F[Collector Region - n type Si - Bulk Substrate]
    F --> G[Collector Terminal - Positive Bias]
    C --> H[Emitter Terminal - Ground or Vout]
    D --> I[Base Terminal - Optional External Bias]
    E --> J[Electron-Hole Pair Generation - E h nu greater than E sub g]
    J --> K[Hole Swept into Base - Equivalent Base Current I sub ph]
    J --> L[Electron Swept into Collector - Photocurrent Pathway]
    K --> M[BJT Transistor Action - I sub C equals 1 plus h sub fe times I sub ph]
    L --> M
    M --> N[External Load Resistor R sub L]
    N --> O[Output Voltage V sub out across R sub L]
```

## 4.2 Circuit Symbol and Biasing (Common-Emitter Configuration)

The standard schematic symbol of a phototransistor is **a BJT symbol with two small arrows pointing toward the base**, indicating incoming light. Below is the common-emitter biasing arrangement, drawn as a functional flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V sub CC - Positive Supply] --> B[Collector Pin of Phototransistor]
    B --> C[Phototransistor Q1 - NPN with Light-Sensitive Base-Collector Junction]
    C --> D[Emitter Pin]
    D --> E[Resistor R sub L - Load to Ground]
    E --> F[Ground 0V]
    C --> G[Incident Light h nu - Modulates I sub C]
    G --> C
    D --> H[Output Node V sub out - V sub CC minus I sub C R sub L]
    H --> I[Microcontroller ADC or Logic Input]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Signal Processing Inside the Device

This diagram shows how a photon entering the device becomes a measurable output voltage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Incident Photons of Energy h nu] --> stepB[Photon Absorption in B-C Depletion Region]
    stepB --> stepC[Electron-Hole Pair Generation]
    stepC --> stepD[Carrier Separation by Reverse-Bias Field]
    stepD --> stepE[Photo-generated Base Current I sub ph]
    stepE --> stepF[BJT Current Amplification - Gain 1 plus h sub fe]
    stepF --> stepG[Collector Current I sub C]
    stepG --> stepH[Load Resistor R sub L Converts Current to Voltage]
    stepH --> stepI[Output V sub out - Proportional to Light Intensity]
    stepI --> stepJ[Microcontroller ADC / Comparator Input]
```

## 4.4 Application Block — Line-Following Robot Sensor

The most common robotics application is a line-follower using an IR phototransistor. The Mermaid block diagram below captures the full signal chain from emitter LED to microcontroller decision.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IR LED Emitter - 940 nm] --> B[Surface - White Reflects, Black Absorbs]
    B --> C[Phototransistor - Detects Reflected IR]
    C --> D[Load Resistor R sub L - Converts to Voltage]
    D --> E[Comparator or ADC of Microcontroller]
    E --> F{Microcontroller Decision Logic}
    F -->|Black Line Detected| G[Adjust Motor PWM - Turn]
    F -->|White Surface Detected| H[Continue Forward]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU Part B answers, **always include a labelled circuit / block diagram**. A neatly drawn Mermaid-style or hand-drawn diagram with proper labels and annotations typically secures 2–3 bonus marks even when the textual explanation is average.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
Define a phototransistor. Why is the base region of a phototransistor kept physically thin and wide?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A phototransistor is a light-sensitive bipolar junction transistor in which the base–collector junction is exposed to incident optical radiation. The photo-generated carriers act as the base current, and the transistor's current gain amplifies this into a much larger collector current.

The base is kept **physically thin** (typically 1–3 µm) to (i) maximise the probability of photon absorption taking place in the base–collector depletion region rather than the neutral base, and (ii) minimise the base transit time, which improves response speed. It is kept **wide (large area)** to capture more incident photons, thereby increasing sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "thin" reason, 1 mark for "wide" reason.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**  
Compare the response time and sensitivity of a photodiode and a phototransistor. In which robotics application is each preferred?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|---|---|---|
| Response time | Very fast (ns) | Slower (µs) |
| Sensitivity (output per mW) | Low (no gain) | High ($1+h_{fe}$ times photodiode) |

- **Photodiode** is preferred in **high-speed, precision applications** such as laser range finders, optical communication receivers, and LIDAR modules in autonomous robots.
- **Phototransistor** is preferred in **low-speed, low-cost, high-sensitivity applications** such as line-following robots, IR proximity sensors, optical encoders, and TV-remote-like modulation receivers.

*(1 mark each for the comparison, 1 mark for the application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper – Module 3, Q1(a)(b)]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an NPN phototransistor. (7 marks)

**(b)** A silicon phototransistor has a quantum efficiency of 0.65 at 900 nm and a current gain of $h_{fe} = 120$. It is illuminated with optical power of 100 µW. The load resistor is $R_L = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ and supply $V_{CC} = 5\ \text{V}$. Determine:
   1. The photocurrent in the base–collector junction.
   2. The collector current.
   3. The output voltage across $R_L$.
   4. The effective responsivity of the phototransistor. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks):**

The NPN phototransistor has three semiconductor layers: an n-type emitter on top, a thin p-type base in the middle, and a thick n-type collector at the bottom. A transparent glass window above the emitter allows photons to reach the **base–collector junction**, which is the photosensitive region. The B–C junction is reverse-biased by the external supply through a load resistor $R_L$.

Working principle: Photons with energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ are absorbed in the B–C depletion region and generate electron–hole pairs. The strong electric field sweeps electrons into the collector and holes into the base. This hole current into the base acts as the base current $I_{ph}$ of the transistor, which is amplified by the current gain $(1+h_{fe})$, producing a large collector current $I_C = (1+h_{fe})I_{ph}$. The load resistor converts this current into a measurable output voltage.

*(2 marks diagram, 3 marks working steps, 1 mark output relationship, 1 mark application mention.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Photocurrent:**

$$I_{ph} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{opt}}{h \cdot c}$$

$$I_{ph} = \frac{0.65 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$I_{ph} = \frac{0.65 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 9 \times 10^{-11}}{1.987 \times 10^{-25}}$$

$$I_{ph} = \frac{9.372 \times 10^{-30}}{1.987 \times 10^{-25}} = 4.717 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{A} \approx 47.17\ \mu\text{A}$$

*(2 marks)*

**2. Collector current (neglecting dark current):**

$$I_C = (1 + h_{fe}) \cdot I_{ph} = 121 \times 47.17\ \mu\text{A} = 5.708\ \text{mA}$$

*(1 mark)*

**3. Output voltage:**

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L = 5 - (5.708 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1 \times 10^{3})$$

$$V_{out} = 5 - 5.708 = -0.708\ \text{V}$$

The negative value indicates the device is **saturated** (operating in saturation region). For linear operation, the minimum $R_L$ required is:

$$R_{L,min} = \frac{V_{CC}}{I_C} = \frac{5}{5.708 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 876\ \Omega$$

*(2 marks)*

**4. Effective responsivity:**

$$R_{eff} = \frac{I_C}{P_{opt}} = \frac{5.708 \times 10^{-3}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 57.08\ \text{A/W}$$

*(2 marks — including the correct identification that effective responsivity includes the transistor gain.)*

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper – Module 3, Q2(a)(b)]**

**(a)** Draw the V–I characteristics of a phototransistor and explain its three operating regions. How is the device used as a switch in digital robotics applications? (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expressions for (i) spectral responsivity, (ii) quantum efficiency, and (iii) the cut-off wavelength of a phototransistor. Use Si parameters: $E_g = 1.12\ \text{eV}$ and compute the cut-off wavelength in nm. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) V–I Characteristics and Switching Application (7 marks):**

The V–I characteristic plots $I_C$ (y-axis) against $V_{CE}$ (x-axis) with incident light intensity $E$ (in mW/cm² or lux) as the parameter. Three regions exist:

- **Active region** ($V_{CE} > V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2\ \text{V}$): $I_C$ is nearly independent of $V_{CE}$ and equals $(1+h_{fe})I_{ph}$. This is the analog amplification region.
- **Saturation region** ($V_{CE} \le 0.2\ \text{V}$): $I_C$ is limited by the external load; the device behaves as a closed switch.
- **Cut-off region** (no light, $I_{ph} = 0$): only the tiny dark current $I_{CEO}$ flows; the device behaves as an open switch.

**Switching application:** A digital optocoupler or an IR proximity sensor uses the phototransistor in the **saturation/cut-off** mode. When the IR beam is unbroken, the phototransistor is in saturation → output LOW (logic 0). When the beam is broken, the phototransistor enters cut-off → output HIGH (logic 1). This gives a clean digital signal to a microcontroller GPIO for object detection, limit sensing, or rotary encoding in robotics.

*(2 marks V–I diagram, 3 marks region explanation, 2 marks switching application.)*

**(b) Derivations (7 marks):**

**(i) Spectral responsivity:** Starting from the number of photons per second, $N_{ph} = P_{opt} \lambda / hc$, of which a fraction $\eta$ generate collectable e–h pairs, the photocurrent is $I_{ph} = \eta q \lambda P_{opt} / hc$. The responsivity is:

$$R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$$

*(2 marks derivation)*

**(ii) Quantum efficiency:** Solving the responsivity expression for $\eta$:

$$\eta = \frac{R \cdot h \cdot c}{q \cdot \lambda}$$

with the constraint $0 \le \eta \le 1$.

*(2 marks derivation)*

**(iii) Cut-off wavelength:** Photons of energy less than $E_g$ cannot generate e–h pairs, so the cut-off condition is $h\nu = E_g$, i.e., $hc/\lambda_c = E_g$. Therefore:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{h \cdot c}{E_g}$$

For silicon, $E_g = 1.12\ \text{eV} = 1.12 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} = 1.794 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$:

$$\lambda_c = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 2.998 \times 10^{8}}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}}$$

$$\lambda_c = \frac{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.107 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 1107\ \text{nm}$$

*(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final answer with units.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in phototransistor questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include $(1+h_{fe})$ in the collector-current equation** — many students write $I_C = I_{ph}$ instead of $I_C = (1+h_{fe})I_{ph}$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 2. **Mixing wavelength units** — entering $\lambda$ in nm instead of m (or vice versa) gives an answer off by $10^9$. Always do a sanity check: typical $I_{ph}$ for µW optical power should be in the µA range.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** — "neglecting dark current" and "active region operation" must be explicitly written in the answer script. Examiners deduct ½ mark for every missing assumption.
> 4. **Confusing photodiode equations with phototransistor equations** — phototransistors have an extra $(1+h_{fe})$ factor. Marking it wrong costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — a 14-mark question without a labelled diagram (block/circuit) typically loses 1.5–2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting quantum efficiency is dimensionless** — if your $\eta$ comes out greater than 1, double-check units before submission.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- A **phototransistor** is a light-sensitive BJT where the base–collector junction is the photon-absorbing element.
- It provides **internal current gain** of $(1+h_{fe})$, typically 100–1000.
- Photocurrent: $I_{ph} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda \cdot P_{opt}}{h \cdot c}$.
- Spectral responsivity: $R = \dfrac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h \cdot c}$, units **A/W**.
- Quantum efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{R \cdot h \cdot c}{q \cdot \lambda}$, dimensionless, $0 \le \eta \le 1$.
- Collector current: $I_C = I_{CEO} + (1+h_{fe})I_{ph}$.
- Output voltage (common-emitter): $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L$.
- **Cut-off wavelength:** $\lambda_c = \dfrac{h \cdot c}{E_g} \approx 1.107\ \mu\text{m}$ for Si.
- **Three operating regions:** cut-off, active, saturation (same as BJT).
- **Two-terminal** mode = simpler, slower, higher dark current. **Three-terminal** mode = adjustable, faster, lower noise.
- **Typical bandwidth** of a phototransistor: 100 kHz – 1 MHz (much lower than photodiodes).
- **Spectral peak** for Si phototransistor: 800–900 nm — ideal match for IR LEDs at 850 nm / 940 nm used in robotics.
- **Primary applications in robotics:** line-following, object detection, optical encoders, optocouplers, ambient light sensing.
- **Physical constants** (must memorize): $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$, $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$.
- **Comparison mantra:** Photodiode = fast + linear + low-output. Phototransistor = sensitive + amplified + slow + non-linear.
- **Saturated device** → $V_{out}$ is negative (or near 0 V); pick $R_L$ large enough to keep the device in the **active region** for analog use.
- **Dark current $I_{CEO}$** is amplified dark current; can be reduced by providing an external base–emitter short or low-resistance path.
- In KTU 2024, the **(1 + $h_{fe}$) factor is non-negotiable** — it is the single most important feature distinguishing phototransistor from photodiode calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Range Sensor (LIDAR)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Range Sensor (LIDAR)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**LIDAR (Light Detection and Ranging)** is an active, opto-electronic remote sensing modality that determines the distance (range) to a remote target by illuminating it with coherent, monochromatic laser radiation and analyzing the properties of the returned (back-scattered) optical signal. In the context of **Robotics and Automation**, a LIDAR sensor is a non-contact ranging instrument that produces a high-resolution spatial map of the robot's surroundings in the form of a 2D or 3D point cloud, enabling functions such as obstacle detection, Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM), and autonomous navigation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis**
> A LIDAR is classified as an **exteroceptive**, **active** sensor. Unlike passive sensors (cameras), it generates its own illumination source, making it largely invariant to ambient lighting conditions and operable in total darkness.

The two foundational operating principles dominating robotics-grade LIDARs are:

1. **Time-of-Flight (ToF) / Pulsed LIDAR** — measures the round-trip time $\Delta t$ of a short laser pulse.
2. **Phase-Shift (Amplitude-Modulated Continuous Wave, AMCW) LIDAR** — measures the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ between transmitted and received modulated signals.
3. **Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) LIDAR** — measures beat frequency from a chirped signal (coherent detection).
4. **Triangulation LIDAR** — uses geometry of a projected light spot (sub-meter range only).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "A Bat with a Flashlight"**
> Imagine you are standing in a dark, vast, pitch-black football stadium and you want to know the distance to the opposite wall. You do not have a measuring tape. You take a flashlight, flash it once, and a wall-mounted mirror sends the flash back. You measure the round-trip time with a precise stopwatch.
> - **Speed of light** $c \approx \mathbf{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}$ is the "speed of the photon."
> - For a wall **1 meter** away, the round-trip is **2 m**, taking only $\approx \mathbf{6.67 \text{ nanoseconds}}$.
> - That is why LIDAR electronics are exquisitely fast — they must resolve **billionths of a second**.

Another analogy: a LIDAR is essentially a **"camera that knows the depth of every pixel."** A regular camera captures color/intensity; a LIDAR captures **distance** at every angular direction.

## 1.3 Key Wavelengths and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| Most common near-IR laser wavelength | **905 nm** (silicon detectors) |
| Eye-safe, telecom-grade wavelength | **1550 nm** (InGaAs detectors) |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c = 2.998 \times 10^8$ m/s |
| Eye-safety classification (IEC 60825) | **Class 1** (eye-safe) for robotics |
| Typical range (automotive/AMR) | **0.05 m – 200 m** |
| Typical scan rate (2D) | **5 – 30 Hz** |
| Angular resolution | **0.1° – 1°** |
| Range accuracy | **± 2 cm – ± 5 cm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **905 nm vs 1550 nm — Why does the wavelength matter?**
> 905 nm is cheap and uses silicon sensors, but the eye transmits and focuses it onto the retina, risking retinal damage — so power is legally capped. 1550 nm is absorbed by the cornea before reaching the retina, allowing **much higher transmit power** and consequently **longer range (300+ m)** at the same Class 1 safety level. This is why premium automotive LIDARs (e.g., Aeva, Innoviz) prefer 1550 nm.

## 1.4 System-Level Block View

A LIDAR is composed of four tightly coupled subsystems:

- **Laser Emitter (Transmitter):** pulsed or modulated laser diode + driver.
- **Receiver Optics & Detector:** focusing lens, narrow-band optical filter, photodiode (APD / SPAD / PIN).
- **Scanning Mechanism:** rotating polygon mirror, MEMS mirror, Risley prism, or solid-state OPA.
- **Timing / Signal Processing Electronics:** TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter), FPGA, point-cloud generator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle 1 — Pulsed Time-of-Flight (ToF) LIDAR

This is the **most common** technique used in robotics.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. A laser driver emits a very short optical pulse (typical width $\tau_p = \mathbf{1\text{–}10 \text{ ns}}$) of peak optical power $P_t$.
2. The pulse travels to the target at speed $c$ and is diffusely reflected (Lambertian scattering in most robotics scenarios).
3. A small fraction of the photons return to the receiver aperture and are focused onto a high-speed photodetector.
4. A **TDC** measures the time interval $\Delta t$ between the outgoing trigger and the detected return pulse.
5. The range $R$ is computed as half the round-trip optical path:

$$
R = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{2}
$$

6. The process is repeated while the beam is scanned azimuthally (and/or elevationally) to build a **point cloud** $\{(R_i, \theta_i, \phi_i)\}$.

**Why ToF works so well for robotics:**
- **Long range** capability (hundreds of meters).
- **Insensitive to target reflectance variations** for timing-based detection (only amplitude is affected).
- **Single-shot** measurement — no need for modulation/demodulation.

## 2.2 Operating Principle 2 — AMCW (Phase-Shift) LIDAR

Used in mid/short-range indoor robotics (e.g., robot vacuums, AGVs).

**Step-by-step logic:**
