P_{\text{load}} = \frac{F}{A_{\text{piston}}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{3}}{25 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.2 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure drop across FCV (1.5 marks):**

$$
\Delta P_{\text{FCV}} = P_{\text{supply}} - P_{\text{load}} = 20 - 3.2 = 16.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Orifice area (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_o &= \frac{Q}{C_d \sqrt{2\Delta P / \rho}} \\
&= \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.62 \cdot \sqrt{2 \times 16.8 \times 10^{6} / 870}} \\
&= \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.62 \cdot \sqrt{3.862 \times 10^{4}}} \\
&= \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{0.62 \times 196.52} \\
&= \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4}}{121.84} \\
&\approx 5.13 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2} \approx 5.13 \text{ mm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Sub-result | Value | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Q | 37.5 L/min | 1.5 |
| P_load | 3.2 MPa | 1.5 |
| ΔP_FCV | 16.8 MPa | 1.5 |
| A_o | 5.13 mm² | 2.5 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall for B2(b)**
> (i) Many students apply the load force over the *annular* area on the extension side. **The piston (cap-end) area is correct for extension** because pressure acts on the full piston face. (ii) Failing to convert L/min to m³/s is the second most frequent error. (iii) Confusing the *direction* of the load — if the load *aids* motion (gravity-driven extension), the load pressure subtracts differently and may even go *negative*, requiring a regenerative or load-holding circuit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (memorize the night before ESE)**

- ☐ **Hydraulic actuator** = energy converter from pressure × flow → force × velocity.
- ☐ The three primary valve families are **DCV, PCV, FCV** — know one application for each.
- ☐ **Valve notation "P/Q"** → P = number of ports/ways, Q = number of positions. 4/3 valve is the robotic industry default.
- ☐ **Centre conditions of 4/3 DCV:** Tandem (energy saving, hold), Open (mobile, drift), Closed (precision, dead-head).
- ☐ **Solenoid + spring centred** is the standard electrical interface for robotic DCVs.
- ☐ **Pressure relief valve setting** = 1.10 to 1.25 × P_max working; protects pump.
- ☐ **Counterbalance valve** is *non-negotiable* for vertical/overhung robotic loads.
- ☐ **Meter-out** is preferred over meter-in for gravity-loaded actuators.
- ☐ **Servo vs Proportional:** servo has *closed-loop inner feedback* → higher bandwidth (100–500 Hz) and lower hysteresis (<1%).
- ☐ **Force asymmetry** of single-rod cylinders: F_extend > F_retract, v_extend < v_retract — same hydraulic power in both directions.
- ☐ **Master formulas:** F = P·A, v = Q/A, P_hyd = P·Q, t = AL/Q, Q = C_d·A_o·√(2ΔP/ρ).
- ☐ **Power loss in FCV** is dissipated as heat (ΔP × Q) — a major reason for load-sensing designs in modern robotics.
- ☐ **Hagen-Poiseuille** drop ΔP = 32μLv/D² is used for return-line sizing to keep pressure drop < 5% of supply.
- ☐ **C_v factor:** Q_US_gpm = C_v · √(ΔP_psi / SG); commonly used in industrial valve selection charts.
- ☐ **Bulk modulus β ≈ 1.4–1.7 GPa** governs the *effective stiffness* of the hydraulic spring — crucial for natural-frequency calculation of hydraulic servo systems.
- ☐ **Standard ISO VG oils:** 32, 46, 68; viscosity measured at 40 °C; used in robotic HPU selection.
- ☐ **Memorize** the symbol conventions of ISO 1219 for KTU's drawing-based questions.
- ☐ **Applications remember-table:** injection moulding (sequence + relief), excavator arm (DCV + counterbalance), CNC clamping (sequence), aircraft flaps (servo), injection-mould clamp force (reducing valve), die-casting safety (relief).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 — Hydraulic Actuator: Control Valves and Applications**
> This concludes the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 generated study notes. Cross-reference the formulas and examples with the official KTU 2024 Scheme PCRAT205 Module 4 syllabus and the Herbert Merritt / Majumdar hydraulic-control textbook chapters for additional practice problems. Best wishes for your ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Actuating Systems: Electric Motors as Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING) > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electrical Actuating Systems: Electric Motors as Actuators # MODULE 4 — ELECTRICAL ACTUATING SYSTEMS: ELECTRIC MOTORS AS ACTUATORS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Electrical Actuating System:** An electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms **electrical input energy** (voltage and current) into **mechanical output energy** (rotational or linear motion) through the interaction of magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors, governed by the Lorentz force principle and Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction. In the context of **robotic systems**, an *electric motor* serves as the primary actuator responsible for generating the **torque**, **speed**, and **positional accuracy** required to drive joints, wheels, grippers, and end-effectors. KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes these under **Module 4 — Electrical Actuating Systems**, emphasizing the link between motor characteristics and robotic manipulator performance. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Muscle of the Robot" > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy:** Think of an electric motor as the **bicep muscle of a robot**. Just as your bicep contracts (using chemical energy) to lift a dumbbell (mechanical work), the motor receives electrical energy from a battery or supply and produces torque to rotate a robotic joint. The **voltage (V)** is like the *signal from your brain* telling the muscle how hard to work, and the **current (I)** is the *blood flow* delivering the actual effort. Different motor "muscle types" (DC, Stepper, Servo, BLDC) are like fast-twitch vs. slow-twitch fibers — each suited to specific tasks in the robotic body. ### 1.3 Classification of Electric Motors Used in Robotics | Motor Type | Symbol | Typical Robotic Application | |---|---|---| | Brushed DC Motor | BDCM | Small mobile robots, toy robots, conveyor drives | | Brushless DC Motor | BLDC | Drones, robotic arms, CNC machines, EV wheels | | DC Servo Motor | DCSM | Industrial robotic arms, CNC axes, camera gimbals | | AC Servo Motor | ACSM | High-precision pick-and-place robots, humanoid joints | | Stepper Motor | STM | 3D printers, small CNC, open-loop positioning | | Induction Motor | IM | Heavy robotic platforms, AGVs, factory automation | | Synchronous Motor | SM | High-speed precision robotics, aerospace | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU Metric:** Motor performance is universally characterized by the **torque-speed (T-ω) curve**, the **power rating in Watts (W)**, the **efficiency (η) in %**, and the **back-EMF constant (Ke)** in V·s/rad. ### 1.4 Why Electric Motors Dominate Robotic Actuation > [!NOTE] > - **Clean operation:** No exhaust, no fuel — ideal for indoor and medical robotics. > - **High power density:** Modern BLDC motors exceed **5 kW/kg** at peak performance. > - **Precise controllability:** Speed, position, and torque can be electronically regulated via PWM, PID, or field-oriented control (FOC). > - **Ease of integration:** Compatible with microcontrollers (Arduino, STM32, ESP32) and industrial PLCs. > - **Repeatability:** Position accuracy of servo systems can reach **±0.001 mm** in high-end robotic arms. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles ### 2.1.1 The Lorentz Force Law (Foundation of All Motors) When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, a force is produced: $$\vec{F} = I(\vec{L} \times \vec{B})$$ - $\vec{F}$ = Force vector (N) - $I$ = Current (A) - $\vec{L}$ = Length vector of conductor (m) - $\vec{B}$ = Magnetic flux density (T) In a rotary motor, this linear force becomes a **torque** about the shaft axis. For a single coil of $N$ turns in a uniform radial field $B$ with effective rotor radius $r$: $$T = N \cdot B \cdot I \cdot A \cdot \sin(\theta)$$ where $A$ is the coil area and $\theta$ is the angle between the magnetic moment and field. ### 2.1.2 Back-EMF (Electromotive Force) When a motor's armature rotates inside a magnetic field, it acts as a **generator**, producing a voltage that opposes the applied voltage (Lenz's law). This is called **back-EMF**: $$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$ - $E_b$ = Back-EMF (V) - $K_e$ = Voltage constant (V·s/rad) - $\omega$ = Angular velocity (rad/s) > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Back-EMF is the reason a motor draws **high current at stall** (no rotation → no back-EMF) and **low current at no-load** (high back-EMF opposes supply). This is critical for robot designers to compute thermal limits. ### 2.1.3 Motor Equivalent Circuit & Voltage Equation Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit: $$V = E_b + I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt}$$ - $V$ = Applied armature voltage (V) - $R_a$ = Armature resistance (Ω) - $L_a$ = Armature inductance (H) At steady state ($\frac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$): $$V = K_e \omega + I_a R_a$$ ### 2.1.4 Torque Equation from Electrical Input The mechanical power developed equals electrical power delivered minus copper losses: $$P_{mech} = E_b \cdot I_a = K_e \omega \cdot I_a$$ Since $P_{mech} = T \cdot \omega$, we equate: $$T \cdot \omega = K_e \omega \cdot I_a$$ $$\boxed{T = K_t \cdot I_a}$$ where $K_t$ is the **torque constant** (N·m/A). In SI units, $K_t = K_e$. ### 2.1.5 DC Motor Torque-Speed Characteristic Combining the steady-state voltage and torque equations: $$V = K_e \omega + \frac{T}{K_t} R_a$$ Solving for $\omega$ as a function of $T$: $$\boxed{\omega = \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} T}$$ This is the **linear T-ω curve** of a DC motor — the foundation for all robotic motor sizing problems. - **No-load speed** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e}$ - **Stall torque** $T_{stall} = \dfrac{V \cdot K_t}{R_a}$ ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = I L B \sin\theta$ | Lorentz force on conductor | N | | 2 | $T = K_t I_a$ | Torque from armature current | N·m | | 3 | $E_b = K_e \omega$ | Back-EMF | V | | 4 | $V = K_e \omega + I_a R_a$ | Steady-state armature KVL | V | | 5 | $\omega = \dfrac{V}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a T}{K_e K_t}$ | Torque-speed line | rad/s | | 6 | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e}$ | No-load speed | rad/s | | 7 | $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t V}{R_a}$ | Stall torque | N·m | | 8 | $P_{mech} = T \omega$ | Mechanical output power | W | | 9 | $P_{in} = V I_a$ | Electrical input power | W | | 10 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Efficiency | % | | 11 | $\tau_e = \dfrac{L_a}{R_a}$ | Electrical time constant | s | | 12 | $\tau_m = \dfrac{J R_a}{K_e K_t}$ | Mechanical time constant | s | | 13 | $f_{step} = \dfrac{1}{\theta_s} \cdot \omega$ | Stepper stepping rate (open-loop) | steps/s | | 14 | $T_{det} = T_h - T_f$ | Detent torque in stepper | N·m | | 15 | $T_{pull-out} = \dfrac{K_t V_{phase}}{2 \pi f L_{phase}}$ | Stepper pull-out torque (approx.) | N·m | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$, where $N$ is in revolutions per minute. ## 2.3 Brushed DC Motor (BDCM) — Construction & Working - **Stator:** Permanent magnets (or field windings) producing a static field. - **Rotor (Armature):** Rotating coil winding connected to supply via **brush-commutator assembly**. - **Commutator:** Mechanical rotary switch that reverses armature current every half-turn to maintain unidirectional torque. **Robotic relevance:** Cheap, simple, high starting torque — used in low-cost wheeled robots (e.g., differential drive bases in educational robots). ## 2.4 Brushless DC Motor (BLDC) — Construction & Working - **Stator:** Three-phase winding (Y or Δ connected). - **Rotor:** Permanent magnet (no windings, no brushes). - **Commutation:** **Electronic** using Hall-effect sensors or sensorless back-EMF detection, driven by a 3-phase inverter (6 MOSFETs). **Advantages over BDCM:** Higher efficiency (**>90%**), longer life, higher power density, less maintenance, better thermal management. **Robotic relevance:** Drones (quadcopter BLDCs), humanoid joints (e.g., Dynamixel XM series), robotic exoskeletons. ## 2.5 DC Servo Motor — Closed-Loop Position Control A **servo motor** is a DC/BLDC motor integrated with: - **Position sensor** (encoder or potentiometer) - **Controller** (PID-based) - **Feedback loop** that compares commanded vs. actual position **Block-level operation:** Command → Error → PID → PWM → Motor → Gearbox → Output shaft → Encoder feedback. **Robotic relevance:** Industrial 6-DOF arms (KUKA, ABB, Fanuc), pick-and-place machines, surgical robots. ## 2.6 Stepper Motor — Open-Loop Digital Positioning - **Construction:** Salient-pole rotor (permanent magnet or variable reluctance) and multi-tooth stator windings. - **Operation:** Energizes stator phases in a specific sequence to rotate in **discrete steps** (commonly **1.8°/step**, i.e., **200 steps/rev**). - **Step angles:** $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_s}$, where $N_s$ is the number of steps per revolution. - **Drive modes:** Full-step, half-step, microstepping (e.g., 1/32 step → 6400 steps/rev). **Robotic relevance:** 3D printers (Prusa, Creality), small CNC routers, camera sliders, low-cost Cartesian robots. > [!NOTE] > **Critical limitation:** Open-loop steppers can **lose steps** under overload → open-loop positional error. Solution: add an external encoder to close the loop (closed-loop stepper / servo-stepper hybrid). ## 2.7 AC Motors in Robotics ### 2.7.1 Induction Motor (IM) - Robust, low cost, high reliability. - **Slip** $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ is essential — induction motors *cannot* run at synchronous speed. - Used in heavy AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) and industrial conveyor robots. ### 2.7.2 Synchronous Motor (SM) - Rotor rotates at synchronous speed $N_s = \dfrac{120 f}{P}$ (where $P$ = number of poles). - No slip, precise speed. - Permanent magnet synchronous motors (PMSM) dominate high-performance humanoid robots (e.g., Tesla Optimus, Boston Dynamics Atlas). ## 2.8 Motor Selection for Robotic Applications — Engineering Decision Matrix | Selection Criterion | Best Choice | Reason | |---|---|---| | High precision position | Servo (DC/AC) | Closed-loop feedback | | Low cost open-loop position | Stepper | No encoder needed | | High power-to-weight | BLDC / PMSM | No brushes, high efficiency | | Battery-powered mobility | BLDC | High efficiency extends runtime | | Very low cost toy robot | Brushed DC | Cheapest, simplest driver | | Industrial heavy payload | Induction / PMSM | Robust, high continuous torque | | Explosive/clean environment | Brushless (no brush sparks) | No commutation arcing | ## 2.9 Power, Efficiency & Thermal Considerations - **Continuous power rating:** Limited by heat dissipation. - **Peak torque:** Limited magnetically (saturation) and thermally (brief overloads allowed). - **Servo sizing rule of thumb:** $T_{motor} \geq \dfrac{T_{load}}{G \cdot \eta_g}$, where $G$ is gearbox ratio and $\eta_g$ is gearbox efficiency. - **RMS torque** for cyclic robotic motions: $$T_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{t_{total}} \sum_{i} T_i^2 \cdot t_i}$$ The selected motor's continuous torque rating must exceed $T_{rms}$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: DC Motor Torque-Speed Equation from First Principles ### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature The armature is an RL circuit with induced back-EMF: $$V = I_a R_a + L_a \frac{dI_a}{dt} + E_b$$ At **steady state**, current is constant, so $L_a \dfrac{dI_a}{dt} = 0$: $$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$ ### Step 2 — Express back-EMF in terms of angular velocity The back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity by the motor voltage constant: $$E_b = K_e \omega$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute back-EMF into the KVL equation $$V = I_a R_a + K_e \omega$$ Solve for armature current: $$I_a = \frac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the torque-current relationship $$T = K_t I_a$$ Substitute the expression for $I_a$: $$T = K_t \left( \frac{V - K_e \omega}{R_a} \right)$$ ### Step 5 — Rearrange to express ω as a function of T $$T R_a = K_t V - K_t K_e \omega$$ $$K_t K_e \omega = K_t V - T R_a$$ $$\omega = \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \cdot T$$ This is the **linear torque-speed characteristic** of a separately-excited (or permanent-magnet) DC motor. ### Step 6 — Identify the two intercepts - At $T = 0$ (no load): $\omega_0 = \dfrac{V}{K_e}$ → **no-load speed** - At $\omega = 0$ (stall): $T_{stall} = \dfrac{K_t V}{R_a}$ → **stall torque** These are the key KTU exam parameters. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Power Flow & Efficiency of a DC Motor ### Step 1 — Electrical input power $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_a$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute KVL expression for V $$P_{in} = (E_b + I_a R_a) I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$$ ### Step 3 — Identify the components - $I_a^2 R_a$ = **Armature copper loss** ($P_{cu}$) - $E_b I_a$ = **Converted (air-gap) mechanical power** ($P_{ag}$) ### Step 4 — Subtract rotational losses $$P_{out} = P_{ag} - P_{rot}$$ where $P_{rot}$ includes friction, windage, and core losses. ### Step 5 — Efficiency $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{P_{ag} - P_{rot}}{E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stepper Motor Step Angle and Resolution ### Step 1 — Step angle definition A stepper motor advances by a fixed mechanical angle per electrical input pulse: $$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_s}$$ where $N_s$ is the number of steps per revolution. ### Step 2 — For a standard hybrid stepper with $N_r$ rotor teeth and stator phases $$N_s = m \cdot N_r$$ where $m$ is the number of phases (commonly 2). ### Step 3 — Numerical example: 1.8° stepper $$N_s = \frac{360°}{1.8°} = 200 \text{ steps/rev}$$ With **1/16 microstepping**: $$N_{s,micro} = 200 \times 16 = 3200 \text{ steps/rev}$$ $$\theta_{s,micro} = \frac{360°}{3200} = 0.1125°/\text{step}$$ ### Step 4 — Linear displacement per step (with leadscrew of pitch $p$) $$L_{step} = \frac{p}{N_{s,micro}}$$ For a 3D printer with $p = 2$ mm leadscrew and 1/16 microstepping: $$L_{step} = \frac{2 \text{ mm}}{3200} = 0.625 \text{ μm/step}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation:** This micro-resolution explains why 3D printers achieve layer heights of **0.1 mm** consistently. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Power Point of a DC Motor ### Step 1 — Mechanical output power $$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the T-ω relation $$P_{out} = T \left( \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{R_a T}{K_e K_t} \right) = \frac{V T}{K_e} - \frac{R_a T^2}{K_e K_t}$$ ### Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to T and set to zero for maximum $$\frac{dP_{out}}{dT} = \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{2 R_a T}{K_e K_t} = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $T_{max power}$ $$T_{max power} = \frac{K_t V}{2 R_a} = \frac{T_{stall}}{2}$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute back to find maximum power and corresponding speed $$\omega_{at P_{max}} = \frac{V}{K_e} - \frac{R_a}{K_e K_t} \cdot \frac{K_t V}{2 R_a} = \frac{V}{2 K_e} = \frac{\omega_0}{2}$$ $$P_{max} = T_{max power} \cdot \omega_{at P_{max}} = \frac{K_t V}{2 R_a} \cdot \frac{V}{2 K_e} = \frac{V^2 K_t}{4 R_a K_e}$$ $$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_a}}$$ since $K_t = K_e$ in SI units. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Insight:** A DC motor delivers **maximum mechanical power at exactly half the no-load speed and half the stall torque** (the midpoint of the T-ω line). ## 3.5 Derivation 5: Servo Motor Transfer Function (Simplified) ### Step 1 — Armature circuit equation (Laplace domain) $$V(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_e \omega(s)$$ ### Step 2 — Torque equation (mechanical load) $$T(s) = K_t I_a(s) = (J s + B) \omega(s)$$ ### Step 3 — Solve $I_a(s)$ from Step 2 $$I_a(s) = \frac{(J s + B) \omega(s)}{K_t}$$ ### Step 4 — Substitute into Step 1 $$V(s) = (R_a + L_a s) \frac{(J s + B) \omega(s)}{K_t} + K_e \omega(s)$$ ### Step 5 — Group the $\omega(s)$ terms $$V(s) = \omega(s) \left[ \frac{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B)}{K_t} + K_e \right]$$ ### Step 6 — Transfer function (angular velocity over voltage) $$\frac{\omega(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{K_t}{(R_a + L_a s)(J s + B) + K_e K_t}$$ This second-order system has two time constants — **electrical ($\tau_e = L_a/R_a$)** and **mechanical ($\tau_m = J R_a / (K_e K_t)$)** — which the robot's PID controller must dominate for stable tracking. ## 3.6 Numerical Problem — Solved Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A permanent-magnet DC motor used in a robotic joint has the following nameplate data: - Rated voltage: $V = 24$ V - Armature resistance: $R_a = 0.6$ Ω - Torque constant: $K_t = 0.05$ N·m/A - Voltage constant: $K_e = 0.05$ V·s/rad - No-load current (friction): $I_0 = 0.4$ A **Find:** (a) No-load speed, (b) Stall torque, (c) Maximum mechanical power, (d) Efficiency at rated load if the motor draws $I_a = 5$ A. ### (a) No-load speed Armature current at no-load: $I_0 = 0.4$ A (overcomes friction only). $$E_{b,0} = V - I_0 R_a = 24 - (0.4)(0.6) = 24 - 0.24 = 23.76 \text{ V}$$ $$\omega_0 = \frac{E_{b,0}}{K_e} = \frac{23.76}{0.05} = 475.2 \text{ rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$N_0 = \frac{475.2 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{28512}{6.2832} \approx 4537.6 \text{ RPM}$$ ### (b) Stall torque $$T_{stall} = \frac{K_t V}{R_a} = \frac{0.05 \times 24}{0.6} = \frac{1.2}{0.6} = 2.0 \text{ N·m}$$ ### (c) Maximum mechanical power Using the derived formula: $$P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_a} = \frac{24^2}{4 \times 0.6} = \frac{576}{2.4} = 240 \text{ W}$$ This occurs at $T = T_{stall}/2 = 1.0$ N·m and $\omega = \omega_0/2 \approx 237.6$ rad/s. ### (d) Efficiency at $I_a = 5$ A Back-EMF at this load: $$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 24 - (5)(0.6) = 24 - 3 = 21 \text{ V}$$ Angular speed: $$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{21}{0.05} = 420 \text{ rad/s}$$ Mechanical output power: $$P_{out} = E_b \cdot I_a = 21 \times 5 = 105 \text{ W}$$ Electrical input power: $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 24 \times 5 = 120 \text{ W}$$ Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{105}{120} \times 100 = 87.5\%$$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification:** Copper loss = $I_a^2 R_a = 25 \times 0.6 = 15$ W, and $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{cu} = 105 + 15 = 120$ W ✓ ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — DC Motor Operating Points | Operating Point | Torque $T$ | Speed $\omega$ | Power $P = T\omega$ | Application in Robotics | |---|---|---|---|---| | **No-load** | $\approx 0$ | $\omega_0$ | $0$ | Joint homing, free spin | | **Quarter load** | $T_{stall}/4$ | $3\omega_0/4$ | $3 P_{max}/8$ | Slow manipulation | | **Half load (Max Power)** | $T_{stall}/2$ | $\omega_0/2$ | $P_{max}$ | Optimum pick-and-place | | **Three-quarter load** | $3T_{stall}/4$ | $\omega_0/4$ | $3P_{max}/8$ | Heavy slow lift | | **Stall** | $T_{stall}$ | $0$ | $0$ | Joint lock, gripping | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Electric Motor Actuation Loop for Robotics > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the closed-loop architecture of a typical robotic electric actuator (servo-style) with all functional blocks: command input, error generation, controller, power electronics, motor, gearbox, load, and feedback sensor. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Command Input
Position or Velocity] --> B[Subtractor
Error Computation] B --> C[Controller
PID or FOC] C --> D[PWM / Inverter
Power Electronics] D --> E[Electric Motor
DC BLDC Stepper] E --> F[Gearbox
Speed Reduction] F --> G[Robotic Joint
Mechanical Load] G --> H1[Encoder / Resolver
Position Feedback] G --> H2[Tachometer
Speed Feedback] H1 --> B H2 --> C E --> I[Back EMF
EMF Coupling] I --> D style A fill:#e3f2fd style C fill:#fff9c4 style E fill:#ffccbc style G fill:#c8e6c9 ``` **Interpretation of the block diagram:** - The **command** (desired joint angle from trajectory planner) is compared with **actual** position from the encoder. - The **error** drives a **PID controller** that outputs a voltage or PWM duty cycle. - The **power electronics** stage (H-bridge for DC, 3-phase inverter for BLDC) amplifies this signal to drive the motor. - The **gearbox** trades speed for torque — typical industrial robots use harmonic drives (ratio 30:1 to 100:1) with efficiency **~80%**. - The **back-EMF coupling** block represents the inherent electromechanical feedback that stabilizes speed naturally in DC motors. ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Electric Motors for Robotics ```mermaid flowchart TB M[Electric Motors
for Robotics] --> DC[DC Motors] M --> AC[AC Motors] M --> SP[Special Motors] DC --> BDCM[Brushed DC
Simple Low Cost] DC --> BLDC[Brushless DC
High Efficiency] DC --> DCSM[DC Servo
Closed Loop Position] AC --> ACSM[AC Servo
High Precision Industrial] AC --> IM[Induction
Heavy AGV Drives] AC --> PMSM[Permanent Magnet Sync
Humanoid Joints] SP --> STM[Stepper
Open Loop Position] SP --> RSM[Reluctance
Variable Reluctance] SP --> LIM[Linear Induction
Linear Actuators] style M fill:#bbdefb style DC fill:#ffe0b2 style AC fill:#c8e6c9 style SP fill:#f8bbd0 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Torque-Speed Operating Regions ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Stall Region
Zero Speed Max Torque] --> B[Acceleration Region
High Torque Mid Speed] B --> C[Constant Power Region
Mid Torque High Speed] C --> D[Constant Torque Region
Rated Torque Rated Speed] D --> E[Field Weakening Region
Low Torque Max Speed] E --> F[No Load Region
Min Torque No Load Speed] A -.Joint Hold.-> A B -.Robot Acceleration.-> B C -.Fast Motion.-> C D -.Normal Operation.-> D E -.High Speed Slew.-> E F -.Free Spin.-> F style A fill:#ffcdd2 style B fill:#ffe0b2 style C fill:#fff9c4 style D fill:#c8e6c9 style E fill:#bbdefb style F fill:#e1bee7 ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — H-Bridge Driver for Bidirectional DC Motor Control > [!NOTE] > Robotic actuators (wheels, arms) need bidirectional rotation. The **H-bridge** is the canonical power-electronic topology that uses four switches (typically MOSFETs or IGBTs) arranged in an "H" pattern around the motor. ```mermaid flowchart TB VCC[DC Supply V plus] --> Q1[Switch Q1 High Side Left] VCC --> Q2[Switch Q2 High Side Right] Q1 --> M1[Motor Terminal A] Q2 --> M2[Motor Terminal B] M1 --> Q3[Switch Q3 Low Side Left] M2 --> Q4[Switch Q4 Low Side Right] Q3 --> GND1[Ground] Q4 --> GND2[Ground] CTRL[Microcontroller
PWM Signals] --> DRV[Gate Driver IC] DRV --> Q1 DRV --> Q2 DRV --> Q3 DRV --> Q4 ENC[Encoder on Motor Shaft] --> FB[Position Feedback] FB --> CTRL style VCC fill:#ffeb3b style CTRL fill:#b3e5fc style DRV fill:#f8bbd0 style ENC fill:#c8e6c9 ``` **Switching truth table:** | Q1 | Q2 | Q3 | Q4 | Motor Action | |---|---|---|---|---| | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | Forward rotation | | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | Reverse rotation | | ON | OFF | ON | OFF | Dynamic braking (regenerative) | | OFF | OFF | OFF | OFF | Coast (free spin) | | ON | ON | — | — | **Shoot-through — DESTROY** | > [!WARNING] > **NEVER** turn on Q1 and Q3 simultaneously (or Q2 and Q4) — this creates a direct short across the supply (shoot-through), causing catastrophic MOSFET failure within microseconds. All H-bridge driver ICs include **dead-time insertion** to prevent this. ## 4.5 Block Diagram — Stepper Motor Microstepping Drive Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR STEP[Step Pulse Input] --> LS[Logic Sequencer
Phase Commutator] DIR[Direction Input] --> LS LS --> DAC1[Sin DAC Channel A] LS --> DAC2[Cos DAC Channel B] DAC1 --> A1[Power Amplifier Phase A] DAC2 --> A2[Power Amplifier Phase B] A1 --> COILA[Stator Phase A Coil] A2 --> COILB[Stator Phase B Coil] COILA --> ROTOR[Permanent Magnet
or Variable Reluctance Rotor] COILB --> ROTOR ROTOR --> SHAFT[Output Shaft
Discrete Steps] SHAFT --> LOAD[Robotic Linear Axis] style STEP fill:#b3e5fc style LS fill:#fff9c4 style ROTOR fill:#ffccbc style SHAFT fill:#c8e6c9 ``` **Microstepping concept:** By energizing the two phase coils with **sinusoidal currents 90° out of phase**, the resultant stator field vector rotates smoothly through $2^N$ intermediate positions per full step, where $N$ is the microstep division. For 1/32 microstepping of a 1.8° motor, the effective resolution becomes $1.8°/32 = 0.05625°$/step — 6400 steps/rev. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper] **Q: Define "back-EMF" in a DC motor. Why is it significant in robotic actuator selection?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Back-EMF** is the electromotive force induced in the armature of a rotating motor, opposing the applied voltage as per Lenz's law. Mathematically, $E_b = K_e \omega$, where $K_e$ is the voltage constant and $\omega$ is the angular velocity. **Significance in robotics (1 mark each point):** 1. **Speed feedback:** Back-EMF is proportional to speed → can be used as a sensorless speed estimator in low-cost BLDC drives. 2. **Current limiting:** At stall, $E_b = 0$, so the entire supply voltage drops across $R_a$, producing a large inrush current (used in stall-detection for gripper force control). 3. **Safe operating range:** The robot's controller must ensure $V > E_b$ always, else the motor will decelerate. This sets the **maximum safe speed** as $\omega_{max} = V / K_e$. **(Total: 3 marks)** --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: List any three differences between a stepper motor and a servo motor used in robotic applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per difference):** | Parameter | Stepper Motor | Servo Motor | |---|---|---| | Control type | Open-loop (no feedback needed) | Closed-loop (with encoder) | | Position accuracy | Can lose steps → uncertain | High accuracy, no step loss | | Torque at high speed | Drops sharply beyond pull-out torque | Maintains torque up to rated speed | | Cost | Lower (no encoder) | Higher (encoder + controller) | | Holding torque | High at standstill (detent torque) | Depends on controller | | Best use | 3D printers, low-cost Cartesian robots | Industrial 6-DOF arms, CNC | **(Total: 3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified] **Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a brushless DC (BLDC) motor used as an actuator in a robotic arm. Derive the torque-speed equation and discuss its advantages over a brushed DC motor in robotic applications.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Working Principle and Block Diagram **Working principle (3 marks):** A BLDC motor is a **synchronous motor** with a permanent-magnet rotor and a three-phase stator winding. Unlike a brushed DC motor, commutation is **electronic** rather than mechanical. The rotor position is detected using three **Hall-effect sensors** (or by sensorless back-EMF sensing), and a 3-phase inverter (six MOSFETs arranged as three half-bridges) energizes the appropriate stator phases in a six-step trapezoidal commutation sequence (or sinusoidal FOC). This creates a **rotating magnetic field** that drags the permanent-magnet rotor along at synchronous speed. **Block diagram (2 marks):** ```mermaid flowchart LR CTRL[Microcontroller] --> GATE[Gate Driver] HALL[Hall Sensors on Stator] --> CTRL GATE --> INV[3 Phase Inverter
6 MOSFETs] INV --> W1[Stator Phase A] INV --> W2[Stator Phase B] INV --> W3[Stator Phase C] W1 --> ROTOR[PM Rotor] W2 --> ROTOR W3 --> ROTOR ROTOR --> SHAFT[Joint Output] SHAFT --> FB[Encoder] FB --> CTRL style CTRL fill:#b3e5fc style INV fill:#ffe0b2 style ROTOR fill:#ffccbc ``` **Commutation sequence (2 marks):** Six steps, each 60° electrical, with two phases energized at any time. The Hall sensor 3-bit code $(H_1, H_2, H_3)$ determines which pair of MOSFETs to switch on. For example, step 1: phases A and B energized (A positive, B negative) when Hall code = 101. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Torque-Speed Equation and Advantages **Torque equation (3 marks):** For a BLDC with $N$ conductors per phase, flux $\phi$ per pole, and current $I$: $$T = \frac{3 \cdot N \cdot \phi \cdot I}{\pi} \quad \text{(for trapezoidal BLDC)}$$ or with sinusoidal commutation (FOC): $$T = \frac{3}{2} \cdot K_t \cdot I_q$$ where $I_q$ is the quadrature (torque-producing) current component. **Torque-speed equation (2 marks):** Like a DC motor, the BLDC has an approximately linear torque-speed curve in the constant-torque region: $$\omega = \frac{V_{dc}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot K_e} - \frac{R_s \cdot T}{K_e K_t}$$ Above base speed, the controller enters the **field-weakening region** by advancing the current angle → speed can exceed base speed at the cost of reduced torque. **Advantages over brushed DC (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Higher efficiency** (>90% vs. 75% for BDCM) — critical for battery-powered mobile robots. 2. **No brush wear** — longer life, no maintenance, suitable for inaccessible joints (humanoid legs). 3. **Better heat dissipation** — windings on stator can be directly cooled. 4. **Higher power density** — smaller and lighter for the same torque. 5. **No electromagnetic brush interference** — suitable for medical and EMC-sensitive robots. 6. **Higher speed range** — can run at 10,000+ RPM safely. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified] **Q: A permanent-magnet DC motor is used to drive the joint of a 6-DOF robotic arm. The motor specifications are: $V = 48$ V, $R_a = 0.8$ Ω, $K_t = 0.12$ N·m/A, $K_e = 0.12$ V·s/rad. Calculate: (a) the no-load speed, (b) the stall torque, (c) the maximum mechanical power and the operating point at which it occurs, and (d) the back-EMF, armature current, mechanical output power, and efficiency when the motor delivers a torque of 2.5 N·m. Also, explain the role of the gearbox in the robotic joint and state two safety considerations in motor selection.** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Motor Calculations **Given:** $$V = 48 \text{ V}, \quad R_a = 0.8 \text{ Ω}, \quad K_t = K_e = 0.12$$ **Step 1 — No-load speed $\omega_0$ (2 marks):** At no load, armature current is small (neglect for ideal calculation): $$\omega_0 = \frac{V}{K_e} = \frac{48}{0.12} = 400 \text{ rad/s}$$ Converting to RPM: $$N_0 = \frac{400 \times 60}{2\pi} = \frac{24000}{6.2832} \approx 3819.7 \text{ RPM}$$ **Step 2 — Stall torque $T_{stall}$ (1 mark):** At stall, $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$ and all supply voltage drops across $R_a$: $$I_{stall} = \frac{V}{R_a} = \frac{48}{0.8} = 60 \text{ A}$$ $$T_{stall} = K_t I_{stall} = 0.12 \times 60 = 7.2 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum mechanical power (2 marks):** A DC motor delivers maximum power at the midpoint of the T-ω line: $$T_{mpp} = \frac{T_{stall}}{2} = \frac{7.2}{2} = 3.6 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\omega_{mpp} = \frac{\omega_0}{2} = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$P_{max} = T_{mpp} \cdot \omega_{mpp} = 3.6 \times 200 = 720 \text{ W}$$ Cross-check using the formula: $$P_{max} = \frac{V^2}{4 R_a} = \frac{48^2}{4 \times 0.8} = \frac{2304}{3.2} = 720 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Operating point at $T = 2.5$ N·m (2 marks):** Armature current: $$I_a = \frac{T}{K_t} = \frac{2.5}{0.12} \approx 20.83 \text{ A}$$ Back-EMF: $$E_b = V - I_a R_a = 48 - (20.83)(0.8) = 48 - 16.67 = 31.33 \text{ V}$$ Angular speed: $$\omega = \frac{E_b}{K_e} = \frac{31.33}{0.12} \approx 261.1 \text{ rad/s}$$ Mechanical output power: $$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 2.5 \times 261.1 = 652.8 \text{ W}$$ Input power: $$P_{in} = V \cdot I_a = 48 \times 20.83 = 1000 \text{ W}$$ Efficiency: $$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{652.8}{1000} \times 100 = 65.28\%$$ #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Gearbox Role and Safety **Role of the gearbox in a robotic joint (4 marks):** Robotic joints typically require **high torque at low speed** (e.g., a 6-DOF arm lifting a 5 kg payload at 0.5 rad/s). However, electric motors naturally produce **high speed, low torque** (e.g., 400 rad/s at only 0.5 N·m). The gearbox performs the following functions: 1. **Speed reduction & torque multiplication:** A harmonic drive with ratio $G = 100:1$ multiplies motor torque by 100 (minus ~20% efficiency loss) → 50 N·m at 4 rad/s at the joint. Formula: $$T_{joint} = G \cdot \eta_g \cdot T_{motor}$$ 2. **Inertia reflection:** The load inertia $J_L$ seen by the motor is reduced by $G^2$: $$J_{reflected} = \frac{J_L}{G^2}$$ For $G = 100$, a 10 kg·cm² load appears as 0.001 kg·cm² at the motor — making the control loop much faster. 3. **Back-drivability and stiffness:** Harmonic drives offer high stiffness (good for positioning) but low back-drivability (robot cannot be pushed easily). Cycloidal and planetary gears offer better back-drive for collaborative robots (cobots). 4. **Resolution improvement:** A 1.8° stepper with a 50:1 gearbox delivers $1.8°/50 = 0.036°$ at the output. **Two safety considerations in motor selection (3 marks — 1.5 marks each):** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently forget to include the **safety factor** in their motor sizing. In an exam, if you size a motor to exactly match the load, you will lose marks for not including a **1.5×–2× safety margin** for transients, wear, and unknown payloads. 1. **Thermal safety:** The motor's continuous torque rating must exceed the **RMS torque** of the duty cycle with at least a **1.5× safety factor** to prevent insulation failure. Add a current limit in the controller. 2. **Mechanical safety:** Peak torque during emergency stops or collisions can be **3×–5× the rated torque**. Ensure the gearbox, couplings, and shafts are rated for these transients, and integrate **current/torque limits** in software. 3. **Electrical safety:** Include a **flyback diode** or **braking resistor** to dissipate regenerative energy when the motor decelerates suddenly, preventing DC bus over-voltage. 4. **Encoder/feedback safety:** Use **absolute encoders** for collaborative robots so that position is known at power-on (no unsafe homing motion required). 5. **EMC and EMI:** Use **shielded cables** and **isolated gate drivers** to prevent noise from corrupting encoder signals or nearby sensors. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist — read this twice before the exam!** - [x] **Motor = electromechanical energy converter**; converts $V \cdot I$ (electrical) into $T \cdot \omega$ (mechanical). - [x] **Lorentz force** $F = ILB\sin\theta$ is the foundational principle. - [x] **Back-EMF** $E_b = K_e \omega$ opposes applied voltage and is **zero at stall**. - [x] **Steady-state DC motor KVL:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$. - [x] **Torque equation:** $T = K_t I_a$ where $K_t$ has units N·m/A. - [x] **In SI units:** $K_t = K_e$ (numerically and dimensionally). - [x] **Linear T-ω equation:** $\omega = \dfrac{V}{K_e} - \dfrac{R_a T}{K_e K_t}$. - [x] **No-load speed** $\omega_0 = V / K_e$; **stall torque** $T_{stall} = K_t V / R_a$. - [x] **Maximum mechanical power** occurs at $T = T_{stall}/2$, $\omega = \omega_0/2$, with $P_{max} = V^2 / (4R_a)$. - [x] **Conversion formulas:** $1$ RPM $= \dfrac{2\pi}{60}$ rad/s $\approx 0.1047$ rad/s. - [x] **RMS torque** for cyclic motion: $T_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{t_{total}} \sum T_i^2 t_i}$. - [x] **Motor sizing rule:** $T_{motor} \geq T_{load} / (G \cdot \eta_g)$ with **1.5×–2× safety factor**. - [x] **BLDC advantages:** Higher efficiency, no brush wear, better heat dissipation, higher power density. - [x] **Stepper resolution:** $\theta_s = 360°/N_s$; with 1/16 microstepping on a 1.8° motor → 3200 steps/rev. - [x] **Servo motors** are **closed-loop** (encoder + PID); **steppers** are mostly **open-loop**. - [x] **H-bridge caution:** **Never** turn on both high-side and low-side switches on the same leg simultaneously (shoot-through destroys the FETs). - [x] **Gearbox reduces inertia** by factor $G^2$ at the motor shaft → faster control response. - [x] **Induction motor slip** $s = (N_s - N_r) / N_s$ is non-zero under load. - [x] **Synchronous motor speed** $N_s = 120 f / P$, independent of load (no slip). - [x] **PMSM (Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor)** is the workhorse of modern humanoid robots due to its high torque density and efficiency. - [x] **Common exam traps:** - Forgetting to convert RPM to rad/s (or vice versa). - Confusing $K_t$ (torque constant) with $K_e$ (voltage constant) — they have different units but equal numerical value in SI. - Calculating stall current without considering the motor's thermal limit. - Selecting a stepper for a high-speed application where it would lose steps. - [x] **Final KTU mantras:** - "At maximum power, torque and speed are both half their extremes." - "Back-EMF is your friend — it limits current and stabilizes speed." - "For precision, use servo. For cost, use stepper. For power, use BLDC."

topics.title: Overview of Electric Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Overview of Electric Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Electric Motor (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **electric motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy into mechanical energy (specifically, rotational mechanical work) by exploiting the interaction between **current-carrying conductors** and **magnetic fields**, governed fundamentally by the **Lorentz Force Law** and **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**.

In the context of **Robotics and Artificial Engineering**, electric motors serve as the **primary actuators** (also called "prime movers" or "drives") of a robotic system. They provide the controlled torque and angular displacement required to actuate joints, drive wheels, position end-effectors, and animate kinematic chains. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for PCRAT205, electric motors are classified as **active actuators** because they require an external power source and control signal to function.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Muscle of the Robot" Analogy:**
> Think of an electric motor as the **"muscle" of a robotic system**. Just as human muscles convert chemical energy (from food) into mechanical motion (to lift, push, or rotate a limb), an electric motor converts electrical energy (from a battery or mains supply) into precise mechanical rotation. The **brain** of the robot (the microcontroller) sends **electrical signals** (analogous to nerve impulses) that tell the muscle (motor) **how fast** to contract (rotate) and **how much force** (torque) to apply.

Imagine a ceiling fan: the **stator** (stationary part) creates a magnetic field, and when current flows through the **rotor** (rotating part), the interaction produces a twisting force (**torque**) that spins the blades. Replace the fan blades with a robot's joint, and you have the core of robotic actuation.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following fundamental constants and metrics govern the operation of every electric motor:

- **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \text{H/m}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$
- **Standard units in motor datasheets:** Torque in **N·m** (Newton-metre), Speed in **RPM** (Revolutions Per Minute) or **rad/s**, Power in **Watts (W)** or **Horsepower (HP)**, where $1 \; \text{HP} = 745.7 \; \text{W}$.
- **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of mechanical output power to electrical input power, expressed as a percentage. State-of-the-art servo motors achieve $\eta > 90\%$.

### 1.4 Why Electric Motors Are Central to Robotics

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Module 4:**
> Electric motors are the *only* class of actuator capable of delivering the **high precision**, **fast dynamic response**, **programmability**, and **clean operation** demanded by modern robotic manipulators, mobile robots, drones, and humanoid systems. Hydraulic and pneumatic actuators, while powerful, are bulky, noisy, and difficult to digitally control — making electric motors the de facto standard.

The five *irreplaceable advantages* of electric motors in robotics are:
1. **Precision:** Sub-degree positional accuracy (typical: $\pm 0.01^\circ$ in servo motors).
2. **Controllability:** Speed and torque are independently controllable via voltage/current.
3. **Cleanliness:** No fluid leaks; suitable for medical and food-grade robotics.
4. **Compactness:** High power-to-weight ratio (e.g., BLDC motors exceed **5 kW/kg**).
5. **Compatibility:** Easily interfaced with digital controllers (Arduino, Raspberry Pi, PLCs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principles

The operation of every electric motor rests on **two foundational electromagnetic laws**:

#### Law 1: The Lorentz Force Law (Force on a Current-Carrying Conductor)
When a conductor carrying current $I$ is placed in a magnetic field of flux density $B$, it experiences a force $F$ perpendicular to both the current direction and the field direction.

$$F = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

where:
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (in Tesla, T)
- $I$ = current through the conductor (in Amperes, A)
- $L$ = length of the conductor within the field (in metres, m)
- $\theta$ = angle between current direction and magnetic field

This force, when applied tangentially at a radius $r$ from the motor's axis of rotation, produces a **torque** $T$:

$$T = F \cdot r = B \cdot I \cdot L \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The torque produced by a motor is **directly proportional to the current** flowing through its windings and the **strength of the magnetic field**. This is why motor controllers modulate current (not voltage, in advanced drives) to regulate torque output.

#### Law 2: Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction (Back-EMF)
When a conductor moves through a magnetic field (or equivalently, when the magnetic flux through a coil changes), an electromotive force (EMF) is induced. In a *spinning* motor, this manifests as the **Back-EMF** ($E_b$), which opposes the applied voltage (per Lenz's Law).

$$E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$$

where:
- $K_e$ = back-EMF constant (in V·s/rad or V/(rad/s))
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of the rotor (in rad/s)

### 2.2 Classification of Electric Motors (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Electric motors are broadly classified into two families based on the type of power supply:

| **DC Motors (Direct Current)** | **AC Motors (Alternating Current)** |
| :--- | :--- |
| Brushed DC Motor | Induction (Asynchronous) Motor |
| Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor | Synchronous AC Motor |
| Servo Motor (DC-based) | Universal Motor (AC/DC) |
| Stepper Motor | Reluctance Motor |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **KTU 2024 Robotics syllabus (PCRAT205)**, the *most important* motor categories are **DC motors, Stepper motors, Servo motors, and BLDC motors**, as these are predominantly used in robotic joints, wheels, and grippers.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **high-yield equations** that appear frequently in KTU University Examinations for this topic.

| **Equation** | **Symbol** | **Physical Meaning** | **SI Units** |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $F = B I L \sin(\theta)$ | $F$ | Lorentz force on conductor | N (Newton) |
| $T = F \cdot r$ | $T$ | Mechanical torque produced | N·m |
| $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $E_b$ | Back-EMF induced in armature | V (Volt) |
| $V = E_b + I_a R_a$ | $V$ | Applied armature voltage | V (Volt) |
| $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ | $T$ | Motor torque constant relation | N·m |
| $P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega$ | $P_{\text{out}}$ | Mechanical output power | W (Watt) |
| $P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_a$ | $P_{\text{in}}$ | Electrical input power | W (Watt) |
| $\eta = \dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta$ | Efficiency of the motor | \% |
| $\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60}$ | $\omega, N$ | Angular velocity from RPM | rad/s |
| $K_e = K_t$ (in SI units) | $K_e, K_t$ | Equality of motor constants | V·s/rad = N·m/A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Sign:** The $K_e$ and $K_t$ constants are **numerically equal in SI units**. This stems from the **conservation of energy** — a motor running in reverse as a generator must produce the same constant. This relationship is testable and frequently appears in KTU derivations.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are not merely academic — they underpin the **selection, sizing, and control** of motors in real robotic systems:

- **Robotic Arm Design:** Engineers use the torque equation $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ to determine the *maximum current* a motor driver must supply to lift a given payload at a given distance from the joint axis.
- **Speed Control:** By varying the applied voltage $V$ (and thus $\omega$), engineers implement **PWM (Pulse Width Modulation)** speed controllers in mobile robots.
- **Battery Sizing:** The power equation $P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_a$ is used to compute the **current draw** of a motor, which dictates the **Ah (Ampere-hour) rating** of the battery needed for a given mission duration.
- **Thermal Management:** The difference $P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}}$ represents **power loss** (largely as heat in $I_a^2 R_a$ copper losses), which dictates the **heat sink** or **cooling fan** design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: The Voltage Equation of a DC Motor

**Objective:** Derive the fundamental voltage equation relating applied voltage, back-EMF, armature resistance, and armature current.

**Step 1 — State Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) for the armature circuit.**
When a DC voltage $V$ is applied across the armature terminals, the current $I_a$ flows through the armature winding. This current encounters two opposing voltages:
- The **voltage drop** across the armature resistance $R_a$ (given by Ohm's law: $I_a R_a$).
- The **back-EMF** $E_b$ generated by the rotating armature (per Faraday's Law).

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop.**
The sum of voltage rises equals the sum of voltage drops:

$$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$

This is the **fundamental DC motor voltage equation**.

**Step 3 — Substitute the back-EMF expression.**
From Faraday's Law, we know that $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$. Substituting:

$$V = I_a R_a + K_e \cdot \omega$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the armature current $I_a$.**
Rearranging the equation to isolate $I_a$:

$$I_a = \frac{V - K_e \cdot \omega}{R_a}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result physically.**
- At **start-up** ($\omega = 0$): $I_a = V / R_a$ — this is the *stall current*, which is very high (often 5–10× the rated current). This is why motors need **soft-start circuits** or **current limiters**.
- At **no-load** (high $\omega$): $I_a$ is very small, just enough to overcome friction.
- At **rated load**: $I_a$ takes its rated (nameplate) value.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in robotics:** Robotic motors must be protected from the *inrush stall current* during start-up, otherwise the driver MOSFETs or the motor windings will burn out. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Torque Equation of a DC Motor

**Objective:** Prove that the electromagnetic torque $T$ developed in a DC motor is directly proportional to the armature current and the machine's geometric/magnetic constants.

**Step 1 — Recall the Lorentz force on a single conductor.**
For a single conductor of active length $L$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ carrying current $I_a$:

$$F = B \cdot I_a \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Account for the number of conductors and poles.**
Let $Z$ be the total number of armature conductors and $P$ be the number of magnetic poles. The conductors are arranged around the armature periphery. The *total tangential force* is distributed among multiple parallel paths. After detailed electromagnetic analysis (which accounts for the number of parallel paths $A = 2$ for a wave winding or $A = P$ for a lap winding), the **net torque** can be expressed as:

$$T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

where $\Phi$ is the flux per pole (in Webers, Wb).

**Step 3 — Define the machine constant.**
Group the geometric and magnetic terms into a single constant $\phi$ (called the *machine constant* or *motor constant*):

$$\phi = \frac{P \cdot Z}{2 \pi A}$$

**Step 4 — Write the compact torque equation.**
Substituting $\phi$:

$$T = \phi \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$$

**Step 5 — Express in terms of the torque constant $K_t$.**
In modern notation, the flux $\Phi$ is often treated as a constant (for a separately excited motor with fixed field current), and we define $K_t = \phi \cdot \Phi$:

$$\boxed{T = K_t \cdot I_a}$$

This proves that **torque is linearly proportional to armature current** — a foundational result in motor control.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mechanical Power Output and Efficiency

**Objective:** Derive the expression for mechanical power output and the overall efficiency of a DC motor.

**Step 1 — Mechanical power from torque and speed.**
The instantaneous mechanical power delivered to the shaft is:

$$P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the torque expression.**
Using $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ and $\omega = E_b / K_e$:

$$P_{\text{out}} = K_t \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{E_b}{K_e}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the SI equality $K_t = K_e$.**
Since the torque constant and back-EMF constant are numerically equal in SI units:

$$P_{\text{out}} = K_e \cdot I_a \cdot \frac{E_b}{K_e} = E_b \cdot I_a$$

**Step 4 — Compute electrical input power.**
The electrical power drawn from the supply is:

$$P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_a$$

**Step 5 — Calculate efficiency.**
The efficiency is the ratio of mechanical output to electrical input:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{E_b \cdot I_a}{V \cdot I_a} = \frac{E_b}{V}$$

Since $E_b < V$ (due to the voltage drop $I_a R_a$), we confirm that $\eta < 1$ (i.e., less than 100%), consistent with the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**.

**Step 6 — Express power losses explicitly.**
The power lost as heat in the armature winding (called **copper loss** or **$I^2R$ loss**) is:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{out}} = I_a^2 \cdot R_a$$

This derivation is critical for **thermal design** of robotic actuators in continuous-duty applications.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Key Relationships at a Glance

| **Operating Condition** | **Speed ($\omega$)** | **Current ($I_a$)** | **Torque ($T$)** | **Back-EMF ($E_b$)** | **Physical Interpretation** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| No-load (ideal) | Maximum | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 0$ | $\approx V$ | Frictionless idealisation |
| Normal loaded | Rated | Rated | Rated | $< V$ | Designed operating point |
| Stall (blocked rotor) | $0$ | Maximum (stall) | Maximum (stall) | $0$ | Highest current, risk of burnout |
| Overspeed (above rated) | $> \omega_{\text{rated}}$ | $< I_{\text{rated}}$ | $< T_{\text{rated}}$ | $> E_{b,\text{rated}}$ | Field-weakening region |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Electric Motors (Hierarchical Architecture)

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the comprehensive taxonomy of electric motors as covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCRAT205.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ELECTRIC MOTORS] --> B[DC MOTORS]
    A --> C[AC MOTORS]
    A --> D[SPECIAL PURPOSE MOTORS]
    B --> B1[Brushed DC Motor]
    B --> B2[Brushless DC BLDC Motor]
    B --> B3[Servo Motor]
    B --> B4[Stepper Motor]
    C --> C1[Induction Motor]
    C --> C2[Synchronous Motor]
    C --> C3[Universal Motor]
    D --> D1[Linear Motor]
    D --> D2[Reluctance Motor]
    D --> D3[Piezoelectric Motor]
    B1 --> B1A[Permanent Magnet DC]
    B1 --> B1B[Shunt Wound DC]
    B1 --> B1C[Series Wound DC]
    B1 --> B1D[Compound DC]
    B2 --> B2A[Inrunner BLDC]
    B2 --> B2B[Outrunner BLDC]
    B3 --> B3A[Positional Servo]
    B3 --> B3B[Continuous Rotation Servo]
    B4 --> B4A[Variable Reluctance Stepper]
    B4 --> B4B[Permanent Magnet Stepper]
    B4 --> B4C[Hybrid Stepper]
    C1 --> C1A[Squirrel Cage IM]
    C1 --> C1B[Wound Rotor IM]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Motor Control System

The following diagram illustrates how an electric motor is *integrated* into a robotic control loop, from command input to mechanical output.

```mermaid
graph LR
    MCU[MICROCONTROLLER OR PLC] --> DRV[MOTOR DRIVER CIRCUIT]
    DRV --> PWM[PWM SIGNAL GENERATOR]
    PWM --> MOT[ELECTRIC MOTOR]
    MOT --> SHAFT[ROTATING SHAFT AND LOAD]
    SHAFT --> ENC[ENCODER OR RESOLVER]
    ENC --> FB[FEEDBACK SIGNAL]
    FB --> MCU
    PWR[DC OR AC POWER SUPPLY] --> DRV
    MOT -. BACK EMF .-> DRV
    style MCU fill:#FFD700
    style MOT fill:#87CEEB
    style ENC fill:#90EE90
    style PWR fill:#FFA07A
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of a Brushed DC Motor

This diagram decomposes the *internal physical structure* of the most fundamental DC motor, showing the stator, rotor, commutator, and brush assembly.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[STATOR ASSEMBLY] --> SP[PERMANENT MAGNETS OR FIELD WINDINGS]
    S --> SY[STATOR YOKE PROVIDES MAGNETIC PATH]
    R[ROTOR ASSEMBLY] --> RA[ARMATURE CORE LAMINATED IRON]
    R --> RW[ARMATURE WINDINGS]
    R --> RC[COMMUTATOR SEGMENTS]
    BR[BRUSH ASSEMBLY] --> BC[CARBON BRUSHES]
    BR --> BH[BRUSH HOLDERS]
    BC -. CONTACT .-> RC
    M[OVERALL MOTOR] --> S
    M --> R
    M --> BR
    style SP fill:#FFB6C1
    style RW fill:#ADD8E6
    style RC fill:#FFFFE0
    style BC fill:#D3D3D3
```

### 4.4 Functional Flow: Energy Conversion Process

```mermaid
graph LR
    ELEC[ELECTRICAL ENERGY INPUT] --> EMF[INTERACTION OF CURRENT AND MAGNETIC FIELD]
    EMF --> MECH[MECHANICAL ROTATIONAL ENERGY OUTPUT]
    EMF -. BACK EMF .-> ELEC
    MECH --> LOSS[FRICTION AND WINDAGE LOSSES]
    EMF --> HLOSS[HEAT LOSSES IN WINDINGS]
    style ELEC fill:#FFE4B5
    style MECH fill:#98FB98
    style EMF fill:#DDA0DD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the *dotted feedback arrow* from EMF to ELEC labelled "BACK EMF." This is the **counter-voltage** generated by the spinning rotor, which is a hallmark of all rotating electric machines and is critical to their self-regulating behaviour.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** Define an electric motor. List any **two advantages** of using electric motors over hydraulic actuators in robotic systems.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An electric motor is an electromechanical device that converts electrical energy into mechanical (rotational) energy using the interaction of magnetic fields and current-carrying conductors, governed by the Lorentz force law.
>
> *Two advantages over hydraulic actuators:*
> 1. **Higher precision and controllability:** Electric motors can achieve sub-degree positional accuracy and are easily controlled via PWM or digital signals, whereas hydraulic systems suffer from fluid compressibility and non-linear valve response.
> 2. **Cleaner and quieter operation:** Electric motors produce no fluid leaks and operate with much lower noise, making them ideal for indoor, medical, and human-collaborative robotic applications.
> *(Third point optionally credited: Lower maintenance, compact size, easier integration with digital controllers.)*

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q2.** What is **back-EMF** in a DC motor? Why is it significant in motor control?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Back-EMF (denoted $E_b$) is the electromotive force induced in the armature winding of a motor *opposing* the applied voltage, due to the relative motion between the armature conductors and the magnetic field. Mathematically, $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$.
>
> *Significance in motor control:*
> 1. **Self-regulation:** Back-EMF causes the motor to draw *more current at low speeds* (when $E_b$ is small) and *less current at high speeds*, providing a natural current-limiting mechanism that prevents runaway acceleration.
> 2. **Speed estimation:** In sensorless motor control, the magnitude of $E_b$ is used as an indirect measurement of the rotor speed, eliminating the need for a physical tachometer.
> 3. **Efficiency indicator:** The ratio $E_b / V$ directly gives the motor's efficiency under load.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Derive the **voltage equation** and **torque equation** of a DC motor. Clearly state the meaning of each symbol used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **220 V DC shunt motor** has an armature resistance of $R_a = 0.5 \; \Omega$ and a field resistance of $R_f = 110 \; \Omega$. When running at no-load, it draws a total current of $5 \; \text{A}$ from the supply and runs at $1200 \; \text{RPM}$. When loaded, the armature current rises to $50 \; \text{A}$. Calculate: **(i)** the no-load back-EMF, **(ii)** the loaded back-EMF, **(iii)** the loaded speed in RPM, and **(iv)** the developed torque under load. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Voltage Equation:*
Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the armature circuit, the applied voltage $V$ is balanced by the voltage drop across the armature resistance and the back-EMF:

$$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$

Since $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$, we obtain:

$$V = I_a R_a + K_e \cdot \omega$$

*Torque Equation:*
The Lorentz force on a single armature conductor of length $L$ carrying current $I_a$ in a magnetic field of flux density $B$ is $F = B I_a L$. For a machine with $Z$ total conductors, $P$ poles, and $A$ parallel paths, the net electromagnetic torque developed at the shaft is:

$$T = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z \cdot I_a}{2 \pi A}$$

Defining the machine constant $K_t = \frac{P \cdot \Phi \cdot Z}{2 \pi A}$, the torque equation reduces to:

$$T = K_t \cdot I_a$$

*Symbol meanings (briefly):* $V$ = applied voltage; $I_a$ = armature current; $R_a$ = armature resistance; $E_b$ = back-EMF; $K_e$ = back-EMF constant; $\omega$ = angular speed; $T$ = developed torque; $K_t$ = torque constant; $P$ = number of poles; $\Phi$ = flux per pole; $Z$ = total conductors; $A$ = parallel paths.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Given:* $V = 220 \; \text{V}$, $R_a = 0.5 \; \Omega$, $R_f = 110 \; \Omega$, $I_{L,\text{NL}} = 5 \; \text{A}$ at $N_{\text{NL}} = 1200 \; \text{RPM}$, $I_{a,\text{loaded}} = 50 \; \text{A}$.

*Step 1 — Field current (assumed constant in shunt motor):*
$$I_f = \frac{V}{R_f} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \; \text{A}$$

*Step 2 — No-load armature current:*
$$I_{a,\text{NL}} = I_{L,\text{NL}} - I_f = 5 - 2 = 3 \; \text{A}$$

**(i) No-load back-EMF:**
$$E_{b,\text{NL}} = V - I_{a,\text{NL}} \cdot R_a = 220 - (3)(0.5) = 220 - 1.5 = 218.5 \; \text{V}$$

**(ii) Loaded back-EMF:**
$$E_{b,\text{loaded}} = V - I_{a,\text{loaded}} \cdot R_a = 220 - (50)(0.5) = 220 - 25 = 195 \; \text{V}$$

**(iii) Loaded speed in RPM:**

In a shunt motor, the flux $\Phi$ is approximately constant (since $I_f$ is constant), so $E_b \propto \omega \propto N$. Therefore:

$$\frac{N_{\text{loaded}}}{N_{\text{NL}}} = \frac{E_{b,\text{loaded}}}{E_{b,\text{NL}}}$$

$$N_{\text{loaded}} = 1200 \times \frac{195}{218.5} = 1200 \times 0.8924 \approx 1070.9 \; \text{RPM}$$

**(iv) Developed torque under load:**
First, compute angular velocity:
$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1070.9}{60} \approx 112.15 \; \text{rad/s}$$

The developed (electromagnetic) power is:
$$P_{\text{dev}} = E_{b,\text{loaded}} \cdot I_{a,\text{loaded}} = 195 \times 50 = 9750 \; \text{W}$$

The developed torque is:
$$T = \frac{P_{\text{dev}}}{\omega} = \frac{9750}{112.15} \approx 86.94 \; \text{N·m}$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $E_{b,\text{NL}} = 218.5 \; \text{V}$ (ii) $E_{b,\text{loaded}} = 195 \; \text{V}$ (iii) $N_{\text{loaded}} \approx 1071 \; \text{RPM}$ (iv) $T \approx 86.94 \; \text{N·m}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **classification of electric motors** used in robotic applications. Compare the salient features of **DC servo motors**, **stepper motors**, and **BLDC motors**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **BLDC motor** used in a robotic drone has the following specifications: rated voltage $V = 24 \; \text{V}$, no-load speed $N_0 = 6000 \; \text{RPM}$, and torque constant $K_t = 0.05 \; \text{N·m/A}$. When the rotor is locked (stalled), the motor draws a current of $I_{\text{stall}} = 12 \; \text{A}$. Calculate: **(i)** the armature (phase) resistance $R_a$, **(ii)** the back-EMF constant $K_e$ in SI units, **(iii)** the no-load current (assuming friction torque $T_f = 0.1 \; \text{N·m}$), and **(iv)** the maximum efficiency at rated conditions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

*Block Diagram (text representation):*
- **Electric Motors → DC Motors (Brushed DC, BLDC, Servo, Stepper) | AC Motors (Induction, Synchronous, Universal) | Special Purpose (Linear, Reluctance, Piezoelectric).**
- For robotic applications, the focus is on: **DC Servo**, **Stepper**, and **BLDC**.

*Comparison Table:*

| **Feature** | **DC Servo Motor** | **Stepper Motor** | **BLDC Motor** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Commutation | Brushed (mechanical) | Open-loop, no brushes | Electronic (ESC) |
| Position Feedback | Required (encoder) | Not required (open-loop) | Required for precise control |
| Holding Torque | Low when de-energised | High holding torque | High, smooth |
| Speed Range | Moderate | Low to moderate | High (10,000+ RPM) |
| Typical Application | Robotic joints, CNC | 3D printers, low-cost positioning | Drones, EVs, robotic arms |
| Cost | Moderate | Low | High |
| Efficiency | 60–70% | 30–60% | 85–95% |

*Verdict:* For high-performance robotic arms → **DC Servo** or **BLDC**; for open-loop positioning → **Stepper**; for high-speed, high-efficiency applications → **BLDC**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

*Given:* $V = 24 \; \text{V}$, $N_0 = 6000 \; \text{RPM}$, $K_t = 0.05 \; \text{N·m/A}$, $I_{\text{stall}} = 12 \; \text{A}$, $T_f = 0.1 \; \text{N·m}$.

**(i) Armature resistance $R_a$:**
At stall, $\omega = 0$, so $E_b = 0$. The entire applied voltage drops across $R_a$:

$$R_a = \frac{V}{I_{\text{stall}}} = \frac{24}{12} = 2 \; \Omega$$

**(ii) Back-EMF constant $K_e$:**
In SI units, $K_e = K_t = 0.05 \; \text{V·s/rad}$ (equivalently, $0.05 \; \text{V/(rad/s)}$).

**(iii) No-load current $I_0$:**
At no-load, the motor must develop just enough torque to overcome friction $T_f$:

$$T_0 = T_f = K_t \cdot I_0 \implies I_0 = \frac{T_f}{K_t} = \frac{0.1}{0.05} = 2 \; \text{A}$$

**(iv) Maximum efficiency:**
Efficiency is $\eta = E_b / V = 1 - (I_a R_a / V)$.
To find the maximum, take the derivative and set to zero, or equivalently, $\eta$ is maximum when the variable loss $I_a^2 R_a$ equals the constant losses. However, a simpler KTU-level approach:

At maximum efficiency, the **variable loss** ($I_a^2 R_a$) equals the **constant loss** (friction + windage). The constant mechanical power loss at no-load is:

$$P_{\text{const}} = T_f \cdot \omega_0 = 0.1 \times \frac{2 \pi \times 6000}{60} = 0.1 \times 628.32 \approx 62.83 \; \text{W}$$

At maximum efficiency, $I_a^2 R_a = P_{\text{const}} = 62.83$:

$$I_a = \sqrt{\frac{62.83}{2}} = \sqrt{31.415} \approx 5.605 \; \text{A}$$

Back-EMF: $E_b = V - I_a R_a = 24 - (5.605)(2) = 24 - 11.21 = 12.79 \; \text{V}$.

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{E_b}{V} = \frac{12.79}{24} \approx 53.3\%$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $R_a = 2 \; \Omega$ (ii) $K_e = 0.05 \; \text{V·s/rad}$ (iii) $I_0 = 2 \; \text{A}$ (iv) $\eta_{\max} \approx 53.3\%$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to separate field current from armature current in a shunt motor** (Question A part b). Students often write $I_a = 5 \; \text{A}$ instead of first computing $I_f = V/R_f$ and then $I_a = I_L - I_f$. **This mistake alone costs 1–2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Unit confusion between RPM and rad/s.** Always convert RPM to rad/s using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ when computing torque from $P = T \omega$. **KTU examiners specifically test this conversion.**
>
> 3. **Mixing up $K_e$ and $K_t$.** Remember: in SI units, $K_e = K_t$, but in other unit systems, this equality does **not** hold without a conversion factor. **State the unit explicitly** in your answer.
>
> 4. **Not stating the assumption of constant flux** in a shunt motor when using $E_b \propto N$. This assumption is what allows the speed ratio formula. **Failing to state it can cost 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **In back-EMF derivations, students often write $E_b = B l v$** without explaining the variables or restricting the derivation scope. Always follow with a substitution like $v = \omega r$ and then the number of conductors and poles.
>
> 6. **Maximum efficiency condition:** Many students incorrectly write $\eta_{\max} = 50\%$ for any DC motor. This is a *myth*. The 50% rule only holds when constant losses equal variable losses *and* the motor is operating at the *specific* current where this balance occurs. You must compute it for the given values.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Module 4: Overview of Electric Motors**

- ✅ An **electric motor** converts electrical energy to mechanical rotational energy using the **Lorentz force** ($F = BIL$).
- ✅ The **back-EMF** ($E_b = K_e \omega$) is the *self-induced opposing voltage* that naturally limits current draw at high speeds.
- ✅ The **fundamental voltage equation**: $V = I_a R_a + E_b$.
- ✅ The **fundamental torque equation**: $T = K_t \cdot I_a$ (torque is *linearly proportional* to armature current).
- ✅ **In SI units:** $K_e$ (in V·s/rad) is **numerically equal** to $K_t$ (in N·m/A). Always state both constants with units.
- ✅ **Mechanical output power:** $P_{\text{out}} = T \cdot \omega = E_b \cdot I_a$.
- ✅ **Electrical input power:** $P_{\text{in}} = V \cdot I_a$.
- ✅ **Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}} = E_b / V < 1$.
- ✅ **Power loss (copper loss):** $P_{\text{loss}} = I_a^2 R_a$ — dissipates as heat, critical for thermal design.
- ✅ **Stall current** ($I_{\text{stall}} = V / R_a$) is the maximum current, occurring at $\omega = 0$ — risk of burnout.
- ✅ **Angular velocity conversion:** $\omega = 2 \pi N / 60$, where $N$ is in RPM.
- ✅ **Classification:** DC (brushed, BLDC, servo, stepper) and AC (induction, synchronous, universal).
- ✅ For **robotics**: BLDC = high efficiency; Servo = precise position; Stepper = open-loop low-cost positioning.
- ✅ In a **shunt motor**, $I_f = V / R_f$ is *constant*, hence $\Phi \approx$ constant, hence $E_b \propto N$ (linear speed-voltage relationship).
- ✅ **Mermaid diagram interpretation** is a key competency — examiners may ask you to *draw and label* the block diagram of a motor control system.
- ✅ Remember the **two-way energy conversion**: a motor becomes a generator when mechanically driven (used in regenerative braking of robotic vehicles).
- ✅ **Faraday's Law** ($E = -d\Phi/dt$) and **Lorentz Force Law** ($F = qv \times B$) are the two electromagnetic foundations — quote them in derivations.
- ✅ Always **label diagrams** in your exam answers with clear annotations of stator, rotor, commutator, and brushes (for brushed DC motors).
- ✅ In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is typically **CO-mapped to CO3** (Analyse and select actuators for robotic systems) at **Bloom's Level: Understand / Apply / Analyse**.
- ✅ Marks distribution in ESE (End Semester Exam): **Part A (3M × 2 = 6 marks) + Part B (14M × 1 = 14 marks) → 20 marks** for this module.
- ✅ **Practice numerical problems** involving no-load and loaded conditions of shunt motors — this is the **most frequently asked numerical type** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid-State Switches, Solenoids, and Piezoelectric Actuator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRAT205 - SENSORS AND ACTUATORS FOR ROBOTICS (ROBOTICS AND ARTIFICIAL ENGINEERING)
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Solid-State Switches, Solenoids, and Piezoelectric Actuator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO3** — *Identify and analyse various solid-state switching devices, electromagnetic actuators, and smart-material-based actuators used in robotic drive and control systems.* It forms the core of Module 4 in the PCRAT205 syllabus.

---

## 1.1 Solid-State Switches (SSS)

### Formal Definition
A **Solid-State Switch (SSS)** is an electronic switching device fabricated from semiconductor materials (silicon, germanium, gallium arsenide, or silicon carbide) that performs the **ON/OFF control** of electrical power or signal flow without any **moving mechanical parts**, **electrical arcing**, or **physical contact wear**. The control is achieved exclusively through the manipulation of charge carriers (electrons and holes) inside the semiconductor crystal lattice under the influence of an external control signal (voltage or current).

The family includes:
- **Power BJT** (Bipolar Junction Transistor)
- **Power MOSFET** (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)
- **IGBT** (Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor)
- **GTO** (Gate Turn-Off thyristor)
- **TRIAC** (TRIode for Alternating Current)
- **SSR** (Solid-State Relay) — hybrid module packaging

### Conceptual Analogy
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Imagine a **faucet (tap) that you control electronically** instead of by hand. In a normal water tap, a mechanical handle physically moves a rubber washer to open or close the flow. A solid-state switch is like a **"magic" tap where you press a tiny button (control signal)** and an invisible electromagnetic field inside a silicon chip opens or closes the water (current) flow — *no handle moves, no washer wears out, no click is heard*. The "magic" is just physics: applying a small voltage to the gate creates an electric field that either permits or blocks electron movement through the semiconductor.

---

## 1.2 Solenoids

### Formal Definition
A **Solenoid** is a linear (or rotary) electromechanical actuator that converts **electrical energy into controlled mechanical linear motion** (or limited angular motion) by means of a **current-carrying electromagnetic coil** generating a magnetic field that acts upon a movable ferromagnetic plunger (armature). The magnetic force developed overcomes a restoring spring force to produce a precisely controlled stroke.

Two main subclasses used in robotics:
- **DC Solenoids** (continuous-duty, pull-type, push-type)
- **AC Solenoids** (shaded-pole, clapper-type)
- **Proportional / Servo Solenoids** (variable-stroke with position feedback)
- **Latching (Bistable) Solenoids** (zero-power holding)

### Conceptual Analogy
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Picture a **wire wrapped around a nail** connected to a battery — the nail becomes a magnet and pulls another piece of iron towards it. A solenoid is **exactly that principle, engineered precisely**: when you energise the coil, a metal rod inside gets *yanked* into the coil's hollow core. When you cut the power, a small spring pushes the rod back to its original position. It is the "**click**" you hear in car door locks, washing-machine latches, and the pneumatic valves of industrial robots.

---

## 1.3 Piezoelectric Actuator

### Formal Definition
A **Piezoelectric Actuator** is a **smart-material-based positioning device** that exploits the **converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect** — the property of certain anisotropic crystals (quartz, lead zirconate titanate [PZT], barium titanate, PVDF polymer) to undergo **mechanical strain (deformation)** when subjected to an externally applied **electric field**. Unlike electromagnetic actuators, piezo actuators produce **nanometre-level displacements** with **sub-millisecond response times** and theoretically infinite resolution.

Key geometric configurations:
- **Stack Actuator** (longitudinal d33 mode)
- **Bimorph / Unimorph Bender** (transverse bending mode)
- **Shear Actuator** (d15 mode)
- **Tube / Ring Actuator**
- **Piezoelectric Motor (USM / Inertia Motor)**

### Conceptual Analogy
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Hold a **salt crystal on a table and rub a balloon** above it — the salt jumps up because the balloon's static electricity distorts the invisible electric field around it. Now imagine a special ceramic that does the same thing *internally*: apply a few hundred volts across a thin ceramic disc and it **physically expands by a few micrometres** — about the thickness of a single bacterium. Stack thousands of these discs together and you can push a robotic gripper with **atomic-scale precision** and **lightning-fast response**. This is exactly how the **probe in a scanning-tunnelling microscope** (which can image individual atoms) and the **tip-tilt mirror of an adaptive telescope** move.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Curie Temperature Constraint:**
> All piezoelectric materials **lose their piezoelectric properties above the Curie Temperature ($T_C$)**, transitioning to a non-polar paraelectric phase. For PZT ceramics, $T_C \approx \mathbf{300^{\circ}C}$ to $\mathbf{350^{\circ}C}$; exceeding this permanently de-poles the material. This is a frequent KTU viva question.
