# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Solid-State Switches — Operational Taxonomy

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises three principal categories used in robotic power electronics:

### (A) Power BJT
A three-layer (NPN or PNP) bipolar device. The collector current $I_C$ is controlled by a small base current $I_B$.

$$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$$

where **$\beta$ (DC current gain)** typically ranges from **20 to 200** for power BJTs. Switching frequency is limited to ~**5 kHz** due to **stored minority-carrier charge** in the base region, making BJT unsuitable for high-frequency PWM robotic drives.

### (B) Power MOSFET
A **voltage-controlled majority-carrier device**. Conduction is controlled by the gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ creating an inversion layer (channel).

**Operational regions:**
- **Cut-off:** $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ (threshold, typically **2–4 V**) → switch OFF
- **Ohmic / Triode:** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ and $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{th}$ → switch ON
- **Saturation (active):** Used in linear amplification (not in switching)

The ON-state resistance is $R_{DS(on)}$, with switching times in the **10–100 ns** range — ideal for **PWM-driven DC motors** in robotic joints.

### (C) IGBT (Insulated-Gate Bipolar Transistor)
A hybrid that combines the **high input impedance of a MOSFET gate** with the **low conduction drop of a BJT**. It is the **workhorse of medium-to-high-power robotic motor drives (1 kW – 1 MW)**.

- **Voltage rating:** up to **6.5 kV**
- **Current rating:** up to **3.6 kA**
- **Switching frequency:** up to **20–50 kHz**

### (D) TRIAC — The Bidirectional AC Switch
Used to control **AC loads** (AC solenoids, single-phase AC induction motors in small robotic HVAC / pump units). Equivalent to two SCRs in anti-parallel. Triggered by a gate pulse in either polarity.

### (E) Solid-State Relay (SSR)
A **packaged module** containing an LED (input), a photodetector (isolation barrier providing **galvanic isolation** > **4 kV RMS**), and a power semiconductor output stage (TRIAC, SCR pair, or MOSFET). Used as a **silent, arc-free replacement for electromechanical relays** in robotic control panels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to *compare* solid-state switches, students must always quote **(i) switching speed, (ii) voltage/current rating, (iii) drive requirement (voltage vs current), and (iv) conduction loss**. This is a guaranteed 7-mark structure in Part B questions.

---

## 2.2 Solenoids — Operating Principle and Force Equation

When a current $I$ flows through a coil of $N$ turns wound on a ferromagnetic core of cross-section $A$ and air-gap length $g$, the magnetic flux in the gap is:

$$\phi = \frac{N \cdot I}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{N \cdot I \mu_0 A}{g}$$

where $\mathcal{R}$ is the reluctance of the magnetic circuit (gap-dominated). The energy stored in the air gap is:

$$W = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{B^2}{\mu_0} \cdot A \cdot g$$

The **magnetic force (Coulomb/virtual-work method)** is:

$$\boxed{F = -\frac{dW}{dg} = \frac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0} = \frac{\mu_0 A N^2 I^2}{2 g^2}}$$

This equation is the **single most important KTU formula** for solenoid analysis.

**Force-vs-Stroke Characteristic:** Because $F \propto 1/g^2$, the force is **maximum at the beginning of the stroke** (small $g$) and **falls rapidly** as the plunger approaches the fully-inserted position. This non-linear characteristic is critical in robotic valve actuators and clutch-engagement mechanisms.

### Latching (Bistable) Solenoid
Uses **permanent magnets** in the magnetic circuit. Two stable positions — the plunger remains held in place **with zero holding current**, drastically reducing thermal dissipation. Essential for battery-powered robotic systems.

### Proportional Solenoid
A **closed-loop** version where the current is modulated by a PWM signal from a **position-feedback sensor (LVDT or Hall sensor)**, yielding continuously variable stroke — used in hydraulic servo valves of robotic arms.

---

## 2.3 Piezoelectric Actuator — Constitutive Theory

The **linear piezoelectric constitutive equations** (in strain-charge IEEE standard form) are:

$$S_i = s^E_{ij} \cdot T_j + d_{ik} \cdot E_k$$

$$D_k = d_{jk} \cdot T_j + \epsilon^T_{kl} \cdot E_k$$

where:
- $S_i$ = mechanical strain tensor
- $T_j$ = mechanical stress tensor
- $E_k$ = electric field vector
- $D_k$ = electric displacement vector
- $s^E_{ij}$ = elastic compliance at constant electric field
- $d_{ik}$ = **piezoelectric charge/strain coefficient** (the figure of merit)
- $\epsilon^T_{kl}$ = dielectric permittivity at constant stress

**The three principal piezoelectric coefficients are:**

$$d_{33}, \; d_{31}, \; d_{15}$$

- **d33 mode** → electric field parallel to polarisation → longitudinal expansion (used in **stack actuators**)
- **d31 mode** → electric field perpendicular to polarisation → transverse contraction (used in **bimorph benders**)
- **d15 mode** → shear deformation (used in **shear motors**)

### Free Strain of a Stack Actuator
A stack of $n$ ceramic discs of thickness $t$ each, under applied voltage $V$:

$$\boxed{\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = \frac{L}{t} \cdot d_{33} \cdot V}$$

For typical PZT: $d_{33} \approx \mathbf{300 \times 10^{-12} \; m/V}$, $L$ (total stack length) ~ **30 mm**, $V$ ~ **200 V** → $\Delta L \approx \mathbf{18 \; \mu m}$.

### Blocking Force (Maximum Generable Force)
$$F_{block} = \frac{\Delta L \cdot Y \cdot A}{L} = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot \frac{Y \cdot A}{L}$$

where $Y$ is Young's modulus (~ **60 GPa** for PZT) and $A$ is the electrode area.

### Capacitive Electrical Behaviour
A piezo stack is electrically a **capacitor**:

$$C = \frac{\epsilon_{33}^T \cdot A}{L} \cdot n^2 = n^2 \cdot C_{single \; layer}$$

Stored electrical energy:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot V^2$$

> **Engineering utility:** This stored energy is the *true* mechanical work output. With round-trip efficiency ~ **30–50 %**, piezo actuators are inefficient for continuous high-power motion but **unrivalled for precision and bandwidth**.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units / Typical Value |
|---|----------|--------|---------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Magnetic force on solenoid plunger | $F$ | $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 A N^2 I^2}{2 g^2}$ | Newtons (N) |
| 2 | Inductance of solenoid coil | $L$ | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g}$ | Henry (H) |
| 3 | Energy stored in air gap | $W$ | $W = \dfrac{B^2 A g}{2 \mu_0}$ | Joules (J) |
| 4 | BJT collector current | $I_C$ | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | A |
| 5 | MOSFET on-resistance loss | $P_{cond}$ | $P_{cond} = I_D^2 R_{DS(on)}$ | W |
| 6 | IGBT switching loss | $P_{sw}$ | $P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} V_{CE} I_C (t_{on} + t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | W |
| 7 | Piezo stack free displacement | $\Delta L$ | $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ | m |
| 8 | Piezo blocking force | $F_{block}$ | $F_{block} = \dfrac{d_{33} V \, Y A}{L}$ | N |
| 9 | Piezo capacitance | $C$ | $C = \dfrac{\epsilon_{33}^T A}{L} n^2$ | F |
| 10 | Piezo stored energy | $U$ | $U = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J |
| 11 | Curie temperature (PZT) | $T_C$ | — | $300$–$350^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ |
| 12 | Curie constant (Curie–Weiss) | $C_{CW}$ | $\epsilon_r = \dfrac{C_{CW}}{T - T_C}$ | K (Curie constant) |

> **Notation note:** In all force/stroke diagrams, the gap $g$ is the **physical air-gap length**, not to be confused with the **gate** terminal $G$ of a MOSFET.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Solenoid Pull Force from Energy Balance

We use the **principle of virtual work**: the magnetic force equals the negative rate of change of stored co-energy with respect to air-gap length at constant current.

**Step 1 — Inductance of the magnetic circuit**
The reluctance of the magnetic circuit is dominated by the air gap (ferromagnetic core has very high permeability, so its reluctance is neglected):

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{g}{\mu_0 A}$$

The inductance of the coil is therefore:

$$L = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Stored magnetic co-energy**

For a current-controlled (linear) magnetic system, the co-energy is:

$$W'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} L(i) \cdot i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g} \cdot i^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply the virtual-work theorem**

The force acts to *reduce* the air gap (attractive), so:

$$F = \frac{\partial W'_{mag}}{\partial g}\bigg|_{i=const} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_0 N^2 A \, i^2 \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial g}\left(\frac{1}{g}\right)$$

$$\boxed{F = \frac{\mu_0 A N^2 i^2}{2 g^2}}$$

This is the **standard pull force of a DC solenoid**, scaling with the **square of the current** and the **inverse square of the gap**.

> **Numerical sanity check:** A robotic gripper solenoid with $N = 1000$ turns, $A = 1 \times 10^{-4}\,\mathrm{m^2}$, $i = 0.5\,\mathrm{A}$, $g = 2\,\mathrm{mm}$ yields:

$$F = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(10^{-4})(10^6)(0.25)}{2(4 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 3.93 \; \mathrm{N}$$

This is realistic for a small robotic latching application.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Piezoelectric Stack Displacement

**Given:** A stack actuator composed of $n$ thin PZT discs, each of thickness $t$, poled along the thickness direction (3-axis). Electrodes are applied to the major faces of each disc. The electrode pair is connected in **parallel electrically** and **series mechanically**.

**Step 1 — Field across a single layer**

If the applied voltage is $V$, and all discs see the same voltage (parallel connection), then the electric field in each layer is:

$$E_3 = \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Strain produced in each layer (converse effect)**

$$S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E_3 = d_{33} \cdot \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Free displacement of one layer**

$$\delta = S_3 \cdot t = d_{33} \cdot V$$

**Step 4 — Total displacement of the stack (sum of $n$ layers)**

$$\Delta L = n \cdot \delta = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$$

**Step 5 — Alternative form using total stack length $L = n \cdot t$:**

$$\boxed{\Delta L = \frac{L}{t} \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot \frac{L}{t}}$$

**Numerical example:** A 30 mm long PZT stack with 100 layers of 0.3 mm thickness, $d_{33} = 300 \times 10^{-12}\,\mathrm{m/V}$, $V = 200\,\mathrm{V}$:

$$\Delta L = 100 \times (300 \times 10^{-12}) \times 200 = 6 \times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{m} = 6\,\mu\mathrm{m}$$

This is the **typical full-stroke displacement** of a mid-range industrial piezo stack.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Piezo Blocking Force

**Step 1 — Mechanical stress at zero free displacement (clamped case)**

When the stack is constrained (blocked), the strain is zero. The applied electric field $E_3$ must be exactly balanced by the mechanical stress-induced strain $s^E_{33} \cdot T_3$:

$$S_3 = s^E_{33} T_3 + d_{33} E_3 = 0$$

$$\Rightarrow T_3 = -\frac{d_{33} E_3}{s^E_{33}} = -\frac{d_{33} V / t}{s^E_{33}}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to force using electrode area $A$:**

$$F_{block} = T_3 \cdot A = \frac{d_{33} V \, A}{t \cdot s^E_{33}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Young's modulus $Y_3 = 1/s^E_{33}$:**

$$\boxed{F_{block} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot V \cdot A \cdot Y_3}{t} = \frac{d_{33} \cdot V \cdot Y_3 \cdot A \cdot n}{L}}$$

since $L = n \cdot t$.

**Numerical example:** With $d_{33} = 300 \times 10^{-12}\,\mathrm{m/V}$, $V = 200\,\mathrm{V}$, $A = 10 \times 10^{-6}\,\mathrm{m^2}$ (10 mm²), $Y_3 = 60 \times 10^9\,\mathrm{N/m^2}$, $t = 0.3\,\mathrm{mm}$:

$$F_{block} = \frac{(300 \times 10^{-12})(200)(10 \times 10^{-6})(60 \times 10^9)}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$F_{block} \approx 1{,}200\,\mathrm{N} = 1.2\,\mathrm{kN}$$

This **kilonewton-class force** in a fingertip-sized device is why piezo actuators dominate in **scanning probe microscopes, adaptive optics, and inkjet printheads**.

---

## 3.4 Force–Stroke Trade-off of a Piezo Actuator

The product of blocking force $F_{block}$ and free displacement $\Delta L$ is the actuator's **figure of merit** (proportional to work output):

$$F_{block} \cdot \Delta L = \frac{d_{33}^2 \cdot V^2 \cdot Y_3 \cdot A \cdot n^2}{L^2} \cdot t = \frac{d_{33}^2 \cdot V^2 \cdot Y_3 \cdot A \cdot n}{L}$$

The **linear elastic characteristic** is:

$$F(\Delta x) = F_{block} \left(1 - \frac{\Delta x}{\Delta L}\right)$$

where $\Delta x$ is the actual displacement delivered against an external load $F$. This is a **straight-line force-stroke curve** — fundamentally different from the non-linear $1/g^2$ solenoid curve.

---

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Solid-State Relay (SSR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CTRL[Control Input 3 to 32 V DC] --> LED[Infrared LED Isolator]
    LED -->|Optical coupling 4 kV isolation| PD[Photodiode Detector]
    PD --> TRIG[Zero Cross Trigger Circuit]
    TRIG --> GATE[Gate Drive Amplifier]
    GATE --> POW[Power Semiconductor Output TRIAC or MOSFET pair]
    POW --> LOAD[Robotic Load AC Motor or Solenoid Coil]
    LOAD --> POW

    style CTRL fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1
    style LED fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style PD fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style TRIG fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style GATE fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style POW fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style LOAD fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Interpretation:** The control input (typically a 5 V logic signal from a robotic microcontroller) energises an infrared LED. The light crosses an **opto-isolator** dielectric gap (the safety barrier), hits a photodiode that drives a zero-cross trigger circuit, which then fires the gate of a TRIAC or MOSFET pair. The output switches the high-power load (robotic motor, solenoid, heater) with **no galvanic path** between low-voltage logic and mains power.

---

## 4.2 Solenoid Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUPP[DC Power Supply V supply] --> COIL[Copper Coil N turns on bobbin]
    COIL -->|Magnetomotive force MMF = NI| FLUX[Magnetic Flux Density B in core]
    FLUX --> GAP[Air Gap g variable with plunger position]
    GAP --> FORCE[Axial Pull Force F equals B squared A over 2 mu0]
    FORCE --> PLUNG[Plunger / Armature Displacement x]
    PLUNG --> SPRING[Return Spring k N per m]
    SPRING --> REST[Rest Position x equals 0]
    PLUNG --> OUT[Mechanical Work Output to robot valve or latch]

    style SUPP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style COIL fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style FLUX fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style GAP fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style FORCE fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style PLUNG fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style SPRING fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238
    style OUT fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

**Interpretation:** The supply energises the coil, creating MMF that drives flux through the iron core and across the air gap. The flux density squared (energy density $B^2/2\mu_0$) integrated over the gap volume gives the energy, and the force is the *gradient* of that energy with respect to gap length. The spring provides a restoring force so the plunger returns when de-energised.

---

## 4.3 Piezoelectric Stack Actuator Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HV[High Voltage Amplifier 0 to 200 V DC or PWM] --> ELEC[Top Electrode]
    ELEC --> L1[Layer 1 PZT d33 poled]
    L1 --> INT1[Internal Electrode]
    INT1 --> L2[Layer 2 PZT d33 poled]
    L2 --> INT2[Internal Electrode]
    INT2 --> L3[Layer 3 PZT d33 poled]
    L3 --> Ln[Layer n PZT d33 poled]
    Ln --> BASE[Base Electrode]
    ELEC --> BASE

    L1 --> STR[Mechanical Strain in each layer s equals d33 times E]
    L2 --> STR
    L3 --> STR
    Ln --> STR
    STR --> SUM[Total Free Displacement Delta L equals n times d33 times V]
    SUM --> OUT[Sub micrometer Precision Output to nanopositioning stage]

    style HV fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style ELEC fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style BASE fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style L1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style L2 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style L3 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style Ln fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style STR fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style SUM fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style OUT fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

**Interpretation:** Layers are connected electrically in **parallel** (every disc sees the full $V$) but mechanically in **series** (strokes add). The poling direction of every layer is identical, and the electrodes alternate polarity so all layers expand in the same direction.

---

## 4.4 Bimorph Piezo Bender Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Applied Voltage V with polarity] --> TOP[Top Piezo Layer poled down]
    TOP --> MID[Middle Bonding Shim or passive layer]
    MID --> BOT[Bottom Piezo Layer poled up]
    BOT --> GND[Ground]

    TOP --> EX1[Top layer expands strain plus s]
    BOT --> EX2[Bottom layer contracts strain minus s]
    EX1 --> BEND[Composite Beam Bends Tip Deflection delta]
    EX2 --> BEND
    BEND --> TIP[Tip Deflection delta equals 3 d31 L squared over 2 t]

    style V fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style GND fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238
    style TOP fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style MID fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style BOT fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style BEND fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style TIP fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
```

**Interpretation:** When voltage is applied, one layer expands while the other contracts. The opposing strains cause the whole composite beam to **bend** like a bimetallic strip, generating a *tip displacement* that is much larger than the free strain but with a much smaller force. Used in **piezoelectric fans, speakers, micro-pumps, and miniature robotic legs**.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Define a solid-state switch. Compare BJT and power MOSFET as switching devices for robotic motor drives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Solid-State Switch (SSS)** is an electronic switching device made from semiconductor material that controls power flow using only the movement of charge carriers, with no moving mechanical parts. (1 mark)

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | BJT | Power MOSFET |
|---|---|---|
| Control quantity | Base current $I_B$ | Gate voltage $V_{GS}$ |
| Drive power | High (current driven) | Very low (voltage driven) |
| Switching speed | Slow (~1–5 µs) | Fast (~10–100 ns) |
| Conduction loss | Moderate ($V_{CE(sat)} \approx 1$–$2$ V) | Low ($I^2 R_{DS(on)}$) |
| Frequency limit | ~5 kHz | Up to ~1 MHz |
| Robustness | Second-breakdown limit | SOA (Safe Operating Area) limited |

> For robotic PWM-driven DC servomotors, **MOSFET is preferred** because of its faster switching and simpler drive requirements.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**State and explain the converse piezoelectric effect. Why is it relevant to actuators?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect** is the phenomenon whereby certain anisotropic crystals (e.g., PZT, quartz, BaTiO$_3$) undergo **mechanical strain (deformation)** when subjected to an **external electric field**. (1.5 marks)

Mathematically, the induced strain is:
$$S = d \cdot E$$
where $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient and $E$ is the applied electric field. (1 mark)

**Relevance to actuators (0.5 mark):** This effect is the *fundamental working principle* of all piezoelectric actuators, enabling direct conversion of **electrical energy → mechanical strain** with nanometre resolution, sub-millisecond response, and no magnetic field or moving parts (except the microscopic crystal deformation).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the pull force of a DC solenoid in terms of air-gap length $g$, number of turns $N$, current $I$, and cross-sectional area $A$. Discuss how the force–stroke characteristic influences its use in robotic gripper mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **latching (bistable) solenoid**. State two key advantages over a conventional solenoid in battery-powered robotic systems. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.A (a)

**Step 1 — Magnetic circuit (1 mark)**
For a solenoid with a movable plunger, the magnetic circuit comprises a high-permeability ferromagnetic core and an air gap of length $g$. The gap dominates the reluctance:

$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{g}{\mu_0 A}$$

**Step 2 — Inductance (1 mark)**
$$L(i) = \frac{N^2}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g}$$

**Step 3 — Co-energy (1 mark)**
$$W' = \frac{1}{2} L i^2 = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A i^2}{2 g}$$

**Step 4 — Apply virtual-work principle (1.5 marks)**
$$F = \frac{\partial W'}{\partial g}\bigg|_i = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 A i^2}{2 g^2}$$

**Step 5 — Discussion on robotic gripper application (2.5 marks)**
- Force varies as $1/g^2$: maximum at beginning of stroke → provides strong *initial gripping impulse*.
- Force decays rapidly as plunger closes → *holding force* is weak, so a **mechanical latch or detent** is often added.
- For a *proportional robotic gripper*, a linear force-stroke profile is required → a **proportional solenoid with closed-loop current control** is preferred over a simple ON/OFF solenoid.
- **Time response:** The solenoid's mechanical time constant is governed by $L/R$ and the moving mass; for fast pick-and-place, the coil is *over-excited* (boost voltage) and then held at reduced current.

---

### Model Solution to Q.A (b)

**Diagram (3 marks) — Description since drawing is rendered above in Section 4.2:**
The latching solenoid has a permanent magnet in the magnetic circuit. Two stable end positions are defined by mechanical stops. The coil is wound such that:
- A *forward* current pulse repels the permanent magnet flux and drives the plunger to the **extended** position, where it is held by the permanent magnet alone.
- A *reverse* current pulse reverses the coil flux, overcomes the permanent magnet, and returns the plunger to the **retracted** position, again held with zero coil power.

**Two advantages in battery-powered robots (4 marks):**
1. **Zero holding-power consumption** — the permanent magnet holds the position indefinitely without current, critical for **mobile robots** and **drone-mounted actuators** where battery life is paramount.
2. **Reduced thermal management** — no continuous $I^2R$ heating → smaller heat sinks, lighter payload, longer coil insulation life.
3. (Bonus, optional credit) **Faster response** — coil only needs short pulses, so it can be designed with low thermal mass and high pulse current.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Sample Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **piezoelectric stack actuator**. Derive the expression for its free displacement and blocking force. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare piezoelectric actuators with electromagnetic solenoids in terms of **displacement, force, bandwidth, energy efficiency, and resolution**. State two industrial robotic applications of piezoelectric actuators. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Q.B (a)

**Construction (2 marks):**
A piezoelectric stack actuator consists of $n$ thin PZT ceramic discs (typical thickness $0.1$–$0.5$ mm) metallised on both faces. Layers are bonded mechanically in series but wired electrically in parallel — every disc sees the full drive voltage $V$ across its thickness $t$. The poling direction is uniform (along axis 3). External electrodes at the two ends carry the high-voltage drive signal (typically $0$–$200$ V, sometimes up to $1$ kV for high-stack designs).

**Working (1 mark):**
When $V$ is applied, every layer experiences $E_3 = V/t$ → strain $S_3 = d_{33} E_3$ → free expansion $\delta = d_{33} V$ per layer. Strokes add up, giving total $\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$.

**Derivation — Free Displacement (2 marks):**
Starting from $S_3 = d_{33} E_3$ and $E_3 = V/t$, with stack length $L = n \cdot t$:

$$\Delta L = n \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = \frac{L}{t} \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$$

**Derivation — Blocking Force (2 marks):**
At zero displacement, $S_3 = 0 \Rightarrow s^E_{33} T_3 + d_{33} E_3 = 0$, so $T_3 = -d_{33} V / (t \cdot s^E_{33})$. Multiplying by electrode area $A$ and substituting $Y_3 = 1/s^E_{33}$:

$$F_{block} = \frac{d_{33} V A Y_3}{t} = \frac{d_{33} V Y_3 A n}{L}$$

---

### Model Solution to Q.B (b)

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Piezoelectric Actuator | Electromagnetic Solenoid |
|---|---|---|
| Typical displacement | 1 – 100 µm (stack); up to 1 mm (bimorph) | 1 – 25 mm |
| Force output | 100 N – 10 kN | 0.5 N – 50 N (small), up to 500 N (large) |
| Bandwidth | Up to 100 kHz | Limited to ~50–200 Hz |
| Resolution | Sub-nanometre (theoretically infinite) | Limited by friction & hysteresis (~10 µm) |
| Energy efficiency | Low (30–50% round-trip) | Moderate (50–70%) |
| Power consumption at hold | Capacitive (near zero leakage) | Continuous I²R |
| Susceptibility to EM interference | Immune | Generates EMI |
| Vacuum compatibility | Excellent | Needs magnetic materials |

**Two industrial robotic applications (3 marks):**
1. **Scanning Probe Microscopy (SPM / AFM) nanopositioning stages** — piezo stacks provide the sub-Å positioning required for atomic-resolution imaging.
2. **Inkjet printheads** — thousands of piezo channels eject ink droplets at >50 kHz with precise drop volumes; widely used in industrial graphics and bioprinting.
3. (Bonus) **Active vibration damping in robotic arms** — piezos integrated into the structure sense and counter flexural vibrations at kHz rates.
4. (Bonus) **Adaptive optics in space robotic telescopes** — bimorph deformable mirrors correct wavefront distortion in real time.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Loss Zones**
> 1. **Forgetting to square the current in the solenoid force equation** — writing $F \propto I$ instead of $I^2$ costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up $d_{33}$ and $d_{31}$** — $d_{33}$ is the *longitudinal* coefficient used in stack actuators; $d_{31}$ is the *transverse* coefficient used in bimorphs. Confusing them gives an order-of-magnitude error.
> 3. **Not stating the operating mode of a solid-state switch** — BJT is *current-controlled*, MOSFET and IGBT are *voltage-controlled*. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Forgetting the Curie temperature de-poling caveat** in piezo questions — always mention $T_C$ when discussing material selection.
> 5. **Ignoring the opto-isolation voltage rating** in SSR questions — the **4 kV RMS** galvanic isolation is a *defining* feature, not an incidental one.
> 6. **Writing "TRIAC = two SCRs in anti-parallel"** without mentioning the **bidirectional gate trigger** — partial credit only.
> 7. **In a force-stroke derivation, jumping straight to the final equation** without showing the virtual-work differentiation step — at least 2 marks lost on the 7-mark sub-part.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must-know for KTU ESE)**

- A **Solid-State Switch (SSS)** has *no moving parts* and uses only semiconductor charge-carrier control to switch power; the three workhorses are **BJT, MOSFET, IGBT**; **TRIAC** for AC; **SSR** is the *packaged* version.
- **BJT** = current-driven, slow, robust; **MOSFET** = voltage-driven, fast, high-frequency PWM; **IGBT** = combined advantages, used in industrial motor drives above 1 kW.
- A **TRIAC** conducts in both AC half-cycles, triggered by a single gate, equivalent to two anti-parallel SCRs.
- A **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** uses **opto-isolation** (>4 kV) between control and power sides — the keyword for KTU is *galvanic isolation*.
- A **DC Solenoid** produces an **attractive force** $F = \dfrac{\mu_0 A N^2 I^2}{2 g^2}$ that scales with the **square of current** and **inverse-square of gap** — peak force at the start of stroke.
- **Inductance** of the energising coil: $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{g}$ — *not constant*, decreases as the plunger closes.
- **Latching solenoids** use **permanent magnets** to hold position with **zero holding current** — preferred in battery-powered robots.
- **Proportional solenoids** use **closed-loop current control** with **position feedback** to provide variable stroke — used in hydraulic servo valves of robotic arms.
- A **Piezoelectric Actuator** uses the **converse piezoelectric effect** ($S = d \cdot E$) in materials like **PZT** to produce nanometre displacements.
- **Stack actuator** → uses **d33 mode** → $\Delta L = n d_{33} V$; **bimorph** → uses **d31 mode** → large bending tip displacement.
- **Blocking force** $F_{block} = d_{33} V Y_3 A / t$ — can reach **kilonewtons** from a fingertip-sized stack.
- **Curie temperature** $T_C$ (~ 300–350 °C for PZT) is the operational ceiling; above it, the material **permanently de-poles**.
- **Resolution** of a piezo is *theoretically infinite* (limited only by noise); **bandwidth** can exceed **100 kHz**.
- **Energy efficiency** of a piezo is *low* (30–50%) — not used for continuous high-power work; ideal for **precision positioning, fast valves, vibration damping, nanopositioning stages, inkjet printheads, and adaptive optics**.
- A piezo stack is electrically a **capacitor** ($C \propto n^2$); stored energy $U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$.
- **Comparison mantra for KTU:** *Piezo = precision + bandwidth, Solenoid = stroke + simplicity, SSS = silent + arc-free switching.*
